title: Analysis of DCE Iterations Using ResNetDCE for Low-Light Enhancement
authors: Riyadi Jimmy Iskandar, Chastine Fatichah, Anny Yuniarti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Low-light image enhancement is a critical challenge in computer-vision systems. The Deep Curve Estimation (DCE) framework provides an efficient zero-shot solution. However, its performance is closely tied to the number of iterative enhancement cycles used. This study analyzed the effect of DCE-Net iterations on the new hybrid architecture, ResNetDCE. This model uses a pre-trained ResNet18 to extract features. It also includes a modified DCE-Net for adjusting the illumination. We tested iteration counts of 3, 6, 9, and 12 iterations. We measured performance using three metrics: Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM), and Learned Perceptual Image Patch Similarity (LPIPS). We also considered the computational cost. Our tests on the LOL dataset demonstrate that 9-iterations yield the optimal balance, achieving a peak PSNR of 21.05 dB and SSIM of 0.8006, with negligible increases in the inference time and memory footprint. This study found a link between pixellevel and perceptual metrics. The LPIPS improved beyond the PSNR peak. This suggests a trade-off between pixel-fidelity and perceptual naturalness. This study offers the first evidence for selecting the optimal DCE iterations. Hybrid feature-curve architectures work well and set benchmarks for future research on low-light enhancement.

title: MBSE in Space Mission Concept Development
authors: Blake DeVaney, Maxwell Harrow, Debarati Chattopadhyay, Matthew Morris, Dannie Smith, Jason Arcido
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: In recent years, Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) has been in the spotlight as a powerful methodology for defining and designing highly complex aerospace systems. MBSE facilitates a systems engineer's ability to create relationships between elements of a system's design and the underlying analytical models, and simplifies the process of creating diagrams to communicate the system design to stakeholders. This paper presents an application of MBSE techniques to address interdependencies in a system design to improve system budgets, trade space analysis, and traceability through the system from stakeholder needs to performance metrics. In space mission design, interdependencies exist between all subsystems/components in a system. Some interdependencies are direct (such as mass affecting propulsion needs), and others are indirect (such as power affecting solar array mass and therefore propulsion needs). The conceptual design of space missions thus involves collaborative efforts from system and subsystem engineers, conducting trades and analysis to develop valid designs. However, traditional approaches often result in outdated subsystem evaluations due to evolving interdependencies. To resolve this, we propose an MBSE framework, ACEBox, which integrates subsystem analytical models with a unified system-level model. By providing a digital thread that exchanges information between a SysML Systems model and an array of spacecraft subsystem analysis tools, ACEBox enables digital capture of design changes that propagate through the system. This approach enables rapid, parameterized analysis of design alternatives, dynamically sizing systems based on predefined rules and rollups for critical budgets like the Master Equipment List (MEL), the Power Equipment List (PEL), the Data Budget, and the Link Budget. The single source of truth MBSE model enables the systems engineer to generate block diagrams (hierarchical and internal), showing the connections and interfaces between components linked to the architecture used for budget generation. To validate this approach, a case study is presented showcasing the performance of spacecraft design decisions against a set of mission requirements. This demonstrates the agility of using MBSE methods across a set of digitally integrated engineering design and analysis tools for system concept development, concurrently saving time and improving system performance.

title: Maneuver Detection Based Adaptive Transition Probability Matrix for Improved IMM Estimate
authors: Bibhabasu Mondal, Viji Paul Panakkal, Rajbabu Velmurugan, A Vengadarajan, A.K. Pathak
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: A tracking system can efficiently track a target if the motion model used by the system match the target's motion model. The Interacting Multiple Model (IMM) algorithm is commonly employed in tracking systems for this purpose. IMM uses a set of models to represent the possible evolution of the target's state. Since the target's motion can switch between different modes, the IMM algorithm must switch accordingly. The switching between models is governed by the Transition Probability Matrix (TPM), which plays a key role in determining both the estimation accuracy and the response time of the tracker. In the conventional Interacting Multiple Model (CIMM) algorithm, the TPM is pre-defined and set heuristically. The diagonal elements of the TPM represent the probability that a model will continue in its current state, which directly affects the accuracy of the estimate. A higher value for the diagonal elements typically leads to more accurate estimates in the case of model matched filtering. However, larger diagonal elements also result in slower model switching, as the off-diagonal elements determine the speed at which models switch during changes in the target's motion. This creates a tradeoff between accuracy and responsiveness, limiting the performance of the CIMM algorithm. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a likelihood-based approach (ATPM_L) for updating the TPM. In the proposed method, the likelihood of each model is computed with respect to the current scan measurement and the TPM update factor is derived based on these likelihoods. This likelihood-based approach satisfies both accuracy and switching speed requirements of TPM in IMM algorithm. This algorithm also offers a faster model switching mechanism compared to all existing TPM update algorithms.

title: Closed-Loop Modeling and Simulation of Phase-Shift Full-Bridge Converter with Current Doubler Output
authors: Kasemsan Siri, Joshua Stewart, Markell Hardee, Natan Ranjbar
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: To fulfill low-voltage high-power applications in RF communication, a large-signal averaged model is developed for a zero-voltage-switched (ZVS), phase-shifted full-bridge (PSFB) converter with current doubler output (CDO). Analytically included in the model derivations are the large-signal effects due to the leakage inductance of the converter's isolation transformer for autonomous operation in both continuous and discontinuous conduction modes (CCM & DCM). Its fundamental small-signal frequency-response characteristics are also extracted from the developed model, which can be easily implemented into a circuit analysis program (PSPICE & LTSpice) for both CCM and DCM. Furthermore, the closed-loop model of the PSFB-CDO converter is derived to include the large-signal average model of both the outer voltage-regulation control loop and the inner current control loop using current-mode control (CMC), which includes the active slope (RAMP) compensation, guaranteeing sub-harmonic free operation across CCM and DCM boundaries. The average model of the closed-loop PSFB-CDO converter is also developed and further validated by an as-is pulse-by-pulse switching model of the same dual-closed-loop control converter. Additional lessons learned from this analytical modeling and simulation are (1) discovery of the non-uniform current sharing within the two output inductors in the conventional CDO stage under transient closed-loop and steady-state operation at light load conditions, (2) discovery of a practical solution, employing two synchronously controlled rectifier switches in addition to the two conventional output rectifiers. The added synchronous rectifiers (SR) have restored uniform current sharing within the two CDO's inductors, ensuring symmetric utilization of the B-H magnetic characteristics of the transformer's core material, and leading to no saturation of magnetic flux density in the transformer.

title: IoT-Enabled Smart Surveillance with Raspberry Pi and CNN for Real-Time Missing Persons Identification
authors: Marthandan T, Premalatha S, Govindaraju P, Tharani G, Mithra Kowri P K, Pradhap S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The purpose of the research is to apply a realtime face recognition system on Raspberry Pi based on CNN to detect faces, CNN and FaceNet to extract and recognize features, and openCV to process images to enhance the recognition efficiency and accuracy under different environmental conditions. Group 1 denotes a control face recognition with the help of OpenCV and CNN, whereas Group 2 denotes the proposed face recognition with the help of CNN, and FaceNet on Raspberry Pi. Each group is taken to have a sample size of 150 facial images and different light and processing conditions are factored in data collection. The performance measures include recognition accuracy, processing time and system latency as key performance metrics.As it was observed in the experiment, the suggested CNN and FaceNet framework on Raspberry Pi was more efficient than the baseline OpenCV and CNN system. In particular, the recognition accuracy of the proposed system was <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 6. 9 \%}$</tex> as compared to 89.4 of the proposed baseline system. The system latency went down by 7.3 % and the processing time came down to 1.6 seconds per image. Such findings indicate that the suggested framework is more accurate, quicker to process, and more effective in general about the conditions of different lighting. The results show that the suggested CNN and FaceNet framework on Raspberry Pi has better accuracy and efficiency in face recognition compared to traditional ones and is applicable to real-time embedded applications.

title: Frequency-Constrained Unit Commitment Incorporating Wind Farm PFR Parameters and System Damping Dynamics
authors: Min Wang, Pengfei Zhu, Chaoyang Du, Zhenxin Huang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: As wind power penetration continues to rise, the inertia of power systems declines, posing substantial challenges to frequency security and stability in the event of sudden power disturbances. To tackle these issues, this study introduces a frequency security-constrained unit commitment (FCUC) model that incorporates variable frequency regulation parameters of wind turbines and system damping ratios. First, a system frequency response (SFR) model is constructed, and this model incorporates frequency assistance provided by wind turbine units and synchronous generators. Analytical expressions for frequency constraints under different system damping levels are then derived. Subsequently, this virtual inertia and droop coefficients for wind turbines have been converted into decision variables, enabling further development of the FCUC model with variable wind turbine frequency regulation parameters and system damping ratios. Finally, this IEEE 39-bus test system has been employed for verifying the proposal model. The results indicate that adjusting these frequency parameters for wind turbines allows effective coordination between diverse frequency regulation resources, thereby enhancing the economic operation of the system.

title: International Experience and Insights on Energy Storage Price Formation Mechanisms
authors: Zhaoyu Chong, Lizi Zhang, Ying Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: New energy storage serves as a core technology and foundational infrastructure for the emerging power system, and rational, effective pricing mechanisms are critical to its development. However, price formation mechanisms for energy storage participation in electricity markets differ substantially across countries and market designs, highlighting the need for a systematic examination of the current development landscape. This study investigates pricing mechanisms for new energy storage power plants, synthesizes practices and lessons from representative countries, analyzes China s ongoing explorations of storage pricing, and proposes recommendations to improve China s energy-storage price formation mechanisms.

title: Crowd Vision: A Real-Time Zone-Based Crowd Density Monitoring and Early Overcrowding Alert System Using YOLOv8
authors: Hemaswathi S, Jeya A Celin J, Sivagurunathan R, Sanjai K, Sheik Abdul Rahuman M, Sanjaykrishnaa T
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In busy public spaces such as transportation hubs, religious gatherings, festivals, and large events, crowd conditions can shift quickly and unpredictably. If crowd buildup is not detected early, it may lead to unsafe overcrowding and serious risks. Traditional monitoring systems rely mainly on total headcount, which does not reflect spatial distribution, allowing localized congestion to go unnoticed. This paper presents CrowdVision, a real-time vision-based monitoring and alert system focused on zone-based density analysis. Using YOLOv8 with IoU and center-distance deduplication, it ensures accurate counting. Grid-based zoning detects hotspots, and critical zones trigger instant audio-visual alerts through a Flask dashboard for proactive crowd management.

title: Multi-Agent Research Synthesis Under Governed Communication: A Deontic-Agentic Approach to Epistemic Governance
authors: Varun Joshi, Nikita Kothari, Lav Kumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: As Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) evolve into autonomous research orchestrators, they have developed a critical vulnerability known as Emergent Collusion. When optimized for convergence speed or performance, agents frequently engage in “Spontaneous Cartelization,” a behavior where they prioritize mutual agreement over factual grounding. This leads to a “hallucination of consensus” where agents negotiate away safety constraints to reach plausible but inaccurate results. Traditional governance models are often reactive, inspecting only terminal outputs and missing logical drift buried within intermediate dialogue. To address this, we introduce the Governed Communication Protocol (GCP), a framework implementing a Deontic Orchestration Layer (DOL) within a stateful LangGraph architecture. Our primary contribution is In-Process Dialogue Governance, which treats communication edges as active “Semantic Firewalls”. This allows for the enforcement of formal Modal Logic constraints-Obligation (O), Permission (P), and Prohibition (F)-directly on inter-agent messages. Evaluated within a Synthetic Regulatory Sandbox (SRS), the framework demonstrated a 98.8% Safety Violation Adherence rate and reduced the Safety Violation Rate from 22.4 % to 1.2 %. This architecture provides a robust, verifiable path for agentic reasoning in high-stakes regulatory environments by ensuring”Truth-by-Design”

title: Robust PMSM Traction Control for Electric Vehicles: An Improved Reaching Law Based Non-Singular Fast Terminal Sliding Mode Approach
authors: Guolong Zhong, Guangping Li, Li Liu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper develops a robust traction-drive control strategy for permanent magnet synchronous motor (PMSM) in electric vehicle (EV) propulsion systems, aiming to improve transient behavior while alleviating chattering. A redesigned reaching mechanism is combined with a non-singular finite-time terminal sliding mode manifold to promote rapid error elimination and to smooth the discontinuous switching action. Simulation studies under speed changes and step load disturbances verify that the proposed approach yields quicker transients and stronger disturbance rejection than traditional reference controllers.

title: IoT Basedintegrated Design for Aeroponic Plant Cultivation Using GRU Algorithm
authors: K.R. Gokul Anand, B.T. Tharanisrisakthi, Nithyakumar M, Boopathy. S, J. Dhanasekar, Dinesh E
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Innovative methods for sustainable and efficient food production are needed since the population is growing, the climate is changing, and there isn't enough land. Aeroponic systems, which grow plants without soil, help plants develop faster, save water, and make them less likely to get sick. Combining aeroponics with IoT-enabled Smart Farming lets you monitor and change things like temperature, humidity, pH, and fertilizer levels in real time. This improves plant growth and resource use. This paper presents a Gated Recurrent Unit-based predictive paradigm for IoT-enabled aeroponic systems. The GRU model incorporates temporal correlations in time-series environmental data for accurate real-time forecasting and adaptive management with little computer complexity. To optimize plant growth, the NodeMCU microcontroller processes sensor data in the cloud and monitors critical operational components. We used MAE, MAD, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$R^{2}$</tex> to assess the effectiveness of the proposed GRU model. As opposed to more common methods like Random Forest (RF), Modified Long Short-Term Memory (MLSTM), and Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN). Compared to earlier approaches, the GRU model has the best <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{R}^{2}(0.96)$</tex> and the lowest MAE (1.88) and MAD (1.69). This shows that it accurately predicts photosynthetic rates. This study shows that predictive intelligence with GRUs improves aeroponic farming, crop yields, and sustainability.

title: ABIS 2026: The Effects of the Adaptive Web on Society
authors: Laura Stojko, Eelco Herder, Jannis Strecker-Bischoff, Julia Seitz Sänger, Thomas Neumayr, Enes Yigitbas, Mirjam Augstein
publish: Companion Publication of the 2026 18th ACM Web Science Conference

title: Preset-Time Distributed Optimization for Nonlinear Multiagent Systems With a Time-Varying Equality Constraint Over Directed Networks
authors: Wanli Jin, Huaguang Zhang, Yapeng Yang, Zhijie Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Informatics
abstract: This work focuses on a constrained distributed optimization problem for nonlinear multiagent systems over directed communication networks. Under the information constraint that each agent observes only its own objective function and resource signals, a distributed observer is devised to locally reconstruct the constraint-related signals and Hessian-related information needed for optimization. Using these estimates, a preset-time (PST) distributed optimization scheme is formulated for the problem subject to a time-varying equality constraint and guarantees convergence to the optimal solution within a user-assigned time, irrespective of algorithm parameters. In contrast to existing prescribed-time optimization methods, the proposed approach applies to directed networks without requiring weight balance. Moreover, feasible initialization is not required, and the equality constraint is satisfied exactly at the PST and thereafter. The effectiveness and the practical applicability of the proposed algorithm are validated via an economic dispatch problem in power systems.

title: FDPNet: Frequency Guided Prior Decoupling Network for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Kaisheng Li, Zhaolong Gao, Haiyan Du
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing change detection(RSCD) aims to precisely identify the areas where the types or states of ground objects change over time. However, remote sensing(RS) images are often disturbed by factors such as seasons or changes in lighting in practical applications, resulting in pseudo-change noise that is difficult to be effectively suppressed, and the boundary details of subtle change targets are easily lost during downsampling. To solve these problems, this paper designs the Frequency Guided Prior Decoupling Network (FDPNet). Specifically, the frequency guided change modulation (FGCM) module proposed for pseudo changes in this paper uses FFT to capture global frequency differences and effectively suppress high-frequency pseudo change noise. At the same time, the direction aware enhancement module (DSEM) uses direction aware pooling to explicitly aggregate spatial context along the horizontal and vertical directions, strengthening the model's ability to perceive anisotropic geometric structures. Moreover, to achieve precise target localization, we propose the Polarization Background Decoupling Module (PBDM). By utilizing a deep supervision strategy to generate semantic guidance maps, PBDM explicitly decouples foreground changes from background clutter, thereby effectively alleviating the class imbalance problem.

title: Velocity First? Rethinking 3D Object Detection with 4D Millimeter Wave Radar
authors: Changxian Zeng, Wenbo Chu, Lili Fan, Yulong Ding, Yue Tian, Xiaolin Tang, Keqiang Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Multimedia
abstract: Propelled by advances in autonomous driving, accurate vehicle perception is essential for reliable decision-making. Compared with LiDAR, 4D millimeter-wave radar (4D MMW) provides stronger robustness under adverse conditions, lower deployment cost, and direct velocity measurements, motivating increasing research. However, its sparse point clouds limit scene description. This work presents Velo3DF (3D Velocity-aware Multimodal Fusion Network), a radar-camera fusion model that incorporates 3D velocity vectors into 4D MMW-based 3D detection. To mitigate positional redundancy and better utilize velocity information, a lightweight Velocity-Component-Aware module is introduced, compatible with backbones such as PointPillars. For cross-modal integration, an Alignment-Fusion module performs geometric alignment and deep fusion of image and point-cloud features. A single-modality variant, Velo3DF-R, contains 0.702M parameters and runs at 74.02 FPS while maintaining competitive accuracy. Our code is open-sourced at: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/luoyoucode/Velo3DFusion</uri>.

title: Coordinating Multiple Conditions for Trajectory-Controlled Human Motion Generation_supp1-3695772.pdf
authors: Changxing Ding
publish: N/A
abstract: Trajectory-controlled human motion generation aims to synthesize realistic human motions conditioned on both textual descriptions and spatial trajectories. However, existing methods suffer from two critical limitations: first, the conflict between text and trajectory conditions disrupts the denoising process, resulting in compromised motion quality or inaccurate trajectory following; second, the use of redundant motion representations introduces inconsistencies between motion components, leading to instability during trajectory control. To address these challenges, we propose CMC, a decoupled framework that effectively coordinates text and trajectory conditions through a divide-and-conquer strategy. CMC follows a divide-and-conquer paradigm, comprising two cascaded stages: Trajectory Control and Motion Completion. In the first stage, a diffusion model generates a simplified representation of the controlled joints under trajectory guidance, based on the given trajectories, ensuring accurate and stable trajectory following. In the second stage, a text-conditioned diffusion inpainting model generates full-body motions using the simplified representation from the first stage as partial observations. To mitigate overfitting caused by limited inpainting training data, we further introduce the Selective Inpainting Mechanism (SIM), which alternates between text-to-motion generation and motion inpainting tasks during training. Experiments on HumanML3D and KIT datasets demonstrate that CMC achieves state-of-the-art performance in control accuracy and motion quality, demonstrating its effectiveness in coordinating multimodal conditions and representations. Project page: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/CDLCHOI/cmc_release</uri>

title: KubeAI-An AI powered pod placement scheduler for kubernetes
authors: H Dhanyatha, M Lavanya, Rishabh Kumar Lal, N Srivatsha, Veena Gadad, K R Usha Rani, Subrata Gowswami, Harsh Mehra
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Efficient pod scheduling is vital for low latency, balanced resource use, and reliable performance in Kubernetes clusters that handle dynamic microservice workloads. Current methods, including the standard heuristic-based Kubernetes scheduler and reinforcement learning-based schedulers like Deep Reinforcement Scheduler (DRS), mainly depend on static rules or infrastructure-level metrics. This reliance limits their ability to respond to real-time changes in workloads and service interaction patterns. This paper introduces KubeAI, a pod placement scheduler that considers observability and is based on Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). KubeAI integrates real-time telemetry from OpenTelemetry and the Kubernetes Metrics Server. This telemetry includes CPU and memory usage, disk I/O pressure, network latency, node temperature and inter-pod communication frequency. The scheduling policy is applied using a Flask-based Kubernetes scheduler extender. This setup allows for real-time inference, smooth integration with the native Kubernetes scheduler and flexible updates to policies without changing core scheduling components.Tests on a real Kubernetes cluster show that KubeAI reduces pod scheduling latency significantly compared to the default heuristic scheduler. In compute-intensive situations, it has latency levels comparable to DRS. The results also show that KubeAI makes better use of CPU and memory resources and reduces scheduling failures by up to 35% relative to the heuristic scheduler by using infrastructure-level metrics for observability-aware reinforcement learning. KubeAI also reduces time taken for inter-service communication by about 15% compared to heuristic and DRS schedulers by incorporating service interaction patterns into pod placement decisions. These findings indicate that integrating reinforcement learning with comprehensive infrastructure-level and service-level observability enables more efficient and reliable Kubernetes scheduling.

title: An End-to-End Multi-Output Deep Learning Model with Attention Mechanism for Joint Prediction of Ship Position, Course, and Speed Based on AIS Data
authors: Agung Mulyo Widodo, Shao-Hua Hsu, Mao-Hsin Huang, Lie Yang, Chien-Erh Weng, Hsing-Chung Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: For safety at sea, navigation, and traffic management, it is important to be able to accurately forecast the path of a ship. This research introduces a comprehensive multi-output deep learning model incorporating an attention mechanism for the simultaneous prediction of vessel position, course, and speed utilizing Automatic Identification System (AIS) data. The architecture merges bidirectional gated recurrent units (BiGRU) with the Hybrid Grey Wolf Optimizer–Simulated Annealing (GWO-SA) for loss-weight tuning and the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) for prioritizing. Tests using real AIS data from Taiwan’s territorial waters show that the suggested BiGRU-AM-GWO-SA-AHP outperforms LSTM, GRU, and BiLSTM baselines in terms of spatial accuracy (latitude MAE 0.1798; mean Haversine distance 64.41 km), stable course prediction (cos(cog) RMSE 0.2747), and high speed prediction accuracy (SOG R² 0.9932). The results show that combining attention-based bidirectional recurrent modeling with hybrid optimization is a good way to make strong, real-time predictions about maritime trajectories.

title: MSN: Rethinking Cross-Domain Automatic Modulation Recognition via Decoupled Modulation Signal Normalization
authors: Yifan Chen, Xiangrong Zhang, Guanchun Wang, Ziyi Liu, Licheng Jiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Normalization is known to enhance the generalizability of deep neural networks. Curiously, most normalization methods are designed for images, language, and time-series data, without considering the unique characteristics of communication signals. Due to joint impairments from the entire transmission-propagation-reception chain, existing normalizations cannot be directly adapted to Automatic Modulation Recognition (AMR). To this end, this paper rethinks normalization in the context of AMR and uncovers the underlying factors that determine its effectiveness. Specifically, it first identifies that distribution shifts in AMR hinder model performance and systematically classifies distribution shifts in AMR into two types: (1) intra-chain shift, caused by temporal variations due to various physical effects, and (2) inter-chain shift, resulting from spatial variations arising from inherent environmental factors. From this characterization, we argue that intra-chain shifts manifest as temporal variations of signals, while inter-chain shifts introduce instance-specific spatial distortions. Motivated by this insight, this work proposes Modulation Signal Normalization (MSN), a decoupled normalization mechanism. MSN mitigates intra-chain shifts by smoothing feature correlations to extract stationary temporal components. Simultaneously, it extracts instance-specific statistical information and localized spatial details as stationary spatial components, which serve as discriminative cues for inter-chain shifts. This targeted design effectively addresses both types of shifts, thereby enhancing the cross-domain robustness of AMR models. Finally, extensive experiments demonstrate that MSN consistently enhances the generalizability of AMR models, achieving outstanding results.

title: Behavior-Guided Identity Learning for Multiagent Cooperation
authors: Yuan Li, Yuhang Zhou, Shibo Dai, Jianyu Wang, Xiang Wu
publish: IEEE Systems, Man, and Cybernetics Letters
abstract: Effective cooperation in multiagent systems often requires agents to assume different responsibilities, leading to distinct behavior patterns for complementary roles. To characterize such behavioral differences in cooperative multiagent reinforcement learning, we propose Behavior-Guided Identity Learning (BGIL), which learns identity representations from behavior. First, we introduce an identity contrastive objective that maximizes the mutual information between behavior and identity representations, improving inter-agent discriminability. Second, we design a temporal identity consistency mechanism to ensure that different behaviors of the same agent over time can be consistently associated with the same identity. Extensive experiments on the SMAC benchmark demonstrate that BGIL consistently outperforms competitive baselines, especially on heterogeneous tasks.

title: Magnetoresistive Sensors for Emerging Applications: A Comparative Review of Device Performance and Design Trade-Offs
authors: Namita Bindal, Pankaj Pathak, Zainab Aziz, Ravish Kumar Raj, Shailendra Yadav, Sandeep Soni, Seema Dhull, Dhiman Mallick, Yogendra Kumar Mishra, Hari Srikanth, Mahmoud Rasly, Montserrat Rivas, Hadi Heidari, Qiming Shao, Santhosh Sivasubramani, Sonal Shreya
publish: IEEE Electron Devices Reviews
abstract: Magnetoresistive (MR) sensors are solid-state magnetic sensors that enable highly sensitive magnetic field detection with low power consumption,complementary metal-oxide-semiconductor (CMOS) compatibility, and scalable fabrication. This review examines the device physics, architectures, and performance trade-offs of anisotropic magnetoresistance (AMR), giant magnetoresistance (GMR), and tunnel magnetoresistance (TMR) sensors, highlighting their underlying spin-dependent scattering and tunneling mechanisms. Linearization strategies, noise sources, and sensitivity limits relevant to the detection of weak and low-frequency magnetic fields are discussed from a device-design perspective.The review further explores diverse and emerging application domains that demand stringent magnetic sensing performance, such as biosensing, neuromagnetic signal detection, non-contact current sensing, power grid monitoring, intelligent transportation systems. By comparing MR sensor technologies with respect to sensitivity, noise, dynamic range, and integration complexity, this work outlines key challenges and future directions for compact, low-noise, and energy-efficient magnetic sensors for next-generation electronic sensing systems.

title: Bayesian Channel Estimation With Imperfect Sensing Information for Integrated Sensing and Communication
authors: Jinming Zhang, Chenhao Qi, Zhu Han
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In this paper, we investigate channel estimation for an integrated sensing and communication system with orthogonal frequency division multiplexing, and then propose a sensing information (SI)-based Bayesian channel estimation (BCE) scheme. To improve the accuracy of channel estimation by properly using imperfect SI, we analyze the probability distribution of the channel frequency response (CFR) based on the imperfect gain, delay and Doppler shift estimation provided by the sensing system. This distribution can be used as the prior information of the CFR to assist pilot-based channel estimation. Then, combined with the received pilots, the Bayesian estimation method is exploited to acquire the CFR by computing the conditional probability distribution of the CFR and utilizing maximum a posteriori estimation. Simulation results demonstrate that the imperfect SI-based BCE scheme can achieve improved channel estimation accuracy over existing methods.

title: EvoBTSeg: Evolutionary Architecture Search for Compact and Precise Heterogeneous Brain Tumor Segmentation
authors: Farhana Yasmin, Yu Xue, Mahade Hasan, Anurag Sinha, Weiping Ding, Haipeng Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Emerging Topics in Computational Intelligence
abstract: Accurate and efficient segmentation of heterogeneous brain tumors requires neural architectures that can simultaneously capture diverse tumor subregions with varying appearance, scale, and boundary characteristics while remaining computationally compact. However, manually designed networks with fixed architectures often struggle to balance expressive feature representation and model efficiency across such heterogeneity. To address this challenge, we propose EvoBTSeg, an evolutionary architecture search framework that automatically discovers compact and effective network configurations designed for heterogeneous brain tumor segmentation. A customized U-Net supernet is defined with parameterized encoder, bottleneck, and decoder components, enabling flexible control over network depth, filter size, activation function, and dropout rate. The encoder extracts hierarchical representations through convolution, ReLU activation, max-pooling, and dropout to accommodate heterogeneous tumor patterns, while the bottleneck refines global contextual features via convolution and weighted feature aggregation. The decoder progressively restores spatial resolution using transposed convolution and feature concatenation, preserving fine-grained boundary information critical for accurate delineation of small and diffuse tumor subregions. An evolutionary search strategy based on crossover and mutation is employed to explore the architectural search space in a data-driven manner, selecting architectures that balance contextual modeling and boundary precision. Experimental results on the BraTS 2021 dataset demonstrate that EvoBTSeg identifies a compact architecture with only 0.12 M parameters, achieving a dice similarity coefficient (DSC) of 92.83% and a Hausdorff distance (HD95) of 1.38 mm. Furthermore, EvoBTSeg demonstrates reliable generalization, maintaining competitive segmentation performance on BraTS 2020 (DSC 89.37%, HD95 1.33 mm) and the ACDC dataset (DSC 88.03%, HD95 1.41 mm). These results indicate that EvoBTSeg effectively discovers lightweight yet accurate architectures for heterogeneous brain tumor segmentation, highlighting its potential for practical clinical deployment.

title: Buckbeak: A 1.2 TFLOPS/W Reconfigurable Vector/Scalar RISC-V Processor for Precision Scalable Near-Sensor Computing in 22nm FDSOI Technology
authors: Marco Bertuletti, Amirhossein Kiamarzi, Mattia Sinigaglia, Gianmarco Ottavi, Matteo Perotti, Giuseppe Tagliavini, Luca Benini, Davide Rossi
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Solid-State Circuits Society
abstract: Near-sensor computing requires concurrent execution of data-parallel and control-intensive tasks under tight latency and power constraints. Vector processors excel on data-parallel tasks, thanks to their single-instruction multiple-data (SIMD) execution model. However, scalar control-intensive processing can still saturate their instruction fetch frontend, undermining utilization and efficiency. To address this challenge without compromising flexibility and efficiency for data-parallel computations, we present a runtime-configurable dual-core RISC-V vector processor that switches in 5 cycles between Split Mode (two independent vector engines) and Merge Mode (one wide vector plus a scalar programmable core). On a sensor fusion pipeline that mixes scalar-vector workloads, data parallel tasks can be executed on the wide SIMD processor, while the programmable core, decoupled from the vector engines, takes care of scalar tasks, achieving up to 1.47× speedup over Split Mode. The dual-core reconfigurability allows Split Mode performance optimality on homogeneous tasks with modest data parallelism, delivering up to 1.93× speedup over Merge Mode. Implemented in GF22FDX technology, the processor delivers 10.78 GFLOPS@FP64 to 85.64 GFLOPS@FP8 peak performance (at 1 V) and 1.2 TFLOPS@FP8/W peak energy efficiency (at 0.65 V). Thanks to the vector ISA semantics and underlying architecture, Buckbeak is up to 4× more energy-efficient than state-of-the-art multi-core accelerators implemented in the same node. The streamlined reconfigurable design and lightweight instruction fetch frontend yield up to 14.5× better energy efficiency and 15× better area efficiency compared to larger vector processors for edge processing application.
