title: HarmonyQA: A Unified Framework for Harmonization Quality Assessment
authors: Zitong Xu, Huiyu Duan, Guangji Ma, Liu Yang, Jiarui Wang, Sijing Wu, Yunhao Li, Qingbo Wu, Xiongkuo Min, Guangtao Zhai, Patrick Le Callet
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Image or video composition aims to combine the foreground object and the background content to generate a realistic composite image or video, in which harmonization algorithms play a crucial role in adjusting the color or light of the foreground object to make the entire image or video harmonious. However, existing image quality assessment (IQA) and video quality assessment (VQA) methods often fail to align with human preference in evaluating harmonization algorithms due to insensitivity to minor color or light inconsistencies. To address the issue and facilitate the advancement of harmonization, we introduce the first harmonization quality assessment database, termed HarmonyQAD, which consists of Harmony-IQAD comprising 1,800 harmonized images generated by 9 different image harmonizaion algorithms (IHAs), and HarmonyVQAD comprising 1,600 harmonized videos generated by 2 IHAs and 6 video harmonization algorithms (VHAs). All harmonized images and videos are accompanied by mean opinion scores (MOSs), collected through a meticulously designed subjective evaluation experiment. Based on the HarmonyQAD, we propose a unified framework for harmonization quality assessment, termed HarmonyQA, to predict the harmonization quality of both harmonized images and videos. Extensive experiments show that HarmonyQA achieves state-of-the-art performance on our datasets, and also achieves competing results on traditional IQA and VQA tasks. Furthermore, cross-dataset evaluation also shows that HarmonyQA exhibits better generalization ability than self-supervised learning-based IQA methods. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/IntMeGroup/HarmonyQA.

title: Optimistic Thompson Sampling for No-Regret Learning in Unknown Games
authors: Yingru Li, Liangqi Liu, Wenqiang Pu, Hao Liang, Zhi-Quan Luo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: We study the problem of learning to play a repeated multi-player game with an unknown reward function and bandit feedback. The central challenge arises from the need to balance exploration and exploitation under bandit feedback while strategically responding to other players. To address this, we propose Thompson Sampling-based algorithms that leverage available information about opponents’ actions and reward structures, resulting in a significant reduction in regret bound. Building on these insights, we introduce the Optimism-then-NoRegret framework, which encompasses various game algorithms as special cases. Simulation evaluations on three distinct types of games show that our proposed algorithms consistently and substantially outperform standard baselines.

title: Vision-Language Guided Pseudo-Labeling for Multi-Label Waste Classification
authors: Billy Eden William Asrul, Indrabayu, Syafruddin Syarif
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Cost efficient multi-label waste recognition in canals is hindered by clutter, occlusion, and illumination variability, while exhaustive annotation remains costly. This paper proposes TGS a vision-language-guided pseudo-label curation framework that integrates a ConvNeXt-Tiny (Convolutional Neural Network Next Tiny) classifier with Grounding DINO (text-conditioned detection) and the Segment Anything Model (SAM) for spatial verification. In TGS, a ConvNeXt-Tiny teacher trained on a small labeled subset produces image-level confidences on unlabeled images; selected labels are converted into text prompts and verified by Grounding DINO to generate prompt-consistent regions, which are refined into pixel-level masks by SAM. The core novelty lies in SAM-based mask verification, which gates pseudo-label retention using pixel-level evidence rather than confidence-only or box-level cues. Pseudo-labels are retained only when region and mask evidence satisfy quality filters, then combined with human labels to train a student model within a Semi-Supervised Learning (SSL) loop. Experiments use a canal dataset from Makassar, Indonesia (855 images, 18 classes, 2,662 instances); after quality control, 769 images are retained, with a fixed 171image test set and labeled fractions of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$5 \%, 10 \%$</tex>, and 20 % sampled from a 598-image development pool. At <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 \%}$</tex> labeled data <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(86 / 512 / 171)$</tex>, TGS achieves micro-averaged F1-score (micro-F1) 0.9393 with pseudo-label coverage 0.9199, improving reliability over naïve self-training (micro-F1 0.9376, coverage 0.9512) and outperforming FixMatch (micro-F1 0.9036). At 20 % labeled data, micro-F1 reaches 0.9581, approaching the fully supervised upper bound (0.9896). Future work will audit pseudo-label quality on a manually annotated subset, adopt class adaptive thresholds, and benchmark efficiency and robustness for real- time deployment.

title: Predicting Competitive Programming Problem Tags and Ratings from Tutorials Using BERT-Based Models
authors: Albert Yulius Ramahalim, Christoffer Edbert Karuniawan, Tjeng Wawan Cenggoro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In competitive programming, online judges (OJs) are a form of educational technology that host a large number of algorithmic problems. Codeforces is one such OJ that annotates problems with algorithmic tags and difficulty ratings, enabling efficient search for digital learning and practice. However, such metadata is not consistently available across OJs, motivating automated prediction from problem content. Prior approaches mostly use problem statements, whose narrative can obscure the intended technique; in contrast, tutorials and solution code often describe it explicitly. We study whether tutorial text improves prediction quality by comparing BERT-based models trained on (i) statements and (ii) tutorial text plus solution code. We construct CREST, a Codeforces dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8, 9 4 1}$</tex> problems (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{3 6 ~ t a g s}$</tex>) with math-rich statements, tutorials, and solution code, and evaluate multilabel tag prediction and rating regression. For tag prediction, tutorial+code improves PR-AUC from 0.463 to 0.603 and micro-/macro-F1 from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.476 / 0.276$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.563 / 0.371$</tex>. For rating prediction, tutorial+code reduces MAE from 503 to 332 rating points and increases the coefficient of determination from 0.221 to 0.637. Overall, our results indicate that tutorials and solution code provide stronger predictive signals than statements for automated tagging and difficulty estimation.

title: Automated Consistency Checks of Contract Data Requirement Lists and Model-Based Systems Engineering Models Using Natural Language Processing
authors: Matthew Giorgio, Jeremy Marshall, John Roberts, Aleksandra Khusid, Jyotirmay Gadewadikar
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Maintaining consistency between model-based systems engineering (MBSE) models and their corresponding Contract Data Requirement List (CDRL) deliverables is crucial in largescale systems engineering projects. This is especially important when CDRLs are not automatically generated fully and directly from the MBSE tools. Traditionally, consistency check task are manual, labor-intensive, and prone to errors, as it involves line-by-line reviewing of documents against their associated models, which are usually in vastly different formats. This paper introduces an automated tool leveraging machine learning techniques to enhance the efficiency and accuracy of consistency checks between CDRLs and Cameo diagrams. By utilizing Python and its libraries, the tool parses the Cameo models and corresponding CDRLs, employing sequence matching to identify and evaluate document-model mappings of requirements. In addition to this evaluation, the tool also identifies missing information between the CDRL and the models through low matching scores. The implementation of this tool significantly reduces the time required for consistency checks, as demonstrated by a quantitative time study showing a reduction from 22.5 minutes to just 4 seconds for 162 comparisons, with 100 % accuracy. Contractors can often deliver large increments of CDRLs near to their due dates, leaving the Government little time to perform effective reviews without the assistance of such a tool. The tool's limited dependencies facilitate broader applicability across different projects. This research not only addresses existing gaps in automated consistency checks but also provides a foundation for future advancements in the field. The findings underscore the potential for substantial time savings and improved accuracy, while highlighting the necessity of human oversight in handling sensitive information. Future research may further refine and automate these processes, enhancing their utility across diverse programs.

title: State Space Quantification in Reinforcement Learning
authors: Rodney Sanchez, Paul T. Schrader, Jamison Heard
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Neural Network architecture design is currently understood as an art, where the network's hyperparameters are primarily formulated through a synthesis of scientific intuition and investigative methodologies. This process is further exacerbated in reinforcement learning (RL), where informative representations (e.g., t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding or t-SNE plots) are often unavailable to validate the efficiency of an agent's feature extractor. In the context of RL, this amplifies the misunderstanding of the provided algorithm's performance as either a byproduct of learning more descriptive “states” or policy improvements. As a potential solution, we introduce a metric to robustly quantify the agent's internal states by observing the rate of change in the Modern Hopfield Pooling (MHP) associative space. We validate this method's ability to comprehend environmental complexity by testing it on increasingly challenging environments, starting with CartPole and progressing to Breakout. Beyond environment complexity, the method is tested in a variety of convolutional architectures to understand how feature extractors adjust the agent's internal state. Specifically, we test the depth of convolution, filter size, and the inclusion of pooling layers to encapsulate how the agent's internal representation changes. Our results demonstrate that MHP can explain how the complexity of environmental features and the architecture of feature extractors influence the agent's internal state. Overall, the MHP method provides an informative quantitative measure of how an environmental feature or feature extractor forms the agent's internal representation, particularly under complex conditions such as temporal difference learning, or outside the constraints of independent and identically distributed state space representations.

title: An AI-Based Li-Fi Innovative Framework for Optical Security Optimization in Smart Cities
authors: M. A. Hadi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Recent developments in the area of wireless communication have resulted in the introduction of a new type of communication technology called Li-Fi (Light Fidelity). As a new wireless communication method, it has also created new challenges with respect to its security. For instance, the security of data transmitted via Li-Fi can be compromised through a variety of means that traditional wireless-based data security solutions are unable to address. Because of these reasons, we have developed a comprehensive conceptual framework for enhancing the security of Li-Fi, which we refer to as the Innovative Framework for Optical Security Enhancement (IfoSec). Ifo-Sec provides a comprehensive platform for addressing the security-related issues associated with Li-Fi. It provides a multi-layered, integrated security architecture including a physical layer (dynamic beamforming), an optical layer (light) and an application layer (encryption methods). The Ifo-Sec architecture provides an opportunity for the integration of dynamic beamforming with adaptive cryptographic methods and the application of machine learning-based anomaly detection to enhance the security of Li-Fi. The Ifo-Sec architecture addresses several critical areas related to Li-Fi security, such as the need for better integration of the physical and cryptographic layers; challenges associated with the mobility of Li-Fi; and the performance penalty of the traditional cryptographic methods. The empirical results demonstrate that Ifo-Sec offers a superior Eavesdropping Protection Rate (EPR) of 98.7%, with only a marginal Additional Data Throughput Burden (B_DTh) of 4.3%, which is better than those of all existing hybrid security architectures and cryptographic-only architectures. This research presents an opportunity to provide a foundation for developing secure and high-performance optical wireless networks as part of the development of next generation wireless networks.

title: Evaluation of Machine Learning Algorithms and Ensemble Methods for Prediction of Students' Graduation
authors: Soha A. Bahanshal, Vaibhav Verdhan, Bayong Kim, Bushra A. Al-Anesi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Graduation rates at six-year colleges are becoming a more essential indicator for incoming fresh students and for university rankings. Predicting student graduation is extremely beneficial to schools and has a huge potential for targeted intervention. It is important for educational institutions since it enables the development of strategic plans that will assist or improve students' performance in achieving their degrees on time (GOT). A first step and a helping hand in extracting useful information from these data and gaining insights into the prediction of students' progress and performance is offered by machine learning techniques. Data analysis and visualization techniques are applied to understand and interpret the data. The data used for the analysis contains students who have graduated in 6 years in the academic year 2017-2018 for science majors. This analysis can be used to predict the graduation of students in the next academic year. Different Predictive modeling such as logistic regression, decision trees, support vector machines, Random Forest, Naïve Bayes, and KNeighborsClassifier are applied to predict whether a student will graduate. These classifiers were evaluated with k folds of 5. The performance of these classifiers was compared based on accuracy measurement. The results indicated that Ensemble Classifier achieves better accuracy, about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 1. 1 2 \%}$</tex>. This GOT prediction model would hopefully be useful to university administration and academics in developing measures for assisting and boosting students' academic performance and ensuring they graduate on time.

title: HIL Simulation of Multi-Objective Power Quality Mitigation for High-Penetration Renewables and New Loads
authors: Xiaoming Wang, Wenguang Zhao, Bin Xu, Hao Zheng, Yadong Jiao, Mingxing Zhu, Fangfang Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The complex grid integration characteristics of new "dual-high" sources and loads are expected to continue degrading medium- and high-voltage power quality in power systems. The chain Static Var Generator (SVG), capable of implementing multiple mitigation strategies, represents a critical solution to this challenge. To address potential applicability issues arising from limitations in strategy development and validation for medium- to high-voltage applications, this paper constructs a hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) simulation platform for simulating various power quality disturbances and verifying their mitigation. First, the relationship between the grid-connected dynamic characteristics of typical new-type source-load systems and power quality problems is established, clarifying the control requirements and strategic principles for chain SVG. Second, based on the need for controller-in-the-loop verification, the framework of the HIL simulation platform, the aggregated model equivalence method for multi-new-type source-load cluster scenarios, and the operational state setting requirements are outlined. Finally, using an actual controller as an example, targeted validation of multiple control strategies is performed in a renewable energy transmission scenario. The results demonstrate that the constructed HIL platform can comprehensively capture the dynamic response characteristics of various power quality control strategies, providing strong support for full-link verification of complex chain SVG control schemes.

title: Analysis of Cost-Effective Lunar Positioning Constellation with Heterogeneous Satellites
authors: Sae Ogoshi, Yosuke Kawabata, Kenshiro Oguri, Satoshi Ikari, Ryu Funase, Shinichi Nakasuka
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This study proposes a hybrid navigation system that may achieve cost reduction while maintaining positioning accuracy for lunar navigation constellations. Currently proposed Lunar Navigation Satellite System (LNSS) concepts require all satellites to carry expensive GNSS receivers with large high-gain antennas. Our approach strategically divides the constellation into large satellites with GNSS capability and small satellites using only satellite-to-satellite tracking (SST). Through simulations evaluating GNSS+SST performance with 60 -second and 1 -second measurement intervals, we analyzed all 254 possible configurations with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-7$</tex> large satellites out of 8 total. Results suggest that configurations with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1-2$</tex> large satellites can meet the 50 m position accuracy requirement under the assumed conditions, while configurations with 3 or more large satellites show performance variation depending on satellite placement. Additional evaluation of optical navigation revealed performance degradation compared to the GNSS+SST baseline under current implementation. Economic analysis using the USCM8 cost model indicates potential reductions of up to 61 % for initial deployment and 27 % for 10 -year operations compared to an all-GNSS baseline, though these estimates do not account for integration complexity or operational overhead. This analysis assumes ideal clock behavior, employs simplified simulation models, and relies on single runs per configuration rather than Monte Carlo statistics. These preliminary findings suggest that hybrid architectures may warrant further investigation as a pathway toward economically sustainable lunar positioning infrastructure.

title: A Machine-Learning Emulator Framework for Multi-Objective Policy Scenario Simulation and Optimization
authors: Varun Viswapriyan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: We present a computational framework for multi-objective macroeconomic policy optimization that combines a machine-learning dynamic emulator, counterfactual simulation, and surrogate-assisted constrained optimization. Using data series from the FRED-MD macroeconomic dataset, the emulator learns nonlinear transition dynamics from a lagged input state representation. A Sobol low-discrepancy experimental design efficiently explores the feasible policy space and supports surrogate modeling of the policy-response surface for optimization. Multi-objective solutions are obtained via desirability-based aggregation with economic penalty constraints to exclude implausible policy configurations. In counterfactual simulations over a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 2}$</tex>-month horizon, the composite-optimal policy configuration yields consistent improvements relative to baseline, including reductions in financial stress, inflation, and unemployment (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle \text{BAAFFM} \text{<apt>} \mathrm{T} \approx-\text{1. 0}, \triangle \text{C P I} \_\text{Y O Y} \approx-\text{0. 1 2}$</tex>, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\triangle U N R A T E \_L \approx-0.17$</tex>). The reported optimal policy shocks should be interpreted as illustrative scenarios consistent with historical reduced-form dynamics rather than literal policy prescriptions. The proposed framework provides a scalable decision-support approach for systematically exploring policy trade-offs and identifying feasible configurations that satisfy explicit multi-objective criteria.

title: Reliable Maintenance Policy for Distributed Affine Formation Control of UAVs
authors: Hongyu Zhou, Zhonggang Li, Raj Thilak Rajan
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Distributed affine formation control (AFC) enables unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms to achieve coordinated motion while maintaining a desired geometric configuration, thereby enhancing their maneuverability. However, conventional leader-follower-based distributed AFC remains vulnerable to dynamic changes in the network topology, where UAVs may be temporarily unavailable due to maintenance in real-world applications. In this work, we propose a reliable maintenance policy that enables individual UAVs to detach from the swarm without compromising the formation stability. Our policy introduces an agent homogenization strategy that replaces the conventional leader UAVs with virtual leaders, thereby ensuring all operational UAVs are followers and thus eligible for maintenance. A relative affine localization (RAL) technique is employed, which allows the remaining UAVs to estimate the relative positions of missing neighbors by leveraging the formation geometry. Our proposed framework is validated through a series of experiments with a swarm of Crazyflie quadrotors in an indoor environment, which demonstrate the effectiveness of our policy that allows individual UAVs to be removed and returned in sequence while the rest of the swarm maintains its target configuration with high accuracy. Our proposed maintenance policy enables robust and long-duration deployments of UAV swarms in inaccessible and harsh environments.

title: Comparative Statistical Lifetime Modeling of Red AlGaInP LEDs Under Multi-Stress Accelerated Life Testing: Lognormal Versus Weibull Analysis
authors: Ahowd S. Bahashwan, Mohammed Abdulmajid, Omar Kittaneh
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comparative lifetime modeling study of red AlGaInP LEDs under multi-stress accelerated life testing (ALT) with Type-I right-censored data. Weibull and Log-normal distributions were fitted using maximum likelihood estimation and evaluated via Cramér-von Mises, Anderson-Darling, and Jarque-Bera tests.Results indicate that the Lognormal distribution provides more consistent statistical performance across stress conditions. Based on this selection, Intel and Peck life-stress formulations were compared, where the Intel-Lognormal model demonstrated improved predictive accuracy. The study emphasizes the importance of rigorous distribution and acceleration model validation in ALT-based LED reliability analysis. The findings support sustainability-aware reliability modeling for long-life solid-state lighting systems.

title: Optimal Droop Control Strategy for Coordinated Voltage Regulation and Power Sharing in Hybrid AC-MTDC Systems
authors: Hongjin Du, Tuanku Badzlin Hashfi, Rashmi Prasad, Pedro P. Vergara, Peter Palensky, Aleksandra Lekić
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: With the growing integration of Modular Multilevel Converters (MMCs) in Multi-Terminal Direct Current (MTDC) transmission systems, there is an increasing need for control strategies that ensure both economic efficiency and robust dynamic performance. This paper presents an enhanced Optimal Power Flow (OPF)-based framework for hybrid AC-MTDC systems, integrating a novel droop control strategy that coordinates DC voltage and AC frequency regulation. By embedding frequency control loops into the MMCs, the method enables system-wide coordination, enhancing power sharing and improving system resilience under disturbances. The proposed strategy dynamically adjusts converter operating points to minimize generation costs and DC-voltage deviations, thereby balancing economic objectives with system stability. A modified Nordic test system integrated with a four-terminal MTDC grid is used to validate the approach. Optimization is performed using Julia, while the system’s dynamic performance is evaluated through electromagnetic transient simulations with the EMTP software. Case studies across multiple scenarios demonstrate that the proposed droop control achieves significantly improved frequency and voltage robustness compared to active power control, while maintaining a lower generation cost than the adaptive droop benchmark. The results highlight the ability of the proposed strategy to deliver cost-effective operation without compromising performance, offering a promising solution for the coordinated control of future hybrid AC-DC transmission networks.

title: Consensus Adjustment Mechanism Based on Semisupervised Clustering and Cross Allocation for&#x000A0;Large-Scale Social Network Group Decision&#x000A0;Making_supp1-3686760.pdf
authors: Yanjing Guo
publish: N/A
abstract: Consensus adjustment is essential in group decision-making (GDM) to reduce the judgment bias of decision-makers (DMs). Advances in communication technology enable greater online participation, foster complex social networks, and provide more diverse evaluation information. However, few existing studies on large-scale social network-based GDM explored consensus adjustment from the perspective of DM interactions. To address this gap, this article proposes a novel large-scale social network GDM method that incorporates consensus adjustment considering the interactions among DMs and subgroups. First, a two-stage semisupervised fuzzy C-means clustering method based on information fusion is introduced to handle the complexity of social networks. Then, to deal with the issue of inconsistent minimum adjustment amounts between individuals, subgroups, and the overall group, a two-stage cross-allocation approach is proposed, which considers the favorable allocation preferences of all DMs under the overall minimum adjustments. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified through a case study that demonstrates its practical applicability and performance in achieving consensus in complex social network environments.

title: Resource Allocation in ISTN via Learning and Optimization over Riemannian Manifolds
authors: Joarder Jafor Sadique, Ahmed S. Ibrahim
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cognitive Communications and Networking
abstract: Dynamic time–frequency resource allocation in integrated satellite–terrestrial networks (ISTNs) with fast-moving LEO satellites remains challenging. We aim to maximize delivered multicommodity throughput by allocating time–frequency resources across both inter-satellite links (ISLs) and satellite–UE access links. The network in each slot is represented as a time-expanded graph (TEG), whose regularized Laplacian is a symmetric positive-definite (SPD) matrix. Mapping this SPD matrix on a Riemannian manifold allows us quantify topology changes induced by allocation via manifold distances. Building on this geometry, we develop two alternative low-complexity controllers within a software-defined networking framework: (i) a Riemannian Thompson-Sampling multi-armed bandit (TS-MAB) that uses manifold distance as the reward to select satellites per slot, and (ii) a Riemannian Ant Colony Optimization (RACO) scheme that performs pheromone-guided search using the same geometric signal. Throughput is computed by solving a standard multicommodity max-flow on the resulting TEG. Simulation results show that both methods consistently raise throughput and track the exhaustive-search upper bound closely, while maintaining practical runtimes.

title: UAV-Based Through-the-Wall Radar Sensing for 3D Urban Building Layout Reconstruction Using an Enhanced U-Net
authors: Shichao Zhong, Zhongjie Ma, Xiaolu Zeng, Renjie Liu, Xiaopeng Yang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Three-dimensional (3D) building layout sensing is a key capability for Internet of Things (IoT)–enabled smart city applications, including post-disaster assessment and urban security monitoring. However, acquiring reliable 3D building layouts from an external perspective remains challenging in complex urban environments due to severe signal attenuation and multipath effects. This paper proposes an IoT-enabled unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV)–based through-the-wall radar (TWR) sensing framework for large-scale 3D building layout reconstruction. In the proposed framework, UAV-mounted radar sensors act as mobile IoT sensing nodes to collect multi-view sensing data. A multi-layer wall echo propagation model and an angle-weighted three-dimensional back-projection (BP) imaging algorithm are employed to generate multi-view 3D synthetic aperture radar (SAR) representations. An enhanced U-Net architecture is then developed to fuse the multi-view SAR data and reconstruct clearer 3D building layout representations. The simulation and real-world experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves improved reconstruction performance over representative existing methods under the tested conditions, indicating its potential for IoT-oriented smart city sensing.

title: Robust and Energy-efficient Multi-UAV Trajectory Planning for Data Collection: A Game-theoretic and Deep Reinforcement Learning Approach
authors: Nan Qi, Hua Jiang, Sa Xiao, Daolong Wu, Fuhui Zhou, Chunguo Li, Shi Jin
publish: IEEE Transactions on Green Communications and Networking
abstract: In an open electromagnetic environment, multi-unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) communications may suffer from intermittent data transmissions, incomplete information on jammers and geographical obstacles. This deteriorates the UAV-ground and UAV-UAV wireless communications, potentially leading to physical collisions and posing significant safety risks. While existing studies rarely account for intermittent UAV connectivity and the associated communication costs, this paper proposes an effective cooperative approach utilizing grid map exploration and experience sharing. Specifically, game-theoretic methods are employed to facilitate distributed cooperative information exchange. Although each UAV seeks to maximize its individual utility, the proposed mechanism incentivizes cooperation to achieve the collective mission. To mitigate collaboration interruptions caused by intermittent transmission, we propose an opportunistic cooperative reinforcement learning framework combined with Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM)-based predictive learning, which explicitly accounts for the dynamic communication costs of UAVs. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our algorithm significantly outperforms existing non-cooperative methods, with a 31% improvement in converged reward compared to the non-cooperative baseline. Furthermore, it exhibits superior stability and data collection efficiency compared to established multi-agent baselines (e.g., MADDPG, MAPPO). Particularly, the system’s performance robustness regarding the LSTM prediction accuracy is rigorously evaluated, confirming its resilience against intermittent communication.

title: Self-training Based Source-free Domain Adaptation for Remote Sensing Scene Classification_supp1-3695530.pdf
authors: Xu Tang
publish: N/A
abstract: Remote sensing scene classification (RSSC) is crucial for interpreting remote sensing (RS) images across various applications. Existing deep learning methods have promoted RSSC results to a new level. However, most of them rely on an assumption that training and test data are independently and identically distributed. While this requirement can be readily met under laboratory conditions, it proves to be exceptionally stringent in practical settings, as the collected new RS images may differ significantly in distribution from the RS images used to train the classification model. In this cross-domain case, many successful models would be disabled. To address the above challenges and enhance the practicability of the RSSC model, this paper proposes a self-training based source-free domain adaptation (SFDA) method named STSFDA. First, a multi-task source domain pre-training strategy is developed to provide a strong initialization for the SFDA process. Next, a teacher-student network is adopted to drive the SFDA process. In the teacher network, target RS scenes are divided into two groups based on confidence and then passed to the student network. The student network applies supervised learning to reliable samples and unsupervised learning to uncertain samples. A neighbor-guided pseudo-labeling strategy is also introduced to generate more reliable pseudo-labels with the consideration of neighborhood information. In addition, mixup learning and contrastive learning are applied to all target domain samples to obtain more discriminative feature representations. Experimental results demonstrate that STSFDA consistently outperforms many existing baseline models, highlighting its effectiveness and potential for future research in the RS community. Code is available at https://github.com/TangXu-Group/STSFDA.

title: COMBINER: Composed Image Retrieval Guided by Attribute-based Neighbor Relations
authors: Zixu Li, Yupeng Hu, Zhiwei Chen, Haokun Wen, Xuemeng Song, Liqiang Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Composed Image Retrieval (CIR) represents a challenging retrieval task that targets locating specific images through multimodal inputs. Despite recent progress in CIR techniques, prior approaches often overlook cases where images appear visually alike yet differ in attributes, potentially undermining both multimodal feature fusion and similarity modeling. To mitigate this limitation, we design a unified representation of cross-modal features based on attribute prototypes. Nevertheless, the task is far from straightforward, owing to three core issues: (1) entangle ment in attribute-level semantics, (2) inconsistency across modalities, and (3) supervised signal missing. To tackle the above obstacles, we introduce a COMposed image retrieval network guided By attrIbute-based NEighbor Relations (COMBINER). Specifically, we first design an Adaptive Semantic Disentanglement module, which is capable of disentangling attribute features based on multimodal primitive features. Secondly, we propose a Unified Prototype-based Composition module, which can construct cross-modal unified prototypes (CUP) and facilitate multimodal feature composition. Finally, we introduce a Dual Relations Modeling module, which can mine pairwise and neighbor relations based on attribute similarity. Compared to traditional neighbor relations modeling CIR methods, COMBINER represents the first study addressing the phenomenon of visually similar but attribute unrelated samples. It achieves a more accurate understanding of the semantic relations among samples by employing an attribute prototype-based similarity metric. Comprehensive experiments conducted on three benchmark datasets confirm the effectiveness of our proposed COMBINER. The implementation of our method will be accessed at https://github.com/Lee-zixu/COMBINER.

title: Limitations of Nyquist Criteria in the Discretization of 2D Electromagnetic Integral Equations at High Frequency: Spectral Insights into Pollution Effects
authors: Viviana Giunzioni, Adrien Merlini, Francesco P. Andriulli
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: Electromagnetic integral equations are typically solved numerically using boundary element methods (BEMs). When the frequency of the wave phenomenon under study increases, the discretization of the problem is typically chosen to maintain a fixed number of unknowns per wavelength. Under these conditions, the BEM over finite-dimensional subspaces of piecewise polynomial basis functions is commonly believed to provide a bounded solution accuracy, at least for non-trapping geometries. If proven, this would constitute a significant advantage of the BEM with respect to finite element and finite difference time domain methods, which, in contrast, are affected by numerical dispersion, that causes the number of unknowns per wavelength required to achieve a prescribed solution accuracy to increase with frequency, phenomenon known as pollution. In this work, we conduct a rigorous spectral analysis of some of the most commonly used boundary integral operators and examine the impact of the BEM discretization on the solution accuracy of widely used integral equations modeling the electromagnetic scattering from a perfectly electrically conducting cylinder. We consider both ill-conditioned and well-conditioned equations, the latter being characterized by solution operators bounded independently of frequency. Contrary to the common belief, our analysis reveals a form of pollution that affects, in different measures, equations of both kinds. After elucidating the mechanism by which the BEM discretization impacts accuracy, we propose a solution strategy that can cure the pollution problem thus evidenced. The defining strength of the proposed theoretical model lies in its capacity to deliver deep insight into the root causes of the phenomenon.

title: An Advanced Predictive Control Strategy for Current Harmonic Suppression in Three-Phase Three-Level Vienna Rectifier
authors: Tawfique Uzzaman, Tyrone Fernando, Herbert HoChing Iu, Xinan Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: The three-phase three-level Vienna rectifier (3L-VR) is widely adopted for AC/DC conversion. Nonetheless, high input current distortion and neutral point voltage (NPV) imbalance remain significant challenges. This article proposes a novel sector-optimization-based predictive control, combining finite control set virtual-voltage-vector (FS-V3-MPC)-based predictive current control with an outer loop linear quadratic regulator (LQR) for voltage regulation. FS-V3-MPC improves current tracking via synthesized virtual vectors, while the improved sector optimization minimizes computational burden. The LQR guarantees optimal DC-link voltage regulation and NPV control with robust disturbance rejection. Compared with state-of-the-art methods, the proposed approach achieves up to a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{38}\boldsymbol{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> reduction in input current THD with <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mathbf{74}\boldsymbol{\%}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> faster dynamic response. Extensive simulations and experimental results validate the effectiveness and practical applicability of the proposed approach.

title: Instrument-embedded sampling oscilloscope for in-situ waveform characterization and its implementation in the Ocean Color Instrument of the NASA PACE mission
authors: Kim S. Jepsen, Ulrik Gliese, Omar Haddad, Matt Owens, Steve Marlow, Bradley Tse, Leland Chemerys, Gerhard Meister, Robert H. Estep, Jeremy Werdell
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: We propose and demonstrate the use of an embedded sampling oscilloscope technique for characterizing and troubleshooting certain broadly used sampled measurement systems. Good examples of these are imaging and lidar sensors where a repetitive waveform carries the output information from the system. The method makes it possible to perform in-situ, remote troubleshooting with a clarity that is difficult to obtain by other methods, and is well suited for use in, e.g., satellite-based observation platforms. The implementation is based solely on a simple and switchable firmware core, making the method inherently low Size, Weight, Power and Cost (SWaP-C). The embedded sampling oscilloscope has been implemented in the firmware of the Ocean Color Instrument (OCI) on the NASA Plankton, Aerosol, Cloud, ocean Ecosystem (PACE) mission. We demonstrate its use in both pre-launch ground testing and post-launch, on-orbit characterization.

title: Data Distributional Attention Based Heterogeneous Representation Learning for PolSAR Land Cover Classification
authors: Jianlong Wang, Bingjie Zhang, Wenke Wang, Meijuan Yang, Siyun Hou, Junding Sun
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) data is typically represented as a complex matrix, and different polarization decomposition parameters result in varying data distributions. Directly applying deep learning methods to extract features from PolSAR data, without accounting for the impact of these distributional differences, leads to incomplete representations. To address this issue, a data distributional attention based heterogeneous representation learning method is proposed for PolSAR land cover classification. This method first analyzes the distribution patterns followed by different polarization decomposition parameters. It combines the eigenvector polarization decomposition method to obtain parameter vectors that follow the Gamma distribution and those that follow the Gaussian distribution. To extract features from the two distinct distribution forms, a distributional convolutional autoencoder representation learning framework is proposed. By leveraging the information entropy of different data distributions, data distribution attention can incorporate distribution characteristics into representation learning features. Secondly, a heterogeneous feature fusion strategy enables simple yet effective fuse of feature representations with different distributional characteristics. This strategy employs a multi-head self-attention mechanism to assign weights to features from different distributions, thereby filtering out those that are beneficial for classification. Moreover, it allows for the further extraction of global information from PolSAR data. Finally, to make feature fusion architecture better adapt to each stage of optimization, an H-CE loss function for combinatorial optimization is utilized. The experimental results on four standard datasets show that the proposed method has better classification results compared with the existing methods. The source code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/Bigice.Zhang/DCAE-Code/tree/main</uri>.

title: High-Bandwidth Flash for KV Caches: Endurance and Performance Implications
authors: Kwanhee Kyung, Yeon Ji Moon, Juhwan Cho, Jung Ho Ahn
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: During Large Language Model (LLM) inference, the key-value (KV) cache is a critical bottleneck in both capacity and memory bandwidth as context lengths surge. While High-Bandwidth Memory (HBM) can meet current bandwidth demands, its capacity has not kept pace with the rapidly growing footprint of the KV cache. Consequently, KV caches are frequently offloaded to CPU memory for higher capacity, yet performance remains constrained by limited interconnect bandwidth. High-Bandwidth Flash (HBF) has emerged as a promising alternative, offering sequential read bandwidth comparable to HBM4 with up to an order-of-magnitude higher capacity. Although Flash memory has limited write endurance, KV caches exhibit a favorable access pattern, being written once and read many times during inference. Our analysis demonstrates that the KV cache read-to-write ratio increases with longer context lengths, particularly with larger output sequences. This characteristic enables KV caches to be stored in HBF while maintaining an acceptable device lifetime, addressing both the capacity and bandwidth challenges of contemporary LLM systems.

title: Time-Domain Linear Model-based Framework for Passive Acoustic Mapping of Cavitation Activity_supp1-3695753.pdf
authors: Tatiana Gelvez-Barrera
publish: N/A
abstract: Passive acoustic mapping enables the spatial mapping and temporal monitoring of cavitation activity, playing a crucial role in therapeutic ultrasound applications. Most conventional beamforming methods, whether implemented in the time or frequency domains, suffer from limited axial resolution due to the absence of a reference emission onset time. While frequency-domain methods, the most efficient of which are based on the cross-spectral matrix, require long signals for accurate estimation, time-domain methods typically achieve lower spatial resolution. To address these limitations, we propose a linear model-based beamforming framework fully formulated in the time domain. The linear forward model relates a discretized spatiotemporal distribution of cavitation activity to the temporal signals recorded by a probe, explicitly accounting for time-of-flight delays dictated by the acquisition geometry. This model is then inverted using regularization techniques that exploit prior knowledge of cavitation activity in both spatial and temporal domains. Experimental results show that the proposed framework achieves enhanced or competitive cavitation map quality while using only 20% of the data typically required by frequency-domain methods. This highlights the substantial gain in data efficiency and the flexibility of our spatiotemporal regularization to adapt to diverse passive cavitation scenarios, outperforming state-of-the-art techniques.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp4-3693439.mp4
authors: Shanshan Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp2-3693439.xls
authors: Shanshan Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Hybrid BEMT-Vortex Theory Model to Predict Amphibious Propeller Performance
authors: Md Umar Farooq, Sayani Biswas, Jagadeesh Kadiyam
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Accurate modelling of propeller performance is essential for predicting thrust, torque, and power across diverse operating conditions, enabling the design of efficient and reliable rotor systems. This study presents a hybrid modelling approach that integrates Blade Element Momentum Theory (BEMT) with Vortex Theory to improve performance predictions of amphibious propellers operating in dual-media environments, namely air and water. Classical BEMT, while widely used, suffers from inherent limitations such as inaccurate handling of high axial induction factors, stall behaviour, and tip losses, particularly under transitional flow regimes. To address these shortcomings, the study proposes a modified BEMT framework incorporating high-induction factor corrections by Shen and Spera, along with the Viterna-Corrigan stall model to account for post-stall airfoil behaviour. In addition, vortex-based modelling is employed to capture tip vortex dynamics, which classical BEMT fails to represent accurately. The hybrid model combines sectional blade discretization with velocity-based transition criteria to determine the onset of stall and dynamic effects. The methodology is validated through comparison against an extensive set of experimental data from APC propellers operating under various conditions. Results indicate that the hybrid model significantly outperforms both pure BEMT and vortex-only models, especially in predicting performance across transitional regimes where air-water interaction dominates.

title: Comparative Analysis of Large Language Model Performance in News Content Classification
authors: Kendricko Adrio, Yaya Heryadi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In today's fast-paced digital landscape, manually sorting through thousands of daily news articles has become an inefficient and error-prone task. While Large Language Models (LLMs) provide a powerful solution for automated text classification, media organizations often struggle to find the right balance between high accuracy and high computational costs. This study explores that trade-off by comparing three distinct architectures: the traditional Transformer, BERT, and the Llama 3.2-1B model across news datasets from HuffPost and AG News. To optimize performance without requiring massive resources, we employed Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for the larger models and used Bayesian Optimization to fine-tune our settings. Our findings reveal an interesting trend: while the Llama 3.2-1B model achieved the highest performance with an F1-score of 0.93, it only surpassed the significantly smaller BERT model by a narrow 2%. Furthermore, we observed that billion-parameter models like Llama are highly prone to “catastrophic overfitting” when working with smaller datasets. In contrast, BERT, when paired with LoRA, offered a much more stable and reliable training experience. Ultimately, this study suggests that for standard news classification, increasing model size offers diminishing returns. These insights serve as a practical guide for media organizations, helping them select resource-efficient models that deliver high-precision results without the need for excessive computing power.

title: AI-Enabled Automated Transcription and Clinical Data Extraction for NHS 999 Call Handling Using Wav2Vec 2.0 and Clinical BERT Models
authors: Zain Ul Abideen Ayaz
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: NHS ambulance emergency call handlers work under high pressure and are under considerable cognitive pressure. They often have to multitask, like communicate with the patient effectively, gather all necessary clinical and personal information, perform instant clinical triage while simultaneously entering the details into their respective clinical information systems. This study proposes an AI-enabled transcription and decision-support pipeline, which is designed to automate the transcription of emergency 999 calls. This paper aims to enable the NHS emergency call handlers to focus more on patient interaction and clinical assessment. This paper uses an automatic speech recognition (ASR) using a fine-tuned Wav2Vec 2.0 architecture, which is combined with BERT-based natural language understanding (NLU) models for structured data mapping and entity extraction as per NHS protocols. Model development and evaluation will use publicly available datasets from NHS Digital England, specifically the Emergency Care Data Set (ECDS) for structured clinical coding patterns and the Ambulance Data Set (ADS) for call-related operational descriptors. These datasets will be used to develop domain-specific vocabularies, train clinical entity-extraction components, and validate the mapping of free-text ASR outputs to standardized fields. ECDS and ADS were used to define the coding framework, while the ASR model was trained on synthetic emergency call audio designed to reflect key features of 999 calls. The proposed approach aims to reduce administrative load on the NHS staff, improve data input accuracy, and reduce triaging time in emergency calls. By combining AI/ML techniques with existing NHS practices, this study outlines the integration of AI in healthcare and presents a pathway towards safer, more efficient emergency call handling.

title: Quantum Game Coordination Mechanism for Transformer District Prosumers with Grid Constraints
authors: Jingwen Xu, Yi Chen, Qing Luo, Ruichong Li, Jiayue Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High distributed photovoltaic penetration often triggers transformer overloads and voltage violations due to uncoordinated individual arbitrage. To resolve these challenges, this paper proposes a collaborative prosumer trading strategy based on quantum game theory. By mapping the strategy space into the Hilbert domain via quantum entanglement, the mechanism reshapes the classical payoff matrix to incorporate transformer capacity and carbon reduction targets. An integrated entanglement contract is designed to internalize carbon externalities and congestion risks, effectively enforcing low-carbon cooperation through economic penalties. Simulation results confirm that the proposed model achieves a social welfare of 321.84 RMB and a local consumption rate of 76.92%, representing improvements of 36.55% and 13.64% over classical games. These findings demonstrate the model's effectiveness in aligning economic incentives with grid security.

title: Real-Time Sign Language Recognition System with Web-Scraped Datasets and Image Annotation for Accessibility and Human-Computer Interaction Applications
authors: S. Umamaheswari, R. Krishanveni, G. Lingeswaran, V.S. Akshaya, A. Rajendran, B. Shuriya
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper proposed the methodology for developing a real-time sign language recognition system using web-scraped datasets and image annotation through Roboflow. Models like CNN and RetinaNet were trained using Python frameworks, and text-to-speech tools translated gestures into native and English languages. The system is deployed as a Streamlit-based web application with robust security measures to protect user data. The system provides high accuracy in gesture classes, low latency, and high throughput. This application can be given to the accessibility and human-computer interaction application.

title: Towards an AI-Augmented Monitoring and Control Applications Lifecycle Management System
authors: Luigi Palladino, Francesco Croce, Diogo Vicente, Joao Silva
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The rate at which satellites are being placed in orbit is creating visible challenges on the management, development and maintenance of ground segment software applications. Ensuring maintainability and reliability, while scaling systems and teams is a challenge, particularly across a diverse set of specialised domains such as the satellite operations sub-segment with its critical applications such as mission control, flight dynamics, mission planning, simulation, among others. In the context of Mission Control Applications and Tools maintenance and development, EUMETSAT has defined a common Application Lifecycle Management Requirements (ALM-R) for Mission Control Applications and Tools (MCAT) covering engineering and processes specifications aimed to ensure that lifecycle of any/most of mission control applications is defined in a cohesive, coherent and-harmonized way. The requirements have then been mapped to an engineering Application Lifecycle Management System (ALM-S) made up of tools and implementation processes enabling the different stakeholders (e.g. developer, verification manager, librarian, QA team) to manage the phases of the engineering lifecycle according to their role and responsibility. The current combined ALM-R and ALM-S is the result of years of lifecycle management evolution and lessons learned which has been so far considered reasonably fulfilling the overall goal, but recently showing difficulties to scale with the increase number of ground segments to develop and maintain, complexity in terms of knowledge (technologies, design, etc..) and processes management, effective and intelligent automation, all projecting teams in the future to act with a suboptimal level of efficiency. In addition, this scenario is experiencing increasing budget constraints raising concerns on efficiency sustainability. Due to all these factors, assessment towards an ALM-R and ALM-S evolution addressing the mentioned factors has become a clear strategic necessity which we have addressed in the definition of a roadmap including indeed evolution, but also new technologies taking advantage of Artificial Intelligent (AI) technologies and paradigms. AI has been identified having a core role, specifically deployed with assistant and agentic capabilities to equip stakeholders with a configurable AI + Human-in-the-loop integration addressed by a system design capable to evolve both in terms of use-cases and technologies advancements. The approach covers both a revision of known ALM use-cases with their formalization into use-case taxonomy and in parallel their mapping to an advanced prototype implementation of a system layered architecture. The layered architecture presents at its bottom a knowledge base layer in charge to store and maintain into vector databases information artefacts from different data sources and made them available to the upper agentic layer (in charge of implementing back-end use-cases functionality) and the presentation layer providing ALM end-users with diverse UI/UX access to ALM use-cases. This paper introduces what we consider the fundamental phase of the roadmap for the ALM-R and ALM-S evolution named AI Augmented-ALM (AA-ALM) and specifically its implementation solution referred as AA-ALM-System (AA-ALM-S).

title: A Cyber-Physical Testing Framework for Evaluating Space Habitat Performance
authors: Herta Montoya, Manuel Salmeron, Motahareh Mirfarah, Sreehari Manikkan, Christian E. Silva, Shirley J. Dyke
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Designing resilient and efficient space habitats is critical to support long-term human presence on the Moon or Mars. These habitat systems must not only withstand harsh and unpredictable extraterrestrial environments but also integrate autonomous fault detection and intervention capabilities. To validate smart habitat technologies and address the complexities of operational scenarios, innovative testing methods are required. This study presents the development and implementation of a thermomechanical cyberphysical testing (CPT) framework, a novel and cost-effective approach that couples real-time numerical simulations with physical components to evaluate habitat system responses under extreme dynamic conditions. Unlike conventional testing or modeling alone, this hybrid methodology captures critical emergent behaviors and interactions vital to system resilience that are otherwise inaccessible. The CPT framework is demonstrated through a series of scenarios utilizing a system-of-systems lunar habitat testbed integrated with an autonomous health management system. Scenarios are designed to replicate the thermomechanical cascading effects that would occur in a lunar habitat due to a micrometeorite impact damaging its structural protective layer during lunar night. The framework allows for real-time execution of both damage and repair interventions on the numerical subsystem, while simultaneously imposing the resulting conditions on physical components. Enabling and performing thermomechanical CPT tests for complex systems undergoing disruptions, with real-time damage and repair capabilities, can be far from straightforward. Thus, this paper outlines the experimental methods and systematic approaches necessary to design and configure tests that successfully execute challenging experiments, highlighting the challenges and solutions in recreating realistic fault scenarios. The CPT approach offers a new pathway for testing ideas and enhancing autonomous fault detection and decision-making in off-world habitats, providing crucial insights into fault propagation, systemlevel interdependencies, and the limitations of current diagnostic strategies.

title: Cost-Effective Assessment: An Estimation of Diversity Factor for Sizing of Transformer in Selected High-Rise Residential Building
authors: Abee Jule B. Torres, Eugenia R. Zhuo
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The rapid increase of high-rise residential developments in Metro Manila has heightened the need for cost-effective and efficient power system design. Transformer sizing, typically based on demand factors prescribed by the Philippine Electrical Code, often results in oversized equipment and underutilized capacity. This study proposes using the diversity factor as an alternative approach to achieve more practical transformer ratings. Actual load demand and occupancy data from four residential buildings in Makati and Pasig City were analyzed over a four-year period (2016-2019). Statistical methods, including correlation and regression analysis, were applied to establish the relationship between occupancy rates, load demand, and diversity factor values. Results indicate that the practical diversity factor for high-rise residential buildings ranges between 1.2 and 2.3, with a strong negative correlation <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(r=-0.802)$</tex> between load demand and diversity factor. By applying these values, transformer procurement costs can be reduced by an average of 22%, with potential savings up to 35%. The findings highlight the benefits of diversity factor-based transformer sizing in promoting cost-effective design and improved energy management in high-rise residential projects.

title: ARGOS: Leveraging Visual Priors for Scalable Wireless Navigation in Dynamic Environments
authors: Arko Datta, Tharaneeshwaran V U, Aravindh Sriram Kumar A G, Ayon Chakraborty
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Performance of wireless navigation systems degrade sharply in industrial environments dominated by metallic clutter and heavy multipath. The primary cause lies in how anchors are placed or selected. Methods that succeed in open spaces fail under severe non-line-of-sight (nLoS) and frequent layout changes, for instance, induced by moving forklifts or shifting shelves. We introduce Argos, a multimodal wireless digital twin that fuses visual and RF information to optimize anchor selection. Visual imagery reconstructs the 3D layout, updated continuously via existing surveillance cameras, while RF measurements capture material-specific attenuation and reflections. We show that layout priors alone are insufficient; combining them with the required RF optics yields a material-aware channel model that predicts range errors under severe nLoS. Argos adapts proactively to environmental changes, without the requirement of repeated RF calibration or retraining, sustaining sub-meter localization accuracy in dynamic scenes. We validate Argos in a 120m<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> factory testbed spanning over 700 locations, where the digital twin is built from 5K RGB images and 0.4M UWB CIR samples. To our knowledge, this is the first system to exploit visual priors for adaptive orchestration of wireless navigation infrastructure.

title: Machine Learning Based Event Level Structural and Expressive Modeling of Sampled Audio for Digital Instrument Transformation
authors: Saurish Nanduri, Subham Kumar Jalan, Shashank Mishra
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Sampled audio has been used for nearly a century in music production and audio design. Despite its widespread use, sampled audio remains structurally static, typically treated as fixed recordings whose internal structures are not semantically reconfigurable. To enable new methods for analysing and utilizing sampled audio, quantitative modelling is required as a foundation for digital instrument transformation. However, there is currently limited research on event-level expressive modelling of sampled audio based on statistically validated acoustic features. Existing segmentation approaches often focus primarily on pitch, rhythm, or genre classification rather than structural expressivity. In this study, event-level segmentation is performed using onset detection, allowing the extraction of note-like acoustic units from audio signals. For each event, several temporal and expressive features are extracted, including Duration, Root Mean Square (RMS) energy, Spectral Centroid, Zero-Crossing Rate (ZCR), Mel-Frequency Cepstral Coefficients (MFCC), and Event Density. Statistical validation is conducted using Analysis of Variance (ANOVA), while supervised classification is performed using a Random Forest model. In addition, regression modelling is applied to predict event duration. Experimental evaluation produced 17,770 segmented events from 25 audio recordings. Statistical analysis revealed a highly significant difference in event duration across categories (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p} \approx 10^{-76}$</tex>). The classification model achieved an accuracy of approximately 99%, with event density identified as the most influential feature. The regression model achieved an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex> value of approximately 0.35. The proposed quantitative expressive modelling framework provides a foundation for transforming static sampled audio into controllable digital units for future instrument modelling applications.

title: Collaborative LLM-Based Agents for Autonomous Multi-UAV Mission Execution
authors: Jeremy Ludwig, Daniel Tuohy, Forest Finnigan
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper investigates the use of large language model (LLM)-driven agents to coordinate multi-UAV missions, with emphasis on reliability, adaptability, and human-agent teaming. A proof-of-concept system, the Collaborative LLMbased Agents (COLA) framework, was developed to illustrate how modular language-based agents can be integrated with existing autopilot software and human supervision. The architecture uses an LLM server that uses structured prompts to convert natural language commands into JSON-formatted API calls, enabling intuitive operator control while maintaining precise execution of UAV functions. Validation and verification combined simulation, live flight trials, and automated prompt testing. Results showed that agents could coordinate search tasks, manage control handoffs, monitor telemetry, and reallocate routes dynamically, including in response to unplanned operator commands. Simulation enabled exploration of advanced behaviors, while live flights demonstrated feasibility under real-world constraints and revealed additional challenges not observed in simulation. The study contributes an illustrative architecture and initial insights into validation of LLM-based autonomy, establishing a foundation for scalable, safe, and reliable application of LLMs to aerospace autonomy.

title: Quantum Image Processing for Medical Image Analysis: A Comprehensive Literature Survey
authors: K. L. Sujitha, Umamaheswaran S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This literature review analyses the convergence of unsupervised learning and quantum image processing for medical imaging applications. It aims to look at advancements in quantum convolutional neural networks (QC-NNs), variational quantum algorithms (VQAs), quantum-enhanced neural architectures, and hybrid quantum-classical systems with a focus on image classification and enhancement. The goal of this project is to explore possible solutions for medical image analysis, so that it can be more accurate and efficient by using unsupervised learning and quantum computing frameworks. The survey explores how quantum-assisted workflows can enhance the images from medical images, providing new avenues to diagnosis and visualization. While the NISQ era is promising, the limitations of NISQ are not permitting full quantum advantage in clinical practice at this time. To augment medical imaging with quantum-assisted unsupervised learning, this study presents research gaps and a path forward.

title: Robust Delay-Compensated Control of Systems with Uncertain Time Delay using a Predefined-Time Sliding Mode Strategy
authors: Salma Khatory, El Mehdi Mellouli, Houcine Chafouk, Abdelaziz Fri, Zakaria Chalh
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The autonomous vehicle lateral control is necessarily associated with time delays in sensing, computation, and actuation, and it can negatively affect the stability and tracking performance substantially. This paper explores the control of a vehicle that is carrying out a double-lane-change maneuver, and then there is an uncertain delay in the input. The strategy of predefined-time sliding mode control (PTSMC) is created to guarantee the rapid and delay-robust convergence of the tracking error. The new sliding surface using nonlinear error terms is generated so that the tracking dynamics converge to the known predefined-time when the system is in the manifold. A smooth reaching law is used to minimize chattering without sacrificing robustness in order to bring the system trajectories to the sliding surface within a given time interval. The controller gains are obtained in a systematic form as explicit functions of the intended predefined times, and they give direct relationships between the performance specifications and control parameters. The closed-loop delayed system is studied in terms of stability with arguments based on Lyapunov, and presents practically predetermined-time convergence to a small neighbourhood of the sliding surface with underbounded disturbances. The outcomes of the simulations prove the suitability of the proposed method and its ability to track the paths correctly and be stable in the conditions of different time delays.

title: Progress Towards a Near-Unity Fill Factor FIR Thermopile Detector for Space-Based Remote Sensing
authors: Ricardo Braga Nogueira Branco, Byeong H. Eom, Brian Pepper, Matthew E. Kenyon
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: High-priority science can be accomplished by using thermal detectors for thermal imaging and spectroscopy of planets, satellites, and primitive bodies throughout the Solar System. Thermopile detectors are uncooled thermal detectors that are reliable and can be packaged within a small instrument for use in cubesats, smallsats, and rovers. We demonstrate in this work the latest advancements in JPL's thermopile detector technology. Thermopile detectors do not currently have the large format sizes currently available in competing technologies such as bolometers, and do not have the design architecture optimized for far-infrared (FIR) remote sensing. The nextgeneration thermopile detector being developed at JPL will have 16x the number of pixels compared to the current state-of-theart thermopile, diffraction-limited pixels, and near-unity fill factor that will enable a compact instrument with hyperspectral imaging capability for demanding future missions for Earth science, planetary science, and Moon exploration.

title: TG-CausalX: A Threshold-Gated Causal AI Framework of Explainable and Autonomous GPON Network Optimization
authors: Sreenivasa K., C Fancy
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Modern broadband networks rely heavily on Gigabit Passive Optical Networks (GPON), which offer high-speed connectivity but are complex to manage due to multi-layer dependencies, bursty traffic patterns, and rapid state changes. Faults in such environments rarely occur in isolation; a single issue, such as optical signal degradation, can quickly impact higherlayer metrics like latency and Quality of Experience (QoE). Traditional approaches based on static thresholds or black-box models often fail to accurately identify root causes or support real-time decision-making. This paper presents TG-CausalX, a Threshold-Gated Causal AI framework designed for explainable and autonomous GPON network management. The system combines anomaly detection using Isolation Forest and classification via XGBoost, with a gating mechanism that activates causal reasoning only during abnormal conditions. A LiNGAM-based causal model constructs a directed acyclic graph to identify root causes, while Five-Why analysis and do-calculus enable interpretable and safe corrective actions. Experimental results demonstrate 90 % diagnostic accuracy, 92 % remediation success, and low-latency operation (14.3 ms), highlighting the effectiveness of TG-CausalX for intelligent network optimization.

title: Calibrating IMU Pitch Misalignment in the Field to Reduce Wheel-Inertial Odometry Elevation Drift
authors: Tushaar Jain, David Wettergreen, Michael Kaess
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Rovers estimate their position (“positioning”) and map their surroundings, but these tasks are complicated by measurement errors, which accumulate. This effect is severe in the vertical channel, as drift in the rover's elevation estimate is particularly difficult to mitigate. One of the main contributors to this drift is pitch misalignment between the rover's velocity vector and its Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU). This motivates our contribution: a method for calibrating an IMU's pitch misalignment. Our method does not require an initial ground calibration, external infrastructure, a source of ground truth pose, accounting for wheel slip, or sensors beyond an IMU and wheel encoders. We simply require the rover to drive in loops on approximately planar (but not necessarily level) terrain. This can be done autonomously in a planetary environment, requiring little oversight from mission operations. Our theoretical analysis, simulation results, and physical tests prove our method's effectiveness. Additionally, we analyze the effect of terrain, IMU quality, and calibration path on the calibration. Though our idea to drive in loops is simple, our main contributions are that we prove why our method works, analyze it extensively in theory, simulation, and practice, determine what parameters do and do not matter, and provide a comprehensive argument for applying our method to any wheeled rover.

title: A Multi-Agent Cognitive Isolation Framework for Defect-Driven Power Grid Document Assessment
authors: Jingjing Yang, Zhiqi Ao, Haowen Ren, Haibo Zhu, Lifu Ding, Minghao Wang, Xuejun Jiang, Qinmin Yang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Automated Document Quality Assessment (DQA) in the power system domain struggles with the "black-box" nature of traditional deep learning and the challenges of LLM-based few-shot scoring, including role confusion and stochastic drift during cold starts. To address these issues in smart grid operations, we propose CREDIT, an interpretable Defect-Driven Few-Shot Rule Induction framework via Multi-Agent Collaboration. CREDIT achieves cognitive isolation by decoupling rule generation, scoring, and diagnosis across independent agents. It introduces a Spearman correlation-guided cold-start strategy to rapidly identify the optimal optimization direction. Rather than relying on unguided reflection, the framework utilizes "abnormal relative samples" as explicit negative feedback, driving dynamic rule iteration via Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). Extensive experiments on structured power grid business documents validate the framework’s effectiveness, achieving a 0.835 weighted F1-score using a small-scale sample set. Crucially, to address strict data privacy constraints in high-stakes power dispatch and compliance scenarios, we demonstrate the framework’s lightweight deployment feasibility.
