For some missions, a kinetic impact or gentle continuous force (e.g. ion beam or gravity tractor) will suffice. However, if the warning time is too short, or if the asteroid is too large, then only a nuclear detonation will impart the required change in velocity to safely steer the asteroid away from Earth or robustly disrupt it in a way that minimizes the probability of impact by dangerous fragments. In this paper, we propose a novel method of reliably fuzing the nuclear explosive device (NED) using the rapid and intense flash of light emitted upon impact into the asteroid by a small companion spacecraft on a leading trajectory. When an impactor hits a target at high speeds (often exceeding <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$2.5 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>), it produces a high-intensity pulse of light that can be detected at considerable distance. The standoff distance, or height of burst (HoB), of the NED is a critical mission parameter that must be optimized. For deflection, the gamma radiation source must be far enough to ensure that a large area of the asteroid's surface ablates, but close enough that the asteroid captures sufficient flux while remaining within the safety margin to avoid unintended disruption. For intentional robust disruption, the timing can be adjusted to yield a near-surface burst that would optimize the disintegration and rate of fragment dispersal. Significantly, the HoB could also be adjusted on approach to account for information obtained by a leading flyby reconnaissance spacecraft to characterize the asteroid's size, shape, and other properties. Many laboratory experiments have been performed which characterize the emissions produced by this impact as it produces high temperature ejecta and shock waves that travel through both the impactor and target. This body of work establishes credibility to use an impact flash trigger in a realworld scenario. In practice, the impactor would be separate spacecraft from the NED module. We can use lidar transmitters and receivers on the impact module, which can be controlled with small thrusters to ensure that it stays a nominal distance ahead of the NED and that it remains at rest in its reference frame. As an example, if the closing speed of the asteroid is roughly <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$10 ~\text{km} / \mathrm{s}$</tex>, and if the two craft are 100 meters apart, then the impact flash would be detected by the NED 10 ms before it would strike the target. This would provide ample buffer time for the NED to count down and detonate. The brightness and rapid rise time of an impact flash would be difficult to mistake for any other source, and its magnitude can be estimated from theory and tests in order to calibrate the NED's receiver. The advantage of this approach over other systems is that the high impact speeds are guaranteed to generate an impact flash between the impactor and the target. This could be crucial if there is a debris field surrounding the asteroid. It also does not require passive information, such as sunlight collected by optical cameras. Furthermore, this approach would allow the effective prescription of the height of burst in-situ. If the asteroid turns out to be larger than predicted, or if a reconnaissance mission cannot be flown, then this mission type could function as its own reconnaissance. The optimal height of burst could be achieved by the impactor craft's thrusters putting it a little closer to, or a little farther from, the NED, or by resetting the count-down clock interval to prescribe the time between impact and detonation.

title: PULSE-A: Polarization-Modulated Optical Communications at the CubeSat Form Factor
authors: Logan Hanssler, Juan Ignacio Prieto Asbun, Seth Knights, Sofia Mansilla, Everette Spencer Shelton, Catherine Todd, Elizabeth Rosario, Graydon Schulze-Kalt, Leah Vashevko, Daniel Lee, Robert Pitu, Rodrigo Spinola e Castro, Aidan Etterer, Akash Piya, Brian Yu, Vidya Suri, Lauren Ayala, Rohan Gupta, Mason McCormack, Vincent Redwine, Danielle Zumi Riekse, Michael Lembeck, Tian Zhong
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Recent advancements in space-based sensing and observation technology have created an unprecedented need for space-to-ground communication bandwidth. While traditional radio frequency (RF) technology has innovated to meet this increasing requirement, most available options present significant size, weight, power, and cost (SWaP+C) restrictions, often forming a bottleneck for mission and science operations. Optical communication provides a promising alternative, capable of meeting many of the same <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SWaP}+\mathrm{C}$</tex> requirements while providing orders of magnitude improvement in available bandwidth. The Polarization-modUlated Laser Satellite Experiment (PULSE-A) is a CubeSat mission currently under development at the University of Chicago aiming to demonstrate space-toground optical downlink at a data rate of up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{10 Mbps}$</tex>. Along with meeting the intense <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SWaP}+\mathrm{C}$</tex> requirements of a university CubeSat mission, PULSE-A's optical payload is designed to use circular polarization shift keying (CPolSK), making it the first demonstration of polarization modulation for space-to-ground optical communication. In addition to probing the viability and possible advantages of CPolSK for optical downlink, PULSEA works to make optical communication technology more accessible. The project is entirely open-source and relies heavily on hardware designed and built by students to support both its technical and educational mission. The PULSE-A team is developing the mission's 1U optical payload, 3U CubeSat bus, portable optical ground station (OGS), and RF ground station (RFGS) almost entirely in-house, making use of commercial off-the-shelf components alongside student-designed hardware to reduce cost and development overhead. The optical payload functions as a CPolSK-based, transmit-only optical communications terminal. Two data-encoding lasers emitting orthogonal linearly polarized light at 1550 nm are driven in complementary mode, producing a linear polarization-modulated beam when combined. The beam is then amplified and converted to CPolSK states in free-space, where the data beam is then transmitted out of the spacecraft and toward the OGS. To meet the stringent pointing requirements of optical communications, PULSE-A makes additional use of a fine-steering mechanism informed by a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1064 nm}$</tex> ground-to-space beacon laser to account for body pointing error from a COTS Attitude Determination and Control System. The transmission beam is collected via an 11” amateur telescope at the OGS and then separated by polarization state. Detection is accomplished with high gain avalanche photodiodes, and a field programmable gate array (FPGA) demodulates and decodes the received data. In this paper, we will provide an overview of the design and expected operations for PULSE-A's payload, bus, OGS, and RFGS. We will highlight design considerations and analyses made to support polarization modulation of the optical downlink as well as

title: V2G Operation Strategy for Electric Bus Stations in Multi-Stage Markets
authors: Zhongguang Yang, Weipeng Liang, Yang Du, Lingyu Guo, Simin Wu, Jicheng Hui, Yangguang Lu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To fully exploit the energy storage potential of electric buses (EBs) and maximize their electricity market value, this paper proposes a multi-market coordination strategy enabling EB stations to participate in both energy and frequency regulation markets. A comprehensive operational framework integrating day-ahead, intra-day, and real-time stages is first established. By collecting real-time EB data and considering individual constraints alongside fleet aggregation characteristics, the dispatchable power boundary of the bus cluster is precisely determined. A multi-stage optimization model is then developed to maximize total station profit, incorporating energy procurement costs, frequency regulation capacity revenue, and mileage compensation. Simulation results across four seasons demonstrate that the strategy achieves the highest total profit in autumn, followed by summer, with spring slightly lower and winter the lowest due to battery degradation. This approach effectively balances EB charging demands with grid regulation requirements, supporting multi-product market participation.

title: Physical Maxillofacial Simulator with Variable-Stiffness Sensorized Soft Tissues
authors: Alessio De Bona, Alberto Villani, Domenico Prattichizzo, Gionata Salvietti
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Realistic physical simulators for maxillofacial surgeons are still limited due to the lack of soft-tissue models capable of reproducing the complex mechanical behavior of human facial anatomy. This work presents a novel physical maxillofacial simulator specifically designed for diagnostic palpation. It integrates a high-fidelity 3D-printed skull with sensorized, pneumatic soft pads. The latter can actively modulate stiffness and shape to emulate both physiological and pathological tissue conditions. Each pad reproduces the layered structure of selected facial regions, i.e., mandibular, zygomatic, and maxillary. They are fabricated from silicone-based materials optimized to match human tissue compliance. Moreover, the system records palpation forces and dynamically varies internal pressure to simulate edematous or fibrotic responses. Experimental characterization demonstrates repeatable inflation–pressure dynamics, nonlinear stiffness modulation, and anatomically consistent swelling behavior. Validation with a maxillofacial surgeon confirms the simulator’s anatomical realism, tactile fidelity, and training effectiveness. Therefore, the proposed platform bridges the gap between static anatomical phantoms and virtual haptic systems, providing a controllable and high-fidelity environment for surgical training and diagnostic skill assessment.

title: An Intra-Day Allocation Strategy Using PMP for Hybrid Energy Storage in Shanxi's Frequency Regulation
authors: Zikui Lv, Gang Li, Yu Lu, Xiao Zhou, Zixuan Wang, Mengke Duan, Hao Xu, Hao Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: In response to the frequency fluctuations in power systems caused by the high penetration of renewable energy under the "dual carbon" goals, and to enhance the comprehensive performance and economic benefits of hybrid energy storage systems participating in Shanxi's primary frequency regulation market, this paper proposes an intra-day power allocation strategy based on the discrete-event Pontryagin's Minimum Principle(PMP). Targeting the complementary characteristics of flywheel and electrochemical energy storage in terms of response time, regulation rate, and capacity, and comprehensively considering Shanxi's multidimensional performance assessment system centered on the K-value, operational constraints of energy storage devices, and State of Charge management requirements, a cost function is constructed with the core objectives of optimizing frequency regulation assessment scores, mitigating device operational stress, and maintaining state of charge stability. By transforming the discrete-time assessment points specified in market rules into boundary conditions and path constraints within the PMP framework, direct optimization of complex assessment metrics is achieved. Simulation results show that, compared to traditional allocation algorithms, the proposed PMP strategy can effectively co-optimize system frequency regulation performance indicators, delay energy storage lifespan degradation, and enhance market revenue over a one-day time scale, providing theoretical support and a practical solution for the market-oriented and large-scale application of hybrid energy storage under rule-based guidance.

title: Evaluating the Spatial and Technical Potential of Solar Energy Across Europe: A Critical Review
authors: Dushyant Sharma, Mohd. Faisal Jalil
publish: 2025 IEEE DELCON - International Conference on Recent Smart Technologies in Engineering for Sustainable Development
abstract: Globally, more research is being done to figure out how to use renewable energy better. These studies are important for planning energy use, making long-term energy predictions, and setting energy policies. To find the best ways to calculate solar energy potential in Europe, this study starts with a detailed review and comparison of current research. The reasons for the wide range of potential values are looked at. The analysis finds that 74 % of these values are higher than what long-term energy plans expected, showing that future studies are often included in these plans. This contains differences that vary by nation and technology: wind power is generally more limited than photovoltaics, and Germany's solar energy potential is projected to be greater than the capabilities anticipated in some energy transition scenarios. Certain studies that evaluate potentials use additional aesthetic or political standards in order to provide believable value in growth for the potential, while technical criteria are not restrictive. Because these criteria are tied to political will, their applicability in measuring a “potential” rather than a politically viable scenario is questionable. As such, more detail should be included in evaluations of renewable potential and careful in how they handle these standards. This review particularly evaluates solar potential across Europe based on solar irradiation, land-use availability, photovoltaic technology efficiency, installation density, and socio-economic constraints.

title: SecuGRC: A Machine Learning Secure SDLC Code Compliance Auditor Based on Governance Framework Mapping
authors: Harshini T.V, Adina Auldwin Paul, B. Uma Maheswari
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Access authorization failures continue to be a leading source of security failures in enterprises, often because of the disconnect between the officially stated access control policies and their real-world implementation. Traditional Role-Based Access Control audit and checklist-based compliance verification methods do not offer detailed visibility of attribute level breaches and incident based policy deviation. The research paper presents an incident based Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC) policy auditing and compliance validation model that is aimed at reinforcing safe Software Development Life Cycle governance by formal representation of each access request in terms of subject, object, action and contextual environment parameters, and then by deterministic rule assessment with regard to pre-defined ABAC policy constraints and IT General Control mappings. Access attributes based on incidents are methodically matched with organizational definitions of control to reveal overprivileges, unfinished policy restrictions and inconsistencies in control and policy disparities. To prioritize access violations on the attribute level, a quantified policy deviation severity index is computed. Remediation advice and traceability matrix between incidents, attributes, and access policies and governance controls are automatically produced and meet audit compliance criteria and 94.6 % policy gap detection and 92.1 % remediation recommendation accuracy with experimental validation against enterprise-scale synthetic access datasets. The outcome of the evaluation proves that audit transparency is improved, access governance visibility is improved, and regulatory interpretability is improved in contrast to the use of RBAC-based compliance validation methods. Results prove incident-based ABAC policy auditing to be a powerful, regulator-congruent tool of diminishing access control risk in secure SDLC settings.

title: Optimizing Project Management through Real-Time Collaboration System Development Using Visualization Dashboards
authors: Hery, Franky, Andree Emmanuel Widjaja, Calandra Alencia Haryani, Arnold Aribowo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: At this time, project complexity is increasing, and the need to manage and analyze data is becoming very important, so improvements in project management are needed. In practice, technology is very helpful, making it easier to create documents and manage projects. One type of technology that is often used is collaboration tools. The research methodology involved data collection and application development, resulting in the creation of a Real-Time Collaboration System. The system aims to simplify project documentation creation, increase collaboration among team members, and reduce errors and costs associated with traditional project management approaches. The Real-Time Collaboration System was tested using black-box testing to evaluate its functionality, interface, and user experience. The final result of the Real-Time Collaboration System is a set of visualization dashboards that can assist in project management by detailing, managing, and analyzing project information more efficiently.

title: The Power of AI Literacy in Developing Students' Innovative Behavior and Wellbeing in Tourism and Hospitality Education: The Roles of Creative Self-Efficacy and Learning Agility
authors: Sarah Abdelrahman, Yehia Elzek, Abdelaziz Aborya
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) literacy has become one of the essential competencies in higher education due to its positive influence on students' creativity, adaptability, and overall wellbeing. This research aims to explore the role of AI literacy in enhancing students' innovative behavior and wellbeing within tourism and hospitality education. The study also seeks to examine the mediating role of creative self- efficacy in the relationship between AI literacy, students' innovative behavior, and wellbeing. In addition, the research aims to identify the moderating effect of learning agility on the relationships between creative self-efficacy, innovative behavior, and wellbeing. To achieve these objectives, the research depended on the quantitative approach. A structured electronic questionnaire developed and distributed to a sample of tourism and hospitality students in Egypt and Saudi Arabia. The data collected was analyzed using WarpPLS V. 8 to test the hypothesized relationships among the study variables. The findings revealed that AI literacy has a significant positive effect on students' innovative behavior and wellbeing. Creative selfefficacy plays a key mediating role in the relationships between AI literacy, innovative behavior, and wellbeing, while students' innovative behavior further mediates the link between creative self-efficacy and wellbeing. Most hypothesized relationships were supported, whereas the moderating effect of learning agility was not significant. Based on the findings, a set of recommendations proposed to help tourism and hospitality education effectively integrate AI literacy and develop agile learning environments that support creativity, innovation, and student wellbeing in the era of digital transformation.

title: An Online Estimation Method for Wave Impedance of MMC-HVDC Grids Based on Least Squares
authors: Yang Song, Xiuyong Yu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Accurate wave impedance is critical for single-ended fault protection in MMC-HVDC grids, yet existing methods treat it as a fixed, offline value and fail to adapt to varying fault conditions. This study introduces a specialized online wave impedance estimation technique for MMC-HVDC transmission lines based on least squares. By analyzing the fault characteristics of DC lines, the feature between voltage and current at the non-faulted line port on the same DC bus is derived and expressed in discrete form as overdetermined equations. The one-mode and ground-mode wave impedances are then solved via least squares, enabling real-time recalibration under each fault. A ±500 kV ring topology MMC-HVDC grid is simulated in PSCAD/EMTDC. Simulation results show that the proposed method accurately acquires wave impedance parameters under different fault resistances and distances, with strong dynamic adaptability. This work provides a reliable basis for developing high-sensitivity single-ended main protection schemes.

title: Research and Application of Steam Path Efficiency Increase Retrofit for LP Modules of Nuclear Power Plant Steam Turbines
authors: JieYun Zhang, XiaoLiang Fu, YingFeng Xiong
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Aiming at resolving the problems of low steam path efficiency and expired design life of the low pressure (LP) modules of 1000MW-class steam turbines in a nuclear power plant, this study conducts research and analysis on steam path efficiency increase, service life extension, and power uprate of the LP modules. The LP modules’ retrofit scope covers components such as rotors, diaphragms, diaphragm carriers and inner cylinders, and replaces the original impulse technology blading (ITB) with reaction technology blading (RTB) with small enthalpy drop per stage. Under the constraints of unchanged steam inlet parameters, exhaust parameters, feed water parameters, unit spatial layout, and no replacement of the LP outer cylinder, the thermal design of the steam turbine and the structural design of the LP modules were optimized, and the compatibility assessment and adaptive adjustment of relevant system equipment were carried out. The key advanced technologies applied are modern full 3D curved-twisted blades, adding 2 additional stages from 2x5 stage ITB to 2x6 stage RTB per module, welded rotor, longer last stage blades etc. After the retrofit implementation, the nuclear power plant achieved successful first start-up, with significant power output uprate by 34MW and 30MW for Unit 1 and Unit 2, good economic benefits, enhanced equipment reliability, extended interval between LP module major inspections required LP cylinder disassembly, and reduced major outage duration and maintenance costs. This research provides valuable reference for the steam path efficiency increase retrofit of similar domestic steam turbines.

title: SMARTFLOW: Intelligent Traffic Management with Real-Time Density and Emergency Response
authors: R. Ratna Kowshik, V. Pavani, R. Anandh
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: However, due to the rapid growth of urbanization, there has been an increase in traffic congestion, which causes the inefficient management of the traffic signals and the delayed response to emergencies. In the traditional system of controlling traffic signals, a static plan is used for the timing purpose, which cannot be adapted to the real-time situation. In order to overcome the problems faced in the traditional system, the AI-assisted adaptive traffic signal control system with emergency vehicle prioritization is introduced in this paper. In the proposed system, the video captured from the roadside cameras is used to identify the vehicles using the YOLOv8 model. The vehicles are classified into two-wheelers and four-wheelers. The weighted density is used to identify the congestion on a per-lane basis. Emergency vehicles such as ambulances and fire trucks are identi- fied using visual and auditory signals to provide the right-of-way priority. The entire system is developed using the Python pro- gramming language along with the OpenCV and PyTorch libraries. The proposed system is a highly efficient solution for the modern traffic signals that replaces the traditional static timers with the AI-assisted adaptive solution.

title: Classification Quality of Air with Real Time IoT Based Air Monitoring Device Analysis
authors: Alya Rafilah Ramli, Rifqi Abdul Hanif, Panglima Abdan Diwan Syakura, Muhammad Fahri Hairul, Andi Seylo Irham Irawan, M. Saleh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Air pollution remains a major environmental and public health concern, with conventional monitoring systems often limited by high costs, restricted coverage, and slow data reporting. This research develops a high-accuracy real-time air quality (AQ) classification system by integrating Internet of Things (IoT) technology with machine learning (ML) methods, resulting in a scalable, reliable, and cost-efficient framework. Utilizing an ESP32 microcontroller with DHT22, MQ135, and GP2Y1010AU0F sensors, the system measures key environmental parameters and transmits data to a Google Spreadsheet for real-time cloud storage, while also providing instant user notifications via a Telegram Bot. The K-Nearest Neighbor (K-NN) algorithm is employed to classify AQ indices into ‘Good,’ ‘Insufficient,’ or ‘Hazardous’ categories. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed K-NN model achieves a classification accuracy of 96.77%, confirming the effectiveness and reliability of the system as a practical tool for continuous AQ monitoring.

title: Research on Control Strategies for Renewable Energy and Electromagnetic Transient Modeling of New Power Systems
authors: Hongming Shen, Congying Wu, Zhihong Xiao, Xi Lu, Peili Yan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The large-scale integration of renewable energy sources (RES) into the power system poses challenges to its secure and stable operation. Due to the distinct operational and control strategies of RES compared to traditional synchronous generators, their simulation modeling and the study of their characteristics in large-scale systems present significant difficulties. This work analyzes the modular modeling methods for various power equipment in the power system, investigates the output waveform characteristics of RES under different control strategies during faults, simulates the power angle instability problem in large-scale new-type electric power system (NPS), and validates the reliability of the model. Modular modeling facilitates the construction of models and enhances simulation efficiency.

title: Adaptive Speech Enhancement Using a Hybrid CUDA and Deep Learning Pipeline
authors: Anumakonda Vyshnav Kumar, Ron Varghese Sam, Arun M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: We describe a real-time pipeline for speech enhancement that unites GPU-parallel digital signal processing with a pretrained deep-learning model, targeting noise suppression, voice amplification, and hearing-loss compensation within a single framework. To create test conditions that mirror real urban environments, clean utterances from the LibriSpeech dev-clean subset were mixed with genuine STRAFFIC traffic recordings at input signal-to-noise ratios of 0,5, and 10 dB. Processing occurs in the frequency domain throughout: cuFFT and handwritten CUDA kernels carry out Wiener-style suppression and formant-sensitive amplification over 513 spectral bins for every frame, while a SpeechBrain MetricGAN+ model trained on VoiceBank+DEMAND supplies a high-fidelity denoised signal that enters the GPU gain stage directly. Experiments run on an NVIDIA T4 in Google Colab show that the CUDA-only path raises SNR by roughly +3 to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$+6 \mathbf{d B}$</tex>, whereas the combined ML+CUDA setup achieves gains of + 6 to + <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12 \mathbf{~ d B}$</tex> over the unprocessed input, with per-frame GPU latency staying between 0.3 and 1.2 ms. The pipeline also accommodates audiogram-derived, per-bin gain curves, confirming that GPU-resident frequencydomain processing is a realistic and practical foundation for personalized, hearing-aid-style enhancement.

title: Causal Graph-Guided Multi-source Data Fusion for Power System State Estimation
authors: Chao Wang, Zheng Guo, Zhouyue Hu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the low accuracy of state estimation for non-PMU nodes due to limited PMU coverage and SCADA precision, this paper proposes a multi-source data fusion method based on causal graphs. By explicitly modeling causal dependencies among nodes, high-precision PMU information is effectively propagated to non-PMU nodes. An adaptive causal graph integrating physical models, statistical analysis, and deep learning is constructed to generate high-quality pseudo-measurements. A causal graph-guided Kalman filter improves the prediction step via a causal state transition matrix, enabling non-PMU nodes to borrow high-precision information from PMU-equipped nodes, while incorporating bad data detection and physical constraints in the update step. Simulations on the IEEE-39 bus system show that the proposed method improves state estimation accuracy by over 40% compared to traditional methods, providing effective support for emergency control in complex power grid environments.

title: Low-Control Satellite Formations for Enhanced GNSS-R Resolution in Perturbed Orbits
authors: Angel Pan Du, Lawanya Awasthi, M. Amin Alandihallaj, Andreas M. Hein
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: High-fidelity Earth observations for measuring geophysical parameters have become indispensable for areas such as climate science and resource management. In recent years, this industry has driven a shift toward formations of small and cost-effective satellites rather than single large platforms. These distributed systems enable simultaneous, multi-point measurements across wide areas, improving temporal coverage and resilience. Global Navigation Satellite System Reflectometry (GNSS-R) exploits navigation signals reflected from the Earth surface to retrieve geophysical parameters, and provides a low-cost and resilient approach for Earth observation, but is constrained by coarse spatial resolution, typically above 10 km. This study investigates a novel concept to overcome this limitation by employing a synchronized CubeSat formation that synthesizes a large aperture through beamforming, enabling sub-kilometer resolution. A comprehensive framework is developed to analyze the relationship between formation geometry, beamforming performance, and station-keeping requirements under orbital perturbations. The methodology combines Clohessy-Wiltshire-based relative motion modeling, perturbation analysis including Earth oblateness, and beamforming simulations to evaluate spatial resolution and sidelobe performance. A multivariable optimization explores trade-offs between resolution, ground-illumination stability, and formation complexity. The results indicate that spiral formations provide favorable performance, with a configuration of 29 satellites achieving near-optimal resolution while minimizing formation size. However, perturbations such as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$J_{2}$</tex> induce drift that degrades resolution beyond 1 km after a few orbits, highlighting the need for efficient formation-keeping strategies. Given the constraints of CubeSats, including limited volume, mass and power, propellant-less control methods, such as differential drag or solar radiation pressure, emerge as promising solutions for enabling long-duration, scalable missions within CubeSat constraints. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of distributed GNSS-R missions with significantly improved spatial resolution and lay the foundation for future work on control strategies and end-to-end mission design, enabling new applications in fine-scale Earth observation.

title: Inverse Model Predictive Control for Bidirectional Power Flow in Wireless EV Charging Systems
authors: M. Álvarez-Duque, W.J. Gil-González, A. Escobar-Mejía
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Wireless power transfer (WPT) systems based on inductive coupling have emerged as a promising solution for battery charging in energy storage applications and electric mobility. Most existing studies on WPT systems have primarily focused on unidirectional power flow, primarily addressing battery charging operation. In contrast, bidirectional power transfer-enabling both charging and discharging functionalities-has received comparatively less attention. Recently, bidirectional dc-dc converter topologies, particularly the dual active bridge (DAB), have begun to be explored in WPT applications due to their inherent bidirectional power flow capability, galvanic isolation, modular structure and flexible phase-shift-based control. However, conventional control strategies for DAB-based WPT systems, such as PI phase delay control and self-Tuning control, exhibit sensitivity to coupling and load variations, causing implementation complexity and a large number of tuning parameters; on the other hand classical Model Predictive Control (MPC) entails a high computational burden. This paper presents the design and validation of an Inverse Model Predictive Control (IMPC) strategy for a DAB-based WPT system aimed to regulate the dc output current during battery charging and discharging. The proposed controller is compared with a conventional PI controller. Time-domain simulations will be carried out using MATLAB/Simulink environment and is subsequently validated experimentally using a low-scale prototype. The results obtained from both simulation and experimental evaluations demonstrate that the IMPC controller improves the dynamic response by reducing the settling time and minimizing the signal's steady state error.

title: Research on Adaptive Semantic Task Map Construction and Autonomous Inspection Strategy for Substation Robots
authors: Kaibo Dai, Qun Yang, Hongbin Cao, Chunxiang Mao
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the challenges of low autonomy and heavy reliance on manual teaching in traditional substation inspection robots, this paper proposes a novel autonomous inspection system based on an Adaptive Semantic Task Map. First, a frontier-based exploration strategy is employed, enabling the robot to autonomously navigate unknown environments and incrementally construct a semantic map containing equipment categories. Second, a knowledge-driven Point of Interest (POI) calibration mechanism is proposed. By leveraging an expert knowledge base, the system automatically generates inspection task coordinates and angles upon recognizing specific equipment, effectively eliminating the need for manual task teaching. Finally, a semantic-aware path planning algorithm is developed. It constructs an environmental risk field based on equipment voltage levels to generate inspection routes that balance efficiency with safety distances. Simulation experiments in a high-fidelity substation environment demonstrate that the proposed system achieves 100% autonomous mapping coverage of reachable areas, accurate POI generation for complex equipment, and significantly reduces safety risks compared to traditional geometric path planning methods.

title: Locational Marginal Pricing (LMP) Analysis in a Two-Settlement System Using PyPSA
authors: Samiksha Koirala, Xin Wang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This paper presents a comprehensive analysis of Locational Marginal Pricing (LMP) within a two-settlement electricity market using the IEEE 14 -bus test system modeled in PyPSA. The study simulates both the Day-Ahead Market (DAM) and Real-Time Market (RTM) using DC Optimal Power Flow (DC-OPF) to evaluate the economic and operational impacts of transmission congestion and power losses. LMP is decomposed into Marginal Energy Cost (MEC), Marginal Loss Component (MLC), and Marginal Congestion Component (MCC). Additional metrics such as Generation Shift Factors (GSF), loss sensitivities, and flowgate shadow prices are computed to assess grid flexibility and investment signals. Results show significant LMP variation due to congestion, especially in buses distant from the slack generator. The system demonstrates how open-source modeling tools can support transparent, nodal-level market analysis, enabling better planning and real-time decision-making. Future extensions include AC-OPF modeling, stochastic demand scenarios, and distributed resource integration.

title: Contextual Models for Indonesian Abbreviation Disambiguation and Expansion
authors: Hansen Halim, Aurelia Yap, Lili Ayu Wulandhari, Noviyanti Tri Maretta Sagala
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Abbreviations and acronyms are widely used today in Indonesian news articles and formal writing, unfortunately for machines, their meanings are often context dependent and ambiguous. This paper will talk about the full end to end pipeline for Indonesian abbreviation expansion and disambiguation starting from data collection to evaluation. We first scrape 3,500 Kompas articles and constructed a new abbreviation corpus by automatically extracting abbreviation and definition pair using rule-based search. The pairs were manually validated. We then try out the performance of 4 disambiguation models: 1. A TF-IDF + LR 2. BiLSTM, 3. BiLSTM + TF-IDF, 4. Fine-tuned IndoBERT. The models are evaluated using Top-K accuracy and MRR under two conditions: standard decoding over all possible expansions and candidate-constrained decoding where predictions are restricted to expansions observed for the abbreviation. On the held-out test set, IndoBERT performs best under standard decoding (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$87.0 \% \quad$</tex> Acc <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${@} 1,0.913 \quad$</tex> MRR), substantially outperforming the TF-IDF baseline (66.5 % Acc @ 1, 0.721 MRR). We additionally introduce WSD-style retrieval baselines-an embedding-centroid prototype model and kNN retrieval-which are highly competitive in the candidate-constrained setting and provide a strong non-generative alternative to sequence models. Slice evaluation by ambiguity level shows that performance degrades as the candidate set grows (i.e., more expansions per abbreviation), indicating that genuinely ambiguous cases remain the primary challenge. Finally, error analysis of IndoBERT reveals that remaining mistakes are dominated by unseen abbreviations and named-entity sense confusions, with fewer errors attributable to near-string or near-synonym variants. Overall, these results suggest that contextual transformers are most beneficial for highly ambiguous abbreviations, while retrieval/lexical baselines are strong when candidate expansions are known, providing a foundation for Indonesian abbreviation processing and supporting downstream tasks such as machine translation and information extraction.

title: The Design and Performance of the BAE Systems FASTER Hyperspectral Microwave Radiometer
authors: David Newell, David Draper, Benjamin Davis, John Galantowicz, Allison Zapalac, Pan Liang
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: BAE Systems is conducting the R1 mission on the International Space Station (ISS) which includes a hyperspectral radiometer named the Full Atmosphere Sounding Radiometer (FASTER). The R1 mission is a BAE Systems activity to develop and demonstrate a minisat/CubeSat-compatible hyperspectral radiometer and advance the technology readiness level (TRL) of this hardware through space operation. Hyperspectral radiometry is being used in the design of next-generation microwave instruments that exceed the performance of current operational sensors while reducing size and mass to a minisat/CubeSat-compatible form factor. The FASTER instrument is providing hyperspectral sampling across the oxygen absorption lines from 55 to 58 GHz to provide Atmospheric Vertical Temperature Profile (AVTP) Environmental Data Records (EDRs). This paper gives an overview of the R1 mission and the FASTER instrument and provides the on-orbit performance results. The FASTER instrument is made up of an antenna, radio frequency (RF) receiver, and the digital receiver. An overview of the instrument design is provided. Limitations on the R1 technology demonstration mission are identified. Weighting functions are provided for FASTER and for heritage instruments. Atmospheric weighting functions for the FASTER instrument show the fine vertical spacing and coverage into the upper atmosphere provided by the FASTER hyperspectral instrument. The R1 mission has ended its one-year mission. The on-orbit performance results from the data collected during the year of operation are presented. Atmospheric vertical temperature profile retrievals are summarized and show good correlation to Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) model products. These results are presented and show performance within the operational instrument performance requirements. The overall performance of FASTER to NWP is presented. Since the ISS orbit crosses the Advanced Technology Microwave Sounder (ATMS) orbit multiple times per day, many FASTER samples are co-located with ATMS have been collected. Comparison of the FASTER performance to ATMS is presented. Performance through the middle of the atmosphere, where both FASTER and ATMS have RF channels, is nearly identical. The expected performance of the operational FASTER is provided. The benefit of having additional channels with weighting functions in the upper atmosphere is shown and future work in this area is identified. The FASTER instrument has demonstrated a hyperspectral radiometer with reduced mass and envelope compatible with minisat/CubeSat missions. Even with the limitations of this technology-demonstration mission, FASTER has demonstrated the capability of a hyperspectral instrument with AVTP EDR performance equaling the operational ATMS instrument.

title: Automated Debris Localization in Water Bodies Using Feature Mapping
authors: N. Giridharan, S. V. Dhananjay, J. Rahul, J. K. Shalini
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Debris floating on water surfaces presents major environmental issues, and manual monitoring techniques are ineffective, require much labor, and are not suitable for widespread use. This study introduces a method utilizing a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for identifying and pinpointing floating debris through RGB images of water surfaces. The suggested system combines image preprocessing, convolutional feature extraction, classification, and bounding-box localization to precisely detect debris areas. The model undergoes evaluation using a dataset of water surface images gathered under diverse environmental conditions, which encompass variations in lighting, reflections, and water textures. Experimental findings show that the suggested method enhances detection accuracy from 0.78 to 0.89 and increases the F1-score from 0.74 to 0.87 in comparison to conventional image processing methods. The streamlined architecture guarantees lower computational complexity while preserving dependable detection capabilities, rendering the system appropriate for real-time environmental observation and implementation on budget-friendly hardware platforms like drones and embedded systems.

title: Impedance Prediction of PMSG in Circuit Form Under Variable Operating Points
authors: Zicheng Wang, Duange Guo, Xingyu Shi, Yanjian Peng
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the development of renewable energy, stability issues of modern power systems have attracted widespread attention. Currently, impedance-based methods are favored by researchers owing to the elimination of their requirement for detailed knowledge of the device's control structure. This paper presents a support vector machine(SVM)-driven approach to realize regression prediction of component parameters for equivalent circuits converted through vector fitting. Firstly, the impedance of the model under test is obtained through either model-driven or data-driven approaches. Subsequently, the rational function model corresponding to the impedance is derived using the vector fitting (VF) algorithm, which is then decomposed into three corresponding types of equivalent sub-circuits for component parameter calculation. Finally, the SVM algorithm is adopted to predict the component parameters of the impedance equivalent circuit under different operating conditions. Compared with existing methods, this method offers a clear physical meaning and requires less data volume and fewer training models. This paper uses Grid-Following Permanent Magnet Synchronous Generator (GFL-PMSG) as the simulation model.

title: A Fine-Tuned ResNet-50 Approach for Enhanced Pneumonia Classification
authors: M Anirudhan, Nikhitha S E, H. N Mandal
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Pneumonia is a serious global health problem that affects children and the elderly the most. Early diagnosis is very important for lowering death rates First, reading chest X-ray images by hand is time-consuming, and individual approaches to doing it probably vary from one person to the next in resource-limited clinical settings. In this paper we present a hybrid ensemble-based deep learning framework which achieves a high accuracy in pneumonia classification from chest X-ray images. The proposed architecture is a combination of some convolutional neural network architectures, so it can benefit from the advantages of all different architectures and make diagnosis more reliable. Image preprocessing and augmentation methods help to enrich the data for better quality of data and generalization. We use accuracy, precision, recall and F1-score to check how well the model performs. Experimental results show that the ensemble approach outperforms individual models and therefore provides more reliability and consistency.

title: A Hybrid Deep Learning Model Integrating AraBERT and Parallel Dual-CNN Branches for Arabic News Credibility Assessment
authors: Shahad Almazrui, Mounira Taileb
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: In recent years, utilization of social media networks is rising, owing to numerous reasons such as high-speed communication, low cost, and news exchange. The serious challenge of social networks is that there is no verification of the credibility of the news. Consequently, news is spread regardless of its credibility. Unfortunately, this has a negative impact on individual opinions, societies, and lives. The <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}$</tex> platform is the most commonly used social media network, with a million users around the world who write and share news. Especially in the Arabic world, many users treated <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}$</tex> as a trustworthy source of news. In contrast, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}$</tex> lacks a tool to assess whether the news is real or fake, yet the most news is fake news spread between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$X$</tex> users rapidly. In this study, a new hybrid model to assess the Arabic news credibility on <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{X}$</tex> platform is proposed. The model utilizes AraBERT to extract contextual features in addition to parallel dual-CNN branches. In order to achieve the highest performance, intensive experiments are performed on a dataset comprising 5000 Arabic news, by considering different Arabic word embedding models and deep learning models. The results indicate that the highest accuracy is reached by the AraBERT and parallel dual-CNN branches model with an accuracy equal to 99.20 % outperforming the model evaluated on the same dataset.

title: Small-Signal Stability Analysis of Wind Farm Integration System via MMC-HVDC Based on LTP Theory
authors: Zewen Li, Jianhang Zhu, Yue Wu, Hengwang Zhou, Jiali Kui, Hongqiang Xiao, Hao Deng, Dongsheng Jin
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Electromagnetic oscillations pose a threat to wind farm systems grid-connected via the modular multilevel converter-based high voltage direct current (MMC-HVDC). Given that the system demonstrates nonlinear and periodic time-varying characteristics, the modeling and analysis approach based on linear time-periodic (LTP) and harmonic state space (HSS) theory is superior. Therefore, this paper establishes the LTP model by means of the trajectory linearization method. Then, based on the Fourier decomposition and the harmonic balance principle, the LTP model is transformed into the HSS model. Through the HSS participation factor analysis, the key links affecting the dominant modes are identified, mainly including the phase-locked loop (PLL) control and voltage-frequency (V-F) control. The influence of these key links on system stability is analyzed based on the eigenvalue analysis. Finally, the validity of the above analysis is verified by comparison with the nonlinear model.

title: Re-Evaluation of Solar PV-Battery Sizing Optimization for Cost-Effective Hybrid Microgrids
authors: Pinar Oguz Ekim, Elias Ouedraogo, Erhan Demirok
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This paper re-evaluates the cost-optimal sizing of solar photovoltaic and battery systems in hybrid microgrids using full-year simulation data. The objective is to minimize the cost of energy by jointly optimizing solar and battery capacities under realistic load and solar generation profiles. Case studies across five European cities show that hybrid configurations, which allow limited grid interaction, consistently outperform fully renewable systems in terms of cost of energy. Notably, energy costs are reduced by 40% to 70% in hybrid setups, with solar fractions exceeding 90% in high-irradiance locations like Madrid. The results also highlight the economic inefficiency of over-sizing batteries to eliminate PV curtailment. Instead, allowing moderate curtailment results in better overall system economics. Optimal battery durations range between 1.3 and 2.0 hours, questioning the cost-effectiveness of longer-duration storage under current research trends. This study provides actionable insights for designing technically feasible and economically competitive distributed energy systems in rural and semi-urban contexts.

title: Enhancing IoT Security with Optimized Machine Learning Models for Malicious Traffic Detection
authors: Abdulrhman I. AlSahafi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The rapid expansion of Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystems has introduced significant cybersecurity challenges, particularly in detecting malicious traffic within complex and dynamic network environments. This study proposes a framework for enhancing malicious traffic detection using machine learning (ML) algorithms optimized through Tree-structured Parzen Estimator (TPE). The M-En dataset (a comprehensive resource encapsulating diverse IoT communication patterns) serves as the foundation for experimentation. The methodology encompasses rigorous data pre-processing, including imputation, categorical encoding, and feature scaling, followed by hyperparameter optimization to identify the most effective ML configurations. Evaluation metrics (including precision, recall, F1-score, accuracy, and specificity) are utilized to evaluate model performance. Experimental results demonstrate that Histogram Gradient Boosting (HGB), paired with Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) and Standard scaling, achieves superior performance, with an average accuracy of 98.48 % and an F 1 -score of 98.07 %. Statistical analyses (including swarm and violin plots) confirm the robustness and consistency of the proposed approach across multiple trials. Comparative evaluations reveal significant improvements over baseline methods, highlighting the framework's potential for realworld IoT cybersecurity applications.

title: ConvGNNs-Based Fast Estimation Model for Photovoltaic Capacity of Distribution Grid
authors: Shuting Lin, Zhongjie He, Weihao Chen, Liucun Lu, Sijian Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address challenges such as voltage violations and flow reconfiguration caused by high penetration of photovoltaic (PV) integration into distribution networks, quickly and accurately assessing the optimal upper limit of photovoltaic capacity has become a key issue in power grid planning and operation. Traditional offline optimization methods based on physical models are computationally complex and difficult to meet real-time requirements. Therefore, a new method for rapid estimation of PV hosting capacity in distribution networks based on Graph Convolutional Neural Networks (ConvGNNs) is proposed. This method first abstracts the distribution network into a graph structure, designs a penalty function based on operational constraints, and captures complex coupling relationships between nodes through information transmission and aggregation mechanisms, enabling rapid mapping and prediction of PV hosting capacity at nodes. Simulation results based on the IEEE 33-bus system show that the proposed fast calculation method and model can effectively estimate the PV integration potential at different nodes. While ensuring the required accuracy for engineering applications, it significantly improves computational efficiency and speed. Although the model is somewhat sensitive to key parameters, they remain within reasonable ranges. This article provides a new approach based on neural network models for rapid estimation of distribution network capacity, demonstrating the potential of deep learning techniques in PV hosting capacity estimation for distribution networks.

title: HoloQRam: Efficient Real-Time Spatial Video Delivery via Animated QR Codes
authors: Jaewon Choi, JeongGil Ko
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Public displays remain limited to flat, network-dependent media, missing the opportunities for spontaneous immersive user interaction. In this work, we present HoloQRam, a system for real-time, infrastructure-free spatial video delivery using animated QR codes and mobile RGB-D reconstruction. HoloQRam transmits lightweight 128×128 RGB streams encoded into sequential QR symbols on commodity displays, avoiding device pairing or network connectivity. On the receiver side, a genetic algorithm (GA)-optimized filtering pipeline enhances decoding robustness under various QR image capture conditions, while a hybrid Transformer-CNN jointly performs 4× super-resolution and depth estimation to reconstruct high-quality RGB-D videos. Implemented on smartphones and tested across LCD, OLED, and projector displays, HoloQRam achieves up to 70% higher decoding success rates than baselines, maintains >30 fps throughput, and delivers 30.8 dB PSNR with consistent depth accuracy. Our results demonstrate the feasibility of spontaneous, one-to-many spatial video broadcasting, transforming ordinary public screens into rich volumetric media sharing portals.

title: DPPO-Based Real-Time Dispatch for IEHS with Wind Power Uncertainty
authors: Sihan Li, Bingshen Tong, Zhilin Sun, Menglin Zhang, Jie Teng, Yang Lei
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The integrated electricity-heat systems (IEHS) can improve energy utilization efficiency and facilitate renewable energy integration. To address the challenge of wind power uncertainty in real-time dispatch, this paper proposes a distributed proximal policy optimization (DPPO)-based framework for real-time IEHS dispatch. A dispatch model is formulated to minimize total operating costs while incorporating electricity-heat coupling and operational constraints, and is cast as a Markov decision process (MDP). The framework employs generalized advantage estimation (GAE) and parallel environment sampling to learn secure and cost-effective dispatch strategies. Simulation results on an IEEE 30-bus system show that the DPPO algorithm effectively handles wind fluctuations, ensures system safety, and significantly enhances renewables accommodation, thereby satisfying the real-time dispatch requirements of the IEHS.

title: Detecting Craters in Images Using Machine Learning and Classical Techniques for Lunar Navigation
authors: Trupti Mahendrakar, Kyle Smith, Davis Adams, Shaurya Gupta, Max Marshall, Jorge Chong, Sofia Catalan
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Loss of ground-based communication is an increasing concern for crewed and uncrewed spacecraft as cislunar traffic grows. To address navigation challenges in lunar environments, a Target & Range-adaptive Optical Navigation system (TRON) was developed to enable onboard navigation independent of ground communication. One component of the TRON suite is a technique designed specifically for terrain relative navigation (TRN) through the identification of lunar craters from orbital altitudes. The effectiveness of this method is directly tied to crater detection performance, as many false positives or false negatives can deteriorate the navigation results downstream. This paper presents a combination of machine learning and traditional computer vision methods for crater identification with built-in fault prevention. The approach is evaluated on synthetic imagery from Gateway's NRHO orbit and real lunar imagery collected during the Artemis I mission, showing promising results and establishing a clear path toward future flight demonstrations.

title: Entity Resolution for Aircraft Type Matching on Heterogeneous Aviation Data Sources
authors: Karna Bryan, Irene Tsapara
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The lack of standardization in aircraft type identification across diverse aviation databases hinders safety analysis by creating barriers to data integration. Studies that aggregate data by aircraft type are time-consuming and require substantial manual cleaning due to the absence of common keys, inconsistent representations, and varying levels of granularity, including multiple versions of make-model-series, International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) codes, and popular or marketing names. These inconsistencies make it difficult to align safety event records with operational exposure metrics, complicating risk calculations that rely on linking event counts (numerators) with exposure data (denominators) stored in separate and often incompatible systems. This research applies the entity resolution (ER) framework to the aviation domain, introducing a novel benchmark for evaluating ER techniques. ER enables automated matching of records across datasets lacking common identifiers by leveraging similarities in attribute content and structure. A key contribution is the development of a hierarchical, hand-labeled “gold” dataset aligning aircraft types across two aviation taxonomies, serving as a benchmark for training and evaluation. Both a baseline feature-based model and a deep learning model using the opensource DITTO framework are developed. DITTO employs Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers (BERT)-based sequence-pair encodings with a fine-tuned binary classifier. Results show that the deep learning model performs the taxonomy-to-taxonomy matching with high performance (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F}_{1} \boldsymbol{=} \mathbf{0. 9 8}$</tex>) and demonstrates potential for scalable, automated aircraft type resolution across heterogeneous aviation datasets.

title: Improved Droop Control Based on Soft Actor-Critic Deep Reinforcement Learning in Islanded Microgrids
authors: Xin Xu, Zhanqiang Zhang, Chuan Sun, Xiaodong Li
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: In order to achieve accurate power sharing in islanded microgrid under line impedance mismatch, an improved droop control method is proposed. A theoretical analysis of the distributed generator (DG) model is first conducted to reveal the inherent power sharing deviation under line impedance mismatch. Based on analytical insights, adaptive compensation signals are designed and integrated into droop control to counteract these imbalances. In order to find the optimal compensation signal, a soft actor-critic deep reinforcement learning (SAC-DRL) framework is employed for offline training, allowing the controller to learn effective policies from historical operational data. The well-trained policy is then applied online to adjust the droop characteristics without requiring frequent communication and regulation. Simulation implemented on a 3-DG microgrid model demonstrates that the resulting control behavior aligns with theoretical predictions. The proposed method achieves accurate and robust power sharing under both steady-state and dynamic load conditions, significantly improving voltage and frequency regulation performance compared to conventional approaches.

title: Node-Embroidery Actuator Leveraging Localized Large Bulging Induced by Post-Bulging of a Rubber Tube*
authors: Yusuke Yamashita, Yuki Ota, Takayoshi Itoda, Yuki Funabora
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This paper presents a Node-Embroidery Actuator that leverages localized large bulging generated by post-bulging in a rubber tube. An embroidery actuator features a rubber tube cord-embroidered onto a fabric substrate with embroidery threads, enabling fabric-integrated actuation. When pneumatic pressure exceeds a threshold, the tube undergoes localized large expansion due to post-bulging, producing large deformation. This post-bulging is induced by setting the width of embroidery to 12mm; however, the initiation site varies substantially across specimens. To mitigate specimen-to-specimen variability in initiation sites, a Node-Embroidery Actuator is proposed. The Node-Embroidery Actuator is patterned with regions having a width of embroidery of 12mm embedded within a baseline width of embroidery of 5mm. This configuration enables deterministic initiation of post-bulging at intended locations. Finally, Node-Embroidery Actuators with multiple nodes were fabricated, confirming the generation of large deformation at multiple positions. Furthermore, a simplified linkage model was shown to coarsely predict actuator behavior.

title: From Biometric Surfaces to Contextual Intelligence: A Comprehensive Survey of Multimodal Authentication and the Trimodal Fusion Paradigm
authors: Dummy. Nurdin, Armin Lawi, Syamsuddin Toaha
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The rapid advancement of Generative AI-based cyberattacks and deepfakes has exposed the inherent vulnerabilities of unimodal biometric systems. This article presents a systematic and comprehensive survey of multimodal authentication, evolving from traditional physical biometrics to a “Trimodal Fusion” paradigm that integrates physiological (face), behavioral (voice), and contextual intelligence (location). Through a rigorous review of literature from 2008 to 2025, we map the technological transition from classical statistical methods, such as Gaussian Mixture Models (GMM), to adaptive Deep Learning (DL) architectures utilizing attention mechanisms. Our analysis identifies a critical security paradigm shift toward a Zero Trust model. We propose a novel HybridSequential Architecture that prioritizes a Pre-Fusion Validation Layer incorporating liveness detection via internal modalities (e.g., ECG) and data integrity checks before feature-level integration. A unique contribution of this survey is the conceptualization of the “Location Trust Anchor,” which utilizes spatial context to dynamically adjust security thresholds while mitigating privacy risks through Deep Hashing and Blockchain integration. Furthermore, we explore the dual role of Generative AI, identifying it not only as a sophisticated threat vector but also as a defensive mechanism for missing modality recovery. This study concludes by addressing the implementation feasibility of edge devices and providing a roadmap for future research in privacy-preserving, resilient multimodal authentication ecosystems.

title: Low-Voltage, Repairable EDS Coating for Lunar Dust Mitigation on EVA Spacesuit Textiles
authors: Keerthana Srinivasan, Sasha Przybylski, Rakesh P. Sahu, P. Ravi Selvaganapathy, Solomon Boakye-Yiadom
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: In recent decades, renewed interest in lunar exploration has emphasized the need for effective extravehicular activity (EVA) spacesuits, particularly in addressing the challenges posed by lunar dust on outer spacesuit textiles or ortho-fabric. During the Apollo missions (1968-1972), abrasive lunar dust severely compromised EVAs, as its jagged, microscopic particles easily adhered to ortho-fabric, inducing significant health risks to the eyes and lungs. A well-known lunar dust mitigation technique is electrodynamic dust shielding (EDS), which allows electrostatically charged lunar dust to be suspended from a surface. Conventional EDS implementations on rigid surfaces such as solar arrays have demonstrated removal efficiencies near 90% under laboratory conditions, but translating EDS systems to flexible substrates such as ortho-fabric presents distinct challenges. Flexible EDS electrodes have shown promising removal efficiencies in the range of 85-95% in lab demonstrations, yet typically require high actuation voltages of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$0.8-1 \text{kV}$</tex> and raise insulation/safety concerns for use on wearable systems. To address these constraints, we present a low-voltage, repairable EDS electrode based on an aromatic thermosetting copolyester/multi-walled carbon nanotube/polytetrafluoroethylene (ATSP/MWCNT/PTFE) composite, engineered for direct application to ortho-fabric through spray deposition. The nitrogen-assisted spray process cures at 40° C in 30 minutes, suggesting in-situ repairability without specialized infrastructure. The composite simultaneously delivers mechanical strength (ATSP), enhanced surface conductivity for 3-phase pulsating square-wave actuation (MWCNT), and low coefficients of friction for dust repellency (PTFE). Potential health hazards are overcome through the insulation offered by our ATSP/PTFE matrix and the biocompatibility of ATSP/PTFE composites, as seen through its use in prosthetics in recent years. Tribological testing of our electrode on Kevlar-one of the three materials composing ortho fabric-with LMS-D1 simulant from Exolith Labs demonstrates a 56% reduction in the coefficient of friction compared to uncoated Kevlar, significantly lowering mechanical dust adhesion. Using a boundary element method (BEM) simulation implemented in Julia, we modeled the lunar dust particle trajectory under a three-phase pulsating electric field generated by our spiral electrode geometry. The results predict suspension and horizontal transport of particles down to 0.1 m in radius at an applied voltage of 0.3 kV, which is attributable to both enhanced surface conductivity from MWCNTs and electric-field concentration from the spiral geometry. Future work will focus on demonstrating the suspension of lunar dust in real-time, further durability testing in vacuum chambers, and benchmarking the EDS coating with prior EDS results.

title: Development of an UAS with Geomatics and Bioinspired Features for ISR Missions
authors: Rodrigo Kuntz Rangel, Mauricio Pazini Brandão, Rhaclley F. F. de Araújo, Eduardo Emanuel Fonseca
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper describes the development of a multifunctional navigation system with bioinspired features that uses a management software and Geomatics system, updated by Artificial Intelligence (AI) routines, to define/monitor/update the mission trajectory and mission procedures of a customized UAS. The development considers the concept of Geomatics systems and aerial mobility applied in Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (ISR) missions as an alternative way to collect field data, identifying and learning the characteristics of the field through AI routines to update the navigation system of the aerial platform, to improve the geolocation precision when the GPS signal fails or is denied. A fixed-wing platform with VLOS and BVLOS capabilities and with onboard capacity of processing AI (Artificial Intelligence) and CV (Computer Vision) algorithms and equipped with navigation and optic sensors, will process real-time data to represent some biologically inspired characteristics of flying animals, such as stabilization, bio echolocation and solar direction estimation. In this context, considering the intrinsic characteristics of the field, due to the profile of ISR missions, the system can be used to collect image data from different frequency spectra, obtaining their geographical position after the onboard processing and/or post-processing, where the aerial platform scans the field to identify targets, or known points, through pattern recognition (mimicking migratory birds). Pattern recognition is achieved by AI routines, which perform object segmentation on the locations and definition and classification of each object to create the respective pattern. Once created, the pattern is used as a reference point to update the navigation process of the airborne platform in case of GPS signal failure or denial. Therefore, the resulting navigation solution can be communicated to the Command and Control Station (C2S) in order to improve the mission situational awareness and provide insights to decision makers. The described development illustrates the feasibility of combining aeronautical requirements, low-cost off-the-shelf hardware with AI, CV, and Geomatics routines, to develop UAS with bio-inspired navigation and control subsystems. The result is a “next-generation” UAS, in terms of bio-inspired AI and CV capabilities, for possible use in Intelligence, Surveillance and Reconnaissance (ISR) applications.

title: A Bi-level Model for Wind and Photovoltaic Power Prediction under Extremely Cold Disasters with Uncertainty Scenarios
authors: Xiaohui Wang, Tong Li, Wei Cheng, Yi Wan, Tian Ni, Hui Hou
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the intensification of global climate change and the rapid development of renewable energy, meteorological conditions have become a critical factor influencing power grid stability and supply. We propose a bi-level model for wind and photovoltaic power prediction under extremely cold disasters with uncertainty scenarios. The first layer of the model incorporates a Wasserstein Generative Adversarial Network (WGAN) to process uncertainties. By combining the WGAN model with an improved K-means clustering technique, typical uncertainty scenarios are generated, enabling optimal management of renewable energy variability. The second layer involves a wind and photovoltaic power prediction model tailored for extreme cold conditions, utilizing Stacking and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) techniques. Through iterative interactions between the two layers, the most accurate wind and photovoltaic generation predictions are obtained. Finally, using real power data from a clean energy base in Xinjiang, China, the results demonstrate that the optimization approach significantly enhances prediction accuracy, highlighting the effectiveness and innovation of the proposed model.

title: A Novel Q-Learning Architecture for Non-Stationary Markov Decision Processes
authors: Daniel Clancy, Natalie Fitch
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Single and multi-agent architectures and learning protocols that ensure stability, robustness, and performance guarantees for unknown and changing environments are essential for operational AI/ML systems. The traditional Q-learning protocol is applicable to stationary Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) and in the infinite-horizon, discounted case, is guaranteed to produce the optimal policies when infinite state/action sampling and mild learning rate assumptions are satisfied. Traditional Q-learning for the general case of a non-stationary MDP typically fails to find more than one of the time-dependent, optimal Q value and policy solutions. The primary reason for this failure is that once the learning rate has decayed sufficiently, the Q value and policy solutions effectively become stationary. This paper presents a novel extension to the on-line, infinitehorizon, Q-Learning algorithm that is applicable to both stationary and non-stationary MDPs. One of the fundamental building blocks of this new paradigm is our previously developed, completely observable, Course of Action (CoA) state function. The CoA state function is a difference equation based on the current update of the Q-matrix and the previous value function where the optimal state-action pairs converge to their corresponding positive optimal Q-values and the non-optimal state-action pairs converge to negative values. This new Q-learning protocol is applied to a dynamic surveillance mission (DSM) modelled as a non-stationary MDP (NSMDP) adversarial environment consisting of two stationary MDPs with independent and disjoint activation and deactivation times. The implementation of Double Deep Q Networks (DDQNs) for this new Q-learning architecture will be discussed and the corresponding results for the DSM will be presented.

title: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Nonlinear Model Predictive Control: A Comprehensive Framework with Evolutionary Optimization
authors: Kiruba R, Boobalan J, M. Saranya, Sumitharaj R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a potential paradigm for solving large differential equations that incorporate controlling physics into neural structures. Leveraging this capability, an integrated framework that unites PINNs with Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) is proposed to improve the regulation of nonlinear dynamical systems. A key innovation lies in replacing conventional gradient-based training, which is prone to spectral bias and local minima, with an evolutionary optimization strategy that enhances convergence reliability and model stability. This hybrid learning-control approach merges data-driven approximation with physics-guided constraints, yielding predictive models that are both accurate and physically consistent. The framework is validated on a mass-springdamper benchmark, where the PINN-enhanced NMPC demonstrates faster convergence, better generalization across varying conditions, and improved control precision compared to standard methods. Importantly, the reduced computational overhead enables real-time trajectory tracking, highlighting the practicality of the approach. By linking machine learning with physics-informed control, this work establishes a pathway toward scalable and intelligent control architectures for advanced engineering systems.

title: DIGSAFE: A 5G-Enabled Smart Helmet for Worker Safety in Hazardous Environments
authors: Pradeep D, Arun Sanjeev M S, Aswanth K, Ganesh R, Kumar Mohit M
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Workers in hazardous workplaces like construction sites and mining firms are exposed to hazards like toxic gases, extreme temperatures, and unexpected health complications. Conventional safety helmets are passive protection devices and cannot monitor environmental or physiological conditions on a real-time basis. To address this limitation, this paper introduces a 5G-based smart helmet, DIGSAFE, that is equipped with multi-modal sensors and Explainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to support proactive worker safety, which make the detection more reliable. The proposed system includes sensors that will be used to detect the level of gases, temperature, humidity, heart rate, and movement, which will allow the constant collection and analysis of data. A 5G communication module will guarantee rapid, low-latency communication of data to a centralized monitoring system that will assist in fast reaction, and life-saving. XAI can be used to generate explainable alerts to enable the system to give clear justification of risks identified and to enhance user trust. Moreover, there is an SOS option that allows workers to send a distress signal in case of an emergency. The system can detect hazardous conditions and issue real-time alerts, as experimental validation shows that the system can detect hazardous conditions accurately. Through its sensing, communication, and explainable intelligence, DIGSAFE transforms the safety management process to a reactive process to a proactive and predictive model. The proposed system provides a scalable and reliable solution to improving the safety of workers working in dangerous conditions.

title: HoSig-Align I: Edge-Native Threat Attribution using Homology Blocks in IoT-Pervasive Networks
authors: Aiting Yao, Shantanu Pal, Chengzu Dong, Di Shao, Wenying Feng, Ruonan Li, Zhaoquan Gu
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
