title: Augmenting Human Hazard Situational Awareness With Haptic Interface for Heterogeneous Autonomous Vehicles_supp1-3688755.pdf
authors: Yang Xing
publish: N/A
abstract: As vehicle autonomy increases, human operators become more susceptible to distractions and a loss of situational awareness (SA) due to cognitive limitations. Rapidly enhancing human SA in hazardous situations is, therefore, critical for timely hazard perception and collision avoidance, particularly in human-vehicle teaming contexts that demand fast, accurate hazard reasoning. This study evaluates the efficiency of a low-cost vibrotactile interface for enhancing hazard SA of human operators when teaming with heterogeneous autonomous vehicles, including both ground and aerial autonomous vehicles. To do so, we evaluate the effectiveness of a vibrotactile interface in challenging time-critical scenarios considering adversarial attacks to better understand the cognitive constraints faced by human operators. Our quantitative analysis, based on the data collected from 39 participants, demonstrates that: first, haptic cues can significantly enhance human hazard SA across various metrics for the ground and aerial scenarios; second, perception of aerial attacks in a 3-D environment is more challenging than ground risk perception.

title: A Communication-Free Carrier Synchronization Strategy for Suppressing Circulating Current in Multi-Inverter Parallel Systems
authors: Tao Zhao, Xinyuan Zhang, Zhijian Feng, Jiansai Li, Songlin Lu, Qianlei Cao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: The parallel system of multiple three-level grid connected inverters with LC-type filter has become the mainstream architecture in photovoltaic power generation due to its cost-effectiveness and flexible scalability. However, without the grid-side filter inductance, the parallel system is prone to severe switching circulating current resonance problems, which will deteriorate the power quality and significantly affect the operational stability and equipment reliability. To address this issue, this paper constructs an equivalent circuit model of the parallel system to explain the formation mechanism of switching circulating currents, and then establishes a mathematical representation model to reveal the spectrum evolution based on carrier phase distribution characteristics and frequency coupling. It has been found that even minor differences in carrier frequency can lead to cumulative carrier phase deviation, which in turn leads to larger switching circulation current. On this basis, a novel phase-locked loop variable-frequency sampling synchronization strategy is proposed, which completely ensures carrier synchronization across inverters without communication or hardware changes, effectively overcoming DSP crystal oscillator frequency differences, thereby significantly suppressing the switching resonant circulating current phenomenon. Experimental results confirm the effectiveness and feasibility of this synchronization strategy.

title: Minimizing the Communication Gap between Citizens and Municipal Authorities: An AI-Based Case Study in Canaã dos Carajás, Brazil
authors: Vitor Hugo Macedo Gomes, Pedro Bacelar Moreira, Gustavo Henrique Broechl Moreno, Hugo Pereira Kuribayashi, Marcela Alves De Souza, Evelin Helena Cardoso Gomes, Jasmine Priscyla Leite De Araújo, Nandamudi Lankalapalli Vijaykumar, Marcelino Silva Da Silva, João Crisóstomo Weyl Albuquerque Costa, Carlos Renato Lisboa Francês
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The accelerated growth of urbanization imposes increasing challenges on public management, demanding innovative solutions to optimize urban services and strengthen the bond between citizens and municipal authorities. This article presents a Smart City Architecture (SCA) developed for the municipality of Canaã dos Carajás in the Brazilian Amazon, focusing on the application of state-of-the-art Artificial Intelligence (AI) techniques to minimize the communication gap between citizens and municipal authorities. The proposed framework integrates a mobile application for incident reporting, a web system for administrative management, and machine learning models utilizing BGE-M3 semantic embeddings to automatically classify urban service requests in Brazilian Portuguese. To ensure algorithmic fairness and mitigate class imbalance, the Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) was strategically applied. Five classification algorithms were evaluated: XGBoost, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine (SVM), and Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM). The architecture was designed under a privacy-by-design paradigm, ensuring local data sovereignty and compliance with the Brazilian General Data Protection Law (LGPD) and the European General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) through an on-premise processing approach. A case study conducted in Canaã dos Carajás (Brazil) demonstrated the efficiency of the approach, revealing high performance in categorizing incidents (with AUC up to 0.98 and F1-scores reaching 0.94) and highlighting the potential of AI to support public decision-making while significantly improving the recall of underrepresented municipal departments. The results indicate that AI-based solutions, when combined with semantic richness and ethical governance, can foster more efficient, participatory, and data-driven management, bringing citizens and municipal administration closer.

title: Incorporating Transition Matrix with Prior Knowledge and Dynamic Graph Attention within Graph CRF for Joint Slot Filling and Intent Detection
authors: Reza Zadkamali, Hossein Zeinali, Saeedeh Momtazi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Dialogue systems are widely used in human-machine interaction applications, such as voice assistants and service robots. Among the core tasks in these systems, intent detection and slot filling are particularly important. The strong semantic interdependence between these two tasks has driven the development of joint models capable of effectively capturing their mutual relationships. Traditional approaches, including both pipeline and joint models, predominantly rely on Linear-Chain Conditional Random Fields (CRFs) to optimize slot filling performance. However, with recent advances in the field, new techniques have emerged to address both tasks simultaneously. In this paper, we propose a modified graph-based CRF that overcomes key limitations of existing methods and delivers consistent performance improvements. The proposed model establishes a bidirectional interaction between intent and slot labels by incorporating a prior-knowledge-aware transition matrix and a Graph Attention Network (GAT) layer. The transition matrix encodes precomputed co-occurrence statistics between intent and slot labels, while the GAT layer enables dynamic, attention-weighted message passing among nodes to better capture contextual dependencies. Additionally, the Viterbi algorithm is integrated to decode the globally optimal sequence of slot labels. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our approach outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods across all evaluated metrics.

title: Prescribed-Time Cooperative Output Regulation of Nonlinear Multiagent Systems under DoS Attacks
authors: Ming-Juan Guo, Yuan-Xin Li, Heng Wang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Nonlinear multi-agent systems (MASs) capitalize on cooperative output regulation strategies to achieve synchronized behaviors. However, the influence of unknown time-varying parameters complicates the control design, and denial-of-service (DoS) attacks present risks to the stability of the communication topology. To this end, a prescribed-time hierarchical control framework is proposed for MASs with time-varying parameters and DoS attacks. First, a distributed resilient observer with predefined time convergence is proposed to obtain the information of the exosystem signal. To obtain the exosystem state within a predefined time and guarantee the reference signal with the existence of high-order derivatives, a high-order signal generator based on the concept of high-order filters is established, with its dynamics driven by the observer state. Then, by viewing the output of the high-order signal generator observer as the reference signal and utilizing a prescribed-time adjustment function, a prescribed-time controller is designed for each agent to solve the reference-tracking problem in the back-stepping design framework. By introducing a smooth function with positive integrable time-varying properties, novel adaptive laws are developed and incorporated into the controller design, in which a new compensation mechanism is constructed to more effectively counteract the effects of unknown time-varying parameters. It is concluded by Lyapunov stability theorem that the proposed method can not only guarantee that all the closed-loop signals are globally bounded, but also ensure that the output regulation errors converge to zero within prescribed time. Finally, an example is employed to validate the correctness and good performance of the designed algorithm.

title: Machine Learning–Assisted Power Optimization of PMOS-Biased Sense Amplifiers at 180 nm
authors: Amritanshu Yadav, Preetam Priyadarshan, Aditya Raj, Bipin Chandra Mandi
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Analog and mixed-signal circuits such as PMOS-biased sense amplifiers are fundamental in electrical and electronics engineering education; however, students' practical exposure is often limited due to expensive laboratory infrastructure and the complexity of commercial computer-aided design (CAD) tools. This work presents an educational and analytical framework that combines circuit-level simulation at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 8 0 ~ n m}$</tex> CMOS technology with machine learning (ML)-based analysis to extract power-critical insights from waveform-level data. Using Cadence Virtuoso, extensive transient simulations were performed under varying control signals, bias currents, load capacitances, and initial conditions to generate time-aligned datasets containing power dissipation information. A Random Forest regression model was trained to predict average power consumption from engineered timing and control features, achieving a mean absolute error of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.06 \times 10 ~\mathrm{W}$</tex>, while feature-importance analysis revealed that enable pulse widths and timing activity dominate power dissipation. In addition, a Logistic Regression classifier was used to distinguish low- and high-power operating regimes. The ML analysis enabled automatic identification of minimumand maximum-power operating points that are difficult to infer through manual waveform inspection alone. The novelty of this work lies not in proposing a new sense amplifier topology or ML model, but in demonstrating how machine learning can serve as an effective analysis lens to extract actionable design insights from simulation data. The proposed workflow functions as a reproducible virtual laboratory, connecting analog circuit design with data-driven decision support, and provides a scalable, low-cost approach for teaching power-performance trade-offs in analog circuits.

title: Grasp Optimization for Space Manipulation Using Multiple Underactuated Spacecraft
authors: Jun Yang Li, M. Reza Emami
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Multiple underactuated spacecraft agents can rigidly attach to a large target object and cooperatively use their actuators, e.g., thrusters and reaction wheels, for tasks such as in-space assembly. This paper proposes a method for optimizing the agent attachment positions and orientations to minimize the energy cost of a given trajectory or manipulation task, while maximizing the general manipulability of the agent-target system and ensuring force closure. The proposed method assigns the available agents to the two separate objectives. Trajectory energy cost is optimized using genetic algorithm whose genome is defined by the assigned agent thruster directions. The remaining agents are used to maximize manipulability by optimizing the volume of space spanned by all the agent thruster vectors. Finally, the optimal agent attachment positions are obtained via a depth-first search that matches each obtained thruster vector with an attachment position, with the objective of minimizing residual moments, while avoiding undesirable collisions. The proposed method of grasp optimization is verified through simulations. A case study on orbit and attitude manipulation is performed with a known desired trajectory and required net wrench profile, with KOMPSAT-1 as the target, and underactuated CubeSats as the agents. The results demonstrate low trajectory energy cost and large manipulability volume compared to those obtained using alternative optimization algorithms, such as an exhaustive search using sparse candidate positions and orientations.

title: A Cultural Prompt Alignment Framework for Mitigating Cross-Lingual Bias in Healthcare and Education with Multilingual LLMs
authors: Agustinus Bimo Gumelar, Adi Suryaputra Paramita, Paul L Tahalele, Khamim Thohari, Afid Kurniawan, Ageng Salmanarrizqie
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The rising use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in significant areas of Society, such as Education and Health Care, underscores the need to create material that aligns culturally within certain Locations. Cross-lingual cultural bias results when the prompt of a more commonly spoken language (e.g., English) leads to the generation of output inconsistent with Knowledge and practices of the Local Area (e.g., Indonesia). As a result, this violates the idea of true Educational Value and creates potential issues in Health Care. To reduce Cross-Lingual Cultural Bias, this study proposes a Cultural Prompt Alignment (CPA) Framework, which includes three components: a Cultural Bias Detector with Multilingual Embeddings, a Prompt Rewriter with Cultural Markers, and an Output Validator with Cultural Relevance Score computation. Based on Evaluation across Indonesian-English datasets from Health and Education, the CPA framework has reduced Cross-lingual Cultural Bias by 32% and increased Local alignment to Medical Guidelines by 40% versus Standard Prompts. These results validate CPA's usefulness for developing culturally appropriate output to enable Equitable AI to make use of High-Stakes Areas.

title: A Closed-Form CLF-CBF Controller for Whole-Body Continuum Soft Robot Collision Avoidance
authors: Kiwan Wong, Maximillian Stölzle, Wei Xiao, Daniela Rus
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Safe operation is critical for deploying robots in human-centered 3D environments. Soft continuum manipulators offer passive safety through compliance but require active control to ensure reliable collision avoidance. Existing methods, such as sampling-based planning, are computationally expensive and lack formal safety guarantees, limiting real-time whole-body collision avoidance. This paper presents a closedform Control Lyapunov Function-Control Barrier Function (CLF-CBF) controller that enforces real-time, 3D obstacle avoidance without online optimization. By analytically embedding safety constraints into the control input, the method guarantees stability and safety under the stated modeling assumptions, improving optimality guarantees, avoiding feasibility issues commonly encountered in online optimization-based approaches, and being up to 10x faster than common online optimization-based approaches for solving the CLF-CBF QP and up to 100 x faster than traditional sampling-based planning approaches. Simulation and hardware experiments on a tendondriven soft manipulator demonstrate accurate 3D trajectory tracking and robust avoidance in cluttered environments. The proposed framework enables scalable, provably safe control for soft robots operating in dynamic, safety-critical settings.

title: Multi-Objective Optimization Model for Energy Stations Based on the Energy-Exergy-Anergy Multi-Flow Mechanism in Integrated Energy Systems
authors: Peiyang Wu, Dan Wang, Hongjie Jia
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Aiming at the problems of traditional optimization methods for integrated energy system stations, which focus solely on the utilization efficiency of energy quantity while neglecting the degradation of energy quality—easily leading to issues such as the high-grade but low-use of high-quality energy and elevated comprehensive operating costs—this paper, based on the energy-exergy-anergy multi-flow mechanism of IES, constructs a multi-objective optimization model for energy stations that considers both energy quantity and quality. The model takes the heat production ratio of energy conversion equipment as the decision variable, with the minimization of equivalent annual total cost, system annual anergy increase, and annual power loss as optimization objectives. Finally, verification through a case study of a small-scale integrated electricity-gas-heat system shows that the optimal solution has a CHP heat production ratio of 65.23%. Compared to extreme solutions and traditional two-objective optimization schemes, it can significantly reduce system anergy increase and total costs, achieving the coordinated optimization of energy cascade utilization, economy, and utilization efficiency. This provides scientific support for the planning and operation of integrated energy stations.

title: Soft Surfaced Vision-Based Tactile Sensing for Bipedal Robot Applications
authors: Jaeeun Kim, Junhee Lim, Yu She
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Legged locomotion benefits from embodied sensing, where perception emerges from the physical interaction between body and environment. We present a soft-surfaced, vision-based tactile foot sensor that endows a bipedal robot with a skin-like deformable layer that captures contact deformations optically, turning foot-ground interactions into rich haptic signals. From a contact image stream, our method estimates contact pose (position and orientation), visualizes shear, computes center of pressure (CoP), classifies terrain, and detects geometric features of the contact patch. We validate these capabilities on a tilting platform and in visually obscured conditions, showing that foot-borne tactile feedback improves balance control and terrain awareness beyond proprioception alone. These findings suggest that integrating tactile perception into legged robot feet improves stability, adaptability, and environmental awareness, offering a promising direction toward more compliant and intelligent locomotion systems. For the supplementary video, please visit: https://youtu.be/ceJiy9q_2Aw

title: Robust Pre-allocation Strategy of Electric Buses for Enhancing Distribution Network Resilience
authors: Guilian Wu, Nuoling Sun, Naixuan Zhu, Wenhui Wu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: In recent years, frequent extreme natural disasters such as typhoons have posed severe threats to the secure operation of distribution networks. Electric buses (EBs), with mobility and large-capacity batteries, have great potential as mobile energy storage to enhance distribution network resilience. However, existing studies mainly focus on post-disaster scheduling, ignoring uncertainties from unpredictable fault scenarios in the pre-disaster pre-allocation phase. This paper proposes a two-stage robust optimization strategy for EB pre-allocation to boost distribution network resilience. First, a strengthened confidence set with physical consistency constraints is constructed via the line vulnerability model and Monte Carlo simulation to accurately characterize the ambiguity of line fault probability distribution under typhoons. Second, a dual-objective two-stage robust model is established to minimize pre-allocation costs and load losses in the worst post-disaster scenarios, optimizing EB pre-allocation in the first stage and network reconfiguration as well as EB charge-discharge strategies in the second, solved by the Column-and-Constraint Generation (C&CG) algorithm. Case studies on the modified IEEE 33-bus system verify that the proposed method effectively addresses uncertainties in fault probability distribution and greatly reduces system losses under extreme scenarios compared with conventional stochastic programming.

title: A Hierarchical Clustering Method for Flexible Loads Based on t-SNE Dimensionality Reduction and CFSFDP Algorithm
authors: Huiying Lu, Yu Liang, Xudong Song, Jinhua Huang, Haoxi Xiang, Junhong Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Under the Background of High Penetration of Renewable Energy Integration. Due to the strong heterogeneity of regulation characteristics and high dimensionality of flexible loads, traditional "one-size-fits-all" scheduling strategies suffer from low efficiency and difficulty in guaranteeing user comfort. To achieve refined regulation of flexible loads, this paper proposes a hierarchical clustering method integrating t-distributed Stochastic Neighbor Embedding (t-SNE) and Clustering by Fast Search and Find of Density Peaks (CFSFDP). First, the basic physical characteristics and regulation characteristic features of flexible loads are extracted. The t-SNE algorithm is used for dimensionality reduction and visualization of high-dimensional features, eliminating feature redundancy and the curse of dimensionality. Subsequently, the CFSFDP algorithm is adopted to adaptively identify cluster centers, enabling accurate load classification without presetting the number of clusters. Finally, the silhouette coefficient is used to verify the validity of clustering, and differentiated scheduling strategies are designed based on the clustering results. Experiments based on data from 1,800 users show that this method can divide 1,260 SL loads into 3 Groups and 14 clusters (silhouette coefficient: 0.75-0.82), and 540 DR loads into 1 Group and 9 clusters (silhouette coefficient: 0.78). Loads within the same cluster have high similarity in regulation characteristics, while significant differences exist between clusters. This provides an accurate hierarchical basis for the collaborative scheduling of flexible loads, effectively improving regulation efficiency and user experience.

title: Structural and Thermal Analysis of a Lithium Battery for Interceptor Missile AFTS Application
authors: Steven Karpov, Timothy Anderson
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper presents the structural and thermal analysis of a lithium battery system designed to power an autonomous flight termination system (AFTS) integrated into an interceptor missile. The battery, developed by Space Information Laboratories (SIL), a 3.7 Ah configuration and is subjected to qualification testing based on the thermal and shock environments projected for the missile's flight profile. Structural and thermal assessments were performed using Siemens SimCenter 3D to ensure the battery meets performance requirements outlined in program specifications. The structural analysis evaluated the battery's ability to withstand launch-induced dynamic loading, modeling glued contact interactions between aluminum structural supports and the battery module. The model incorporated foam encapsulants and detailed mechanical features of the housing and PCBA. The thermal analysis accounted for heat loads generated by onboard electronics, with discrete modeling of components dissipating 0.030 W or more, and smeared load distribution for lower-power components. Thermal contact resistance and detailed PCB stack-ups were included in the model. A steady-state solution was used to analyze PCBA temperatures, while a transient thermal solution over a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{1 5 0 0}$</tex>-second mission duration assessed battery cell temperature response. Results from both analyses confirmed that the battery design satisfies all structural and thermal requirements for the interceptor application. The comprehensive modeling approach ensures that the battery will perform reliably under the demanding conditions of flight, contributing to the overall safety and effectiveness of the AFTS.

title: How Trust and Technology Acceptance Shape Luxury Purchase Intention in Recommender System Contexts
authors: Valenthresia, Gilbert, Jonathan, Dyah Wahyu Sukmaningsih
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: E-commerce of luxury goods has high financial and psychological risks, so trust is needed as an important factor in making decisions to purchase luxury goods. This study examines a trust-aware recommendation framework to explain how the purchase intention of luxury goods is influenced by e-commerce platforms in the context of recommendation systems. Data collected from consumers in Indonesia with online luxury shopping experiences (507 responses, 438 were retained after screening) and analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The results show that luxury goods brand trust and trust in the platform are the main factors that support purchase intent. Meanwhile, the perceived usefulness factor of the system and product knowledge significantly affects the attitude of buying luxury goods. Besides that, the perceived ease of use of the system has a significant negative relationship with attitudes, showing that the ease of buying goods online doesn't show credibility in luxury goods in e-commerce and highlights the importance of trust-building mechanisms in high-risk environments.

title: A Hierarchical Screening Method for High-Risk Scenarios in Power Systems Based on Multi-Dimensional Risk Indicators
authors: Zheng Guo, Chao Wang, Zhouyue Hu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Renewable energy generation is a key pathway to achieving carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals. However, the complexity of power systems and the frequent failures caused by high penetration of renewable energy pose significant threats to their safe and stable operation. This paper proposes a hierarchical screening method that integrates multidimensional risk indicators with an efficient computational algorithm to construct a comprehensive risk assessment framework. A three-layer screening architecture is designed: a hash lookup table is used for rapid pre-screening to significantly reduce the scale of candidate scenarios; a clustering model is employed to refine the selection of highly relevant scenarios; and a complete model is applied for precise evaluation and physical constraint verification. Additionally, a dynamic maintenance mechanism based on concept drift detection is introduced to ensure the long-term effectiveness of the scenario library. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a high-risk scenario recall rate of 98.9%, significantly outperforming traditional machine learning methods. This approach provides strong support for real-time risk assessment and emergency control decision-making in power systems with high penetration of renewable energy.

title: DrowsyGuard: A Low Cost Personalized IoT Wearable for Real-Time Driver Drowsiness Detection
authors: Yuwono Marta Dinata, Vebrillia Santoso, Andreas Jodhinata, Agustinus Bimo Gumelar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Driver drowsiness is one of the major causes of traffic accidents in many countries around the world. This work presents DrowsyGuard, which is a personalized IoT-based wearable system for detecting driver drowsiness in real time. The proposed system captures facial images using a camera and then calculates the EAR and MAR through the detection of facial landmarks. One of the most important novelties introduced in this work is a user-specific calibration phase to establish personalized EAR and MAR thresholds, which significantly improves the accuracy of the detection and reduces false alarms due to natural expressions such as smiling. Whenever the EAR of the user drops below their personalized threshold for a continuous time span, an immediate multimodal alert is triggered. The multimodal alert runs through a low-latency ESP-NOW protocol between ESP32-C3 Mini microcontrollers - an audible warning from a buzzer with a haptic vibration via a wearable wristband. Results of extensive testing in diverse environmental conditions, including low-light environments, and subjects with glasses proved that the proposed system achieved a high accuracy with an average response time of 1.12 seconds. The results confirm DrowsyGuard's potential as an effective, user-adaptive solution to enhance driving safety and prevent drowsiness-related accidents.

title: Portable Spectrophotometer in Diabetes Analysis
authors: Ezekiel Nwokolo, Tahmina Akter Anondi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The diagnosis and management of metabolic diseases, particularly diabetes mellitus, rely heavily on the accurate measurement of glucose levels in body fluids. With the global prevalence of diabetes projected to increase substantially in the coming years, the demand for precise and real-time glucose monitoring is more critical than ever. This paper presents the development of a portable visible spectrophotometer tailored for glucose analysis, with a particular focus on its integration with digital microfluidics (DMF) platforms. The spectrophotometer utilizes colorimetric methods and microfluidic technology to enable rapid and efficient glucose detection. It features a userfriendly interface and a robust control and processing subsystem, making it suitable for point-of-care applications. Preliminary experimental validation demonstrates the device's optical performance using food dyes, while glucose assay measurements reveal the need for improved reaction protocols and voltage stabilization. This work highlights both the potential and current limitations of the proposed system for diabetes management, with specific engineering solutions identified to enhance reliability in clinical settings. Future improvements will focus on regulated light source drivers, heat insulation, and optimized glucose assay protocols to ensure consistent concentration-absorbance relationships.

title: A Short-Term Load Forecasting Method Based on Adaptive Data Cleaning and CNN-BiLSTM-Attention
authors: Ziqi Fan, Shuolin Zhang, Pengfei Chen, Yingying Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Short-term load forecasting is crucial for the secure and economic operation of power systems. However, its accuracy is often constrained by data quality issues and the complex characteristics of load profiles. This paper proposes a novel forecasting method that integrates adaptive data cleaning with a Convolutional Neural Network-Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory-Attention (CNN-BiLSTM-Attention) model. First, a data cleaning framework based on dual-dimensional anomaly detection and Mean Squared Error (MSE)-optimized correction is designed to enhance data quality at the source. Second, a multi-source fused feature system is constructed to comprehensively mine load-driving features from five perspectives: basic attributes, cyclical patterns, domain knowledge, external influences, and temporal dependencies. Finally, the architecture of the hybrid CNN-BiLSTM-Attention model is optimized to strengthen the synergistic effects of local feature extraction, bidirectional dependency modeling, and key information focusing. Experimental results on real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly outperforms traditional benchmark models across key performance metrics, including Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), and Relative Root Mean Square Error (RRMSE). Ablation studies validate the critical contribution of the data cleaning module and the Attention mechanism. Sensitivity analysis confirms the robustness of the model's parameters and proposes a load-characteristic-based tuning strategy. Industrial feasibility analysis evaluates the engineering potential from multiple dimensions: prediction accuracy, computational efficiency, and resource consumption. This research provides a comprehensive, robust, and engineering-ready solution for enhancing short-term load forecasting accuracy.

title: SPECTRA: An Efficient Spectral-Informed Neural Network for Sensor-Based Activity Recognition
authors: Deepika Gurung, Lala Shakti Swarup Ray, Mengxi Liu, Bo Zhou, Paul Lukowicz
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Real-time sensor-based applications in pervasive computing require edge-deployable models to ensure low latency, privacy, and efficient interaction. A prime example is sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR), where models must balance accuracy with stringent resource constraints. Yet many deep learning approaches treat temporal sensor signals as black-box sequences, overlooking spectral–temporal structure while demanding excessive computation. We present SPECTRA, a deployment-first, co-designed spectral–temporal architecture that integrates short-time Fourier transform (STFT) feature extraction, depthwise separable convolutions, and channel-wise self-attention to capture spectral–temporal dependencies under real edge runtime and memory constraints. A compact bidirectional GRU with attention pooling summarizes within-window dynamics at low cost, reducing downstream model burden while preserving accuracy. Across five public HAR datasets, SPECTRA matches or approaches larger CNN/LSTM/Transformer baselines while substantially reducing parameters, latency, and energy. Deployments on a Google Pixel 9 smartphone and an STM32L4 microcontroller further demonstrate end-to-end deployable real-time, private, and efficient HAR.

title: Analysis of 64-Bit Booth-MAC Based Processing Element for NTT Acceleration
authors: S.M. Swamynathan, V Barath Kumar, R Deepak Kumar, B Raghul, R Yaswanth
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Number Theoretic Transform (NTT) is an important computational kernel applied in polynomial multiplication for post-quantum cryptography and other high-security applications. High-precision arithmetic and fast multiply-accumulate (MAC) operations are necessary for efficient hardware realization of NTT. Unfortunately, most existing hardware implementations of NTT are based on low bit-width arithmetic and traditional multipliers, which are performance and scalability bottlenecks for NTT acceleration. This paper introduces the design and RTL implementation of a 64bit Booth-MAC-based processing element (PE) tailored for NTT acceleration. The proposed design combines a Radix-4 Booth multiplier, a 64-bit adder, and an accumulator register to support efficient signed multiply-accumulate operations. Radix-4 Booth encoding minimizes partial products, thus enhancing computational efficiency and hardware complexity. The whole design is developed at the front-end VLSI level using Verilog HDL and validated by simulation using various signed test vectors. Simulation results confirm the correctness of the proposed processing element for positive, negative, and zero operands. The modular and scalable design can be applied as a building block for systolic array-based NTT accelerators. The proposed solution fills the existing gap between algorithm-level NTT architectures and RTL-level hardware realization for high-precision cryptographic systems.

title: Optimal Energy Management of Hydrogen-Blended Integrated Power-Gas Systems via Sequential Convex Programming
authors: Ahmed Rabee Sayed, Mostafa Shaaban, Ahmed Osman-Ahmed, Faisal Alkaabneh, Mohammad Hamdan, Abdelfatah Ali
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The integration of renewable energy increases the need for flexible scheduling in integrated power-gas systems (IPGS). Hydrogen blending into existing natural gas pipelines forms hydrogen-rich compressed natural gas (HCNG), offering a cost-effective alternative to dedicated hydrogen transport while enhancing system flexibility. However, conventional IPGS models fail to capture the altered gas properties and coupled dynamics introduced by hydrogen injection and linepack. This paper proposes a tractable day-ahead scheduling framework for hydrogen-blended IPGS (HBIPGS) based on adaptive sequential convex programming (SCP). The model explicitly accounts for hydrogen blending effects, AC branch-flow constraints, linepack dynamics, and bidirectional gas flows. The adaptive SCP algorithm iteratively tightens convex relaxations through dynamic penalty updates, achieving feasible and cost-efficient solutions. Case studies demonstrate that moderate hydrogen blending provides the best techno-economic trade-off, hydrogen storage significantly improves electrolyzer efficiency, and the adaptive SCP method ensures smooth and reliable convergence.

title: Elevation-Aware BLER-Centric Link Adaptation for Dynamic LEO 5G NTN Systems
authors: Eric Forbes, Ethan Silverstein, Ying Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Link adaptation in fifth-generation (5G) NonTerrestrial Networks (NTN) faces unique challenges compared to terrestrial systems, including long round-trip latencies, large Doppler shifts, stale channel state information (CSI), and limited on-board computational resources. Conventional Outer Loop Link Adaptation (OLLA), designed for terrestrial deployments, struggles under these conditions. This paper introduces a Block Error Rate (BLER)-targeted link adaptation framework tailored for Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite systems. Unlike OLLA, which reacts to error outcomes through stepsize adjustments, our approach anchors adaptation decisions directly to explicit BLER targets. The framework integrates baseline comparisons, machine learning (ML)-enhanced lookup tables (LUTs), and over-the-air (OTA) validation on a commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) 5G testbed using the open-source platforms srsRAN and Open5GS. Simulation and OTA results show that the BLER-centric algorithm converges more quickly and achieves higher throughput than conventional approaches, particularly as satellites move from low to high elevation angles. ML-based refinements further improve performance in specific signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) regimes, while OTA experiments confirm the stability-throughput trade-off central to NTN link adaptation. These findings demonstrate that explicit BLER anchoring, supported by ML and OTA validation, offers a practical pathway for improving 5G NTN performance.

title: Cross-Region Mineral Mapping with Hyperspectral Data-Trained Multispectral Data Features
authors: Hideki Tsubomatsu, Satoru Yamamoto, Hideyuki Tonooka
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Hyperspectral (HS) remote sensing offers superior spectral resolution for mineral identification compared to multispectral (MS) data. However, HS sensors present a tradeoff between spatial resolution and coverage; high-resolution sensors like AVIRIS and HISUI are typically limited to narrow swaths, while some new sensors designed for wide-swath coverage, such as EMIT, consequently have a coarser spatial resolution. In contrast, MS imagery (e.g., ASTER) provides more comprehensive regional coverage. In many operational scenarios, MS images cover an entire region of interest, while HS data is available for only a sub-region. This discrepancy motivates the development of reliable MS-based classifiers that can be trained or validated using partial HS data. This study proposes a domain adaptation methodology to enhance the generalization of MS-based mineral classification across different geographical areas. The proposed method, distinct in its data-driven approach, derives feature transformation rules to bridge the spectral disparities between a source domain (training site) and a target domain (evaluation site). The process begins by identifying corresponding pixel clusters between sites based on spectral similarity (cosine similarity) for each mineral class. From these corresponding pixels, a multitude of candidate features—including band reflectance, adjacent band differences, and various spectral indices-are generated. Single regression models are then trained for each feature to map the target domain's spectral values to the source domain's values. A selection of the most stable and highly correlated transformation models, evaluated by their coefficient of determination and residual standard deviation, is used to generate a new set of transformed features for both domains. These transformed features are concatenated with the original MS spectral data to form an augmented feature set. This augmented set enables a Random Forest classifier to learn from both the original spectral information and the domain-invariant transformed information. The framework was validated through a comprehensive cross-transfer experiment involving three arid regions in the southwestern United States: Cuprite, Gold Field, and Silver Peak. ASTER data was used as an MS data for classification, with HS data from AVIRIS, EMIT, and HISUI serving as ground-truth references. The results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly improves the portability and robustness of MS-based classifiers across geographically and spectrally diverse regions, supporting the operational use of widely available MS archives for large-scale mineral mapping, especially when HS data for calibration and validation is limited.

title: Geometric Interpretation of Quantum-Optimum Detection for Weak Optical Signals
authors: Victor Vilnrotter
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The mathematical structure of the quantum-optimum detection operators for optical signals is derived, and approximated in a three-dimensional number-state basis for the case of weak optical signals. It is shown that mathematical rotation of the received signal-states in Hilbert-space approaches the quantum-optimum detection performance for optical signals such as On-Off Keying (OOK), BPSK (binary phase-shift keying), and binary Pulse Position Modulation (2-PPM) signaling formats originating from interplanetary or even greater interstellar distances. The approximate three-dimensional number-state model is interpreted to help identify the physical operations required to implement the quantum-optimum receiver with well-known field-preparation and signal-processing techniques for these three modulation formats. Graphs demonstrating the practical performance of receivers approximated by three-dimensional detection operators are evaluated, and compared with the theoretical limit on ideal receiver performance dictated by the laws of quantum mechanics.

title: A Lyapunov-Based Magnetorquer-Only Sun-Pointing Controller
authors: Paulo Fisch, Pedro Cachim, Max Holliday, Rodrigo Ventura, Pedro Lourenço, Zachary Manchester
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: We present a control scheme that can spin stabilize a spacecraft about its minor or major axis of inertia and simultaneously point this axis at the sun using only magnetorquers and low-cost sensors. The controller is implemented as a switched system, alternating between a spin-stabilizing and a target-pointing control law. Each control law locally minimizes a Lyapunov function. We perform a preliminary stability analysis to evaluate the controller's ability to drive the system to a Lyapunov-stable equilibrium in finite time within defined tolerances for the axisymmetric case. We evaluate our controller's performance through both extensive Monte-Carlo simulations for the axisymmetric and the general tri-inertial case and on orbit as part of the recent PY4 mission. The controller points within fifteen degrees of the sun in less than half an orbit in the PY4 mission and achieves nearly 100% success in numerical simulations.

title: A Systematic Review of Deep Learning Approaches to Drowning Detection
authors: Taofik Krisdiyanto, Igi Ardiyanto, Sunu Wibirama
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Drowning is one of the leading causes of accidental death worldwide, particularly in open water and swimming pool environments. Early and reliable detection of drowning incidents is critical to enable timely rescue and reduce fatalities. However, conventional surveillance systems that rely on human observation are prone to fatigue, delayed response, and reduced accuracy, especially in crowded or low-visibility conditions. As a result, deep learning-based intelligent Computer-Aided Detection (CAD) systems have emerged as a promising solution for automated drowning detection. Despite the growing number of studies proposing deep learning models for drowning detection, the existing literature remains fragmented across different model architectures, datasets, and evaluation strategies, making it difficult to obtain a comprehensive understanding of current research trends and challenges. This paper presents a systematic literature review that analyzed 25 peer-reviewed studies (from 243 initial records screened) published between 2021-2025. The review systematically identified, analyzed, and synthesized recent studies with respect to employed methods, datasets, evaluation metrics, and reported challenges. The findings reveal a strong dominance of object detection-based models, particularly YOLO variants, while most studies rely on custom and privately collected datasets evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP). Furthermore, the review highlights key challenges and future research opportunities, including data scarcity, environmental complexity, behavioral ambiguity, and real-time deployment constraints. This study provides a structured overview of current research trends and offers insights to guide future developments in deep learning-based drowning detection systems.

title: Learning Safety-Guaranteed, Non-Greedy Control Barrier Functions Using Reinforcement Learning
authors: Minduli Wijayatunga, Nathan Wallace, Salah Sukkarieh, Roberto Armellin
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Spacecraft rendezvous and proximity operations (RPO) present inherent safety risks to high-value assets, making safety guarantees essential for mission success. However, overly conservative control policies developed for safety can reduce mission efficiency. This work proposes a unified two-stage reinforcement learning (RL) framework that addresses the complementary limitations of a traditional safety approach—Input Constrained Control Barrier Functions (ICCBFs)—in safety-critical, fuel-limited spacecraft control. For a certified safe set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{S}$</tex>, ICCBFs yield a provably invariant inner set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}^{*} \subseteq \mathcal{S}$</tex> under input bounds, but the resulting per-step Quadratic Program (QP) is greedy and fuel-inefficient inside <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}^{*}$</tex>, and states in the residual set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{S} \backslash \mathcal{C}^{*}$</tex> that are recoverable are conservatively abandoned. Stage 1 of the proposed framework learns state-dependent class-<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{K}_{\infty}$</tex> parameters that adapt the ICCBF/CLF decay rates to embed long-horizon cost awareness while maintaining invariance in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{C}^{*}$</tex>. Stage 2 then learns a residual barrier <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$h_{\text{RL}}(\boldsymbol{x})$</tex> that provides recoverability for a subset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{S} \backslash \mathcal{C}^{*}$</tex>. At run-time, the framework selects the appropriate barrier formulation (Stage 1 or Stage 2) and solves a lightweight QP under zero-order hold. Both stages are trained with PPO using rewards that penalise constraint violations, control effort, and task-specific metrics. The framework is evaluated on three problems: cruise control, spacecraft rendezvous with a rotating target, and a spacecraft inspection task that maximizes observability while respecting keep-in and keep-out zone constraints. The test cases show a reduction in median fuel consumption compared to the ICCBF baselines by 25.00% to 12.00%, and a 7.00% to 8.00% increase in the number of cases that remain inside the safe set <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathcal{S}$</tex>. These results show that RL can embed long-term cost awareness into safety-critical control structures while maintaining the computational efficiency of QPs.

title: Stability Without Control: A Passive Approach for Robust and Agile Domestic Mobile Robots
authors: Huthaifa Ahmad, Yutaka Nakamura
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This paper presents Pixie, a self-balancing mobile robot developed to investigate a passive mechanical approach for achieving robust and agile motion in domestic environments. The proposed approach enhances these capabilities without compromising embodiment features essential for safe and intuitive operation in unstructured home settings - such as intrinsic stability, adaptability, safety, compactness, and expressive interaction modalities. Unlike conventional robots that rely heavily on active control strategies, Pixie employs an equilibrium-oriented design and strategic mass distribution to achieve intrinsic stability and compliant motion without continuous feedback control. Two experiments were conducted to evaluate the proposed approach: an obstacle disturbance test and a maneuverability test. Results demonstrate that Pixie passively recovers from external disturbances, traverses uneven terrain, navigates narrow spaces, and adapts to spatial constraints without active posture correction. These findings validate the effectiveness of a passive equilibriumbased design for achieving both robustness and agility, offering a promising foundation for safe, efficient, and humancompatible domestic robots.

title: An Intelligent IoT-Enabled Fire Fighting Robot with Real Time Environmental Monitoring
authors: R. Panneerselvi, Harish. R, Muthaiah Stalin. S, Sumithra. M, M. Dharma Teja, Seethalakshmi. K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The goal of this project is to create a design for an Arduino-based Firefighter Robot. Arduino is an open-source electronics platform that uses simple and easy-to-use hardware and software. The Arduino board may be controlled by Sending a series of instructions to the Arduino. Fire sensors notice possible dangers and tell the robot to respond. When the robot spots a fire, it activates a relay to start the water pump motor so it can spray water to extinguish the flames. The robot can move autonomously, navigate through different places, and check assess fire characteristics, and successfully extinguish flames. Once a fire is spotted, the system initiates its operation. A firefighting robot is made to protect the human lives and the properties in the building stop and manage fires so that people and buildings are kept safe lives and property. The IoT-enabled firefighting robot it tries to reduce the risks that human firefighters encounters, improve situational awareness, and maximize resource usage during emergency situations.

title: AI-Augmented Climate Intelligence for Planetary Regeneration and Resilience
authors: Devi Sanal, Gulam Jilani, Jayendra Gopal Thatipudi, D Shanthi Chelliah, Apurva D. Fuladi, M. Anuradha
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The accelerating pace of climate change necessitates intelligent, scalable solutions capable of predicting environmental shifts and recommending actionable regenerative strategies. The project is an AI-enhanced climate intelligence system, GaiaMind, incorporating multi-source climate data, hybrid spatiotemporal modelling, causal inference and regenerative scenario generation. The framework integrates satellite (Landsat) and ground-level IoT sensors, past climate data, and physics-based models with the use of spatiotemporal alignment algorithms. GaiaMind infers dynamic, nonlinear connections among ecological systems at a high degree of fidelity by the deployment of a hybrid AI architecture that consists of Convolutional LSTMs and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). Statistically significant relationships between anthropogenic drivers and regional climate anomalies were found in more than 90 % of test regions using causal discovery using Granger causality. The Earth subsystem (cryosphere, hydrosphere, biosphere) digital twin model can be simulated in the real-time, and the multi-objective regenerative scenarios can be explored by generative AI (GANs and diffusion models) on ecological, carbon, and social scales. GaiaMind selected the highest prediction accuracy of 96.82 %on the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex> global climatic zones, when compared to individual ConvLSTM (91.88) and GNN (89.82) models. Resilience indices were increased by an average of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$21.72-46.15. 01$</tex> after the intervention, which confirms the success of AI-based solutions. The system also detected early-warning tipping points through change-point detecting which provided a predictive benefit to adaptive policymaking. To sum up, GaiaMind can be viewed as the paradigm shift between reactive modeling and proactive regeneration of the planet. The future work will involve further extension of federated learning in decentralized climate labs and enhance explainability to stakeholders to achieve trust in the work and adoption.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Foundation Agent for Hyperparameter Tuning in Multi-UAS Positioning
authors: Yajie Bao, Deeraj Nagothu, Dan Shen, Genshe Chen, Samson Badlia, Simon Khan, Erik Blasch, Khanh Pham
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper presents a design of a foundation model (FM) agent for hyperparameter tuning (HT) under the neuro-symbolic (NS) framework. Hyperparameters are critical for the performance of algorithms, which could take many human and computation resources to find the optimal hyperparameters. While many methods have been proposed for HT, human involvement is still inevitable for these methods to work. FMs have opened up possibilities for developing intelligent agents to perform tasks that require human participation. In this work, we design a workflow for an FM agent to tune hyperparameters and achieve the desired performance. By incorporating NS reasoning into the workflow, our design enhances FM reasoning by specifying the logic rules for (generating) responses and enabling human verification and intervention. Experiments on the HT of the range-only positioning algorithm for multiple unmanned aerial systems (UASs) in a Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)-denied environment are provided to demonstrate that the proposed design can achieve comparative performance against human experts with improved efficiency.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Deep Learning Model for Knee MRI Analysis in Ligament Injury Detection
authors: Abdulhaq Khalifah, Ahmed Al-Malki, Mohammed Al-Shehri, Hossam Meshref
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Knee injuries, particularly Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) tears, are prevalent musculoskeletal disorders that require precise and timely diagnosis. This study presents the first known application of deep learning on this specific dataset. It proposes a robust 3D Convolutional Neural Network (3D-CNN) framework. The framework enables automated classification of knee conditions into three categories: healthy, partially injured, and severely injured. Unlike traditional approaches, our method does not convert 3D volumes into 2D slices. It processes the full MRI volumes directly. This preserves critical depth-wise spatial information. We addressed major challenges related to real-world data quality and class imbalance. We implemented a rigorous preprocessing pipeline. It included data cleaning and comprehensive augmentation. This ensured robust and reliable model training. Crucially, we prioritized clinical safety in the proposed framework. It demonstrated exceptional reliability in detecting severe injuries. The model achieved approximately 97% sensitivity, minimizing false negatives in critical cases. It also reached a solid overall test accuracy of 83.55%. Furthermore, we integrated Explainable AI (XAI) using Grad-CAM. The model generates visual heat maps. These maps enhance interpretability and clinical confidence. They highlight the exact regions driving the model's decisions. These results highlight the potential of our 3D deep learning system to serve as a reliable, automated second-opinion tool in clinical radiology.

title: An Intelligent Fuel Theft Detection and Call Alert System Using ESP8266 Microcontroller
authors: A Sampath, S. Abishek, R. Arunkumar, G. Naveenkumar
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Fuel theft is a major issue in automobiles, generators and industrial fuel storage systems, leading to significant economic losses and safety risks. To address this problem, proposes the Implementation of a Fuel Theft Detection and Real-Time Call Alert System using ESP8266 Microcontroller. The system continuously monitors the fuel level using a fuel level sensor and detects abnormal or sudden fuel drops that indicate possible theft. The ESP8266 microcontroller acts as the central control unit, processing sensor data and enabling real-time communication through cloud connectivity. When fuel theft is detected, the system immediately triggers a GSM module to place a call or send an alert to the registered user. Simultaneously, a buzzer is activated to provide a local audible warning and a relay module can be used to cut off the fuel pump or engine operation to prevent further loss. For remote monitoring, the system integrates with the Blynk IoT platform, allowing users to view live fuel level data and alert notifications through a mobile application.

title: Deep Learning Leveraged Synergistic Agriculture Ecosystem for Indian Farming
authors: Manikandan A, Thamizhvel R, Vaithianathan N, Prabhakaran R, Abitha M, Vignesh G
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The Agricultural sector in India needs efficient, data-driven tools urgently that can facilitate farmers in crop selection, help in identifying plant diseases, and using fertilizers in a most effective way. Mostly, these traditional ways of farming lead to inappropriate crop selection and the diseases are detected at a very late stage, resulting in the reduction of the total yield. The paper here presents a deep learning-based smart agriculture system Indian farming environment specific. The system is made up of three main modules: a crop recommendation model that incorporates soil nutrients and weather parameters, a fertilizer optimization module that determines NPK deficiencies, and a plant disease recognition model that is a convolutional neural networks-based one. The introduced system is a machine learning/deep learning hybrid that ensures the correctness of decision-making processes. The experiments confirm that AI systems can become a real source of productivity in agriculture and can be used as reliable tools for farmers' automated guidance, by showing very high accuracies in crop prediction and disease classification.

title: Physics-Aware Graph Reinforcement Learning for Millisecond-Level Dispatch of High Proportion Renewable Energy Power Grids
authors: Bingzhang Li, Yinye Shen, Fengrui Liu, Guokun Cui, Chenlu Lei, Ruiyang Weng, Wenxi Zeng
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy sources (RES), power systems face significant challenges such as reduced system inertia and increased operational uncertainty. In this context, traditional model-based optimization methods (e.g., AC optimal power flow) struggle to meet real-time dispatch requirements due to their high computational complexity. To address this issue, this paper proposes a Physics-Aware Graph Reinforcement Learning (P-GRL) framework for real-time economic dispatch under dynamic topology and high uncertainty. The proposed method employs a graph convolutional network (GCN) to extract spatial topological features of the power grid and integrates the Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm to learn generator dispatch strategies in a continuous action space. In addition, a physics-informed soft-constraint reward function and a deterministic action correction mechanism are introduced to explicitly embed power balance and line security constraints into the reinforcement learning decision loop. Simulation results on the modified IEEE 39-bus system demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a favorable trade-off between economic efficiency and operational security while enabling millisecond-level decision-making. In addition, the framework exhibits strong robustness and generalization capability under N-1 contingency scenarios.

title: Structured Visual Summarization of Biomedical Articles with BiomindBERT: A SciBERT-Based and Hierarchical Clustering Approach
authors: Edward Engelbert Wiguna, Feliks Victor Parningotan Samosir
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: In the biomedical domain, the rapid growth of scientific literature makes it increasingly difficult for healthcare professionals and researchers to efficiently access and understand key findings. To address this challenge, we propose biomindBERT, a system that automatically generates mind maps from biomedical and medical articles. The system leverages SciBERT embeddings to capture semantic relationships between sentences and applies agglomerative hierarchical clustering with Ward linkage and cosine distance to uncover conceptual hierarchies. Representative sentences from clusters are selected using centroid-based selection (i.e., the sentence whose embedding is closest to the cluster centroid) to construct structured visual summaries, with the number of clusters determined by the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\mathrm{N}+1) / \log _{2} \mathrm{N}$</tex> heuristic. We evaluate the system using ROUGE, BERTScore, and a structured manual assessment by five domain experts with inter-rater agreement reporting. Evaluation shows competitive performance (ROUGE-1: 0.3620, BERTScore-F1: 0.8429) and expert ratings of 4.7 conciseness, 4.6 coherence, and 4.95 relevance (scale 1-5). Ablation studies comparing centroidbased vs. first-sentence selection and SciBERT vs. general Sentence-BERT embeddings demonstrate the benefit of domain-specific embeddings and principled sentence selection. Additional baselines including K-Means flat clustering and PEGASUS abstractive summarization contextualize the results. These results demonstrate the system's effectiveness in enhancing biomedical informatics workflows, providing clinicians and researchers with an intelligent information technology tool to navigate complex biomedical knowledge.

title: Accurate and Robust Realization of DN-Aggregated VPPs for Primary Frequency Support
authors: Zirong Xu, Youbo Liu, Zhiyuan Tang, Tingjian Liu, Ji’ang Liu, Junyong Liu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper addresses primary frequency support (PFS) provision from distribution networks (DNs) by accurately and robustly implementing DN-aggregated virtual power plants (VPPs) with specified power-frequency droop characteristics. To this end, a dynamic linearization relationship model between the overall frequency response deviation and control inputs is built. Then, data-driven control schemes for distributed energy storage units are developed only relying on the system input and output data. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed control method enables a more accurate and robust realization of DN-aggregated VPPs for PFS provision.

title: GEN-Z: Generative Honeypot Ecosystems Using Adversarial Deep Learning for Proactive Cyber-Defense
authors: Mohammed Ishaque, Zainab Siddiqui, Rimas Shami Almasari, Jouri Bassem Bukhari
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Conventional honeypots provide valuable threat intelligence but are increasingly constrained by static configurations, low-fidelity interaction, and susceptibility to fingerprinting. These limitations reduce attacker engagement and degrade the quality of collected telemetry. We present GEN-Z, a generative honeypot ecosystem that uses adversarial deep learning to continuously synthesize high-fidelity, targeted decoys and to harden them against automated detection. GEN-Z combines (i) an asset-mirroring reconnaissance module that profiles production services, (ii) a generator that produces decoy configurations and human-like artifacts (e.g., user histories, files, and command outputs) using a GAN and a constrained large-language-model (LLM) templating layer, and (iii) a discriminator that implements attacker-grade fingerprinting tests and trains the generator to evade them. Interactions are streamed into an automated intelligence pipeline that extracts indicators of compromise (IoCs) and deployable mitigations (e.g., SIEM rules). In a controlled testbed evaluation, GEN-Z improves deception rate against automated fingerprinting to 95% and increases red-team engagement rate to 78%, outperforming Cowrie/MHN and recent LLM-based terminal honeypots. These results motivate a shift from static deception to continuously learned, generative counter-intelligence for proactive cyber-defense.

title: Green Energy Electric Vehicles in Battery Storage System Applications
authors: L. Manjunath, Inderpreet Kaur, S. Kumaresh, G. Narsimlu, P. Neeraja, A Padma, G. Ramachandran
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The Energy-storing electric vehicles (EVs) can stabilize their batteries to increase grid stability and productivity. On the other hand, Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) is an enabling technology that enables electric vehicles to feedback when storage is most critical in addition to drumming electricity when plugged in. Both the penetration of traenalde venergies and the maintenance of ground stability now depend on this bidirectional energy. The rise in the significance of EVs and energy assets is a response to india demand for supply and demand balance, renewable energy solutions, and Global energy transition and climate change. The significant advancements in battery technology-such as lithium-ion batteries-that had the potential to be used with electric motors gave rise to the current viability of EVs as transportable power units. Notably, a few early experimental projects shown that EVs could be helpful not just for transportation but also for storing electricity. These prototypes were referred to as proof-of-concept projects, demonstrating that V2G was, in fact, an example, company indian demonstration projects. Their decentralized system allows them to store energy during off-peak periods and release it during peak demand periods. By balancing out supply fluctuations, this renewable penetration lowers the demand for fuel kapa. Additionally, participating in the V2G program offers benefits that strengthen the appeal of energy alternatives. Despite these benefits, there are a number of obstacles that prevent widespread adoption.

title: Torque Ripple Mitigation of Switched Reluctance Motors Using an Optimized Generalized Two-Stage Torque Sharing Function
authors: Chenyi Zhao, Hanqi Liao
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Switched reluctance motors (SRMs) are attractive for robust electric drives, yet their doubly salient structure and nonlinear torque-production mechanism lead to severe torque pulsations. This paper proposes a generalized two-stage torque sharing function (G-TSTSF) that inserts an adjustable internal boundary angle within the commutation interval and adopts two independent shaping functions for the rising and falling torque-allocation segments while maintaining continuity over one electrical period. The turn-on angle and two shape factors are optimized by a genetic algorithm (GA) using the torque ripple factor as the objective. A 6.5 kW, 8/6 SRM supplied by a 314 V dc bus is evaluated at 500, 1000 and 1500 r/min. Compared with a conventional experimental TSF, the torque ripple factor is reduced from 31.50%, 33.80% and 34.05% to 21.56%, 21.58% and 22.43%, respectively, together with mitigated commutation torque peaks and smaller current spikes.

title: Smart Footwear for Non-Intrusive Vital Signs and Gait Health Monitoring
authors: R. Mothi, Shalini S., Srivaishnavi S, Vasanthi V
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Smart footwear is a potential answer for seamlessly monitoring vital signs and gait health and overcomes the limitations of conventional wearables, such as watches and wrist bands. With miniaturized sensors embedded in footwear, the user can have parameters such as heart rate, body temperature, <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SpO}_{2}$</tex>, hydration status, and gait parameters monitored continuously without altering the user experience. Not only does this improve the accuracy of gait, but it provides a suite of overall health measures for early detection of gait impairment and chronic health conditions. Using smart footwear in everyday life can be catalytic to potential distance-based interventions in healthcare, elderly care, sports performance, and rehabilitation, establishing an economic and accessible approach to health monitoring.

title: An Adaptive Computer-Assisted Language Learning System Using Recurrent Neural Networks and Deep Reinforcement Learning
authors: Jagadeesan S, Mathirvishnu L K, Arnald M, Vetrivel E
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: An adaptive computer-assisted language learning (CALL) system is an advanced technology used to create a customized language-learning experience while increasing student participation. This study describes the development of a unique adaptive CALL framework using Recurrent Neural Networks (RNN) and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for automatic feedback and individualized English language acquisition. The proposed approach uses sequential textual and auditory input to evaluate a learner's grammatical structure, pronunciation, and sentences; based on this data, an adaptive reinforcement learning process continuously modifies task/evaluation complexity, feedback techniques and paths to knowledge through the relationship of state, action and reward. By performing experimental tests, an overall training accuracy of 92 % was achieved and an 88 % validation success was recorded, demonstrating steady growth and effective transferability. The adaptations made from the adaptive learning process improved resultant language proficiency by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 2} \boldsymbol{\%}$</tex> to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{2 6 \%}$</tex>, lowered the number of recurring learner errors by 37 %, and increased the level of learner engagement by approximately 35 %. This study shows how the combination of deep and reinforcement learning enhances and adapts today's computer-assisted language learning model.
