title: Towards Communication-Efficient and Data-Free Collaborative Fine-Tuning between Small and Large Language Models
authors: Zhenya Ma, Yongheng Deng, Ziqing Qiao, Yongjian Fu, Sheng Yue, Ju Ren
publish: IEEE Transactions on Networking
abstract: While large language models (LLMs) exhibit impressive general capabilities, their performance on domainspecific tasks often requires fine-tuning with private data that cannot be shared due to privacy constraints. Directly deploying LLMs on resource-constrained clients for local fine-tuning is impractical due to their significant computation and communication costs. In addition, pre-trained LLMs are valuable intellectual property, and model owners are reluctant to distribute full model weights. To address these challenges, we propose CoT-LM, a communication-efficient, computation-light, and data-free framework for collaborative fine-tuning between small (SLMs) and large language models (LLMs). In CoT-LM, clients fine-tune lightweight SLMs locally without uploading models or private data. These SLMs provide task-specific feedback to guide server-side LLM enhancement via an efficient communication protocol that exchanges only lightweight synthetic data and feedback. The framework supports both synchronous and asynchronous collaboration and enables mutual enhancement: the LLM improves its task-specific capabilities, while clients benefit from refined synthetic data or distilled knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CoT-LM significantly boosts natural language understanding (NLU, up to 18.3% for LLMs and 8.0% for SLMs) and natural language generation (NLG, up to 31.7% for LLMs) performance across diverse tasks while preserving data privacy, model intellectual property, and generalization capabilities, achieving significant reductions in computation and communication overhead.

title: Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp3-3693439.docx
authors: Shanshan Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Density Peak Anchor Selection for Multi-View Clustering
authors: Tianchuan Yang, Haoyan Yang, Man-Sheng Chen, Chang-Dong Wang, Jipeng Guo, Xiangcheng Li, Youming Sun, Fuying Huang, Haiqiang Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Multi-view clustering (MVC) typically enhances clustering performance by leveraging consistent and complementary information across views. With increasing data scale, many MVC methods employ few anchors to represent data, effectively reducing complexity. Traditional anchor selection methods include random sampling and k-means. Recent methods leverage variance or inter-class error to guide anchor selection. However, their anchors are selected sequentially, where each newly selected anchor focuses on divergences from the previous anchor. This local perspective leads to unstable anchor coverage and fails to fully represent complex cluster shapes. An anchor selection algorithm, termed DPAS, is proposed to solve this problem. From a novel density perspective, it selects density peaks satisfying criteria as anchors and reduces complexity based on granular balls. As a result, the efficient DPAS exhibits stronger representativeness and robustness. Furthermore, the order and number of anchors selected from different views typically need to be aligned. The assumptions of existing alignment methods are often too restrictive, compromising the quality of alignment. Thus, an alignment method FAA based on flexible assumptions is designed to improve generalization. In this paper, we propose a new fast MVC method without alternating optimization named DPAS-MC. First, DPAS is applied to select high-quality anchors for views. Then, anchor graphs are computed, aligned, and fused via FAA. Finally, a low-rank tensor is calculated to obtain clustering results. Experiments show that our methods outperform existing state-of-the-art methods in performance and efficiency. The code is available at https://github.com/ytccyw.

title: OutDreamer: Video Outpainting with a Diffusion Transformer
authors: Linhao Zhong, Fan Li, Yi Huang, Jianzhuang Liu, Renjing Pei, Fenglong Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
abstract: Video outpainting is a challenging task that generates new video content by extending beyond the boundaries of an original input video, requiring both temporal and spatial consistency. Many existing methods utilize latent diffusion models with U-Net backbones but still struggle to achieve high quality and adaptability in generated content. Diffusion transformers (DiTs) have emerged as a promising alternative because of their superior performance. We introduce OutDreamer, a DiT-based video outpainting framework comprising two main components: a video control branch and a conditional outpainting branch. The video control branch effectively extracts masked video information, while the conditional outpainting branch generates missing content based on these extracted conditions. Additionally, we propose a mask-driven self-attention layer that dynamically integrates the given mask information, further enhancing the model’s adaptability to outpainting tasks. Furthermore, we introduce a latent alignment loss to maintain overall consistency both within and between frames. For long video outpainting, we employ a cross-video-clip refiner to iteratively generate missing content, ensuring temporal consistency across video clips. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that our OutDreamer outperforms existing video outpainting methods on widely recognized benchmarks.

title: A Common Pool of Privacy Problems: Legal and Technical Lessons from a Large-Scale Web-Scraped Machine Learning Dataset
authors: Rachel Hong, Jevan Hutson, William Agnew, Imaad Huda, Tadayoshi Kohno, Jamie Morgenstern
publish: Proceedings of the Symposium on Computer Science and Law
abstract: None

title: Evaluating Generative AI in Benefits Administration: A Demonstration Project
authors: Olivia Martin, Varun Magesh, Faiz Surani, Kit Rodolfa, Daniel E. Ho, Amy Perez

title: Antiparallel-Coupled Oscillation in a Spin-Torque Oscillator for MAMR Using Negative-Spin-Polarization Materials
authors: Yuji Nakagawa, Naoyuki Narita, Masayuki Takagishi, Yuto Hayakawa, Junki Numata, Ryo Osamura, Tomoyuki Maeda
publish: IEEE Transactions on Magnetics
abstract: Antiparallel coupling of oscillating magnetizations in spin-torque oscillators (STOs) can generate an ideal microwave field for microwave-assisted magnetic recording (MAMR). Conventional STOs typically employ spin-injection layers (SILs) to drive the oscillation of field generation layers (FGLs), which increases design flexibility but complicates the device structure. Here we propose, formulate, and simulate an SIL-free STO composed of two FGLs, one of which uses a negative-spin-polarization material such that the electron spin polarization is opposite to the magnetization direction. The mutual spin-transfer torque interaction between the two FGLs induces and stabilizes an antiparallel-coupled oscillation. We validate this concept using micromagnetic simulations based on a recording head model. We also discuss further improvements in oscillation stability via negative exchange coupling between the FGLs. Negative spin polarization offers a promising approach for designing simpler STO structures that achieve higher recording density with MAMR.

title: Author Index
authors: N/A

title: Alternating-Excitation Wireless Power Transfer for Next-Generation Capsule Endoscope
authors: Jinyang Gao, Chenzhi Lu, Xinyu Li, Kewei Zhu, Qiulin Tan, Li Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Electronics
abstract: Inductively-coupled wireless power transfer (WPT) provides a promising solution for powering the next-generation capsule endoscope (CE). Existing multi-D transmitting coils (TCs) for this application are all excited simultaneously, posing the issue of difficult-to-eliminate mutual inductance electromotive force (EMF). To avoid the adverse effects of mutual inductance EMF, this study develops an alternating-excitation WPT system, which consists of an alternatively excited 2-D TC, an annular 1-D receiving coil (RC) with embedded magnetic core, an onboard attitude detection module to provide excitation feedback, and a button battery to ensure continuous power supply during excitation switching. This study details the design and testing of each consisted module. Experiments have shown that the developed system can supply stable and sufficient power to the CE with changing attitude and position, achieving an average power transfer efficiency (PTE) of 13.05%. In addition, the PTE outperforms that of the simultaneous-excitation WPT in 66.16% of the attitude space. This demonstrates that the alternating- excitation WPT, which is more feasible in practice, can enable more efficient power supply for the next-generation CE.

title: PROTEUS: A 40 nm Programmable General-Purpose Digital Compute-In-Memory Accelerator With eNVM and Hierarchical ISA for Versatile Edge AI
authors: Luqi Zheng, Amir Massah Bavani, Mufeng Chen, Shuting Du, Te-Yu Hsin, Win-San Khwa, Ping-Sheng Wu, Ashwin Sanjay Lele, Bo Zhang, Brian Crafton, Harry Chuang, Yu-Der Chih, Meng-Fan Chang, Haitong Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Solid-State Circuits
abstract: We present PROTEUS, an 18 mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> programmable general-purpose digital compute-in-memory (GP-DCIM) accelerator integrating 4 Mb resistive random access memory (RRAM) and 2.6 Mb tensor static random access memory (SRAM) with a 32-bit hierarchical DCIM instruction set architecture (ISA). PROTEUS features fine-grained 1-D matrix tiling and a reconfigurable DCIM datapath/pipeline for near-100% memory utilization, supporting INT8/INT16/FP8/FP16 DCIM computations. PROTEUS unifies SRAM/RRAM dataflows and embeds nonvolatile micro-programs in RRAM to enable rapid switching among prestored kernels without incurring off-chip instruction feeds or RRAM rewrites. Fabricated in 40 nm ultra-low power (ULP) CMOS with foundry RRAM, PROTEUS delivers 702 GOPS throughput, 6.4 TOPS/W energy efficiency, and 0.039 TOPS/mm<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> compute density. It is validated on ResNet-20, BERT-Tiny, MobileViT, GraphSAGE, and Vision Mamba, demonstrating versatility across CNN, Transformer, hybrid CNN-Transformer, graph neural network (GNN), and state-space model (SSM) workloads.

title: Quantum Partial Sorting for Signal Decoding in Wireless Communication Systems
authors: Abdulmohsen Alsaui, Ibrahim Al-Nahhal, Octavia A. Dobre, Hyundong Shin
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: This work proposes a novel quantum-assisted partial sorting algorithm, called multi-minima Dürr–Høyer (MMDH), designed to reduce query complexity in scenarios where only a small subset of elements must be sorted. Empirical results show that MMDH significantly outperforms classical algorithms in these settings, achieving over an order of magnitude reduction in query complexity. The algorithm is applied to signal detection in multiple-input multiple-output systems and is particularly effective when integrated into a newly introduced variable-complexity sphere decoder, called progressive tree expansion (PTE), which inherently benefits from partial sorting. Compared to fixed-complexity sphere decoders (FCSDs), the PTE algorithm substantially reduces the computational complexity, especially at high signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs). When augmented with MMDH, the quantum-assisted PTE decoder achieves near maximum-likelihood error performance while mitigating the query overhead commonly associated with tree-based decoders. In contrast, conventional FCSDs benefit less from MMDH, as they require selection of multiple minima rather than partial ordering, a task where classical methods such as heap-based selection remain competitive. Although MMDH introduces a small failure probability, the resulting error floor stays below practical thresholds in high-SNR regimes.

title: Development of a Structural Thermal Model for a Water-Based Propulsion System on the GEO-X Mission
authors: Ryo Minematsu, Ten Arai, Masaki Fujii, Minwoo Han, Hayato Yoshida, Isamu Moriai, Ryota Fuse, Koki Ikeda, Taiki Achiha, Sanguk Jeong, Natsumi Hirota, Kenshin Yamakawa, Hiroyuki Koizumi, Ryu Funase, Mariko Akiyama, Yuichiro Ezoe
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: A water-based propulsion system is scheduled to be integrated into the GEO-X (Geospace X-ray image) mission. GEO-X mission aims to visualize the Earth's magnetosphere through X-Ray imaging and demonstrates deep space access using the spacecraft's own propulsion system. A structural thermal model (STM) of the GEO-X mission has been designed and developed in preparation for the planned launch in 2027. The STM includes the propulsion system and is used to evaluate the performance of the system. The propulsion system comprises a resistojet thruster for momentum unloading and delta-V maneuvers, as well as a gridded ion thruster for delta-V operations in deep space. Water is employed as the propellant for all thrusters and water propellant offers the advantage of liquid-phase storage, thereby facilitating a more compact tank design compared to conventional noble gas propellants. Although the reduced performance of water-based thrusters relative to noble gas propellant can result in longer orbital transfer time and labor hours associated with spacecraft operation, this mission avoids such labor hour increases by employing an autonomous operation system with the ion thruster. The autonomous system controls thruster operation by adjusting the propellant flow rate and accommodating variations in the surrounding thermal environment, in response to inputs specifying the target delta-V and orbital transfer time. Furthermore, a water circulation system is employed to support thermal management. This circulation system serves a dual purpose: it provides cooling for the water propulsion system and also functions as the water propellant source. Simulations indicate that the system achieves a thermal conductance of 0.74 W/K. The water circulation system is also planned to be incorporated as a temperature control parameter within the autonomous control system for the ion thruster.

title: Active-Reactive Coordinated Voltage Optimization in Emerging Distribution Networks: An Approximate Dynamic Programming Approach
authors: Tong He, Han Wang, Runfan Zhang, Gengfeng Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the ultra-high penetration of distributed renewable energy, distribution networks are frequently confronted with severe challenges, including reverse power flow and nodal voltage violations. Due to the temporal coupling of the state of charge during the continuous operation of distributed energy storage systems, traditional voltage optimization methods are highly prone to computational bottlenecks caused by the dimensional explosion of the decision horizon. Consequently, they struggle to meet the strict timeliness requirements of efficient online regulation. To address this issue, this paper proposes a time-domain decoupled voltage control strategy for emerging distribution networks based on approximate dynamic programming. First, an active-reactive coordinated voltage optimization model is constructed, incorporating multidimensional economic and security indices. Second, the optimization process considering complex temporal constraints is reformulated as a Markov decision process. Within this framework, a piecewise linear function is employed to efficiently approximate the optimal value function, and an offline training mechanism based on the forward-backward iteration of dual multipliers is designed to extract the true marginal value of energy. By thoroughly reducing the full-horizon high-dimensional decision-making process into a single-step sequential optimization problem, this strategy fundamentally eliminates the curse of dimensionality in the temporal domain. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method can achieve fast online optimization, successfully balancing the global economic efficiency of system operation with high engineering application value.

title: Correlation-Informed Time-Series Forecasting and Anomaly Detection
authors: Lauren Bailey, James Carnal, Jamie Coble, Xingang Zhao
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Early and efficient detection of system anomalies is vital for ensuring safety for both the system and operators, as well as preventing growing concerns that could lead to increased system downtime. Time-series forecasting using machine learning (ML) techniques offers a way to predict future system states and enhance anomaly detection. This work explores these ML methods, particularly long short-term memory (LSTM) neural networks (NNs), to see if data-driven approaches can outperform physics-based methods in identifying anomalies. Additionally, a new technique called correlation-informed LSTM (CI-LSTM) is introduced. The CI-LSTM is a knowledge-guided ML method that leverages feature correlations to create a linear equation predicting each feature. The residuals based on these linear equations are included in the ML loss function. Data for this study was collected from the Tri-Twin, a three-loop flow system at the University of Tennessee, Knoxville (UTK). The baseline LSTM achieved excellent accuracy, with the highest error occurring for the flow rate in loop three, at a relative root mean square error (rRMSE) of 4.93%; all other errors were below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{0. 5 0 \%}$</tex>. The CI-LSTM provided even higher accuracy, reducing the flow rate rRMSE in loop three to 3.0%, while other features either improved or remained similar to the pure ML results. A third method, feature-controlled CI-LSTM, was also explored; in this approach, features that performed better under pure LSTM used only the data-driven loss function, while features that performed better under CI-LSTM used the combined loss function. This method outperformed the standard CI-LSTM in some cases but requires further development to become a more robust approach. Based on the results, an anomaly detection routine was employed to determine the time needed to identify an anomalous period. Using the LSTM predictions, the anomaly was detected within 8 seconds of its occurrence. This was compared to anomaly detection results from a physics model in past work, and the results were comparable, with that approach taking 12 seconds to identify the anomaly. This shows that the ML approaches perform just as well as the physics-based approaches, making them useful in instances where the complete physics model may not be accessible. Future work will focus on expanding the anomaly detection techniques to improve the detection timeline. Overall, this work highlights the significant potential of LSTMs for anomaly detection and the effectiveness of CI-ML methods for improved time-series forecasting.

title: Coordinated World Model Learning for Deep Space Robot Teams
authors: Andrzej M.J. Skulimowski
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: World model (WM) is a key component of the mobile robot's software, a complex module that integrates various machine learning (ML) methods, pattern recognition and image understanding algorithms, with a dynamic knowledge base, sensors and other cyber-physical subsystems. Although significant progress has been made in developing WMs for single robots, the theory and practice of team WM learning lack a unified framework. This article addresses the above gap from a general perspective and introduces a novel concept for autonomous multi-robot WM learning, which combines synergistic methods such as multi-agent model-based reinforcement learning (MA-MBRL), federated learning, and other collaborative ML paradigms and techniques. Multi-robot learning can also be related to self-supervised learning and to the combinations of individual and team ML. We will propose a group WM-building process that leverages coordination mechanisms enhanced by anticipatory behaviors within planetary autonomous robot teams. The resulting WMs will support simultaneous solution of multi-criteria Multi-Robot Task Allocation and Multi-Agent Path Planning (MRTA-MAPP) problems. Finally, we will provide arguments that the appropriately defined WM building process may result in the minimization of the risk of robot damage while maximizing its exploration efficiency, measured by the expected scientific benefits achieved within the given time.

title: Cyber Resilient Provenance Tracking for Virtual Certification
authors: Malte Christian Struck, Andreas Schreiber, Alexander Weinert, Paula Ruß, Michael Felderer
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The Virtual Product House (VPH) is an integration and testing center for virtually certifying aircraft components. This is important because developing an aircraft component is costly and time-consuming. If a production team discovers that a component does not meet requirements, the team must repeat the entire design, build, and test process. This repetitive development process is costly and inhibits future innovation. Provenance refers to the origin or history of an artifact, object, or piece of data. In the digital age, provenance has become increasingly important for digital content. While digital artifacts change frequently, provenance records typically do not. In aircraft development, we use provenance to track the development steps of the aircraft, as well as the digital processing workflows. We assume that traceability of aircraft data and reliability of tools and toolchains are both essential for simulation-supported aircraft certification. Based on the requirements of a virtual design process, we have extended the provenance tracking system that we developed for the VPH by applying a digital signature scheme. Our solution is adaptable to emerging security technologies, including post-quantum cryptography. We provide a high-level overview of the proposed system and discuss the design choices behind our approach. Our goal is to demonstrate the architecture, key features, and functionality of our proposed provenance tracking solution for digital aerospace design and certification processes.

title: Terrain-Adaptive Strategies to Prevent and Recover from Rover Wheel-Slip
authors: Jasper Grant, Mae Seto, Paul Grouchy
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Wheel-slip in planetary rovers creates localization error, wasted power, worn tires and occasionally, mission failure. While wheel-slip estimation and sensing has advanced, active online compensation for wheel-slip has received less attention. Existing approaches use real-time terrain measurements or respond to proprioceptive feedback with only adjusted wheel speeds and torques. Therefore, there is potential to improve proprioceptive-only strategies by not only leveraging wheel speeds, but also potentially steering angles and active suspension, to actively respond to slip. A solution which uses these additional inputs is not confined to conventional driving and can consider unconventional gaits such as “walking” or “inch worm” style locomotion. Unconventional gaits can increase the range of navigable slopes beyond limits established by previous rovers. Despite their improved performance, unconventional gaits still rely on terrain parameter knowledge. Existing physics-based simulation models also require knowledge of the soil properties of the navigated terrain. A proposed two-stage offline then online learning solution generates wheel-slip compensation controllers that are not confined to conventional gaits and iterates until a controller is identified to be high-performing on the terrain considered without directly sensing the terrain parameters. Preliminary results of this adaptation successfully converge on controllers that perform well on the simulated terrain, without directly sensing the terrain. Additionally, unconventional gait solutions were observed in initial archives of elites indicating their performance and uniqueness. The contribution includes a software framework to evaluate and validate rover wheel-slip compensation solutions on realistic deformable terrain. Future work will trial this system on a rover model in real terrain.

title: Beyond the Bid: Navigating the Risks of Using Commercial Spacecraft for NASA Missions
authors: Rebecca Kelly, Andrew Driesman
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Over the past decade, NASA science missions have shifted toward the use of more commercial spacecraft and fixed price contracts. This shift has resulted in fundamental disconnects between commercial incentives and government expectations with one key source being risk. While risk is inherent in space exploration, the introduction of lower-cost commercial options has shifted the paradigm for characterizing and defining acceptable risk in a way that often proves challenging for both parties to maintain scope and cost. At the NASA level, this is particularly evident when expectations on industry performance (pricing, schedule) clash with heritage standards for reducing risk (GEVS, gold rules) which can result in disconnections between the two entities. This occurrence, if left unchecked, can lead to increased implementation risk and limit new and emerging technology in the service of science missions. To better address potential disconnects, all parties would benefit from participating in this discussion for a deep dive into the unique characteristics and challenges associated with commercial buses and how they would be best incorporated into the NASA model. This paper explores several examples of the common NASA-toindustry disconnects and their impacts on mission risk including: unique requirements levied on production-line spacecraft (scope creep), discrepancies between expectations and performance, and NASA vs. industry standards. While this list is not all-inclusive, the authors believe these to be key contributors to the fundamental disconnects that disrupt the outcomes of partnerships between NASA and commercial providers. Furthermore, to address these concerns, this paper includes a number of potential mitigation strategies that can be employed including: utilizing experienced mission implementors to navigate the interfaces between NASA and industry, streamlining or tailoring existing standards for simpler implementation and flexibility to industry input, collecting and analyzing lessons learned from previous NASA missions to identify best practices that can be applied in the future, designing and testing processes that are clearly explained and signed off to early in the program, vetting commercial partner selection and management with an emphasis on assessing quality control and assurance, design and testing processes, and partner experience and expertise, and maintaining frequent communication and partnership-level collaboration to increase the flow of information between institutions and limit areas of ambiguity. A thorough understanding of both the risk factors and mitigation options discussed in this paper will enable NASA to effectively utilize new capabilities offered by the industrial base at historically lower prices while minimizing implementation risks associated with Agency-to-commercial interfaces. By effectively addressing the disconnects that lead to these risks, NASA will be better-equipped for future commercial partnerships that provide the opportunity to accomplish science goals faster and cheaper.

title: Early-Phase Design of Distributed Space Antennas and Constellations for D2D Communications
authors: Seang Shim, Ryusei Komatsu, Yuta Takahashi, Hideki Yoshikado, Harunobu Kobayashi, Sumio Morioka
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper proposes a framework for the early-phase feasibility analysis of Distributed Space Antenna (DSA) constellations designed for Direct-to-Device (D2D) communication. DSAs, which form a large virtual antenna by controlling a cluster of small satellites, are a promising solution to overcome the physical aperture limitations of conventional monolithic antennas. For long-life D2D communication infrastructures, we employ Electromagnetic Formation Flight (EMFF), a propellantless control method that utilizes magnetic forces to maintain the formation. This approach allows for sustainable operation by using standard magnetic torquers (coils). The design of such constellations, however, involves complex, hierarchical tradeoffs between the performance of a single DSA and the overall cost and capability of the entire constellation. Failure to resolve these conflicting requirements in the initial design phase can lead to the discovery of system-level infeasibility in later stages, resulting in costly rework and schedule delays. To mitigate this risk, our framework formulates the end-to-end design process as a multi-stage optimization problem. The first stage focuses on the feasibility of a single DSA. Here, we extend previous work by incorporating a critical new constraint: the thermal feasibility of the satellite. This is essential because the continuous power consumed to counteract the dominant <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$J_{2}$</tex> orbital disturbance acts as a constant internal heat source. This internal heat needs to be balanced against external thermal inputs to keep the satellite within its operational temperature limits. By solving this optimization, we derive the relationship between the number of constituent satellites and key DSA performance metrics, such as Equivalent Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP) and total mass. In the second stage, these performance metrics are used to evaluate the design of the entire constellation. We formulate a multi-objective optimization problem that balances the total constellation cost, defined as the required number of rocket launches, against the communication performance. By solving this problem, the framework identifies the Pareto-optimal set of design parameters. This allows us to determine not only whether a feasible design solution exists for a given set of requirements but also to quantitatively understand the trade-offs between cost and performance. To demonstrate the effectiveness of this framework, a numerical simulation is conducted to present a concrete design example. This integrated methodology provides an early-phase evaluation of DSA constellations, thereby facilitating their development for future D2D applications.

title: Quantitative Assessment Method for Grid Carbon Emission Reduction Contributions Based on Banzhaf Interaction
authors: Shuowei Li, Zhiyong Shi, Xiaoning Ye, Yuchen Cao, Yuluo Bu, Shicong Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Accurately quantifying the contribution of power grids to carbon emission reduction is crucial for measuring their role in supporting society’s low-carbon transition. However, existing research lacks quantitative assessments of how grids assist connected entities in reducing carbon emissions. To address this gap, this paper proposes a Banzhaf interaction-based method for quantifying grid contributions to carbon emission reduction. First, project boundaries and characteristics are defined to determine whether a project facilitates carbon emissions reduction. Subsequently, for projects deemed beneficial, the total carbon emission reduction is calculated using China Certified Emission Reduction (CCER) methodologies, other carbon emission measurement approaches, or direct measurement. Finally, the grid’s carbon emissions reduction contribution is determined based on the Banzhaf index. By analyzing the carbon emission reduction of grid-connected entities under two scenarios—with and without the grid—the contribution of the grid to carbon emission reduction is discussed in different scenarios. This method provides a theoretical basis for measuring the grid’s contribution to societal carbon emission reduction.

title: Breast Cancer Microarray Classification Using Ensemble Feature Selection and Bagging Histogram Gradient Boosting
authors: Akmal Zaki Asmara, Defiano Dhio Bimantara, Fitra Agung Diassyah Putra, Nanik Suciati
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The classification of breast cancer subtypes represents a challenging instance of the curse of dimensionality, where the feature space vastly exceeds available samples. To address the dimensionality issue, we employed RFE-SVM validated with hierarchical clustering analysis as the feature selector and ensemble learning pipeline utilizing Bagging Histogram Gradient Boosting. Our proposed pipeline was crossvalidated and tested on three independent microarray datasets (GSE45827, GSE7904, and GSE42568). Experimental results demonstrated that the identified feature subsets yielded excellent separability, achieving exceptional performance (1.00 accuracy and ROC-AUC) on GSE45827 and GSE42568. While the method initially exhibited sensitivity to the extreme data scarcity in GSE7904, we demonstrated that Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique (SMOTE) successfully stabilized the decision boundaries, recovering accuracy to 97 %. These findings suggest that rigorous feature selection combined with bagging-enhanced boosting offers a highly accurate pipeline.

title: A Next-Generation Multi-Disease Biomedical Diagnostic Platform Using Green AI, Edge Cloud Computing
authors: M. Sowmya, R. P. Meenaakshi Sundhari, S. Sri Ram Suriya, R. Varshini, R. Thangameena, V. Preethi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Early disease diagnosis relies greatly on medical image analysis; however, most existing systems focus on cloud-based analysis and singledisease detection, which increases latency and privacy concerns. In this study, a hybrid Edge-Cloud system is presented for a Green AI-powered multi-disease diagnosis system. For the combined classification of kidney stones, heart disease, pneumonia, and brain tumors from medical images, a light-weight convolutional neural network with transfer learning from MobileNet is employed. Although cloud-based training ensures scalability, edge-based inference reduces latency and energy consumption. With an optimized edge inference time of 0.6 seconds, the results of the experiments demonstrate an overall accuracy of 94.2 %. In real-time smart healthcare systems, the proposed system provides a scalable, secure, and energy-efficient solution.

title: Surrogate-Based Optimization of Open-Die Forging using Reinforcement Learning and Simulated Annealing
authors: Christian Linder, Martin Feistle, Syed Irtiza Ali
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Open-die forging processes are usually analyzed and designed using detailed finite element simulations. However, finite element models are computationally expensive, which severely restricts their use in iterative optimization and online process control. To obtain a real-time capable process control that accounts for both geometric and recrystallization requirements, a surrogate-based optimization approach using reinforcement learning is proposed and compared with simulated annealing. The workpiece state is represented as a point cloud with spatial coordinates and recrystallization values. A Pointnet++ surrogate is trained on finite element simulations at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1160^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1180^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1200^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, 1220^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$</tex>, and 1240° C to predict the next workpiece state for a given workpiece state, forging action and temperature. A temperature-based cross-validation scheme is used to assess generalization. The surrogate model achieves low deformation errors relative to the workpiece dimensions and recrystallization errors of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1. 5 \% - 2. 2 \%}$</tex>. The surrogate model is then used to optimize forging strategies with a cost function that combines geometric and recrystallization constraints. Simulated annealing is employed as a baseline metaheuristic to approximate the global optimum and is compared to reinforcement learning. Optimization is tested for 1200° C. Simulated annealing attains the lowest total cost with 0.28, while reinforcement learning yields only a moderately higher cost of 0.42. Once trained, the reinforcement learning policy can be applied in real time, which makes the approach suited for process control in open-die forging.

title: Optimal Planning of PV-Dominated Distribution Systems Considering Seasonal Hydrogen Storage
authors: Li Zhang, Qinyue Guan, Menglin Zhang, Jin Tan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of photovoltaic systems is leading to PV-dominated distribution networks, which face the dual challenge of intra-day fluctuations and a marked seasonal energy imbalance. While battery energy storage can balance short-term fluctuations of renewables, seasonal hydrogen storage offers a promising solution for long-duration energy shifting. Existing planning methods, however, are often limited by oversimplified representation of annual energy chronology. To address these gaps, this paper proposes a novel coordinated planning model that integrates battery and seasonal hydrogen storage. The proposed method employs clustering techniques to select representative days and seasonal time windows, effectively preserving essential annual chronology while maintaining computational tractability. Case studies on a modified IEEE 14-bus system demonstrate the effectiveness of the method, showing a reduction in total annualized cost by 39.4%, a substantial reduction in load shedding, and significant enhancement in PV integration.

title: Supercapacitor-Integrated MMC for Power Quality Compensation and RBE Management of Co-Phase TPSS
authors: Xiang Liu, Fan Peng, Rui Kong, Yinyu Chen, Wei Wang, Minwu Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To solve negative-sequence issues, neutral-section problems, and regenerative braking energy (RBE) recovery challenges in traction power supply system (TPSS), a co-phase compensator composed of a full-bridge modular multilevel converter (FB-MMC) and supercapacitors (SCs) is proposed. First, SCs are distributed within the full-bridge MMC to form an SC-integrated full-bridge MMC (SC-FBMMC), and the working principle is analyzed based on the topology. Subsequently, six operating modes are defined based on load level and the SCs’ state of charge (SOC). Moreover, a dual-layer control strategy is proposed: the first layer switches operating modes based on load characteristics and SCs’ SOC while calculating current reference values for each port; the second layer performs current tracking control for each port. Finally, simulation results based on MATLAB/Simulink confirm the effectiveness of the proposed topology and control strategy in achieving both negative-sequence current compensation and RBE recovery.

title: Intelligent Evaluation Method for Load Regulation Effect of Multi-Source Data Fusion for New Power Systems
authors: Yinxia Yuan, Zhenping Li, Xinyu Zhi, Xiuliang Liu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High renewable penetration increases power system volatility, challenging traditional load regulation evaluation methods to balance benefits and risks. This paper proposes a risk-aware evaluation method using multi-source data fusion. A three-dimensional indicator system (system pressure relief, execution reliability, economic efficiency) is established. The entropy weight method with covariance correction enables objective weighting and reduces multicollinearity. A risk-penalized comprehensive evaluation index (PEI) is designed. Experiments on 78 weeks of real grid data show that the proposed method achieves the highest average score (85.2) and reduces variance by 54.3% (from 9.6 to 4.4). Variance increase is limited to ≤13% under high volatility, and average score decline is minimized. Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of covariance correction. The method runs 0.41s/10k samples, meeting real-time requirements.

title: A Generative Large Language Model-Based System for Power System Operational Situation Awareness and Decision Support
authors: Yinye Shen, Guokun Cui, Ruiyang Weng, Zhuoying Wang, JiaYe Feng, Jingman Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The increasing integration of distributed energy resources introduces significant complexity to power system operation, challenging traditional rule-based management approaches. Existing AI methods mainly focus on isolated tasks and lack unified situational awareness and decision support capabilities. This paper proposes a generative large language model (LLM) – based system for power system situational awareness and decision support. The method integrates situational language representation, staged decision generation, and rule-based constraint verification within a four-layer architecture. A human-in-the-loop mechanism is further incorporated to enhance decision reliability. A prototype system is implemented and evaluated on typical operational scenarios. Results demonstrate that the proposed approach improves situational understanding, decision compliance, and interpretability, providing an effective solution for intelligent power system operation.

title: Women and Child Safety Device with Tracking and SOS Alerts Using Free RTOS
authors: Tarunika Sharma, Padma E, Geetha Selvarani A, Hariprasad TL, Yuvaraj S, Nafeez Ahmed L
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper presents the design and development of a Women and Child Safety Device based on ESP32-C3 microcontroller integrated with Free RTOS to run efficient real time task management. The system has the A 9 G module built in to give GPS-based location tracking and GSM enabled SMS alerts to communicate with the people in case of an emergency. Upon activation of the SOS button, the device will send the user's real-time location coordinates as well as a predefined distress message to already registered emergency contacts. In addition to manual triggering, the system is equipped with a Galvanic Skin Response (GSR) sensor that is used to monitor stress levels and enable automatic emergency alert if abnormal physiological conditions are detected. An on-board buzzer gives immediate sound alerts in critical situations. The device is powered by a rechargeable Lithium-Polymer (Li-Po) battery so that it is portable and requires little power for continuous operation. The firmware is created with the help of Platform IO and ESP-IDF framework to ensure reliable embedded performance. Cloud-based data storage and monitoring are put in place with the help of Firebase to ensure that data alerts and location are synchronized in real-time. A dedicated mobile application built on MIT App Inventor is used to facilitate user interaction, remote monitoring and co-ordination of emergencies.

title: Design, Modelling and Characterisation of a Miniature Fibre-Reinforced Soft Bending Actuator for Endoluminal Interventions
authors: Xiangyi Tan, Aoife McDonald-Bowyer, Danail Stoyanov, Agostino Stilli
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Miniaturised soft pneumatic actuators are crucial for robotic intervention within highly constrained anatomical pathways. This work presents the design and validation of a fibre-reinforced soft actuator at the centimetre scale for integration into an endoluminal robotic platform for natural-orifice interventional and diagnostic applications. A single-chamber geometry reinforced with embedded Kevlar fibre was designed to maximise curvature while preserving sealing integrity, fabricated using a multi-stage multi-stiffness silicone casting process, and validated against a high-fidelity Abaqus FEM using experimentally parametrised hyperelastic material models and embedded beam reinforcement. The semi-cylindrical actuator has an outer diameter of $18, \mathrm{~mm}$ and a length of $37.5, \mathrm{~mm}$. Single and double helix winding configurations, fibre pitch, and fibre density were investigated. The optimal 100 SH configuration achieved a bending angle of 202.9° experimentally and 297.6° in simulation, with structural robustness maintained up to $100, \mathrm{kPa}$ and radial expansion effectively constrained by the fibre reinforcement. Workspace evaluation confirmed suitability for integration into the target device envelope, demonstrating that fibre-reinforcement strategies can be effectively translated to the centimetre regime while retaining actuator performance.

title: Revectoring a Mission Post-Launch: The AFRL's XVI Mission, Operations and Lessons Learned
authors: Erika A. Chavez, Andrew A. Knuth, Lee Jasper, Adam T. Reinelt
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The Air Force Research Lab's (AFRL) Small Satellite Portfolio (SSP) produces end-to-end smallsat missions. The portfolio has designed, launched and operated four satellites in the last five years and aims to continue to provide the warfighter with impactful and enabling technologies. One of these four missions was the AFRL XVI CubeSat which was launched on SpaceX's Transporter 8 in June of 2023. XVI's primary objectives were to demonstrate that Link 16 network participation was possible from Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and provide the warfighter with a beyond-line-of-sight capability without modifications to existing network hardware or software. XVI pioneered the research, technology and testing for Link 16 participation from LEO. The Space Development Agency (SDA) became XVI's transition partner, given the use of the Link 16 payloads in LEO on the SDA Transport Layer satellites. XVI's mission design, testing and operations are discussed. The focus of this paper is to describe lessons learned and explain how the AFRL team repurposed the mission post-launch due to regulatory delays which led to hardware component failures. Some of the valuable artifacts that emerged from the revectoring include the following: utilizing the unclassified operations center for training of high school students to military generals, providing on-orbit data for artificial intelligence/machine learning (AI/ML) models, ground software automation development, advancing Very Low Earth Orbit (vLEO) operations and ground tracking. The collaboration and achievements with the Space Force's Space Test Course are also discussed. XVI's educational outreach has expanded since launch and has allowed the AFRL's University Nanosat Program (UNP) students to receive on-orbit operational experience and training. XVI's revectoring post-launch demonstrates AFRL's capability to adapt to new mission challenges.

title: An Efficient Machine Learning-Based Email Spam Classification Model
authors: A.Syed Musthafa, Akash M A, Dharaneesh B, Harish K, Janarthanan K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The Recent email spam attacks are more sophisticated, with phishing messages that have been created using AI, identity spoofing, and zero-day social engineering hacks that bypass conventional filters. The article introduces ML- Shield which is a new multi-layer spam filter of emails with Transformer based semantic analysis, stylometric linguistic forensic and real-time URL / domain threat intelligence. MLShield retrieves multimodal information of email content, email header, email sender behavior, email URLs and email attachment, and is trained in a hybrid network composed of BERT, CNN-BiLSTM, and Random Forest metadata students. Adaptive anomaly detection can be used to detect zero-day phishing attacks. ML-Shield is tested on five datasets and its accuracy is 98.2 % that is higher compared to the existing systems. The explainable AI (XAI) using SHAP improves the transparency of the analysts. This model is very performing, as it is flexible and practical relating to the protection of enterprise email.

title: Design and Performance Analysis of Underwater Wireless Optical Communication Link for Different Modulation Techniques
authors: Mohd Ashraf, Nusrat Jahan, Mainuddin, Dinesh Prasad, Khurram Karim Qureshi, Ozair Ahmad
publish: 2025 IEEE DELCON - International Conference on Recent Smart Technologies in Engineering for Sustainable Development
abstract: Underwater wireless optical communication (UWOC) is a promising option for high-speed underwater links. Its performance, however, is limited by scattering, absorption, and background noise in water. This study evaluates OOK and QPSK modulation in a UWOC system modeled in Optisystem and MATLAB. At an SNR of 15 dB, OOK reached a BER of about 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup> and supported 25-30 m of transmission. QPSK showed stronger performance, achieving a BER near 10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−6</sup> and extending the range to 35-40 m in clear water. These results indicate that QPSK offers better potential for future high-speed UWOC applications.

title: Leveraging Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) for Digital Content Marketing Strategy: A Case Study of a Pre-Launch Skincare Brand in Indonesia
authors: Mikael Bryan Ciputra, Brianna Sanjaya, Joseph Setiawan, Antonius Felix, Akbar Lufi Zulfikar, Selly Swandari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The Indonesian skincare market, projected to attain a valuation of USD 2.76 billion, presents substantial growth opportunities while imposing considerable challenges upon emerging brands constrained by limited resources. This study documents the deployment of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) as a Digital Content Marketing (DCM) strategy for L'Essaine, a pre-launch skincare brand targeting the 17-30 age demographic, as an applied single-case study with mixed evidence. Grounded in the SOSTAC framework for planning and the AIDA model for content development, data were gathered via in-depth interviews with brand stakeholders, competitor observations, and an online survey administered to 42 target market respondents. The AIGC implementation utilized Gemini AI, Higgsfield, ElevenLabs, and Canva to produce consistent visual and textual content over an 8-week period (November-December 2025). Key observations include a 3.9 % engagement rate, cumulative reach of 8,062, and net gain of 159 new followers, alongside a context-specific cost reduction of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 3}-\mathbf{9 4 \%}$</tex> compared with conventional production methods. This paper contributes to the AI and data-driven digital marketing track of ICCRAIDS by providing a transparent, reproducible documentation of AIGC adoption workflow, metrics reporting, and practical insights for resource-constrained startups in emerging markets, with findings limited to this single case context.

title: A Training-Free Leaf Disease Lesion Segmentation Method Using HSV Color and Adaptive Thresholding
authors: Shofwatul Uyun, Haerul Anam, Baiq Titik Haerani, Latifatul Mujahidah
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Leaf disease lesion segmentation is a crucial step in plant disease analysis. Although deep learning-based methods have demonstrated strong performance, they typically require large annotated datasets and substantial computational resources, which limits their practicality in agricultural applications. Therefore, lightweight and interpretable training-free segmentation approaches remain a relevant alternative. This paper proposes a training-free leaf lesion segmentation method that integrates HSV color cues and adaptive thresholding through pixel-level logical fusion. The HSV color space is employed to extract lesion color characteristics, while adaptive thresholding enhances robustness to illumination variations. Morphological processing is further applied to refine the segmentation results. The proposed method is evaluated on a pixel-annotated leaf disease dataset consisting of 580 images using Intersection over Union (IoU) and Dice coefficient metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed approach provides stable and consistent segmentation performance across diverse lesion appearances and lighting conditions. This method serves as an effective and interpretable baseline for leaf lesion segmentation in scenarios with limited data availability and computational resources.

title: Modelling and Testing a Real-Time Human-in-the-Loop Controlled Lunar Landing Simulator
authors: Miguel Alfaro Neves
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The International Space Exploration Coordination Group, comprised of 28 space agencies, is advancing the Global Exploration Roadmap to include the establishment of the Lunar Orbital Platform-Gateway, a space station orbiting the Moon. This platform will facilitate astronaut landings on the Moon's south pole. This paper emphasizes the critical role of lunar landers in crewed Moon missions, with specifications tailored to mission requirements such as size, propulsion, payload, landing gear, energy systems, and controllers. It details the development of a Human-In-The-Loop controlled lunar lander simulator at the German Aerospace Center (DLR), featuring a dynamic model with Reaction Control System thrusters and main thrusters for landing. A ground contact detection method with force calculation is used for simulating landing forces. Despite the presence of autopilot Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) systems, manual control by astronauts, as demonstrated in Apollo missions, remains essential. This paper focuses on the astronaut's manual control of the lunar lander. The lunar lander is integrated within DLR's Robotic Motion Simulator framework, including a low gravity-adapted washout filter and Human Machine Interface devices for manual steering. Simulation scenarios, including those based on Apollo 11 mission data, are recreated and tested. The framework's evaluation of controller performance, pilot sensor data and lander path supports advancements in spacecraft design, GNC validation, and astronaut flight control training.

title: A Software-Based Approach to Radiation Mitigation for Planetary Missions
authors: Jeremy Nash, Vandi Verma, Evan Graser, Haoda Wang, Gerik Kubiak, Mark Maimone, Steven Myint, Jeffrey Biesiadecki, Philip Romano
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The Perseverance Mars rover has demonstrated a new onboard global localization capability that has the potential to significantly improve long-distance autonomous navigation [1]. It allows the rover to precisely determine its position by comparing panoramic images from its navigation cameras with high-resolution orbital maps, and use that knowledge to reset its onboard position uncertainty model back to nearly zero meters. Leveraging a fast, commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) Snapdragon 801 processor from the Ingenuity Helicopter Base Station contained within the rover body, the system performs complex image-processing tasks to accurately determine the rover's position relative to orbital maps. This approach of diverting compute-intensive operations to a faster but nonradiation hardened CPU overcomes the limitations of the rover's 20-times slower, radiation-hardened primary processor, and is a paradigm shift in planetary robotics. We focus on the characterization, implementation and mitigation strategies required to use a non-radiation-hardened COTS processor in the harsh space environment. We describe the software-based memory checking and fault isolation techniques developed to address memory corruption potentially caused by aging or radiation effects. Furthermore, we present the performance and operational results from the deployed system on Mars, including its accuracy and robustness. The paper also discusses lessons learned during the development and flight deployment process and outline how this technology will fundamentally change future planetary navigation to support more ambitious and efficient autonomous missions by enabling continued use of commercial co-processors even in the presence of degraded memory capabilities.

title: Explainable Reinforcement Learning for Adaptive Energy Dispatch in Tropical Microgrids
authors: Puguh Hiskiawan, Srava Chrisdes Antoro, I Gede Agus Purwa, Ferdiantono, Chellynia Cristabel
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Photovoltaic-based microgrids in tropical regions face significant challenges for adaptive energy dispatch due to high solar variability, non-stationary residential demand, and limited battery storage capacity. Reinforcement learning (RL) offers a data-driven solution; however, many RL-based controllers operate as black boxes with limited interpretability. This study proposes an explainable reinforcement learning framework for tropical microgrid energy management using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) within a discrete hybrid dispatch structure. The problem is formulated as a Markov Decision Process, and explainability is achieved through behavioral analysis and quantitative energy-based metrics. The PPO agent is evaluated against deterministic rule-based and random baselines using cumulative reward, operational cost, self-sufficiency ratio, and self-consumption ratio. Results demonstrate a 17% reduction in mean energy cost and a 12.2% improvement in self-sufficiency compared to the rule-based strategy, while maintaining physically coherent battery dynamics. The findings show that adaptive reinforcement learning can enhance economic performance without sacrificing policy interpretability in tropical microgrid applications.

title: A Novel Laser-Ablation Resonance-Ionization Mass Spectrometer for In Situ Dating of Lunar Rocks
authors: Rico G. Fausch, F. Scott Anderson, Audrey E. Aebi, Amanda M. Alexander, Edward B. Bierhaus, Sarah E. Braden, Amy L. Fagan, Sierra N. Ferguson, Otso Gächter, Michela A. Gargano, Mary H. Hanson, Sébastien Hayoz, James W. Head, Alex M. Iseli, Katherine H. Joy, Viliam Klein, Julie M. Korsmeyer, Jonathan Levine, Steven Osterman, John F. Pernet-Fisher, Daniele Piazza, Andreas Riedo, Kevin W. Sacca, Vishaal Singh, Remo Sommer, Romain Tartèse, Tina L. Teichmann, Tom Whitaker, Peter Wurz, Marcella A. Yant
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: In situ geochemical and geochronological measurements on the Moon are needed to test models of late-stage lunar volcanism and to interpret crater-retention anomalies on terrains appearing geologically young. The irregular mare patch Ina is a key case: its exceptionally low crater density has been interpreted as very recent volcanic activity, but non-chronologic explanations-unusual surface mechanical properties or resurfacing-remain plausible. Resolving these hypotheses requires radiometric ages that are co-registered with petrologic and geochemical context at the scale of individual rock and mineral domains under the mass-volume-power constraints of landed payloads. We report the architecture of the Chemistry, Organics and Dating Experiment (CODEX), a compact dual-mode time-of-flight mass spectrometer developed for NASA's DIMPLE payload targeting Ina via CLPS. CODEX combines laser-ablation mass spectrometry (LAMS) for major- and trace-element mapping with laser-ablation resonance-ionization mass spectrometry (LARIMS) for interference-free <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{R b - S r}$</tex> isotope analysis in a single-reflection, off-axis reflectron analyzer. In LAMS mode, CODEX produces elemental maps over <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{z} \mathrm{1}-\text{2 5 0}$</tex> with mass resolution <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{m} / \Delta \mathrm{m}$</tex> of about <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$300-400$</tex> (FWHM) to establish mineralogical context. In LARIMS mode, primary (prompt) ablation ions are suppressed and neutral <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{R b}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{S r}$</tex> are selectively ionized, mitigating the <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{87} \text{Rb} /{ }^{87} \text{Sr}$</tex> isobar without requiring ultra-high mass resolution. Three-dimensional ion-optical modeling (SIMION) and a vented, shared ion-source geometry enable rapid electrostatic switching between modes. This two-in-one instrument is implemented in a flight-oriented opto-mechanical architecture compatible with GEVS environments and CLPS-class packaging. CODEX is designed to return co-registered elemental and isotope datasets that can constrain the timing of emplacement at Ina and inform interpretations of the Moon's late-stage thermo-magmatic evolution.

title: PyPSA-Based Analysis of Power Systems with Integrated Renewable Energy Sources
authors: Md Sadman Sakib, Xin Wang
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The increasing integration of renewable energy sources (RES), particularly solar and wind, presents both significant opportunities and operational challenges for modern power systems. While these sources contribute to reduced carbon emissions and advance sustainability objectives, their intermittent nature can affect grid stability and efficiency. This study investigates the impact of solar photovoltaic (PV) integration on the IEEE 14-bus test system using Python for Power System Analysis (PyPSA), an open-source tool for energy system modeling and optimization. Solar PV generators were implemented at buses 9 and 14, and a 24 -hour time-series simulation was conducted using real irradiance data. The analysis emphasizes voltage stability, rotor angle deviation, generator dispatch, and transmission line loading. The results indicate that the system maintains stability under moderate levels of solar penetration, with bus voltages and line flows remaining within acceptable operational limits. Although reverse power flow occurs during peak solar generation hours, it does not adversely impact overall system reliability. These findings support the technical feasibility of integrating distributed solar PV into conventional power system infrastructure.

title: A Smart Bioactive Fracture Bandage for External Stabilization with Real-Time Biomechanical Load and Motion Monitoring
authors: Malliga Varshini U, Vidhya M, Vasuki S, Mohanadharshini T, Yogeshwari R, Nivetha R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Effective fracture management requires both mechanical stabilization and continuous monitoring of biomechanical conditions during the healing process. Conventional fracture casts and bandages primarily provide immobilization but lack the capability to monitor load distribution and limb movement in real time. This study proposes a smart bioactive fracture bandage designed for external stabilization with integrated biomechanical load and motion monitoring. The proposed system incorporates natural fiber-reinforced bioactive bandage materials combined with embedded force and flex sensors to detect stress and limb movement near the fracture region. Sensor data are processed using an ESP32 microcontroller and transmitted wirelessly to a mobile interface for real-time observation. The system enables early detection of excessive loading or abnormal motion, which can prevent complications during fracture healing. The proposed approach provides a non-invasive, cost-effective, and intelligent solution for advanced fracture care and wearable rehabilitation monitoring.

title: Foreground Graphics-Aware Runtime DNN Scheduling on Mobile GPUs
authors: Minju Kang, Jaeho Jin, Sungmin Lee, JeongGil Ko
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: We propose GPUSched, a foreground graphics aware preemptive scheduling framework for concurrent DNN inference and seamless graphics rendering on mobile GPUs. Given that existing GPU scheduling methods relying on offline slicing are inadequate for dynamic graphics workloads, GPUSched is driven by two key components: (i) Render-State Detection via GPUPing, which leverages lightweight probing of GPU queue latency to accurately identify foreground rendering operations and (ii) Adaptive Operator Chunking, which dynamically fits DNN model chunk sizes within GPU idle times in a GPU frequency-aware manner. By combining these mechanisms GPUSched, coordinates DNN inference with graphics rendering in real time, preventing deadline violations while maximizing GPU utilization. Experiments on two commodity mobile devices show that GPUSched consistently outperforms baseline schedulers, achieving lower deadline miss rates while sustaining high rendering quality even under demanding graphics workload.

title: Online Complaint Registration and Management
authors: M. Priya, D. Vikkiramapandian, M. Nithyakalyani, R Venkatalakshimi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This research presents a web-based Complaint Registration and Management System using HTML, CSS, Python (Flask), and SQLite to enhance urban grievance handling. Citizens submit geotagged complaints with images, while authorities receive auto-assigned tasks based on proximity for timely resolution. A live dashboard tracks complaint status, with automated email notifications and escalation logic ensuring accountability. Public verification and predictive analytics identify critical service gaps. The system improves response time by up to 60 %, enhances transparency through crowdsourced feedback, and supports efficient urban governance through real-time tracking and data-driven decision-making.

title: Aggregated Flexibility of Building VPP Subject to the Uncertainties of Meteorological Variables: Modeling and Characteristics
authors: Shuo Liang, Xiaolong Jin, Fangyi Zhao, Zhongda Chu, Yanyu Qin, Yuchen Dong, Jiarui Zhang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: This paper investigates the aggregated flexibility of Building Virtual Power Plants (BVPPs) under multi-dimensional meteorological uncertainties. A high-fidelity stochastic scenario generation framework is proposed, leveraging Vine Copula theory and Auto-Regressive Moving Average (ARMA) models to capture complex non-linear correlations between meteorological variables. By integrating a thermodynamic RC network model, these uncertainties are propagated into building micro-environments. To quantify the aggregated flexibility, the stochastic feasible regions of building clusters are aggregated via Minkowski Sum theory. Results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively characterizes meteorological uncertainties. Notably, the inherent heterogeneity of building clusters induces temporal phase shifts in thermal responses, facilitating the internal offsetting of stochastic fluctuations. This research achieves the characterization of BVPP flexibility under uncertainty, providing a rigorous theoretical basis for aggregators to participate in reliable power system dispatch.

title: Industrial Park Load Modeling with EV-Charging-Dominant Centralized Charging Stations
authors: Yucheng Wang, Boyang Zhao, Xiuli Wang, Qiuming Xu, Lei Yu, Yuan Lyuzerui
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address issues such as large load fluctuations in industrial park areas, high risks of local distribution network overloads, and the difficulty of traditional load models in reflecting equipment and operational constraints, this paper constructs a fine-grained load simulation and source-load-storage-grid integrated collaborative optimization framework for industrial parks. First, the load-bearing capacity boundary and capacity constraints of the distribution network are explicitly introduced on the network side to ensure compliance with distribution network safety verification requirements. Second, a fine-grained model at the charging connector level is established to depict key constraints such as connector occupancy, parallel service, and power allocation, thereby improving the accuracy of load time-series simulation. Furthermore, the operational indicator of the average number of single-vehicle weekly charging times on the user side is incorporated into the objective function, forming a multi-objective trade-off with traditional indicators. Case study results demonstrate that the proposed method can effectively mitigate the net load fluctuations in the industrial park without exceeding the distribution network's load-bearing boundary, and by introducing a daily charging frequency indicator, it achieves a charging load distribution scheme that better aligns with actual operational conditions, providing a reference for the integrated energy and electrified load collaborative operation in industrial parks.

title: Automated Analysis of Color and Style Trends in Fashion Images Utilizing the DeepFashion Dataset with an AI Model
authors: Hassanin Albarhamtoshy, Nesreen Banashmoos, Emadeldin S. Gohar
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The fashion sector is increasingly reliant on data; obtaining actionable insights from extensive image datasets continues to pose challenges. This study introduces a detailed framework for assessing color and style trends in fashion images by utilizing the DeepFashion dataset. The study introduces “Deep Style Analyst,” a holistic framework designed for the automatic analysis and visualization of common colors and garment styles in fashion images. We have developed an AI model with graphical user interface (GUI) application that facilitates the interactive classification of garments through the extraction of dominant colors and recognition of common styles. The system incorporates computer vision methodologies, such as K-means clustering for color evaluation and convolutional neural networks for style categorization. Metrics for evaluation include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score pertaining to style classification, along with consistency in color distribution for trend analysis. This research enhances the field of fashion analytics by offering a userfriendly tool aimed at trend detection and promoting data-driven decision-making within the fashion sector. By leveraging the vast DeepFashion dataset, the initiative will establish a workflow that includes: 1) Data preprocessing and labeling, 2) Training deep learning models for extracting color palettes and classifying garment styles, and 3) Creating an intuitive Graphical User Interface (GUI) to facilitate user interaction with classification outcomes. The system will utilize a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for classifying garment cuts (such as A-line, sheath, tshirt) alongside clustering algorithms for identifying predominant colors. The GUI will support filtering, searching, and visualizing trends based on these characteristics. The efficacy of the classification model will be assessed using established metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score. This tool aims to assist fashion retailers, designers, and analysts by offering a data-driven perspective on style trends that enhances decisionmaking processes.

title: Development of Radioisotope Stirling Generators for High Efficiency Space Power Generation
authors: Ernestina Wozniak, Matthew Stang, Salvatore Oriti, Ronald Leibach, Rebecca Buehrle, Hannah Sargeant, Ramy Mesalam, Kris Bell, Robert Tute, Michael Barron, Natasha Miller
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Radioisotope power systems have been powering space missions for decades, historically using radioisotope thermoelectric generators to convert heat to usable electricity. Teams at NASA's Glenn Research Center have been studying and testing free-piston Stirling convertors, which provide significant efficiency gains for space power generation when compared to state-of-the-art thermoelectric power conversion. Stirling convertors have been validated as stand-alone units with extensive environmental and extended lifetime testing. Recently, several efforts have integrated multiple Stirling convertors in generator configurations representing the interfaces and geometry that would be present in a flight system. Three separate radioisotope Stirling generator testbeds have been designed or conceived: the Plutonium-238 Stirling Generator Testbed, an internal NASA effort; the Americium-241 Stirling Generator Testbed, an international collaboration with a team from the University of Leicester; and the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed, a conceptual study. The Plutonium-238 Stirling Generator Testbed (originally named the Dynamic Radioisotope Power System Testbed) began the initial design process in 2019, with more resources for the effort added in 2021 for design, analysis, and drawing creation. First testing occurred in 2023, with follow-on testing occurring in 2024 and 2025. Initially, the Americium-241 Stirling Generator Testbed was part of a conceptual design in 2022 which showed promise and led to an international collaboration effort to build hardware and test the design in a laboratory environment. The first integration and testing of the Americium-241 Testbed occurred in 2025 at NASA Glenn Research Center, was successful, and validated thermal models of the system. Further efforts are ongoing to implement improvements to the design and test a second version in 2026. The Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed effort began as an idea to adapt to the changing environment surrounding fuel production and planned usage. Debates have been ongoing about the best mission options for the available radioactive material, and due to the current dynamic environment, a Stirling generator that can accommodate various heat sources, while still utilizing significantly less fuel to produce similar power output, is attractive. Recently, the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed idea received funding to develop a project plan for a conceptual design study leading to hardware build, integration, and an environmental testing campaign, contingent on approval of the project plan. Design and analysis efforts for the Heat Source Agnostic Stirling Generator Testbed will show the feasibility and limitations of a Stirling generator design that can accommodate a variety of heat sources.

title: Mars Deep Subsurface Exploration with an Integrated Drill and Instrument Suite
authors: Joseph Palmowski, Kris Zacny, Kathryn Bywaters, Kevin Hubbard, Leo Stolov, Evan Eshelman, Robert May, Nicholas Naclerio
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Investigating Mars via Penetration and Analysis with Coiled Tubing (IMPACT) is a next-generation subsurface access and analysis payload designed for landers and rovers to investigate the Martian subsurface down to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 5}$</tex> meters. For deep subsurface access, IMPACT integrates coiled tubing and injector, pneumatic cuttings removal, and a bottom-hole assembly (BHA) equipped with a rotary-percussive drill and a suite of downhole science instruments for real-time subsurface compositional analysis. Leveraging core technologies advanced by Honeybee Robotics, including the LISTER lunar heat flow probe (TRL 9), RedWater Mars drill (TRL 6), and SMART lunar drill for downhole in situ analysis (TRL 6), IMPACT builds on proven technology heritage to deliver a robust solution. The system is designed to provide direct access to analyze stratigraphic layers, volatiles, and potential materials for in-situ resource utilization (ISRU), overcoming the shallow penetration depths of previous missions. Its instrumentation suite enables in situ data collection on mineralogy, ice, and resource distributions at unprecedented depths, supporting critical investigations into Mars' geologic history, habitability, and resource potential. Initial development and early test results highlight IMPACT's ability to advance both scientific discovery and future mission architectures, including long-duration operations and human exploration.

title: AI-Powered Mental Health Support Using NLP
authors: S.S. Srimun, N. Sankara Narayana, S. Pavithra, P. Dineshkumar
