publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Mental health issues such as stress and anxiety are increasingly common among students and working Professionals, yet access to timely and affordable support remains Limited. This project presents an AI-driven, multimodal mental Health Companion that detects users' emotional states through Text, voice, and video inputs. Natural Language Processing The methods of NLP are applied in order to analyze the textual data, while Voice emotion recognition makes use of audio features such as pitch and energy, while facial emotion detection is done by Computer vision techniques. The emotions detected from each modality are combined via a multimodal fusion approach These include the development of methods and techniques that improve the accuracy and reliability of outcomes. Based on the observed emotional condition, the system produces empathetic responses. and gives the patient self-care advice like relaxation. techniques and motivational guidance. The proposed system Serves as an early mental health support tool; promotes emotional awareness and well-being while being ethical and non-judicial contact. This attitude demonstrates the Potentials of multimodal artificial intelligence in applications to Mental Health.

title: A Comparative Analysis of CNN and Vision Transformer Architectures for Visual Defect Classification Under Data Augmentation
authors: Kevin Julian Tan, Tuga Mauritsius
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Choosing the right deep learning architecture for visual inspection in the field of industrial inspection remains a challenge even with the current advancements in the field of Industry 4.0. Although a number of studies have adopted Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Vision Transformers (ViTs) have their own domain in dealing with the long range modeling of dependencies. This paper presents a comparative study on a state of the art CNN (ConvNeXt V2) and a Hierarchical Vision Transformer (Swin Transformer V2) on the large scale VisA dataset. Both models are subjected to supervised learning with and without data augmentation to analyze data efficiency and defect detection. It was observed that the standard CNN baseline, ResNet50, unexpectedly outperformed modern architectures. ResNet50 demonstrated superior robustness in the imbalanced baseline scenario (F1 0.715) and achieved the highest overall performance ceiling when trained on the augmented, balanced dataset (F1 0.849, AUROC 0.975). This leads to the conclusion that while modern Vision Transformers and ConvNeXt architectures offer theoretical advantages, mature, heavily-optimized CNNs paired with rigorous data augmentation remain the most formidable choice for industrial defect detection.

title: Condition Monitoring and Assessment of Novel Nuclear Power Systems: Multi-Fault Diagnosis and Localization in Reactors
authors: Shujian Li, Xueying Chen, Tongjian Zhang, Jiancheng Lv, Taibo Yang, Zhan Xing
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Small modular reactors (SMRs) have emerged as a promising solution for regional power supply. However, due to the highly integrated structure, fault signals from different components are more likely to propagate through rigid connections and become mutually coupled, which increases signal interference and makes fault diagnosis and localization more difficult. To address this issue, this paper proposes a global-to- local multi-fault diagnosis and localization method for SMRs. First, impact signals are identified from vibration data using a dynamic sliding-window RMS ratio-based method. After removing the impact components, the remaining bearing wear signals are transformed into two-dimensional time-frequency feature images by Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT). A Late Fusion Net is then used to extract and fuse features from dual vibration channels for fault classification. Experimental results obtained from a SMR test platform show that the proposed method achieves an overall fault diagnosis accuracy of 96.875%. The proposed method provides an effective solution for multi-fault diagnosis and localization in SMRs.

title: Automatic Modeling with Power Flow Start-up and Frequency Characteristics Computation for HVDC
authors: Wangchao Dong, Xun Mao, Kai Lv, Wei Tang, Zhen Wang, Li Guo, Yong Zhan, Zhendong Tan, Yang Pu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The large-scale integration of high-voltage direct current (HVDC) systems and the fast dynamic characteristics of power electronic devices have made electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation increasingly critical. However, EMT modeling of multi-infeed HVDC systems faces challenges such as model complexity and parameter matching difficulties, while precisely establishing power flow states during system startup remains a technical hurdle. Hence, this paper proposes an automatic conversion method for constructing EMT models of HVDC systems based on electromechanical transient model parameters. A smooth power flow startup strategy enables the power system to transition steadily from the initial state to steady-state operation. Furthermore, to enhance the efficiency of system stability analysis, an efficient frequency characteristic calculation approach is introduced. The automatic modeling, smooth startup, and frequency characteristic of HVDC systems have been implemented on the CloudPSS simulation platform. Compared with traditional modeling approaches, this automated method significantly improves efficiency.

title: A Data-Driven Surrogate Modeling and Sensor/Actuator Placement Framework for Flexible Spacecraft
authors: Matthew Hilsenrath, Daniel R. Herber
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Flexible spacecraft structures present significant challenges for physical and control system design due to nonlinear dynamics, mission constraints, environmental variables, and changing operational conditions. This paper presents a data-driven framework for constructing reduced-order surrogate models of a flexible spacecraft using the method of Dynamic Mode Decomposition (DMD), followed by optimal sensor/actuator pair placement. High-fidelity simulation data from a nonlinear flexible spacecraft model, including coupled rigid-body and elastic modes, are captured by defining a mesh of nodes over the spacecraft body. The data-driven methods are then used to construct a modal model from the time histories of these node points. Optimal sensor/actuator placement for controllability and observability is performed via a nonlinear programming technique that maximizes the singular values of the Hankel matrix. Finally, the sensor placement and dynamics modeling approach is iterated to account for changes in the dynamic system introduced by sensor/actuator physical mass. The proposed methodology enables initialization of physical modeling without requiring a direct analytical model and provides a practical solution for onboard implementation in model-based control and estimation systems. Results demonstrate optimal design methodology with substantial model-order reduction while preserving dynamic fidelity, and provide insight into effective sensor-actuator configurations for estimation and control.

title: A Virtual-Variable-Length method for robust Inverse Kinematics of multi-segment continuum robots
authors: Weiting Feng, Federico Renda, Yunjie Yang, Francesco Giorgio-Serchi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This paper proposes a new, robust method to solve the inverse kinematics (IK) of multi-segment continuum manipulators. Conventional Jacobian-based solvers, especially when initialized from neutral/rest configurations, often exhibit slow convergence and, in certain conditions, may fail to converge (deadlock). The Virtual-Variable-Length (VVL) method proposed here introduces fictitious variations of segments’ length during the solution iteration, conferring virtual axial degrees of freedom that alleviate adverse behaviors and constraints, thus enabling or accelerating convergence. Comprehensive numerical experiments were conducted to compare the VVL method against benchmark Jacobian-based and Damped Least Square IK solvers. Across more than $\mathbf{1. 8} \times \mathbf{1 0}^{6}$ randomized trials covering manipulators with two to seven segments, the proposed approach achieved up to a $20 \%$ increase in convergence success rate over the benchmark and a $\mathbf{4 0 - 8 0 \%}$ reduction in average iteration count under equivalent accuracy thresholds $\left(10^{-4}-10^{-8}\right)$. While deadlocks are not restricted to workspace boundaries and may occur at arbitrary poses, our empirical study identifies boundary-proximal configurations as a frequent cause of failed convergence and the VVL method mitigates such occurrences over a statistical sample of test cases.

title: A Dual-AI Digital Twin Framework for 7-DOF Redundant Industrial Manipulators: Integrating Generative Design and Dynamic Control
authors: Abdullah Tameem
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: As advanced manufacturing scales to meet the demands of electric vehicle and aerospace assembly, traditional 6-Degree-of-Freedom (6-DOF) manipulators face critical limitations. In dense workcells, these arms encounter kinematic singularities and lack the dexterity to navigate complex structures without collision. While 7-DOF redundant manipulators offer enhanced obstacle evasion through nullspace optimization, the added mass of a seventh actuator severely degrades high-speed dynamic performance. This paper proposes a Dual-AI Digital Twin framework that addresses this simulation-to-reality (Sim2Real) gap through two integrated phases. First, a Mechanical AI phase employs topology optimization to reduce distal link mass by up to 40 %, maximizing the stiffness-to-weight ratio essential for high acceleration. Second, the optimized geometry is bridged into a high-fidelity digital twin where a Control AI phase implements Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) to autonomously resolve underdetermined inverse kinematics. The DRL agent learns to maintain precision trajectory tracking while utilizing the redundant joint for real-time obstacle avoidance-all without deterministic Jacobian inversions. This integrated framework provides a comprehensive blueprint for developing lightweight, dexterous, and high-speed redundant manipulators for nextgeneration automated production lines.

title: AgroVeda: Deep Learning Based Crop Disease Prediction and Adaptive Recommendation System
authors: J. Jeya A Celin, Rajkumar Pandiarajan, I. P. Prabarani, Soundariyan TV, Veeralakshmi U, Mathiswaran P
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Conventional methods adopted for the diagnosis of plant diseases rely heavily on the visual inspection and laboratory testing of the plant. This not only consumes a lot of time but also requires the skills of a specialist. Contributing to the delay in the diagnosis of the health of the crop, many smallscale farmers face hindrances in obtaining qualitative diagnosis. Despite the development of a few digital models being initiated in assisting with the diagnosis of diseases, they still face challenges related to the rigid rule-based system, inability to represent the diversity of diseases, inability to conduct real-time analysis, and inadequate guidance on corrective measures. To counter such limitations, this paper introduces a mobile prediction and recommendation system for plant disease management using deep learning algorithms. The proposed method uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) algorithm for plant disease prediction through leaf image analysis using a smartphone camera. Following prediction, the system provides accurate crop-related recommendations, such as treatment procedures, dosage, and prevention, to reduce disease occurrence. In this technology, predictive analysis combined with a sophisticated recommendation engine will help you make better informed early decisions and to have the ability to intervene if needed at your convenience. Experimental analysis shows that this proposed method helps in efficient disease prediction and relevant recommendations, leading to better crop management and a reduction in agricultural losses.

title: Sentiment Analysis on Bangla Product Reviews with Emojis and Slangs
authors: Shaila Sultana, Shahidul Adham Musrur, Tanvinur Rahman Siam
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: User-generated product reviews in Bangladesh frequently contain Romanized Bangla, English expressions, emojis and informal slang, creating a highly noisy and code-mixed textual environment. Such linguistic characteristics significantly limit the effectiveness of conventional Bangla sentiment analysis models that are typically trained on formal and scriptconsistent text. This paper presents a transformer-based sentiment classification framework designed to analyze informal Bangla product reviews. A dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0, 0 0 0}$</tex> publicly available reviews was collected from Bangladeshi e-commerce and social media platforms including Facebook, Daraz and Rokomari. A tailored preprocessing pipeline was developed to address transliteration variability, slang normalization and emoji-aware sentiment preservation. Four transformer architectures-mBERT, XLMRoBERTa, BanglaBERT and BanglishBERT-were fine-tuned for three-class sentiment classification. Experimental results show that language- and domain-specific models outperform general multilingual models with BanglishBERT achieving the best overall performance with approximately 99 % classification accuracy on the held-out test set. These findings highlight the importance of domain-aware pretraining and robust preprocessing strategies for effective sentiment analysis of informal Bangla text in real-world applications.

title: Predicting Loan Default Risk Using Financial and Behavioral Data Under Big Data Infrastructure
authors: Sadia Tarin, Mohammad Shamsul Arefin, M. M. Musharaf Hussain
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Accurately predicting loan defaults is critical for financial institutions to mitigate risk and maintain stability. This study presents a scalable predictive framework using synthetic financial and behavioral data under a big data infrastructure. A dataset of 10 million simulated loan records was generated to reflect realistic borrower behavior and processed using Hadoop and Apache Drill. Machine learning models-including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and XGBoost-were trained, tuned, and combined into a soft voting ensemble to enhance robustness and generalization. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1score, and ROC-AUC. The ensemble achieved balanced results across all metrics (accuracy: 80.99%, precision: 83.66%, recall: 77.02%, F1-score: 80.20%, AUC: 0.8830), demonstrating reliable prediction of default and non-default loans. SHAP-based analysis provided feature-level interpretability, identifying key risk factors such as debt-to-income ratio, loan term, credit score, and past defaults. This framework provides an effective, transparent, and scalable solution for loan default prediction in high-volume financial environments.

title: TrustHeart: Explainable and Privacy-Preserving AI Architecture for Trustworthy Cardiac Monitoring in IoMT Consumer Applications
authors: Qingyang Liu, Jingwen Tang, Jingsong Shen, Yanhui Cai, Yanhuan Huang, Nanyu Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Consumer wearable devices equipped with AI-powered cardiac monitoring systems face significant challenges including adversarial attacks, privacy vulnerabilities, and lack of interpretability, which undermine clinical trust. Existing privacy-preserving cardiac AI systems struggle to maintain accuracy above 92% under strict privacy guarantees (ε < 5), fail to provide interpretability while preserving privacy, and lack robustness for safety-critical deployments. This paper presents TrustHeart, a lightweight architecture integrating robust privacy, interpretability, and proactive threat detection for consumer wearables. The major contributions include: (i) an adaptive privacy budget allocation mechanism achieving ε = 4.2 with only 2.8% accuracy degradation through gradient-aware noise calibration; (ii) a privacy-aware attention mechanism achieving 0.76 Intersection over Union(IoU) alignment with clinical annotations under differential privacy guarantees; and (iii) lightweight anomaly detection algorithms detecting >93.2% of adversarial attacks with 0.9ms overhead. Experiments on MIT-BIH and CPSC2018 datasets demonstrate that TrustHeart achieves 97.6% accuracy on MIT-BIH and 86.1% on CPSC2018, F1=90.8% under Projected Gradient Descent(PGD) attacks, with 35ms inference latency on ARM Cortex-M4, 295kB model size, and <2.3mW inference power consumption. TrustHeart provides a comprehensive reference framework for trustworthy cardiac AI in consumer wearables.

title: Beyond Single-Frequency Approximation: The Dispersion Model for Ultrawideband Phase-Modulated Signals Propagating in Collisionless Cold Plasma
authors: Miao Qin, Zituo Lu, Kai Yuan, Rongxin Tang, Ziyang Zhao, Wenchong Ouyang, Xiaohua Deng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: During hypersonic re-entry or high-speed flight in near-space environments, plasma sheaths enveloping vehicles induce severe communication outages—commonly known as the “blackout” problem—posing a critical bottleneck for future ultrahigh-speed aerospace communication systems. Conventional models for electromagnetic wave propagation in plasmas, such as the Appleton–Hartree formula, are typically based on monochromatic plane-wave assumptions and thus could fail to accurately characterize the behavior of modern wideband communication signals propagating through such dynamic media. Starting from the electron equation of motion, this article develops a time-varying dispersive propagation model specifically suited for ultrawideband (UWD) phase-modulated signals in collisionless cold plasma. By introducing a time-varying differential operator that captures the instantaneous dynamics of the signal envelope, the proposed model overcomes the narrowband limitations inherent in traditional frequency-domain analyses. Key findings reveal that the effective cutoff frequency for phase-modulated signals in plasma is no longer solely determined by the plasma frequency but is jointly governed by the modulation index and baseband frequency—enabling the signal penetration even when the carrier frequency falls below the classical cutoff threshold. Moreover, the plasma’s effective permittivity exhibits periodic oscillations synchronized with the baseband frequency. In addition, due to frequency-dependent dispersion, individual sideband components accumulate nonuniform phase shifts during propagation, leading to significant nonlinear phase distortion and waveform degradation at the receiver. These insights not only provide a theoretical foundation for mitigating blackout effects through optimized modulation schemes but also uncover new challenges in UWD signal transmission over dynamic plasma channels, offering critical guidance for the design of robust, high-data-rate aerospace communication systems operating in plasma-affected environments.

title: Application of Novel High-Temperature Resistant and High-Efficiency Shielding Materials in CFETR
authors: Miao Yin, Shen Qu, Qixiang Cao, Ziqiang Zhao, Fengchao Zhao, Pengfei You, Shiteng Zhang, Shengqiang Zhao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The China Fusion Engineering Test Reactor (CFETR) requires advanced radiation shielding, particularly for protecting its superconducting magnets. This study investigates a novel shielding strategy based on gadolinium hydride (GdH<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {2}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula>), chosen for its high-temperature stability and exceptional efficacy in attenuating low- and intermediate-energy neutrons. To provide comprehensive shielding across the entire energy spectrum, tungsten (W) is incorporated to address high-energy neutrons and gamma rays, forming a complementary “W + GdH<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\mathbf {2}}{\,}+$</tex-math> </inline-formula> W” sandwich configuration. Numerical simulations incorporating a 15% volumetric fraction of water coolant demonstrate that the optimized design reduces the peak fast neutron fluence on the magnets by up to ~70% and lowers the maximum nuclear heating by over 80%, compared to a conventional steel–water shield of equivalent thickness. This substantial performance gain enables a more compact reactor design.

title: Time-Dependent Response of the ITER Diagnostic Residual Gas Analyzer
authors: Brendan R. Quinlan, Jonathan E. Perry, Chris Marcus, Charles D. Smith, Theodore M. Biewer, C. Christopher Klepper
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Analysis of the ITER diagnostic residual gas analyzer (DRGA) vacuum system is critical for the design and accuracy of the gas composition measurement which is used for the control of plasma heating and fuel-cycle processing. The DRGA is a diagnostic system capable of resolving hydrogen and helium isotopes as well as heavier impurity gases. The DRGA is located approximately 15 m away from the divertor and is intended to measure the composition of gases in the ITER sub-divertor region. The DRGA for the divertor analysis relies on an evacuated sampling pipe to transport the exhaust gases to the analysis station where spectrometers measure the exhaust gas composition. At the sampling pipe entrance, an in-line orifice creates molecular flow conditions in the entire length of the sampling pipe. For plasma heating control and fuel-cycle processing, it is necessary to analyze the time-dependent response resulting from changes in gas composition from injected gas and/or fueling pellets. Molflow+, which is a test particle Monte Carlo (TPMC) simulation code, is used to simulate the time-dependent response of the DRGA from injections of hydrogen gas. An innovative way to model an in-line turbomolecular pump is presented in this work. The results from a simulation code based on the physical geometry of an in-lab test article. The results from the simulation code and physical testing of the in-lab test article are compared and show good agreement. The simulation is then extended to an ITER like geometry and compared to previous work. The results of the time dependent response are shown from the sampling pipe orifice to the RGA and a plasma cell which is used for optical gas analysis (OGA) which are two distinct locations in the DRGA system, and the latter of these locations has not been simulated previously. This study provides important design validation of the ITER DRGA time-dependent response as it relates to fuel-cycle and plasma heating control.

title: Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation for Emotion Recognition
authors: Deyang Zheng, Tianyi Zhang, Wenming Zheng, Shujian Yu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Affective Computing
abstract: Emotional states manifest as coordinated and heterogeneous physiological responses across central and autonomic systems, posing a fundamental challenge for multimodal representation learning in affective computing. Learning such joint dynamics is further complicated by scarce and subjective affective annotations, motivating the use of self-supervised learning (SSL). However, most existing SSL approaches rely on pairwise alignment objectives, which are insufficient to characterize the joint dependencies among more than two modalities and fail to capture higher-order interactions arising from coordinated brain-autonomic responses. To move beyond this limitation, we propose Multimodal Functional Maximum Correlation (MFMC), a principled SSL framework that maximizes higher-order multimodal dependence via a Dual Total Correlation (DTC) objective. By deriving a tight sandwich bound and optimizing it with a functional maximum correlation analysis (FMCA)-based trace surrogate, MFMC directly captures joint multimodal interactions without resorting to pairwise contrastive losses. Experiments on three public affective computing benchmarks demonstrate that MFMC consistently achieves state-of-the-art or competitive performance under both subject-dependent and subject-independent protocols, highlighting its robustness to inter-subject variability. In particular, MFMC achieves substantial gains on CEAP-360VR, improving subject-dependent accuracy from 78.9% to 86.8% and subject-independent accuracy from 27.5% to 33.1% using the EDA signal alone, and remains highly competitive within 0.8 percentage points of the best-performing method on the most challenging EEG subject-independent split of MAHNOB-HCI. Our code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/DY9910/MFMC</uri>.

title: Power Allocation and Pricing Strategy in Relay-Assisted Smart Grid Communication Network with Cooperative Jamming
authors: Kai Ma, Yifu Li, Pei Liu, Yazhou Yuan, Jifeng Liang, Zhixin Liu, Bo Jiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Network Science and Engineering
abstract: The increasing openness and heterogeneity of modern communication networks pose serious challenges to trans mission security. This paper proposes a secure transmission strategy for a dual-layer network where spectrum is shared between electrical power communication (EPC) networks and the macrocell. Cooperative relay in EPC networks emit jamming signals to degrade eavesdroppers' reception, thereby enhancing macrocell security while guaranteeing power service quality. A utility cost model is established by statistically analyzing adjustment errors under direct load control, aiming to capture the analytical relationship between load tracking deviations and communication resources. Additionally, a utility function for the macrocell is formulated, balancing secure data transmission and cooperative jamming compensation. The relay power allocation and jamming pricing problems are jointly modeled as a Stack elberg game. A robust iterative algorithm based on backward induction is proposed to obtain the Stackelberg equilibrium. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces utility cost and significantly improves macrocell profitability.

title: Preliminary Investigation of a Quasi-Direct-Drive Actuation for a Biomimetic Underwater Robot
authors: Muhammad Naveed Raza, Kaisei Harada, Shuto Uchikai, Yuto Konno, Keisuke Iguchi, Yusuke Ikemoto, Jun Shintake
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study presents a biomimetic underwater robot driven by a quasi-direct-drive (QDD) actuation system designed to overcome the continuous torque limitations of conventional direct drive (DD) configurations. The QDD system integrates a brushless DC motor with a low-ratio $4: 1$ planetary gearbox, enabling efficient torque amplification while maintaining high responsiveness and mechanical simplicity. A robotic fish prototype was developed and experimentally characterized in terms of thrust force, tail beat amplitude, and swimming speed. Results demonstrated that the QDD configuration produced a peak thrust of approximately 52 N at 10 Hz, which was 2.7 times greater than that of the DD design. The QDD robot also maintained a tail beat amplitude of about 200 mm at 7 Hz and achieved a maximum swimming speed of $175 \mathrm{~mm} / \mathrm{s}$ at 5 Hz, representing a 16% improvement over the DD robot. These findings verify that the QDD actuation effectively enhances propulsion performance by sustaining large tail kinematics at high frequencies. The proposed QDD approach combines the structural simplicity and robustness of DD systems with improved torque capacity, offering a promising framework for high-performance and agile biomimetic underwater robots.

title: Low-Power ML Acceleration on FPGAs
authors: Ezekiel Nwokolo, Tahmina Akter Anondi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: The rapid evolution of machine learning (ML) techniques for sensor data analytics offers significant potential for Internet-of-Things (IoT) endpoint applications. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) are crucial for 2D pattern recognition tasks, yet their high computational demands often require dedicated hardware acceleration. This paper presents a design methodology for training and deploying ML models with scalable hardware acceleration, specifically targeting lowpower, resource-limited embedded Field Programmable Gate Arrays (FPGAs). A custom reduced floating-point quantization scheme and quantization-aware training method are introduced to improve the generalization and accuracy of CNNs, using less than 8-bit floating-point quantization for trainable parameters. The approach incorporates a fully customizable tensor processor (TP) implementing a pipelined vector dot-product with a hybrid of custom floating-point and logarithmic approximations, significantly reducing energy consumption and resource utilization while preserving inference accuracy. The proposed methodology is demonstrated through a CNN-based sensor analytic application for structural health monitoring (SHM) on the XC7Z007S FPGA, achieving a peak power efficiency of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$4.5 \text{GFLOP} / \mathrm{s} / \mathrm{W}$</tex>, a runtime acceleration of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$55 \times$</tex>, and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$808 \times$</tex> reduction in energy consumption compared to traditional embedded CPU implementations. This work underscores the potential of integrating ML acceleration on low-power embedded FPGAs, paving the way for more efficient and scalable IoT applications.

title: vProChain: Efficient Provenance Verification in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)
authors: Jiamin Deng, Zhe Peng, Chuan Zhang, Shuhang Gu, Xin Xie, Bin Xiao
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) has been widely deployed to enable real-time monitoring and automation. Within IIoT-driven production, supply chain management plays a critical role, necessitating verifiable provenance to ensure the authenticity and traceability of goods across multi-stakeholder networks. While blockchain provides a tamper-proof foundation, traditional storage structures suffer from unsecured data integrity, poor query efficiency, and scalability over provenance data. To address these challenges, we propose vProChain, an efficient provenance verification system to support verifiable and parallel queries over graph-structured provenance data. First, we design an Adaptive DAG Verkle Tree (ADVT) that deterministically maps supply chain dependencies into a graph-native authenticated data structure, enabling constant-size proofs and low-overhead verification. Second, we introduce the Merkle Inverted Patricia Trie (MIPT) to facilitate fast, verifiable multi-dimensional Boolean queries. Third, we develop a parallel provenance query algorithm that accelerates multi-hop path retrieval via consistent hashing and weighted bipartite matching. Finally, formal security analysis and extensive empirical evaluations demonstrate that vProChain can provide provable cryptographic guarantees for the soundness of provenance proofs and the completeness of query retrievals, while achieving high query efficiency in a large-scale IIoT environment.

title: An Adaptive Semantic Pattern Matching Transformer for Traffic Flow Prediction
authors: Chen-Yang Cao, Yin-Xin Bao, Weiwei Jiang, Quan Shi
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Accurate traffic flow prediction is essential for intelligent transportation systems, yet most existing methods still forecast future traffic states mainly from the current historical input window and its derived representations. In real traffic systems, however, similar recent observations may correspond to different future evolutions under different latent traffic regimes, while short-term disturbances can further mislead input-local modeling. To address this issue, we propose an Adaptive Semantic Pattern Matching Transformer (ASPMformer) for traffic flow prediction. Rather than departing from the input-driven forecasting paradigm, ASPMformer complements it in three ways: a Context Embedding module enhances local semantic dependencies between adjacent time steps and reduces sensitivity to short-term fluctuations; an explicit spatiotemporal pattern repository enables the current input to be matched with reusable representative traffic patterns in both temporal and spatial dimensions; and a multi-granularity temporal modeling module enlarges the receptive field to capture long-range traffic trends. Extensive experiments on five public benchmark datasets, including PeMS03, PeMS04, PeMS07, PeMS08, and SZ-taxi, show that ASPMformer achieves competitive and consistent improvements over strong baselines. The results further suggest that the proposed pattern repository is particularly beneficial under heterogeneous and disturbance-prone traffic conditions, where relying solely on the current historical window is less sufficient. For the main content of the model, see https://github.com/ccy123q/ASPMformer.

title: EdgeSense: A Hybrid TinyML and Deep Learning Framework for SNR-Aware Adaptive Data Rate in LoRaWAN
authors: Muhammad Ali Lodhi, Xiaobing Sun, Khalid Mahmood, Anum Lodhi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: In the context of Low Power Wide Area Networks (LPWANs), ensuring reliable communication in dynamic environments, especially for mobile edge devices (EDs), poses significant challenges. Traditional Adaptive Data Rate (ADR) mechanisms, while effective in static conditions, fail to adapt to the rapidly changing channel conditions that are characteristic of mobile networks. To address these limitations, we propose EdgeSense, a hybrid solution that dynamically estimates the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) with high accuracy and adapts in real-time to varying network conditions. EdgeSense combines edge intelligence with centralized temporal modeling to enable intelligent adjustment of the spreading factor (SF) and transmission power (TP). Unlike conventional rule-based approaches, which rely on static thresholds and statistical approximations, EdgeSense allows for intelligent decision-making based on real-time SNR estimations, leading to improved Packet Success Ratio (PSR) and reduced energy consumption. This solution is particularly beneficial in mobile IoT applications, where resource constraints and mobility often compromise network performance.

title: Copyright Page
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)

title: Keynote - Federated AGI: A Generational Opportunity for Pervasive Computing
authors: Nicholas D. Lane
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Current scaling laws indicate that future advances in AI will hinge on access to massive amounts of compute and data. How will we obtain the computing power and data resources required to sustain continued AI progress? I believe all roads lead to federated learning, and approaches of this kind. And, in turn, this change represents the biggest opportunity for pervasive computing in a generation: with the right research priorities and execution, pervasive systems can provide the critical infrastructure for tomorrow’s frontier AI, supplying both compute and data; serving as the missing piece for the AI miracle to continue.The AI world may not realize it yet, but it is ready for this radical change. Today, frontier models are trained on essentially the same web-scraped data and rely on data centers that can only scale through unsustainable capital investment. Instead, pervasive systems of sensors, personal devices, scientific instruments, space satellites, vehicles, and embedded computers offer both a limitless stream of new data and a modular, scalable network of compute. Once resources of this scale and quality are unlocked, today’s AI infrastructure paradigm will not compete. Soon, decentralized and federated techniques built on pervasive systems will be how the strongest LLMs are trained; and in time, how aspirational capabilities like AGI will finally be achieved.In this talk, I will describe why the future of AI will be federated, and describe early solutions from Flower Labs and the Cambridge ML Systems Lab (CaMLSys), which address the technical challenges of this shift and place pervasive computing and communication at the core of how tomorrow’s AI is built.

title: Committees
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)

title: Co-Designed Multi-Objective Fault Detection Filtering for Networked Switched Positive Systems
authors: Shuo Li, Xiaodan Liu, Choon Ki Ahn, Zhengrong Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This study addresses the multi-objective L-/L1 fault detection filtering design for switched positive systems (SPSs) subject to a mode-dependent minimum dwell time (MD-MDT) constraint utilizing a dynamic event-triggered strategy (DETS). First, a novel linear copositive dynamic event-triggered condition is proposed for systems with positivity constraints. Next, sufficient and necessary conditions are derived to ensure that the designed fault detection filter provides a positive asymptotic estimation with an L-/L1 performance index. To address the MDMDT constraint, a novel discretized linear copositive Lyapunov function (DLCLF) approach is developed to establish sufficient conditions for ensuring the corresponding augmented system is exponentially stable. Furthermore, by employing the matrix decomposition technology to design filter matrices, an effective mode-dependent piecewise design scheme is proposed for the fault detection filtering of SPSs in a linear programming (LP) form. The developed LP rather than linear matrix inequality (LMI) based scheme features a simpler structure, lower computational demands, and less conservativeness. Finally, three examples are exhibited to validate the achieved results.

title: Mirrored Surface Use and Measurement in Spacecraft Assembly and Test
authors: Robert Elliott
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This white paper and presentation explains how flat mirrors and cubes are used on spacecraft and how they are used for subsystem alignments. It describes early measurement techniques using optical tooling and manual approaches. The advantages and disadvantages of these measurement methods will be discussed including how different measurement techniques affect the accuracy of results. Improper measurement techniques are discussed including the reasons why they are not acceptable. Instructions on how to use a modern laser tracker to measure a mirrored surface are discussed in detail including equipment setup, best practice and software commands using the Spatial Analyzer software package. Finally, a case study that demonstrates schedule savings resulting from transitioning to modern tools is given that shows the schedule and cost advantages of using a laser tracker for these measurements.

title: Rethinking Contingency in Infrastructure Cost Estimates: Using Smart Heuristics to Address Epistemic Uncertainty
authors: Peter Love, Lavagnon A. Ika, Jane Matthews, Weili Fang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
abstract: When estimating cost contingency, aleatory and epistemic uncertainties are often treated probabilistically in the same manner as risk, while residual uncertainty is typically embedded implicitly within contingency allowances without explicit representation. In early project stages, assumptions are unstable, scope definition is incomplete, and small input changes can lead to large variations in estimated outcomes. Such conditions can create overconfidence in probability-based results, resulting in contingencies that underestimate epistemic effects and inadequately account for residual conditions that cannot be parameterized. The article demonstrates how epistemic uncertainty can be explicitly incorporated into pre-contract contingency estimates to improve risk management and produce more credible predictions of final construction costs. A decomposed contingency approach is adopted to separate probabilistic (aleatory), epistemic, and residual components, enabling their formalized representation and integration. Aleatory uncertainty associated with identifiable risks is treated probabilistically, while epistemic uncertainty—arising from incomplete information, scope development, and unresolved interfaces—is addressed using smart heuristics: simple, empirically grounded decision rules informed by ecological rationality. Illustrative examples show how heuristics such as “take-the-best,” tallying, and recognition can be applied at different project stages to refine epistemic allowances as information improves. Residual uncertainty is treated as a bounded, governance-managed provision.

title: Autonomous Tip-and-Cue Earth-Observing Constellation Tasking with Reinforcement Learning
authors: Mark Stephenson, Hanspeter Schaub
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: While Earth-observing constellations often collect images from an a priori request list, this paradigm greatly limits the phenomena that can be observed: Emergent and unpredictable events are often valuable imaging targets. Although tip-and-cue architectures exist to image such events—usually with a “tipping” leader satellite that cues observations by the follower satellite(s)—these lack the flexibility or capacity desired from modern Earth-observing constellations. In this work, reinforcement learning is demonstrated as a way of autonomously and scalably tasking a homogenous constellation of satellites with scanning and imaging instruments. A per-agent policy is learned that is executable onboard each satellite and able to respond to the high-uncertainty environment, solving a problem that traditional pre-planning approaches cannot handle and demonstrating collaborative behavior between agents. As satellites are added to a constellation, the performance of the satellites working together grows faster than the number of satellites. Depending on the location of a satellite within the constellation, it may assume a specific role that biases it towards scanning or imaging.

title: Explainable AI for Regional Climate and Extreme Rainfall Analysis in Karnataka
authors: Akshatha M, Murali S, Krushna Chandra Gouda
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Karnataka exhibits one of India's steepest climatic gradients, shaped by complex interactions among topography, monsoon dynamics, and large-scale climate teleconnections. Traditional regional climate studies are constrained by sparse observations, linear assumptions, and limited interpretability. To overcome these challenges, we propose an integrated artificial intelligence and machine learning (AI/ML) framework tailored to Karnataka's climatic complexity. The framework fuses multisource datasets, including ground observations, satellite products, and reanalysis fields, and leverages unsupervised learning for climate regionalization, explainable supervised models for driver attribution, and deep learning architectures for extreme event modeling. Specifically, Random Forests, XGBoost, SHAP-based interpretability, CNN-LSTM hybrids, and physicsinformed neural networks are combined within a unified pipeline. Our approach delineates homogeneous climate zones, quantifies dominant rainfall drivers, and captures extreme events with uncertainty estimates. This interpretable, scalable AI/ML framework supports regional climate adaptation, providing actionable insights for water resource management, agriculture, and policy planning in Karnataka and other climate-sensitive regions.

title: Verification of SpaceAGORA.jl 6-DoF Dynamics Using University of Michigan SmallSats Telemetry
authors: Evan Yu, James Cutler, Giusy Falcone
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\boldsymbol{A} \boldsymbol{b} \boldsymbol{s} \boldsymbol{t r} \boldsymbol{a} \boldsymbol{c} \boldsymbol{\text{-High-fidelity six-degree-of-freedom simulation is}}$</tex> a key enabler for developing and verifying autonomous guidance, navigation, and control algorithms. This paper validates SpaceAGORA.jl, an open-source Julia-based simulator that propagates fully coupled orbital and rigid-body attitude dynamics with modular environmental and actuator models. Validation is performed by comparing orbital and attitude dynamics with Basilisk simulations and flight telemetry from operational University of Michigan SmallSat missions (CYGNSS and GRIFEX). Results demonstrate a maximum position error compared to Basilisk within approximately 65 meters and attitude error on the order of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1 \mathrm{e}-2$</tex> radians. Comparisons with real-world CYGNSS and GRIFEX telemetry indicate that SpaceAGORA.jl accurately replicates spacecraft translational dynamics, achieving relative position differences within 1% or less, relative to the total position magnitude. In addition, a telemetry-driven replay of a CYGNSS slew demonstrates that the simulator reproduces measured attitude evolution when supplied with a wheel torque/momentum history consistent with telemetry. These validation results establish SpaceAGORA.jl as a reliable simulation environment for developing and validating autonomy and adaptive GNC algorithms under realistic operational conditions.

title: Attention-Based Graph Reinforcement Learning for Large-Scale Construction Project Scheduling
authors: Deepika Dayalan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In essence, construction project scheduling implies coordinating a given set of interdependent tasks, satisfying precedence relationships, and resource limitations. As the size of projects increases, so does the need to identify the structural relationships among tasks within projects to make scheduling decisions. For scheduling purposes, many existing RL approaches use a flattened feature vector to represent the project state, which could fail to preserve the dependency structure between tasks. This paper introduces an attention-based graph reinforcement learning framework for large-scale scheduling of construction projects. A construction project is represented by a directed acyclic graph where nodes represent tasks and edges represent precedence constraints in the proposed approach. A graph neural network is developed to learn task embeddings through information propagation over the project structure. An attention mechanism is then deployed on the feasible tasks for structural dependencies and resource availability to select scheduling actions. The framework is tested using synthetic construction project instances for different graph sizes and resources. The empirical observations demonstrate that graph-based task representation leads to scalable scheduling and lower project makespan outcomes, compared to multilayer perceptron reinforcement learning models and classical heuristic scheduling strategies. Overall, these results show that graph neural networks and attention mechanisms can be a useful addition to reinforcement learning when dealing with complex construction scheduling problems.

title: From Ground Tests to Orbit: ECSS Error Characterization of SentinelTRAC
authors: Laila Kazemi, Colin Huber, Vincent Yim
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Redwire's SentinelTRAC star tracker has achieved significant flight heritage, with more than 100 first-generation units currently operating on orbit across a variety of missions. This large on-orbit population, combined with extensive ground qualification and simulation using a combination of a software-in-the-loop approach, SentinelTWIN, and a hardware-in-the-loop approach, SentinelSIM, has enabled a detailed assessment of performance against the ECSS-E-ST-60-20C framework. Results are presented for low-frequency spatial error (LFSE) and high-frequency spatial error (HFSE) derived from both controlled ground campaigns and on-orbit telemetry. Comparisons between expected and measured performance highlight the influence of factors such as stray light, detector readout noise, and star magnitude distribution. The analysis demonstrates how individual contributors manifest differently in ground versus flight conditions, and how blended solutions can mitigate variability between star fields and sensor heads. By aligning observed behaviors with ECSS-defined error categories, a clear quantitative baseline of SentinelTRAC's performance is established. These results not only validate the robustness of the first-generation design but also provide opportunities for further improvements. Building on this foundation, a road map is outlined for the next SentinelTRAC generation, ensuring that lessons learned from both laboratory analysis and flight operations directly inform future performance improvements and resilience in diverse mission environments.

title: Integrated Attitude Control Architecture for Manipulator-Equipped SmallSats
authors: Bartłomiej Koszek, Laila Kazemi, Emese Kovács
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: As interest in on-orbit servicing, assembly, and debris removal grows, small satellite platforms must be adapted for contactbased operations using robotic manipulators. These missions introduce strong dynamic coupling between the manipulator and the bus, often with comparable mass and inertia, which challenges conventional Attitude Determination and Control Systems (ADCS). This work presents an ADCS architecture and control algorithm tailored to small satellites equipped with manipulators. A digital twin integrates a multibody dynamics model of the satellite-manipulator system with a full ADCS suite (reaction wheels, star tracker, gyroscope, accelerometer) and a quaternion-based PD controller. To mitigate internal disturbances induced by manipulator, a feedforward control layer anticipates and compensates reaction forces and torques at the manipulator base. The approach is demonstrated during the comissioning phase of the manipulator post de-tumble. Preliminary results indicate that manipulator disturbances can dominate environmental torques, necessitating dedicated compensation. The feedforward controller method improves pointing during slow manipulator motions at the cost of higher actuator workload; controller gains must adapt in real time to manipulator pose. By iterating within the digital twin, the ADCS design is optimized with manipulator behavior and operations, enabling robust, cost-effective control for small-satellite missions with complex dynamics. The use of the digital twin can extend the use of low-cost platforms into domains traditionally reserved for larger spacecraft while preserving systemlevel performance and reliability.

title: Real-Time Flood Prediction System for Ciliwung River Using XGBoost Machine Learning
authors: Christopher M. M. Gijoh, Jennifer, Yoel Soengkono, Irene A. Lazarusli, Feliks V. P. Samosir, Aditya R. Mitra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Floods in Jakarta are a persistent issue causing significant economic disruption, primarily driven by the overflow of the Ciliwung River due to upstream rainfall. A limitation in current mitigation efforts is the lack of accurate, short-term early warning systems with sufficient lead time. This study aims to develop a real-time flood prediction system for the Manggarai Water Gate, a critical downstream point, using the Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) algorithm benchmarked against a Linear Regression baseline and a persistence (naïve) baseline. The method involves processing historical time-series data from December 2023 to December 2024, integrating rainfall and water levels from the upstream Katulampa Weir and midstream Depok Monitoring Post. A key contribution of this study is the feature engineering process, utilizing cross-correlation analysis to establish an 8hour lag time for Depok and a 12-hour lag time for Katulampa. The target variable is the Manggarai water level at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t+3$</tex> hours. The results demonstrate that the Hyperparameter-Tuned XGBoost model achieved a Nash-Sutcliffe Efficiency (NSE) of 0.9293 and a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 6.92 cm, outperforming both the Linear Regression baseline (MAE 7.46 cm) and the persistence baseline (MAE <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim$</tex> 18-22 cm estimated) on average prediction error. The study concludes that while Linear Regression captures peak trends effectively, XGBoost offers superior precision for operational early warning systems with a 3-hour lead time, and that data-driven lag feature engineering effectively encodes hydrological travel time without explicit physical modeling.

title: A Multi-Scale Attention-Based Temporal Network for IGBT Junction Temperature Prediction
authors: Zheng-Wei Du, Kai-Yi Yang, Zekai Bi, Rui Wu, Wen-Yan Yin
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Insulated-gate bipolar transistors (IGBTs) are critical in power converters, and junction temperature monitoring is essential for thermal management and reliability. This paper proposes a multi-scale attention-based temporal network integrating parameter and temporal attention mechanisms with long short-term memory (LSTM) units to predict junction temperatures of four chips in a single-phase IGBT leg. Results show the model outperforms LSTM-only approaches, accurately tracking temperature evolution during heating, steady-state, and cooling phases, capturing multi-chip thermal coupling, and significantly reducing prediction errors. The approach demonstrates high accuracy and reliability for IGBT module junction temperature prediction.

title: Large Language Model Chatbot with Retrievalaugmented Generation and Function Calling for Indonesian Article 21 Withholding Tax Support
authors: Verren Angelina Saputra, Marvella Shera Devi, Ethelind Septiani Metta, Rhio Sutoyo, Mahda Karina
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) plays a crucial role in the taxation domain, in spite of their occasional tendency to provide unreliable answers. As the Indonesian income tax system is highly complicated, this study introduces BeeTax, an Indonesian Withholding Tax Article 21 (WHT Article 21) chatbot that leverages Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to support users in understanding theoretical taxation concepts, and function calling to automate tax calculations. While prior studies have shown that RAG and function calling each improve the accuracy and reliability of LLMs, they have mainly been examined separately. BeeTax bridges this gap by integrating both mechanisms within a unified architecture, demonstrating their mutual benefit in a domain-specific application. The chatbot achieved Retrieval Augmented Generation Assessment (RAGAs) scores of 0.71 (faithfulness), 0.81 (answer relevancy), 0.61 (answer correctness), and 0.79 (context precision and recall), indicating reliable retrieval and high generation accuracy in a technical tax domain. The function-calling mechanism achieved 91 % accuracy, confirming its high consistency in performing tax computations. The qualitative evaluations through experts' User Acceptance Testing (UAT) yielded pass rates of 85.71 % and 95.24 %, while a user survey assessing four usability aspects reported satisfaction scores of at least 4.57 out of 5. Overall, the results confirm that combining RAG and function calling improves the user experience, making BeeTax a practical and accessible Artificial Intelligence (AI) solution for WHT Article 21 assistance.

title: Foundational Analysis of Safety Engineering Requirements (SAFER)
authors: Noga Chemo, Yaniv Mordecai, Yoram Reich
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: We introduce a framework for Foundational Analysis of Safety Engineering Requirements (SAFER), a model-driven methodology supported by Generative AI to improve the generation and analysis of safety requirements for complex safety-critical systems. Safety requirements are often specified by multiple stakeholders with uncoordinated objectives, leading to gaps, duplications, and contradictions that jeopardize system safety and compliance. Existing approaches are largely informal and insufficient for addressing these challenges. SAFER enhances Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) by consuming requirement specification models and generating the following results: (1) mapping requirements to system functions, (2) identifying functions with insufficient requirement specifications, (3) detecting duplicate requirements, and (4) identifying contradictions within requirement sets. SAFER provides structured analysis, reporting, and decision support for safety engineers. We demonstrate SAFER on an autonomous drone system, significantly improving the detection of requirement inconsistencies, enhancing both efficiency and reliability of the safety engineering process. We show that Generative AI must be augmented by formal models and queried systematically, to provide meaningful early-stage safety requirement specifications and robust safety architectures.

title: Linearized Bus-Splitting Configuration Models for VRE Curtailment Minimization in Power System Operations
authors: Pee Jay N. Gealone, Adonis Emmanuel DC. Tio
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The integration of variable renewable energy (VRE) sources into power grids introduces operational challenges stemming from their inherent variability and non-dispatchability. The traditional congestion management approaches rely heavily on infrastructure expansion, redispatch, and demand curtailment, which are costly alternatives. This paper presents a novel mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) framework that integrates topology reconfiguration through substation bus-splitting decisions into operational optimization to minimize VRE curtailment. Unlike conventional methods that treat network topology as static infrastructure, the proposed model treats substation configuration as a dispatchable operational asset. The framework incorporates two distinct substation configurations – double-bus and ring-bus – and systematically develops their mathematical models. The model is validated on the IEEE 6-bus and NREL-118 bus test systems using DCOPF. Results demonstrate that incorporating bus-splitting flexibility and co-optimizing it with dispatch reduces VRE curtailment in both small and large grids. Despite the real-world implications of bus splitting for system protection, reliability, and practical implementation, its potential to support effective VRE integration makes a compelling case for its integration with other congestion management solutions.

title: A Trust-Centric and Explainable Framework for Reliable Content Recommendation Under Information Uncertainty
authors: Nivedita V S, Premalatha K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The increasing prevalent conditions of noisy, biased and subject to varying levels of trust user feedback have further complicated recommender systems to be implemented in environments where reliability and transparency are of paramount importance. The paper presents a reliable content recommendation framework that is trust-based and explainable in situations of uncertainty of information. Using the CiaoDVD data that gives explicit user to user trust relations and ratings and reviews, our framework will collaboratively learn preferences, propagate trust, and filter according to uncertainty. We propose a trust-adaptive aggregation system that dynamically gives preference to recommendations depending on the strength of social trust cues, and an explainable explanation system that generates key influencing users of trust and meaningful review data that supports each recommendation. The uncertainty of information is addressed by the framework using confidencebased scoring which reduces the effects of the sparse or contrasting feedback. Consistent improvements over strong trust-aware and explainable baselines in the accuracy of recommendations, noise resistance, and faithfulness to explanations are shown by extensive experiments on CiaoDVD. The findings underline the usefulness of combining both trust modeling and explainability in creating reliable recommender systems in the uncertain environments.

title: Development of Autonomous PHM for Bearing Fault Diagnosis
authors: David He, Miao He
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Most existing Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) systems are developed using supervised learning approaches, which depend heavily on labeled data. In practice, collecting extensive datasets with labels from real-world assets is expensive, risky, and often impossible, particularly for safety-critical components such as bearings, gearboxes, and engines. This limitation makes it challenging for supervised PHM systems to maintain accurate models across various operating conditions and mission profiles, as they require frequent updates with newly labeled data. Self-supervised learning frameworks that continuously learn from abundant unlabeled operational data, adapt to new domains, and fuse heterogeneous sensor streams without relying on labeled failures represent attractive solutions to address the challenges toward fully autonomous, on-board PHM. In this paper, we introduce the Domain-Informed Functional Guidance Network (DiFG-Net), a fault diagnosis framework that fuses self-supervised functional pretraining with domain-adaptive fine-tuning. DiFG-Net first learns the backbone Prediction of Functionals for Masked Latents with two-view consistency augmentation (PFML-TCA) to preserve task-relevant temporal structures while enhancing representation robustness. At fine-tuning, to mitigate domain shift, we add a conditional CORrelation ALignment (CORAL) mechanism that aligns class-conditional means and covariances between source and target batches using confident pseudo-labels. DiFG-Net yields faster convergence, better calibration, and higher file-level accuracy versus PFML baselines. Experiments on plastic bearing fault diagnosis datasets demonstrate consistent accuracy gains over PFML baselines, particularly in cross-domain settings, while providing actionable diagnostic cues through the learned domain-informed functionals.

title: An Efficient Arbitration Mechanism for AXI-Based Multi-Master Shared Memory Architecture
authors: Ravi V, P. Babu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: In a Modern SOC (System-On-Chip) design, the architecture integrates many numbers of processing units (CPUs, DMA controllers, and hardware accelerators) on a single chip (One chip). These integrated units act as “masters” since they initiate memory requests and all of them connect to shared memory through the AMBA AXI interconnect, which controls and coordinates access, so that all components can use the memory efficiently. As the number of masters increases, like GPU (Graphics Processing Unit), Network Interface Controller (NIC) etc., arbitration becomes a crucial/key factor in system throughput. Latency and fairness. For high-priority masters, the Fixed-priority arbitration provides masters access to memory quickly, but all the masters' requests at the same time create heavy traffic. Low-priority masters may last forever and it can cause starvation. In round-robin, arbitration gives each master a chance to access memory and ensure fairness among masters, but increased latency due to time-sensitive masters having to wait longer. Proposed paper a threshold-based hybrid arbitration mechanism for AXI-based multi-master shared memory architectures combining deterministic priority servicing (high-priority masters get fast, predicable access to shared memory up to a certain threshold in fixed-priority) with starvation-aware round-robin scheduling (If a low-priority master waits too long (crosses a threshold), it gets a turn regardless of priority). The hybrid arbitration mechanism monitors which masters have been granted access using bounded starvation counters and if a master waits too long, its priority is temporarily elevated, so that the master gets access within a finite number of arbitration cycles. So, it prevents starvation while still giving fast service to high-priority traffic. The proposed paper develops a mathematical model to compute the following worst-case latency bounds (maximum timer a master may wait), starvation guarantees (no masters waits forever), and fairness properties (using Jain's Fairness Index, which is a standard metric to qualify fair resource sharing). This design architecture is implemented in VHDL and simulated & verified using ModelSim. This simulations results demonstrate that the proposed hybrid arbitration approach effectively balances fairness and throughput while reducing starvation and is to evaluate its performance in AMBA AXI-based multi-master SoC environments.

title: GridML-Plus: An Improved Machine Learning Model for Predicting Grid Stabilities in Power Grids
authors: Md Ariful Islam, Zhangjie Liu, Mei Su
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Grid stability prediction uses machine learning (ML) to analyze complex power system behavior and prevent disruptions across voltage–frequency, rotor angle, transient, small-signal, thermal, and operational-contingency stability conditions. This research presents GridML-Plus, a hybrid ML framework designed for accurate and scalable prediction of seven grid stability classes, including voltage–frequency instability, rotor angle instability, thermal instability, transient instability, small-signal instability, operational instability, and contingency-related instability. The framework integrates Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) for structural representation learning with a Decision Integrated Tree (DIT) classifier to enhance interpretability and robustness. A fully connected graph is constructed to model global feature interactions, while node embeddings capture relational dependencies critical for distinguishing subtle stability variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GridML-Plus achieves 98.92% accuracy, 0.989 precision and recall, outperforming classical machine learning baselines and matching the consistency of advanced ensemble methods. The model exhibits strong generalization across the multi-class classification space and maintains stable training dynamics, with performance improving steadily across thirty epochs. Comparative evaluations confirm that GridML-Plus effectively balances computational efficiency, predictive reliability, and interpretability, making it suitable for both research and practical grid-monitoring environments. Future work will enhance GridML-Plus for real-time streaming, scalable graph construction, edge deployment, cross-grid generalization, advanced multi-label stability modeling, and practical utility integration for operational deployment.

title: AI IoT Based Smart Body Analyzer: Multi-Parameter Health Monitoring and Early Risk Detection for Preventive Care
authors: T. Sangeetha, Vishmaya V, Sushma Sarayu K, Adithya Sharan S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The growing need of the constant physiological measurement and the early identification of abnormalities in health has boosted the creation of smart wearable health systems. This article reflects an AI-IoT-powered Smart Body Analyzer which is aimed at preventing risk and real-time multi-parameter health control. The suggested system will combine MAX30102, GSR, and MPU6050 sensors to measure the vital parameters of the organism and motion-related body motions, such as heart rate, blood oxygen saturation (SpO2), skin conductance, and body movement patterns. IoT framework captures sensor data and transfers it to a cloudbased storage and analysis system. The machine learning system based on the Random Forest algorithm is used to carry out the regression and classification tasks to calculate the overall health indices, including balance score, gait stability, chances of falls, sleep quality estimation, recovery rate of muscles, and the likelihood of fluid retention as well as the assessment of nutritional effects. The system also uses userreported inputs that are used to augment predictive analytics that contribute to the early detection of menstrual health abnormalities and thyroid-related conditions. The proposed framework uses multi-modal data fusion and the AI-powered contextual analysis, unlike traditional threshold-based monitoring systems, which deliver personalized and adaptive health insights. Web-based dashboard provides real time visualization, trends analysis and automatic alert notification to support making medical decisions in time. The efficiency of the system in the early detection of abnormalities, continuous monitoring of health, and scalable system implementation to preventive healthcare services is proved by experimental evaluation.

title: Olfactory Brain-on-Chip in Microgravity for Accelerated Aging and Drug Discovery
authors: Vivian C. Norris, Jerry Yang Olfera, Zara M. Patel, Kelvin Cooper, Sandeepa Dey, Parnian Lak
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: We present the first planned deployment of a patient-derived human olfactory brain-on-chip (OChip) aboard the International Space Station (ISS) to model Parkinson's disease (PD) progression and therapeutic intervention under microgravity. OChip recreates the human olfactory (smell) pathway's flow of signals and molecules- from olfactory epithelium to olfactory bulb and then to the cerebral cortex- in a tricompartmental platform with unidirectional axonal connectivity. Olfactory epithelium neurons are obtained via minimally invasive nasal swabbing from early-stage PD patients with hyposmia, or reduced sense of smell. These neurons carry endogenous pathological proteins, enabling the study of known disease hallmarks as well as novel early disease signatures absent in stem cell and organoid models previously studied on the ISS. This study will assess to what degree microgravity accelerates neuronal aging and pathological protein propagation, whether disease characteristics can be observed on OChip, and whether therapeutics such as prasinezumab (a PD drug in clinical trials) and our proprietary olfactory drug can slow the progression of pathological proteins. Parallel flight and ground OChips will be analyzed by daily brightfield imaging, compartment-resolved biochemical assays, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry–based drug distribution tracking. Based on prior research by other groups, we hypothesize that microgravity-accelerated aging will compress disease-relevant biological processes into weeks and that our therapeutic interventions will reduce propagation of pathological PD proteins, validating OChip in space as a translational platform for drug discovery.

title: Influence of Turn-to-Turn and Disk-to-Disk Faults on the Frequency Response of a Transformer
authors: Elliton O. S. R. Brandão, Estácio T. W. Neto, Gustavo P. Lopes
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Diagnosing internal faults in power transformers is a highly relevant topic, as such defects can quickly progress to critical conditions, compromising the reliability of the electrical system. Among the various methods investigated, frequency response analysis (FRA) stands out for its ability to identify changes in the electromagnetic behavior of the winding, even in the early stages of failure. In this context, this work aims to investigate how different types of internal short circuits — between turns and between disks — influence the frequency response of transformers. Experimental tests were conducted on two single-phase transformers with access to multiple taps in the winding, applying short circuits between specific taps. The results showed that turn-to-turn faults caused an increase in the magnitude of the response at low and medium frequencies, as well as phase shifts at higher frequencies. For disk-to-disk faults, this increase was observed at high frequencies and significant phase shifts at medium frequencies. The statistical indicators SSD, ASLE, and SD were used and proved to be capable of identifying the presence of faults. The SDD indicator proved to be more accurate, being able to identify the frequency bands in which the main discrepancies are evident.

title: Addressing Extra Voices and Background Noise in Continuous Speech Monitoring: A Case Study on Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease
authors: Salaar Liaqat, Daniyal Liaqat, Tatiana Son, Robert Wu, Andrea Gershon, Eyal de Lara, Alex Mariakakis
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Continuous speech monitoring using smartphones and smartwatches offers numerous opportunities to detect and predict health deterioration. However, the algorithms used to deliver these benefits must account for the realities of periodic audio recording in the wild, including other voices and overlapping noise. To address the former without clean sample voice recordings, we use speaker diarization to identify the person who speaks the most throughout a given day. To address the latter without compromising the validity of extracted speech features, we leverage the intuition that a reliable feature should remain unaltered by a noise-reduction technique when background noise is minimal. We examine both techniques in the context of a longitudinal dataset collected from 16 patients with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) over roughly 3 months. Our best model achieved an AUROC of 0.86 when predicting the presence of respiratory symptoms, outperforming baseline models that rely solely on voice activity detection and noise reduction.

title: Status of Current L3Harris Technologies RTG Programs
authors: Leo Gard, Andrew Lane
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: L3Harris Technologies (formerly Aerojet Rocketdyne) is currently working on two major flight Radioisotope Power System (RPS) contracts. This paper will discuss the current status of those two RPS programs, the MultiMission Radioisotope Thermoelectric Generator (MMRTG) and the Next Generation RTG (NGRTG or Next Gen RTG). The MMRTG has powered two different missions to Mars and will be utilized during the upcoming mission to Saturn's moon, Titan (Dragonfly Mission). The Next Gen RTG is based on the previously utilized GPHS-RTG. The MMRTG is designed to support missions in atmospheric environments and the Next Gen RTG is designed for deep space vacuum missions. The Next Gen RTG has a higher power to weight ratio than the MMRTG due to these differences in environmental requirements. The MMRTG program is in process of completing the generator to be used in the Dragonfly Mission. The Next Gen RTG program is currently testing out the power conversion unicouples and demonstrating the manufacturing and assembly operations.

title: Uncovering the Drivers of TikTok Virality: Feature Importance Analysis of Hashtags Using Machine Learning
authors: Miecel Alicia Angel J, Malvin Ferdinand Tanzil, Noviyanti Tri Maretta Sagala, Lili Ayu Wulandhari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The effectiveness of hashtag usage on TikTok remains widely debated, and existing studies rarely provide quantitative evaluation of how specific hashtag combinations contribute to video virality. This study investigates the role of hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams in predicting TikTok video engagement using an interpretable machine learning framework. A dataset of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5, 8 1 5}$</tex> TikTok videos was analyzed using TF-IDF representations of hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams (unigrams to trigrams) combined with contextual control features within a Random Forest modeling framework. The Random Forest Regressor achieved an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> score of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 6 0 8 0}$</tex>, while the classifier achieved a recall of 0.81 in identifying viral content. Feature attribution using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) indicates that early engagement signals, particularly log-transformed plays and shares, are the most influential predictors of virality. While certain hashtag <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N}$</tex>-grams contribute to content categorization and discoverability, their predictive influence is weaker compared to early engagement dynamics. Temporal posting features, such as posting hour and day, were among the least influential predictors.
