These findings demonstrate the value of interpretable machine learning in analyzing social media engagement patterns and provide empirical insights into how hashtag usage interacts with broader engagement dynamics on TikTok.

title: Rotor Dynamics and Sprayer Efficiency in Agricultural UAVs
authors: Harrison Dean, Srikanth Bashetty
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The use of rotary-wing Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) in agriculture for tasks such as pesticide spraying and field mapping has recently been increasing. Their ability to target specific areas, minimize chemical waste, and adapt to diverse field conditions positions them as invaluable tools in precision farming. However, the airflow generated by the rotors can disrupt droplet dispersion, which in turn reduces the efficacy of pesticide applications. This study examines how UAV design affects sprayer efficiency, with a particular focus on rotor dynamics and airflow interactions. Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations were employed to assess the performance of various sprayer configurations that differed in both the number and spacing of the equipped <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 1 0}$</tex>-degree fan nozzles. An initial literature review informed the setup of the simulations used to model the behaviors of the UAV rotors and the sprayer system. The actuator disk approach was employed to model rotor-induced airflow in ANSYS Fluent, with spray behavior simulated using the Discrete Phase Model. Particle tracking was used to estimate deposition area and distribution for each configuration. Results indicate that a two-nozzle setup with 0.6 -meter spacing provides the most practical balance between coverage area, particle uniformity, and ease of integration. While the actuator disk method simplifies rotor modeling, allowing the simulations to serve as a foundation for future experimental validation, this comes at the cost of bladespecific effects being omitted. Future work will involve integrating the sprayer system into a hexacopter UAV for flight testing, focusing on spray uniformity under varying flight conditions. These findings contribute to the optimization of UAV-based spraying systems and support their broader implementation in precision agriculture.

title: Development of a Force Distribution Control System for Tactile Force Reproduction Using a Fabric Actuator
authors: Ayato Kuno, Shinichi Masaoka, Yanhong Peng, Yuki Funabora
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study presents a CNN-based control system for reproducing distributed tactile forces using a fabric-based actuator. A fabric actuator (Funabot-Sleeve) was integrated with a pressure distribution sensor to realize an open-loop tactile force control system. A dataset consisting of pneumatic input commands and corresponding tactile force distributions was collected using this setup. The learned CNN-based controller successfully reconstructed overall force distributions for unseen test data, except for minor local deviations. For humanapplied touch data, reproducibility decreased due to structural constraints in the current actuator configuration, indicating the need for design optimization. These results demonstrate the feasibility of CNN-based tactile force control and highlight the design limitations of the fabric actuator composed of pneumatic muscle threads.

title: A Low-Power Retrofit RFID-Vibration-GSM Security System for Traditional Motorcycles: Design, Implementation, and Experimental Evaluation
authors: Habibie Satrio Nugroho, Nila Natalia, Ady Setyo Nugroho, Aditia Putra Hamid, Rosyid Ridlo Al-Hakim, Ade Muhammad Syamkirana Putra, Christosie Immanuel Wahyudi, Muhammad Yusro, Rikawarastuti
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Motorcycle theft remains a pressing issue, particularly for older, traditional motorcycles that lack built-in security features found in modern models. This study presents an advanced Arduino-based Radio-Frequency Identification (RFID) security system, specifically designed to protect traditional motorcycles by integrating RFID authentication, vibration detection, and real-time alert mechanisms. The system employs an RFID recognition module (Mifare RC522) with a 4 cm detection range, ensuring secure and efficient tag authentication. A SW420 vibration sensor is incorporated to detect unauthorized movements, triggering an alarm system composed of an LED indicator and buzzer when tampering occurs. The SIM800L GSM module enables real-time notifications to the motorcycle owner via SMS in case of a security breach. Power consumption analysis shows stable operation, with 4.50 Volts in standby mode and 4.43 Volts during authentication. Experimental results confirm a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 0 \%}$</tex> success rate in detecting authorized tags and identifying unauthorized access, with zero false positives. By offering an affordable, retrofit security solution without requiring significant modifications to traditional motorcycles, this system provides a bridge between vintage models and modern anti-theft technology. The findings highlight the potential of this security system to enhance the safety of traditional motorcycles, supporting vehicle owners in protecting their assets against theft.

title: Smartphone Authentication Using Face ID with Mask
authors: Njoud Majed Alharbi, Awny Sayed
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Face recognition has become a key technology for user authentication in modern smartphones. However, the widespread use of face masks during the COVID-19 outbreak has posed significant challenges due to facial occlusion. To address this issue, this paper proposes a hybrid face recognition framework that integrates deep learning embeddings with handcrafted texture descriptors to improve robustness under masked face conditions. Four hybrid configurations are evaluated by combining FaceNet or MobileNetV2 with either Local Binary Patterns (LBP) or Gabor wavelets. Experiments evaluate identity recognition performance under masked, unmasked, and mixed scenarios using both the Real-World Masked Face Dataset (RMFRD) and the proposed Real-World Masked and Unmasked Face Dataset (RWMUF). Notably, the FaceNet with LBP approach achieves high recognition accuracies of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$98.63 \%, 99.21 \%$</tex>, and 99.14 % for masked, unmasked, and mixed faces, respectively, on the RWMUF dataset, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$96.81 \%, 98.81 \%$</tex>, and 96.48 % on RMFRD, clearly outperforming other configurations, and is integrated into a mobile application for real-time face recognition. These results highlight the effectiveness of combining deep learning embeddings with handcrafted features for robust masked face recognition. The RWMUF dataset is available at: https://github.com/NjoudAl-harbi/A-Real-World-Masked-and-Unmasked-Face-Dataset-RWMUF-Dataset-

title: A CUDA-Based Framework for Scalable 3D Drone Swarm Collision Avoidance Using Predictive Spatial Hashing
authors: Yash Ashish Hajare, Abhishek Gaddam, Arun M.
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Scaling collision avoidance for dense drone swarms beyond a few thousand agents quickly stops being a CPU problem and becomes a GPU problem: the amount of work grows too fast for a single core to keep up in real time.[1] In this work we describe a CUDA-based framework that tackles this issue using a predictive spatial hashing scheme, where each drone is assigned to a CUDA thread and future positions are hashed into a grid instead of current ones.[1] This look-ahead hashing lets the system catch possible collisions one step before they would normally occur, while Thrust radix sort groups drones by cell in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O((N / P) \log N)$</tex> time and each drone checks only 26 neighboring cells, keeping the effective per-frame complexity at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$O(N \cdot k / P)$</tex> with constant <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$k=26$</tex>.[4] [5] [13] The framework is implemented entirely on the GPU using the CUDA Runtime API, Thrust, and cuRAND, and was evaluated on an NVIDIA Tesla T4 running CUDA 12.8.[6] [8] For <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=10,000$</tex> drones the GPU implementation completes a full collision-avoidance step in <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 2 6 4 ~ m s}$</tex>, which is roughly a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$413 \times$</tex> speedup over a single-threaded -O2 CPU baseline that takes 113 ms and already violates the 33.3 ms budget for 30 fps. At <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N=50,000$</tex> drones, the full frame time is 0.87 ms and no postavoidance collisions are observed across the tested configurations, showing that the gap between CPU and GPU performance grows with swarm size rather than closing. The results indicate that exploiting GPU parallelism is not just an optimization, but a requirement, for real-time collision avoidance in large 3D drone swarms.[4] [15]

title: Snapping Actuators with Asymmetric and Sequenced Motion
authors: Xin Li, Ye Jin, Mohsen Jafarpour, Hugo de Souza Oliveira, Edoardo Milana
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Snapping instabilities in soft structures offer a powerful pathway to achieve rapid and energy-efficient actuation. In this study, an eccentric dome-shaped snapping actuator is developed to generate controllable asymmetric motion through geometry-induced instability. Finite element simulations and experiments reveal consistent asymmetric deformation and the corresponding pressure characteristics. By coupling four snapping actuators in a pneumatic network, a compact quadrupedal robot achieves coordinated wavelike locomotion using only a single pressure input. The robot exhibits frequency-dependent performance with a maximum speed of $72.78 \mathrm{~mm} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$ at 7.5 Hz. These findings demonstrate the potential of asymmetric snapping mechanisms for physically controlled actuation and lay the groundwork for fully untethered and efficient soft robotic systems.

title: Spatial-Temporal Data Augmentation and Prediction in Power Systems Based on WGAN-ConvLSTM
authors: Qing Peng, Wenjun Pang, Yaodong Li, Xianfu Gong, Xian Zhang, Guibin Wang, Mahmoud Mohamed Sayed Mohamed Hemdan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Driven by global carbon neutrality targets, the widespread integration of renewable energy and electric vehicles defines modern power systems. However, intermittent renewable generation and stochastic EV charging introduce severe bidirectional uncertainty, threatening grid stability. To address this, we propose WGAN-ConvLSTM, a novel hybrid spatiotemporal forecasting framework. The WGAN module overcomes data scarcity by generating high-fidelity synthetic samples that strictly align with real-world distributions. Subsequently, the ConvLSTM network leverages this augmented data to extract complex spatial correlations and temporal evolutionary dynamics, enabling high-precision, collaborative forecasting of renewable generation and EV charging loads.

title: Simple Data Model Approach to Integrate Face® Software Applications Designed from Disparate DSDMs
authors: Travis Rogers, Ryan Wolfe, Scott Tompkins
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Software integration can be difficult, costly and time-consuming. Luckily, the Future Airborne Capability Environment® or FACE® Technical Approach offers methodologies that make integration easier. However, even when developers follow this technical approach, there can still be challenges to integration. One serious challenge occurs when two or more software applications need to be integrated, but each application's supporting message set and transport services originate from a different Domain-Specific Data Model (DSDM). DSDMs are key in creating the message sets that components within an application produce and consume. The process of rectifying the differences in disparate DSDMs to ensure components within a combined application can intercommunicate is daunting. This paper presents two methodologies to integrate software applications that come from disparate DSDMs. The first methodology uses a common data model to reproject both application's message sets from a single, Simple Data Model (SiDM). This allows the software to run on a single TSS. The second methodology applies a solution where both applications integrate while maintaining their own message sets, transport services, and DSDMs allowing all semantic meaning to remain unchanged. To achieve this SiDM is used to bridge the semantically meaningful message sets between two or more applications. This allows for two or more applications with disparate DSDMs to communicate using both their native data models and across-interface with the common data model. This paper will provide the methodology to construct SiDM and explain both integration methodologies. This paper will also reference nonproprietary examples where this technique is implemented. By implementing this methodology, applications become more universally useful as “off the shelf” products that can be integrated without major reconfiguration.

title: Research on the Loading Test Method for Propulsion Motors under Wide Temperature Ranges and Low Pressure Conditions
authors: Jinhua Liang, Haiping Xu, Fei Chen, Wei Liu, Peng Zhou, Xi Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Based on an analysis of the propulsion motor’s operating characteristics and the environmental pressures it faces, a loading test platform has been developed. This platform supports dual, triple, and quadruple loading combinations, integrating alternating high/low temperature cycles, variable environmental pressure, mechanical vibration and shock from airflow disturbances, and cyclic load variations. A novel loading test methodology is then proposed, enabling single factor extreme performance testing, synchronous static coupling tests, alternating cyclic dynamic coupling tests, and comprehensive accelerated life testing. This approach allows for a more realistic simulation of multi field coupling effects under actual service conditions.

title: Dual-Degree-of-Freedom DPWM Strategy for High-Power-Density VSC in Urban Power Systems
authors: Haizhou Ying, Ye Tao, Pengfei Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High-density urban power systems impose severe thermal and power quality constraints on Voltage Source Converters (VSC) where traditional modulation strategies often fail to balance switching losses and harmonic performance. To address this issue, this paper proposes an improved Discontinuous Pulse Width Modulation (DPWM) strategy utilizing dual-degree-of-freedom parametric regulation defined by clamp width ψ and phase shift δ. This approach dynamically reconstructs the zero-sequence voltage component to actively shape switching sequences. Specifically, the phase shift compensates for the power factor angle to align the clamping window with the peak current region for maximum loss suppression while the clamp width enables a flexible trade-off between efficiency and harmonic distortion. The proposed algorithm relies on simple algebraic operations to facilitate real-time implementation in digital controllers without complex look-up tables. Electro-thermal simulations using the PLECS platform demonstrate that the proposed strategy significantly reduces steady-state junction temperatures and thermal cycling amplitudes compared to traditional SVPWM. These results validate the method as an efficient software-based solution for enhancing the thermal reliability of compact power equipment in urban grids.

title: Automated Competency Mapping for Lecturer Recruitment Using Machine Learning and Multisource Professional Data
authors: Fransiskus Adikara, Alaniah Nisrina, Eko Wahyu Prasetyo
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The digital transformation of human resource management has necessitated specialized frameworks for talent acquisition, yet academic recruitment, specifically the selection of lecturer candidates, remains largely dependent on manual screening processes. This research proposes an automated competency mapping framework designed to enhance the efficiency of lecturer recruitment by synthesizing data from professional networking platforms and traditional curriculum vitae. Utilizing the scikit-learn library, the system employs Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Term FrequencyInverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) to transform unstructured textual data into standardized competency profiles. A preliminary evaluation involving 100 candidate samples demonstrates that baseline machine learning architectures, specifically Logistic Regression and Multinomial Naive Bayes, provide high precision in classifying candidates according to academic and pedagogical requirements. Usability assessments from human resource professionals further indicate that the integration of multi-source professional data significantly improves the objectivity and scalability of the initial screening phase. These findings suggest that automated mapping provides a robust foundation for identifying highpotential talent within specialized academic hiring environments.

title: Fully Decentralized P2P Energy Trading in Electric-Hydrogen Integrated Energy Systems with Hybrid Renewable Energy
authors: Jinlong Yang, Pengcheng Chen, Wangzhuo Yang, Shuwang Du, Bo Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Peer-to-peer (P2P) energy trading within integrated energy system (IES) facilitates benefit sharing among prosumers and effective integration of distributed renewable energy sources. This paper proposes a novel fully decentralized P2P trading framework for a coupled electric-hydrogen system. The proposed system incorporates hybrid renewable energy sources (photovoltaic and wind turbine generator), distributed generators (DG), battery energy storage systems (BESS), electrolyzers (EL), fuel cells (FC), and hydrogen storage tanks to establish a comprehensive energy management solution. The alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm is employed to achieve decentralized P2P trading optimization, where self-interested prosumers iteratively negotiate to reach optimal trading strategies for both electricity and hydrogen. The framework addresses the temporal mismatch between renewable energy generation and load demand through electricity-to-hydrogen conversion and storage, enabling long-term energy arbitrage. A IEEE 33-bus distribution network case study demonstrates the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm, showing convergence of dual electric-hydrogen P2P energy trading and enhanced renewable energy utilization through multi-energy synergy.

title: AI-Generated Marketing Content and Consumer Trust: Examining the Role of AI Disclosure
authors: Agung Stefanus Kembau, I Gede Wisnu Satria Chandra Putra, Felliks Feiters Tampinongkol, Arief Perdana Kumaat, Julius Sutrisno, Ali Wardhana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: AI-generated marketing content is increasingly used in contemporary digital advertising, raising critical questions regarding transparency and consumer trust. This study examines how AI disclosure influences trust in AIgenerated marketing content through perceived authenticity and deception concern, while also investigating the moderating role of consumption context. Using a quantitative approach, data were collected from 189 social media users in Indonesia and analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM). The results indicate that AI disclosure positively affects perceived authenticity and negatively influences deception concern. Perceived authenticity significantly enhances trust in AI-generated content, whereas deception concern weakens it. Moreover, the relationship between perceived authenticity and trust is moderated by consumption context, with stronger effects observed in hedonic settings compared to utilitarian ones. These findings underscore the importance of transparency-by-design in AIdriven marketing and demonstrate that consumer responses to AI-generated marketing content are context-dependent. This study contributes to AI marketing literature by integrating cognitive appraisal mechanisms and contextual factors into a unified trust formation framework, while offering practical insights for designing effective and ethically grounded AIenabled marketing communications.

title: A Review of Resilience Research on Integrated Energy Systems Oriented towards Cyber-Physical-Social Integration
authors: Jie Hu, Sihan Liao, Yinye Shen, Guokun Cui, Jia Hu, Ziwei Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the profound transformation of global energy systems, Cyber-Physical-Social Systems (CPSS) have emerged as a new paradigm for future energy systems. This paper systematically reviews the research progress on Integrated Energy System (IES) resilience based on CPSS, organizing literature from four perspectives: three-layer CPSS architecture, extended "4R" resilience assessment framework, evolutionary trajectory of attack-defense games, and integration of emerging technologies. It highlights the significant impact of social behavior on energy system resilience, identifies key research gaps, and proposes future directions, providing comprehensive guidance for constructing a robust theoretical and technological system for IES resilience. Recent studies emphasize the critical role of social sentiment in crisis response, and simulation-based validations demonstrate the effectiveness of digital twin-driven strategies in extreme scenarios.

title: A Full Bridge Inverter for High Frequency Induction Heating of Small Rebar Workpiece
authors: Ittikorn Wongsoison, Thananop Leesupananon, Napanan Klinthoop, Juthathip Haema
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: This paper presents the design and implementation of a full-bridge inverter for high-frequency induction heating of a small rebar workpiece. The proposed system comprises six main components: a step-down transformer, a full-wave bridge rectifier, a pulse-width modulation (PWM) signal generator, a full-bridge inverter utilizing power MOSFETs, a high-frequency transformer, and the induction coil with the workpiece. The rebar specimen, measuring <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$1.2-\text{cm}$</tex> in width and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$25-\text{cm}$</tex> in length, is heated via a double 2 -turn split copper winding. Experimental results show that the workpiece reaches a temperature of at least 740° C within 60 seconds. The system delivers an output heating power of approximately 1.10 kW, demonstrating its effectiveness for small-scale induction heating applications.

title: Novel Suite of Sensors for Recording Spatial, Temporal, and Spectral Optical Lightning Signals
authors: Grant Soehnel, Garrett Giddings, Steve Brown, Grace Dean
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Lightning is by far the most common fast transient optical signal seen by space-based Earth observing sensors. Dedicated optical lightning sensors have historically been limited in their geographical coverage, spatial resolution, temporal resolution, and/or spectral resolution. Sandia National Laboratories has designed and built a low-cost multi-sensor payload to address the gaps in available optical lightning data. This payload includes a set of 16 spectral band-pass radiometers, a high-speed framing camera, and an event-based imager, which are all triggered by a fast-changing optical signal in a wide-band radiometer. All radiometers and cameras are synchronized to collect time-correlated data. The payload is housed inside an air-tight enclosure at 1 atm to reduce the risk to COTS components in the space environment. The first mission will be a threemonth flight on the International Space Station (ISS). The radiometer sample rate is 96 kHz with spectral bands ranging from <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$400-1650 \text{nm}$</tex>. The framing camera will run at up to 1 KHz and provide 0.8 km spatial resolution on the ground. The Prophesee EVK4 event-based camera has an effective temporal resolution approaching <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0 ~ k H z}$</tex> and spatial resolution of 0.25 km on the ground. The optical and opto-mechanical hardware for the radiometers consists of mostly off-the-shelf commercially available components. A unique testbed has been built to calibrate the radiometer responses to known input optical flux as a function of field angle. This mostly automated process involves holding the entire payload with a robotic arm, centering each radiometer aperture to the light source, and tilting the payload to sweep the field-of-view angle. This capability allows a payload with uncertain alignment tolerances to still record radiometrically accurate data.

title: Enhancing Industrial Safety Communication Over Wi-Fi Networks With Frame Replication
authors: Suneel Kumar, Daniel Camps-Mur, Zhibo Pang, Ognjen Dobrijevic, Eduard Garcia-Villegas
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications
abstract: Industrial automation applications increasingly adopt wireless technologies and mixed wired-wireless networking. However, over a wireless channel, meeting the stringent performance requirements of such applications becomes challenging. That is especially critical for safety communication, i.e., exchange of data used to keep workers and machinery protected. This paper investigates an emerging safety solution, Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture (OPC UA) Safety, and explores how Time-Sensitive Networking’s Frame Replication and Elimination for Reliability (FRER) can enhance communication performance for OPC UA Safety over Wi-Fi networks. Using an emulation-based approach, different redundancy strategies and their impact are analyzed. The results demonstrate that, even under worst-case, high-load network conditions, applying FRER across asymmetrically loaded, independent Wi-Fi paths provides a substantial benefit. For example, round-trip time of safety messages is reduced by up to 92% as compared to single-path Wi-Fi, while communication availability is improved from near 0% to over 85%.

title: Multi-Model Local Illumination Compensation for Enhanced Compression Model
authors: Jialin Li, Zhuoyuan Li, Yao Li, Dong Liu, Li Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Circuits and Systems for Video Technology
abstract: Inter prediction is a key technique for reducing temporal redundancy in video coding. However, irregular inter-frame illumination changes are hard to predict, yielding poorly predicted residuals that are difficult to compress. To address this, local illumination compensation (LIC) has been introduced into the next-generation exploratory coding model, which uses a linear model (LM) to approximate and compensate for inter-frame illumination variations, thereby reducing the prediction residual. Due to the simple design of LM and the least squares regression (LSR) solution in ECM, LIC suffers notable limitations, including the limited fitting capacity of the LM and the outlier sensitivity of LSR, which makes the compensation less robust to outliers. To address these limitations, we propose a multi-model local illumination compensation (MM-LIC) framework, which introduces the multi-model paradigm to optimize the model type (MMT) and model solution (MMS) jointly. In MMT, the candidate model types are extended beyond LM by incorporating a non-linear model (NLM) and a gradient linear model (GLM) to better handle diverse illumination variations. In MMS, ridge regression (RR) is further introduced to improve model solution accuracy and stability, especially when handling outliers. Meanwhile, in order to preserve decision accuracy without extra bit overhead, we design separable implicit decision mechanisms for the whole compensation process. The proposed method is implemented in ECM-8.0. Experimental results show that the proposed MM-LIC can achieve, on average, 0.45% and 0.23%, and achieve up to 0.68% and 0.40% BD-rate reduction under LDB and RA configurations, respectively, with only slight increases in time complexity. Furthermore, the proposed scheme also demonstrates its potential in the latest ECM version.

title: Weak Cyclostationary Target Echo Detection for Multi-UAV Enabled Passive Radar Network
authors: Long Zhang, Kui Xiong, Maosen Liao, Xianxiang Yu, Guolong Cui
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of week cyclostationary target echo signal detection for the multiple unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) enabled passive radar network. First, the cooperative detection system model and the signal model of the multi-UAV enabled passive radar network is constructed. Based on the cyclostationary low signal-to-noise radio characteristics of target echoes, the generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) method is employed to detect target echoes in complex colored noise backgrounds. Then, by applying the channel-aware Bayesian minimum risk fusion (BMRF) strategy, the local detection results from each radar receiver are optimized and fused to obtain the final decision for improving the accuracy of global detection, thus generating the cyclostationary generalized likelihood ratio test-Bayesian minimum risk fusion (CGLRT-BMRF) detection algorithm. Finally, numerical simulations are conducted to illustrate the superiority of the proposed algorithm over some typical detection algorithms.

title: Alternating Subspace Method for Sparse Recovery of Signals
authors: Xu Zhu, Yufei Ma, Xiaoguang Li, Tiejun Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: Numerous renowned algorithms for tackling the compressed sensing problem employ an alternating strategy, which typically involves data matching in one module and denoising in another. We present a novel approach, the Alternating Subspace Method (ASM), which integrates the principles of the greedy methods (e.g., the orthogonal matching pursuit type methods) and the splitting methods (e.g., the approximate message passing type methods). Crucially, ASM enhances the splitting method by achieving fidelity in a subspace-restricted fashion. We reveal that such a restriction strategy guarantees global convergence via proximal residual control and establish its local geometric convergence on the LASSO problem. Numerical experiments on the LASSO, channel estimation, and dynamic compressed sensing problems demonstrate its high convergence rate and its capacity to incorporate different prior distributions. Overall, the proposed method is promising in terms of efficiency, accuracy, and flexibility, and has the potential to be competitive in different sparse recovery applications.

title: Enhancing Cloud Network Resilience via a Robust LLM-Empowered Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning Framework_supp1-3694417.pdf
authors: Yuling Liu
publish: N/A
abstract: While virtualization and resource pooling empower cloud networks with structural flexibility and elastic scalability, they inevitably expand the attack surface and challenge cyber resilience. Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based defense strategies have been developed to optimize resource deployment and isolation policies under adversarial conditions, aiming to enhance system resilience by maintaining and restoring network availability. However, existing approaches lack robustness as they require retraining to adapt to dynamic changes in network structure, node scale, attack strategies, and attack intensity. Furthermore, the lack of Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) support limits interpretability and flexibility. To address these limitations, we propose CyberOps-Bots, a hierarchical multi agent reinforcement learning framework empowered by Large Language Models (LLMs). Inspired by MITRE ATT&CK's “Tactics-Techniques” model, CyberOps-Bots features a two-layer architecture: (1) An upper-level LLM agent with four mod ules—ReAct planning, IPDRR-based perception, long-short term memory, and action/tool integration—performs global awareness, human intent recognition, and tactical planning; (2) Lower-level RL agents, developed via heterogeneous separated pre-training, execute atomic defense actions within localized network regions. This synergy preserves LLM adaptability and interpretability while ensuring reliable RL execution. Experiments on real cloud datasets show that, compared to state-of-the-art algorithms, CyberOps-Bots maintains network availability 68.5% higher and achieves a 34.7% jumpstart performance gain when shifting the scenarios without retraining. To our knowledge, this is the first study to establish a robust LLM-RL framework with HITL support for cloud defense.

title: Dependence of optical refractive index of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength in near-infrared waveband for warm and shallow sea_supp1-3693439.xlsx
authors: Shanshan Wang
publish: N/A
abstract: Dependence of optical refractive index (RI) of seawater on temperature, salinity, and pressure is crucial for optical fiber sensors operated in seawater. Expressions of RI of seawater on temperature, salinity, pressure, and wavelength applicable to near-infrared waveband are developed based on a great deal of RI data measured by a semi-encapsulated microfiber Mach-Zehnder interferometer sensor. To verify the accuracy of these expressions, RI measured by infrared refractometer, theoretical calculated sensitivity and experimental measured sensitivity are used, by which the most accurate expression is determined. Based on this expression, a variable sensitivity matrix is established to demodulate temperature, salinity and pressure simultaneously with errors of 10.91 %, 6.09 % and 18.80 %, respectively. The expression proposed here will provide valuable references for theoretical and experimental study on optical fiber sensors operated in infrared waveband and push the applications of fiber optical sensors in ocean investigations.

title: Preliminary Design of the Cesium Oven for the ITER Neutral Beam Injector
authors: Fabio Veronese, Marco Alessandroni, Piero Agostinetti, Michele Fadone, François Geli, Diego Marcuzzi, Andrea Nazzicone, Mauro Pavei, Basile Pouradier Duteil, Andrea Rizzolo, Emanuele Sartori
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The ITER experiment, currently under construction in Cadarache in southern France, will require the injection of a considerable amount of additional power from its heating and current drive (HCD) systems in order to sustain long plasma pulses. Among all of the various systems, neutral beam injectors (NBIs) will account for 50 MW of power over three devices, mediated by fast neutrals at 1 MeV in deuterium (or 870 keV in hydrogen). The required extracted negative ion current at the plasma source (PS) can only be reached through efficient negative ion generation and extraction, which in this type of NBIs is achieved by sufficient surface coating of the inner surfaces of the PS with a low work-function metal, such as cesium. This article describes the cesium oven for the ITER Heating NBI (HNBI), which is a device for the controlled evaporation of cesium inside the PS: its general structure is based on the design of the cesium oven for the Source for Production of Ion of Deuterium Extracted from Rf plasma (SPIDER) experiment, however with the necessary modifications to make it compatible with the more stringent ITER guidelines, such as radiation resistance and remote handling compatibility. A prototype has been designed at Consorzio RFX by taking into account this set of requirements, followed by extensive simulation of the thermal distribution.

title: Updated Electrical Design of the Diagnostic Neutral Beam Injector in RFX-mod2
authors: Marco Barbisan, Bruno Laterza, Luca Cinnirella, Lionello Marrelli, Federico Molon, Simone Peruzzo, Enrico Zampiva
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: The diagnostic neutral beam injector (DNBI) of the RFX-mod2 experiment (Consorzio RFX, Padua) is expected to provide novel and significant information about the reversed field pinch (RFP) confinement of fusion plasmas. The present DNBI, built by the Budker Institute of Plasma Physics, features an arc discharge H<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">+</sup> source, coupled to a 4 grid 50 keV acceleration system, to produce a 50 ms, 5 A ion beam. This contribution presents the latest upgrades of the DNBI. The high-voltage deck (HVD) was completely restructured, and the power transfer was simplified to a single-phase insulation transformer. The 50 kV distribution circuit was modernized and made safer against breakdowns. Several custom power supplies in the HVD were designed and procured; their electronic boards were developed to be multipurpose in the DNBI, simplifying the system and improving its maintainability. The features of the magnetic insulation power supply (MIPS) and gas valve power supplies (GVPS) are presented in detail. Finally, the new PLC control system was improved for better protection of the CPU against overvoltages and for better scalability and maintainability of the system.

title: A Method for Data Augmentation in Vertical Federated Learning Addressing Data Heterogeneity
authors: Yunpeng Xiao, Tingting Lv, Dengke Zhao, Wanjing Zhao, Tun Li, Rong Wang, Guoyin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems
abstract: Vertical federated learning (VFL) can aggregate data features from participating parties and is applicable to data collaboration in various fields. To address data heterogeneity in VFL, this article proposes a framework tailored for heterogeneous environments. First, to mitigate performance degradation caused by imbalanced local data across clients, we exploit conditional generative adversarial networks (CGANs) for targeted data augmentation, and propose a data balancing model named FeCWGAN-GP based on CGAN. This model pretrains a local CGAN for each client to perform local private data compensation, thereby alleviating the problem of decreased model performance. Second, to handle local model parameter distribution shifts induced by heterogeneity, leveraging both the sample size proportions and the Wasserstein distance in the model parameter space to capture parameter distribution shifts due to data heterogeneity, a parameter aggregation algorithm named WFedDA based on sample size and parameter distribution is proposed. This method calculates weights based on the sample size proportions of participants and the distribution difference between local model parameters and global model parameters, thus optimizing the global model. Finally, to address the instability of local model parameters caused by data heterogeneity, a stochastic gradient descent (SGD) method with a dual smoothing mechanism named SGD-MA is proposed. This method uses an exponential moving average (EMA) to process gradients and parameters sequentially, which reduces the fluctuation of gradients and the instability of parameter updates, thereby improving the stability of the training process. Experiments on the public datasets MNIST, CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST, and MIMIC-III demonstrate that the methods proposed in this article can effectively address the issues caused by data heterogeneity in multidata-source environments, significantly improving the generalization capability and stability of the global model.

title: Locally constrained non-negative matrix factorization for data clustering
authors: Xuzhu Shen, Jie Li
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Semi-supervised non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) has been widely used for clustering high-dimensional data, because a small amount of label information can effectively improve clustering performance. Nevertheless, most existing semi-supervisedNMFmethods still face two important challenges: they require carefully tuned hyperparameters to control the effect of supervision, and they cannot directly produce clustering assignments from the learned factorization results. In this paper, we propose a novel semi-supervised NMF framework, called Locally Constrained NMF (LCNMF), to address both issues simultaneously. Different from existing methods that impose global penalty terms or fit predefined label codes, LCNMF injects label information by encoding locally inconsistent elements in the low-dimensional representations of labeled samples as zero, thereby introducing an explicit local structural constraint into NMF. This design eliminates the need for additional hyperparameter tuning and allows the learned representation to carry more direct clustering semantics. Furthermore, LCNMF integrates dimensionality reduction and clustering assignment into a unified framework, so that the final cluster labels can be obtained directly without an extra clustering step.We further formulate two variants of the proposed model and derive the corresponding optimization algorithms. Extensive experiments on several real-world datasets show that LCNMF consistently achieves effective and competitive clustering performance compared with state-of-the-art semi-supervised clustering methods. The code can be obtained at https://github.com/ljisxz/LCNMF.

title: DDoS Attacks Detection and Prevention on Real-time Streaming Data Using Machine Learning Methods with Kafka and Apache Spark
authors: Jameel Ahmad, Usama Ahmed, Saad Farooq, Muhammad Sarwar, Momina Shaheen, Dimitris M. Manias, Jawad Khan
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) attacks in the constantly expanding sphere of cybersecurity are highly important to detect and respond to promptly. This paper proposes an Intrusion Detection System (IDS) that integrate Machine Learning (ML), Apache Kafka and Apache Spark to enable real-time detection and mitigation of DDoS attacks. The proposed framework collects live network traffic data, performs preprocessing and feature extraction, and applies machine learning algorithms within a streaming architecture supported by Apache Kafka and Apache Spark. Kafka enables real-time data ingestion, while Spark provides distributed processing for efficient model training and inference. The methodology supports adaptability to emerging attack patterns through training and evaluation on publicly available benchmark datasets.Machine learning models, including Random Forest and Support Vector Machine (SVM), were evaluated within the proposed streaming framework. Experimental results show that the integrated use of Apache Kafka and Apache Spark achieved an accuracy of up to 95%. The real-time streaming capability of Kafka, combined with the distributed processing power of Spark, enables efficient handling of high-volume network traffic and supports timely detection of DDoS attacks.

title: On Cycle Embedding of Enhanced Hypercubes with Scoped Vertex Faults
authors: Che-Nan Kuo, Yu-Huei Cheng
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: In large-scale interconnection networks, faults caused by natural disasters or human-induced incidents often occur in a localized and correlated manner rather than as isolated random events. Such behavior is frequently observed in practical systems such as data centers, multi-core processors, and network-on-chip architectures, where power failures, thermal hotspots, or regional hardware defects may simultaneously affect a group of adjacent components. This motivates the study of structured fault models that better reflect real-world failure patterns. The enhanced hypercube Qn,k is an important variant of the hypercube architecture, obtained by adding complementary edges between specific vertex pairs, and it has been widely studied due to its strong structural and communication properties. In this paper, we consider a scoped fault model in which all four vertices of an arbitrary cycle of length four, denoted by FC4 , are removed from Qn,k, representing the smallest nontrivial localized failure cluster in hypercube-based networks. We investigate the cycle embedding capability of Qn,k − FC4 and establish that it preserves a wide range of cycle lengths under this fault condition. In particular, for every n ≥ 3 and 1 ≤ k ≤ n − 1, all even cycle lengths from 4 to 2n − 4 exist in Qn,k − FC4 , and for even n ≥ 4 with n and k having different parity, all odd cycle lengths from n − k + 4 to 2n − 5 are also preserved. These results demonstrate that the enhanced hypercube maintains optimal cycle embedding capability under the considered scoped fault model and highlight its robustness against localized correlated failures.

title: A Deep Learning-Enhanced EIS Online Monitoring Framework Based on Sparse DC-DC Measurements for Vehicular Fuel Cell Systems
authors: Ganyun Zheng, Zhaoming Liu, Yuan Zhu, Haotian Wang, Zenggang Xia, Linlin Zhuang, Haifeng Dai
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: Fast online access to fuel cell impedance is important for health diagnosis and fine-grained management, yet existing onboard methods still struggle to balance update rate and measurement fidelity. This paper presents a hybrid monitoring framework that combines sparse physical calibration from a production DC-DC converter with continuous virtual estimation. A six-phase interleaved DC-DC converter injects dual-frequency excitation at 10 Hz and 1 kHz and, with synchronous Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) extraction under integer-period sampling, provides physically grounded impedance references while keeping current ripple below 1%. Between calibration instants, a compact recurrent estimator, FR-LSTM-lite, infers impedance continuously from electrochemistry-guided inputs. Structured pruning and 8-bit quantization reduce the model to a form suitable for automotive microcontrollers. Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL) and 140-kW bench tests show inference latency below 1 ms and impedance error no greater than 1.5%, delivering milliohm-level monitoring without extra hardware or sustained excitation. Together, these capabilities make milliohm-resolution impedance available in real time on production hardware, opening a practical path toward impedance-feedback fuel cell control.
