title: Leveraging Space-Time Synchronization for Ultra-Spot Detection in mmWave/THz UAV-to-UAV Communications
authors: Phuc Duc Nguyen, Ryosuke Isogai, Keitarou Kondou, Satoshi Yasuda, Nobuyasu Shiga, Yozo Shoji
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: In UAV-to-UAV communication, airborne UAVs need to detect the location and direction of ultra-high-speed millimeter-wave (mmWave) and Terahertz (THz) coverage areas, referred to as ultra-spots. This predictive capability allows UAVs to optimally adjust their flight paths, altitude, and velocity, thereby maximizing the utilization of ultra-spot services. A space time synchronization technique employing multiple Wireless Two-way Interferometry devices (multi-Wi-Wi) is proposed in this paper to detect mmWave/THz ultra-spot locations during UAV operations. This paper proposes an algorithm that estimates the likelihood of nearby ultra-spots by considering the UAV flight route and ultra-spot direction, and by sharing location and pose information among UAVs in the network via a 920MHz wireless communication link. For the first time, this work addresses the problem of optimizing UAV flight routes to maximize ultra spot utilization. To address the inherent challenges of Wi-Wi, such as phase data unreliability, RSSI attenuation, or packet loss caused by obstructions from the UAV's own body, this study proposes the use of multiple Wi-Wi devices equipped with antennas positioned at different positions around the arms of the UAV to leverage spatial diversity effects. The proposed method's effectiveness is confirmed through experimental data derived from real-world UAV-to-UAV communication tests. An error of 37.16cm was observed experimentally in ultra-spot location estimation, corresponding to 186ms error in temporal prediction of ultra-spot entry from an in-flight UAV, demonstrating its effectiveness in addressing ultra-spot detection challenges in mmWave communication.

title: Interface Management in the Development of the DTT Remote Handling Facility—REMHAT
authors: Francesco Marino, Andrea Fimiani, Andrea Zoppoli, Andrea Reale, Mauro Dalla Palma, Carlo Carrelli, Giuseppe Di Gironimo
publish: IEEE Transactions on Plasma Science
abstract: Remote handling (RH) procedures in fusion devices are sufficiently complex that a purely virtual verification and validation (V&V) process cannot guarantee reliable operation. Dedicated physical testing in purpose-built facilities is essential to validate and commission RH equipment and to train operators. As part of the divertor Tokamak test (DTT) project, the REMote HAndling Test and training facility (REMHAT) is currently under procurement. Its requirements and functions have been defined, and a preliminary logical architecture has been established. The facility comprises four main subsystems—buildings&services, mock-ups, robots, and control system. Given the integration of multiple subsystems and stakeholders, interface management has been a central focus throughout the procurement packages. Building on a previously presented conceptual design, this article reports advances in the final design of REMHAT and the management of interfaces across subsystems. The mock-ups reproduce the geometry, mass, and stiffness of the relevant DTT components, enabling representative testing of RH equipment and procedures. A model-based approach using the systems modeling language (SysML) has been adopted to manage requirements, functions, and interfaces—particularly between the robots and the control system—resulting in a consistent logical architecture and more efficient conflict resolution among requirements. The final design for the robots and the control system, together with manufacturing activities, is expected to be completed by 2026.

title: A Systematic Review of Load Parameter Identification and Power Management Techniques in Domestic Induction Heating Appliances
authors: Aneel Ahmed, Hyunghu Park, Irfan Ali, Zawar Khan, Geun-Hyeong Lee, Jin-Woo Jung
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: Induction heating (IH) is emerging as a transformative, sustainable, and eco-friendly solution for energy transfer, offering rapid heating, enhanced safety, cleanliness, and automatic pot detection, particularly in domestic induction heating (DIH) appliances. However, the dynamic nature of IH loads poses significant challenges for accurate load parameter identification (LPI) and robust controller design for power management. This review presents a systematic and comprehensive analysis of the LPI and power management techniques in DIH appliances, highlighting recent advancements, challenges, future research opportunities, and recommendations. Unlike existing review literature, which is relatively limited and generally addresses IH, this paper provides a DIH-focused review and organizes DIH control into two major categories: load parameter identification (LPI), which models IH loads (e.g., resistance Re and inductance Le) using both online and offline methods; and power management techniques for ferromagnetic appliances (FMAs), non-ferromagnetic appliances (NFMAs), and all-metal appliances (AMAs), differentiated by distinct behaviors they exhibit. By using systematic classification, this paper addresses key gaps and provides a smooth roadmap for readers to understand existing methods and guide future developments in DIH control.

title: Robots in Inclusive Education of Students with Autism Spectrum Disorder
authors: Kaloyan Damyanov
publish: IEEE Transactions on Learning Technologies
abstract: The study examined how humanoid and social robots affect social and communication skills in inclusive education for children with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). A mixed-methods approach collected and analyzed data using quantitative and qualitative methods. The study included 30 children with ASD who used NAO, Pepper, and KASPAR robots for 12 weeks. The main task was to diagnose children's social sensitivity and communication skills before and after using robots in education to evaluate this approach. Children's social adaptation and robot-mediated interaction were associated with significant improvements, particularly in non-verbal communication and social interaction. Robots can make learning more inclusive and supportive, according to the study. The study examined robotic therapy development theory, including pros and cons. The analysis focused on successful ASD teaching methods in Norway, Japan, Bulgaria, and the US, where inclusive education and the latest technologies are used to support students with special needs. Based on the data, practical recommendations have been developed for teachers on how to integrate robots into teaching, policymakers on equal access to technology, and developers of robotic systems that focus on the needs of children with ASD.

title: Unsupervised Building Change Detection in Remote Sensing Images via Joint Structural and Non-Structural Variation Generation
authors: Yaoyao Wang, Jialei Yu, Han Wang, Xinxiao Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: Remote sensing change detection plays a vital role in urban expansion analysis, ecological assessment, and disaster monitoring. While deep learning-based methods have significantly advanced performance, they often rely on large-scale multi-temporal datasets with pixel-level annotations. To alleviate this dependency, generating paired bi-temporal remote sensing images has been explored, but its effectiveness is limited in the absence of pixel-level semantic masks. Moreover, most generation-based change detection methods focus solely on modeling structural changes such as the construction or removal of buildings, while neglecting non-structural variations (i.e., changes caused by illumination shifts, atmospheric fluctuations, or seasonal dynamics that do not alter the physical structure of objects). In this paper, we propose SNV-GenCD, an unsupervised change detection framework for buildings in high-resolution optical remote sensing images, which generates pseudo bi-temporal remote sensing images by jointly modeling structural changes and non-structural variations. Within SNV-GenCD, a structural change generation module produces object-manipulated images along with corresponding pixel-level change masks, and a non-structural variation module injects radiometric changes that reflect real-world remote sensing conditions. Further, to eliminate temporal bias and enhance robustness against pseudo-changes in the generated data, we introduce two complementary constraints that guide the detector to learn robust, order-invariant representations. Experiments on the LEVIR-CD, WHU-CD, and GoogleCD datasets show that SNV-GenCD achieves F1 scores of 82.2%, 87.6%, and 76.7%, respectively. These results significantly outperform existing state-of-the-art unsupervised methods and are competitive with weakly supervised approaches. The code is available at https://github.com/Shakeyoo/SNV-GenCD.

title: BER Analysis of Rate Compatible Gaussian Modulation
authors: Yuanyuan Ju, Fang Lu, Yan Dong
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: In this letter, an approximate bit error rate (BER) expression for rate compatible Gaussian modulation (RCGM) is derived based on the extrinsic information transfer (EXIT) chart, providing theoretical insights for RCGM design and eliminating computationally intensive simulations. Motivated by the rateless characteristic of RCGM, the convergence point of EXIT curves is approximated as the point on the symbol node demodulator (SND) curve where the input mutual information reaches 1. Under this approximation, the SND processing simplifies significantly, as the decision set for each symbol is reduced to two constellation points. Consequently, a closed-form expression for the probability density function of the SND output messages is derived, from which the approximate BER expression is obtained. Numerical evaluations demonstrate that the theoretical BER closely approximates the simulated results across various RCGM schemes.

title: Hyperspectral Weak Object Detection Using Homogeneous-Unique Feature Fusion
authors: Shaoyang Ma, Xun Huang, Hanzheng Wang, Bolun Cui, Zhicheng Shi, Wei Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral object detection (HOD), as an emerging research field, has attracted growing attention due to its advantage for detecting objects in challenging scenes with weak objects. However, existing methods mainly depend on off-the-shelf RGB detectors for feature extraction, failing to fully exploit the abundant spectral context information in hyperspectral images (HSIs), which results in suboptimal detection performance, especially in the case of weak objects such as tiny or camouflaged objects. In this paper, a method named HYPER-DET is proposed. Firstly, to address the issue of insufficient utilization of spectral context information in feature extraction, a novel cross-modality feature extraction network (CFEN) is introduced to extract visual and spectral features simultaneously, thus enhancing the feature representation of weak objects. In the RGB branch of the proposed backbone, an end-to-end self-sensing regrouping module (SSRM) is presented to capture cross-band correlations and cluster optimal three-channel spectral band groups. In the HSI branch, a spatial-spectral feature extraction module is proposed to fully extract the complementary spatial and spectral information from HSIs. Secondly, to facilitate the efficient fusion of the cross-modal features between RGB and HSI modalities, a homogeneous-unique feature fusion (HUFF) module is designed, which adaptively extract and fuse both homogeneous and unique features across these two modalities. Finally, we introduce a new hyperspectral aerial tiny object dataset, HWOD-tiny. It is tailored for challenging scenarios that involve tiny Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) objects, providing a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating HOD methods in competitive settings. HWOD-tiny is the first dataset designed for hyperspectral aerial tiny object detection. Extensive experiments on multispectral UAV imagery dataset DroneVehicle, general HOD dataset HOD3K and the proposed datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed HYPER-DET, which outperforms existing state-of-the-art object detection methods. The source datasets in the article will be available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/m1ftb1/hyperdet</uri>.

title: Resilient State and Input Estimation for Complex Network Subject to Cyber Attack: A Set-Membership Method
authors: Chenyang Pan, Zhaoxia Peng, Shichun Yang, Guoguang Wen, Tingwen Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Cybernetics
abstract: This article is concerned with resilient state estimation for a class of complex network with the vulnerable communication channel from the sensor to estimator, where both transmitted input and output signals are susceptible to malicious attacks. To overcome these challenges, a novel set-membership filter framework integrated with an unknown input estimator is designed. This framework enables the joint estimation of states and unknown inputs and actively mitigates the adverse impact of corrupted input signals. Furthermore, it circumvents the practical difficulties of direct input measurement in applications. Through rigorous mathematical induction, sufficient conditions are derived to guarantee the state remains bounded within an ellipsoid centered at its estimation, even in the presence of malicious attacks. The input filter gain is determined to decouple the state estimation error from unknown input, while the state filter gain is optimized to minimize the ellipsoid. The proposed framework is subsequently extended to the complex networks with direct feedthrough. Finally, numerical simulations and battery experiments are conducted to validate the resilience and effectiveness of the designed set-membership estimators.

title: An Edge-Enabled Two-Stage Framework for Automated Anomaly Detection and Classification in Metal Coils Using Ensemble Learning
authors: Giardini Formanski Lazzarin, Alexandre Gonçalves Silva, André Tavares Da Silva
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Coil quality inspection in the steel processing industry is critical for quality control but remains challenging due to unconstrained industrial environments, manual error-prone processes, and infrastructure constraints for high-volume data transmission. This paper presents a novel two-stage automated system using Deep Learning for identifying and classifying anomalies in metal coils, optimized for large-scale industrial deployment. Stage 1 comprises a lightweight integrity validation workflow deployed at the edge, achieving a 95.51% F1-score and a 99.48% Negative Predictive Value (NPV), effectively preventing low-integrity data from entering the analytical pipeline while providing real-time operator feedback with latencies between 17 and 42 ms. In Stage 2, validated images are processed by an Expert Committee utilizing a weighted hybrid voting strategy that integrates YOLOv8 through YOLOv12 and RT-DETR architectures. Experimental results on an industrial dataset of over 26,000 images under real production conditions showthat the ensemble improves the classification F1-score to 62.25%, corresponding to a 22.47% gain over the best standalone model. The integrated pipeline achieves a global accuracy of 87.25% and a consolidated NPV of 95.24%, providing a high-security barrier against quality escapes within the production chain. Furthermore, a Region of Interest (ROI) cropping strategy combined with controlled image compression reduces network payload and storage requirements by 77%, enabling a throughput exceeding 87,000 daily inspections. The proposed framework offers a robust, scalable, and economically viable solution for automated visual quality control in Industry 4.0 manufacturing systems.

title: Enhancing Indoor Three-Dimensional Localization through the Integration of Visible Light Communication and Particle Swarm Optimization
authors: Diego Alonso Candia, Pablo Palacios Játiva, César Azurdia Meza, Iván Sánchez
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper presents an active Visible Light Communication (VLC)-based three-dimensional localization system optimized using Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) for accurate indoor positioning. The system employs three optical transmitters, each consisting of a single light-emitting diode (LED), and localizes a mobile target carrying a receiver equipped with three photodiodes (PDs). The study evaluates the impact of the PSO parameters, namely the cognitive factor (c1), the social factor (c2), and the inertia weight (w), on localization accuracy. The best overall parameter combination was c1 = 1.9, c2 = 2.1, and w = 0.8. Furthermore, the numbers of particles and iterations were analyzed to balance accuracy and computational efficiency, with 700 to 1100 particles and approximately 40 iterations yielding the best results. The proposed system eliminates the need for continuous modulated data transmission, thereby enhancing robustness, energy efficiency, and adaptability in various indoor environments.

title: A Single-Layer Wideband Differential Filtering Patch Antenna for 5G IoT Applications
authors: Haoyang Huang, Le Peng Zhang, Shuai-Yu Pan, Yang Zhao, Hai Ke Tang, Huayun Wang, Hao Chi Zhang
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: A single-layer wideband differential filtering patch antenna for 5G IoT applications is proposed. The proposed antenna features a simple configuration, comprising a pair of T-shaped structures, shorted parasitic patches, and defected ground structures (DGS) etched into the metal ground plane. Two shorted parasitic patches are loaded to achieve λ/4 resonance, and a high-frequency radiation null is introduced. A pair of T-shaped structures, composed of meandered slots, is etched into the main radiating patch, which causes strong current disturbance and excites opposite current components around the slots, thereby introducing a low-frequency radiation null. Moreover, a pair of DGS is incorporated into the ground plane, exciting an additional resonance within the passband to broaden the impedance bandwidth effectively. Finally, a prototype of the proposed antenna is fabricated and measured. The measured results are consistent with the simulated values, demonstrating an impedance bandwidth of 16.8% centered at 4.75 GHz, a peak realized gain of 6.1 dBi, and two controllable radiation nulls near the band edges. The designed antenna achieves a broad bandwidth and filtering response, while maintaining a small electrical size of 0.2 λ<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup><sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub> and a low profile of 0.025 λ<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</sub>. These performance metrics make the proposed wideband differential filtering antenna a promising candidate for 5G IoT applications.

title: Performance Bounds for Priority-Based Stochastic Coflow Scheduling_supp1-3690856.pdf
authors: Balakrishna Prabhu
publish: N/A
abstract: We consider the coflow scheduling problem in the non-clairvoyant setting assuming flow sizes are random variables that follow some probability distribution. The goal is to minimize the weighted average completion time of coflows in expectation. We first obtain inequalities for this problem that are valid for all non-anticipative order-based rate-allocation policies and define a polyhedral relaxation of the performance space of such scheduling policies. This relaxation is used to analyze the performance of a simple priority policy in which the priority order is computed by Sincronia from expected flow sizes instead of their unknown actual values. We establish an upper bound on the approximation ratio of this priority policy with respect to the optimal priority policy for arbitrary probability distributions of flow sizes (with finite first and second moments). Tighter upper bounds are obtained for some specific distributions. Extensive numerical results suggest that performance of the proposed policy is much better than the upper bound.

title: TTACO: Trusted Time-Aware Computing Offloading in Air-Ground Integrated Networks
authors: Yujie Song, Yue Cao, Zhenning Wang, Chi-Hung Chi, Wei Ren, Wei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: As efficiency and security requirements emerge in computing offloading fields, trusted computing offloading has grabbed tremendous sights, especially in Air-Ground Integrated Networks (AGINs). Although traditional computing offloading studies have attempted to employ trust to identify malicious devices without mobility, the integration of trust management and computing offloading has not been explored in a high-hazardous environment. Then, with the increasing requirement of adaptation and security in new-generation network architectures (i.e., AGINs), trust management plays a pivotal role in expanding the implementation of trusted computing offloading. Therefore, we propose a trusted time-aware computing offloading mechanism in AGINs based on communication, computing offloading, and trust modeling. Specifically, a maximum reward optimization formulation is designed to generate an optimal offloading strategy, considering service utility trust, opinion service trust, computing efficiency trust, time constraints, rewards, and task volumes. Based on problem analysis, a solution paradigm is condensed to improve the probability of seeking an efficient solution. Moreover, a greedy search algorithm is designed to find the possible efficient solution, including the default solution setting stage, the boundary search stage, and the greedy reallocation stage. Due to the trust threshold affecting identification results, a dichotomy-based dynamic trust threshold method is employed to empower trusted computing offloading mechanism with the adaptive capability in AGINs. Extensive experiments show that our mechanism outperforms other baselines in terms of accuracy, precision, recall, F-measure, task success rate, and average response time.

title: Reconfigurable Dual-Band Millimeter-Wave Filter Based on GaAs Technology
authors: Yu-Hao Shen, Dong-Zhe Pan, Hao Wu, Bin You
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: An on-chip reconfigurable dual-band millimeter-wave (mmWave) bandpass filter (BPF) is presented in a GaAs integrated-varactor platform. Independently tunable passbands with asymmetric reconfigurability are realized using a parallel-coupled topology comprising a varactor-loaded half-wavelength resonator and a dual-mode capacitor-loaded resonator, which effectively decouples the two bands, a functionality seldom demonstrated in reported on-chip mmWave filters. The lower band tunes from 25 to 26.5GHz with 10.2% FBW and 3.48–4.16dB insertion loss, whereas the upper band tunes from 35.25 to 37.25GHz with a reconfigurable 4.4–9.2GHz bandwidth and 3.7–5.15dB insertion loss. Good linearity is achieved, with a maximum IIP3 of 30dBm. Source–load coupling introduces transmission zeros (TZs) to enhance selectivity, and measured results agree well with simulations.

title: Optimal Dispatch of Offshore Wind-Hydrogen-Ammonia System Considering Three-tier Ammonia Reserve Mechanism
authors: Qikun He, Yuxuan Zhao, Ke Tan, Zhaoxia Jing, Disi Mai
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To address the challenges of offshore wind integration and the low-carbon transition of thermal power, this paper proposes an optimal dispatch strategy for an offshore wind-hydrogen-ammonia system incorporating a three-tier reserve mechanism. Existing studies often isolate internal co-firing from external sales, lacking coordinated optimization. To bridge this gap, a novel three-tier architecture—comprising strategic reserve, operational buffer, and tradable surplus tanks—is designed to decouple source-side wind stochasticity from rigid load-side co-firing demands. An optimization model is formulated to maximize total operational profit, incorporating carbon quotas and green premiums. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed mechanism ensures fuel supply security while outperforming single-mode strategies in economic efficiency through flexible surplus trading. The results demonstrate that this strategy plays a crucial role in carbon reduction while maintaining high profitability.

title: Optimal Planning of Embedded VSC-HVDC for Urban Power Grids with High Load Density
authors: Zhenyong Niu, Ting Guo, Yufei Zhang, Liangde Xu, Yong Wang, Yilin Zhong, Yujun Li, Zhengchun Du
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The rising load density and increasingly uneven spatial distribution in urban AC grids are undermining power flow controllability. Congestion in AC corridors and short-circuit limits have become dominant constraints. To address these challenges, an optimized planning approach for embedded voltage source converter based high voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC) is developed, by adding converters and DC lines to form a hybrid AC/DC architecture, which enables rapid inter-area power flow redistribution and mitigates AC bottlenecks. A mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) model minimizes total planning-period cost by unifying DC terminal siting, converter sizing, generator dispatch, load-shedding decisions, and line power flow control within a single decision framework. Nonconvex couplings between DC terminal siting and DC line connectivity are handled via linear relaxation. The total-cost objective integrates capital expenditures of converters and DC lines with operating costs from generation and load shedding. Case studies on the IEEE 30-bus system compute planning solutions at several discrete load levels and demonstrate that optimized DC configurations effectively relieve AC congestion, ensure secure operation, and minimize planning-period cost. These findings highlight the potential of embedded VSC-HVDC for precise power flow steering and improved techno-economic performance, offering both theoretical support and a practical pathway for flexible interconnection upgrades in high-load-density urban grids.

title: Resource Allocation for Resilient Distribution Systems Based on Multisource Data-Driven Failure Scenario Generation
authors: Nuoling Sun, Naixuan Zhu, Hao Chen, Yu Li, Yining Li, Gengfeng Li, Yiheng Bian
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Extreme weather increasingly triggers widespread distribution outages. Resilience planning requires realistic multiple line outage scenarios and restoration aware investments that remain valid under frequent topology changes. This paper couples inductive failure probability learning with a two stage resource allocation model. Each distribution line is modeled as a node in an edge graph, and its feature vector fuses meteorological intensity, geographic exposure, asset conditions, and connectivity cues. An inductive GraphSAGE predictor estimates line fault probabilities and transfers to unseen switching configurations. Independent Monte Carlo sampling converts these probabilities into a finite set of outage scenarios. The scenarios drive a two stage mixed integer linear program that sites dispatchable DG, PV, and energy storage, and schedules post event restoration actions, including optional network reconfiguration, under linearized power flow and radiality constraints. Tests on a modified IEEE 123 bus feeder show improved prediction accuracy and a 61.9% reduction in expected energy not served when reconfiguration is enabled.

title: Epidemics in Orbit: Modeling Space Debris Dynamics to Alleviate the Orbital Traffic Jam
authors: Rachel Sholder
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The accumulation of space debris in Earth's orbit poses an escalating threat to satellite infrastructure, human spaceflight, and the viability of future space missions. As orbital congestion increases due to expanding satellite constellations and human activity in low Earth orbit (LEO), the risk of collision-induced fragmentation grows, potentially triggering cascading chain reactions such as the Kessler Syndrome. To address these dynamics, this study introduces the Junk Accumulation Model (JAM) - a novel adaptation of the classical compartmental SIR (Susceptible-Infectious-Removed) epidemiological model to capture the dynamic spread of space debris in LEO. In this formulation, Susceptible (S) objects are intact satellites not yet involved in collisions, Infectious (I) objects are debris-producing bodies or fragments that actively generate new collision risks, and Removed (R) objects represent satellites and debris naturally eliminated through orbital decay or atmospheric burn-up, as well as debris removed via active debris removal (ADR). The system of ordinary differential equations underlying JAM provides a tractable, deterministic framework for exploring debris proliferation and evaluating mitigation strategies, incorporating parameters such as launch rates, collision rates, orbital decay, and ADR effectiveness. While SIR models are fundamental in the mathematical modeling of infectious diseases and have been extended to other domains, there is no precedent for their application to space debris. This interdisciplinary contribution demonstrates how epidemic-style models can capture cascade thresholds in orbital environments, offer interpretable levers for policy and engineering intervention, and lay the groundwork for future extensions such as multi-layer SIR networks across orbital regimes. Ultimately, JAM provides a novel, accessible toolkit for anticipating debris proliferation, guiding remediation efforts, and informing policies that aim to alleviate the growing orbital traffic jam.

title: Crowd Management During Hajj and Umrah Using Machine Learning
authors: Kayan Alsahli, Munirah Alosimi, Lena Alhaobani, Alhanouf Alhumaidan
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Crowd management during Hajj and Umrah is a major safety challenge due to rapidly changing pedestrian densities and complex movement patterns. This project develops a machine-learning model to predict crowd density 60 minutes into the future using a real Hajj and Umrah crowd-management dataset. The dataset combines temporal, spatial, environmental, and behavioral features and is converted into a regular 5-minute time series. After preprocessing, temporal sorting, categorical encoding, and resampling, a future-density target (Crowd_Density_Future_60m) is constructed by shifting current crowd density 12-time steps (60 minutes). Additional features such as rolling averages, change rates, and temporal indicators (hour and day of week) are engineered. A Random Forest classifier is then trained to predict whether the crowd will be high-density or not high-density in the next hour. On a temporally separated test set, the model achieves an accuracy of approximately 70 %, with balanced performance across precision, recall, and F1-score. These results demonstrate that short-term crowd forecasting is feasible and can support pro-active congestion control, safer route planning, and better manargument of pilgrim movement during Hajj and Umrah.

title: Fluid-structure interaction dynamics and feedback control of multiple flexible bioinspired swimmers
authors: Rose Gebhardt, Haotian Hang, Derek A. Paley, Eva Kanso
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Fish swim by bending their bodies as fluidstructure interactions occur between elastic tissues and the surrounding flow, yet modeling many such swimmers with nearbody hydrodynamics is computationally difficult. This paper presents a modeling framework for multiple flexible fish with near-body hydrodynamic interactions and a low computational cost. We model each elastic fish body using Bergou’s theory of elastic rods constrained to planar movement. We use a vortex sheet method to model the flow around each fish and the wake created by its motion. We introduce a control law to highlight the utility of this platform for control design and evaluation. All components of the model are integrated to simulate several schooling patterns. This approach provides a practical testbed for studying flow-mediated coordination, exploring design rules for energy-efficient collectives, and rapidly prototyping control strategies for bioinspired robotic schools.

title: Smart Logistics Preventing Fruit Spoilage Using IOT
authors: Sujan S, Boopathy. S, Suyash S A, Swathika S, Tamilarsan S, N Prakash
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The purpose of the structure that has been suggested is to assess the effectiveness of cutting-edge sensors in terms of monitoring the gases that are released by fruits, as well as temperature and humidity. After that, sophisticated machine learning algorithms were used to make a prediction about the maturity of the fruit based on the information that was gathered. Because bananas are eaten in such large quantities in India, our inquiry will concentrate exclusively on bananas. Other than that, they offer a wide range of possible uses in research, notably in determining whether something is ripe or has gone bad. Because bananas mature so quickly, they are an excellent topic for research on ripeness indicators because of their fast-ripening process. The analysis of gases that are released throughout the different phases of ripening may give significant data that can be associated with algorithms that are specifically designed for machine learning. To improve food administration and supply chain management in real-world scenarios, the purpose of this research was to create techniques for detecting ripeness and rotting in fruit that are based on machine learning. The framework that has been provided exhibits amazing precision when tracking the nutritional value of fruits and vegetables that are stored in retail shop trays or cold storage chains of custody. This presents a solution that is both practical and practicable.

title: Design and Development of an Intelligent Anti-Tremor Glove for Parkinson's Patients Using Pneumatic Air Cuff Mechanism
authors: Prabakar S, Manikandan K, Haripriya K, HarshaVardhini SS, Madhomitha P B, Rajasekar M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Parkinson is a chronic neurological condition where the patients are affected with continuous shaking of their hands against their will making this condition affect their normal everyday activities like eating, writing, and dressing. Current intervention methods such as medication and surgery have their side effects or inaccessibility to a large number of patients. The Wearable assistive device is a viable alternative, which is non-invasive. The present paper reports about how a miniature, inexpensive anti-tremor glove is constructed and tested, which makes use of pneumatic air cuff to oppose hand tremors in real-time. A glove has an MPU6050 inertial sensor that monitors hand movement. This information is pushed to an STM32 microcontroller which performs signal processing to isolate tremor-induced movement to intentional hand motion. A wavering motion is detected and the microcontroller causes Relay 1 to open a solenoid valve and Relay 2 to activate a small DC motor that drives an air pump. These, in combination, fill the air cuff around the joints of the fingers and wrists, where they produce a slight opposing force which suffocates the tremor. The moment the shaking is over, both the relays are turned off and the cuff is deflated, and the hand is free to use. Tremor tests conducted on ten had the potential to reduce tremor by 65-75 percent, detect better than 93 percent and respond in less than 120 milliseconds.

title: Digital Regenerative Ranging Implementation with Accelerated Acquisition for Testbeds and Next-Gen Spacecraft
authors: Eugene Grayver, Michael A. Varner, Jason Zheng
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Satellite ranging is a mature and somewhat niche field. The approach to regenerative ranging presented in this paper ignores the constrained computational resources available on satellites. The code acquisition uses ‘brute-force’ FFT-based correlation over the entire code period. This technique requires a lot of memory as well as significant compute power but allows acquisition with dramatically less dwell time. Shorter acquisition time is desirable for shared ground stations that support multiple satellites. The PN code regeneration is implemented using a tracking despreader that was originally designed for processing DSSS data links. The despreader is used in a novel configuration where the roles of the ‘input’ and ‘reference’ signals are flipped. The system is intended for simulation, test, and for ground stations and is implemented in real-time software executing on a CPU.

title: Plastic Waste Detection and Removal System in College Campus
authors: Vijay Franklin J, Thirumoorthy P, Karthickkumar N, Absar Ahamed J, Akilan C S, Kaleeshwaran K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The buildup of plastic waste on college campuses has turned into a significant environmental problem because of inadequate disposal methods and slow cleaning efforts. Manual monitoring techniques require considerable time and can't guarantee ongoing cleanliness throughout expansive campus spaces. This study presents a real-time system for detecting plastic waste and facilitating human-assisted removal utilizing the YOLOv8 deep learning model. A personalized dataset featuring various kinds of plastic waste, including bottles, lids, bags, cups, and food packaging, was developed and labeled. The trained system identifies plastic waste items from real-time camera streams and issues notifications for cleaning personnel. The cleaning staff manually removes the detected waste upon receiving alerts. The system logs detection events for monitoring and performance evaluation. Experimental findings indicate that the suggested system attains dependable detection accuracy and operates in real-time across various campus settings. The solution minimizes manual inspection work and promotes a tidier and more eco-friendly campus.

title: Reconfigurable VLSI Architectures for Resource-Constrained Edge AI
authors: Girish Gowtham A, Saravanan Subramanian
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The rapid proliferation of Internet of Things (IoT) devices has created an urgent demand for Edge AI solutions that can process high-resolution image data locally while adhering to strict power and area constraints. Traditional General Purpose Processors (GPPs) often fail to meet the energy-efficiency requirements of battery-operated edge nodes. This work presents a reconfigurable VLSI architecture optimized for real-time image transformation and feature enhancement on RGB colour data. The proposed hardware accelerator implements a unified data path capable of executing four distinct image processing modes: Grayscale conversion, Dynamic Thresholding, Pixel Inversion, and Programmable Brightness Adjustment. To achieve energy efficiency, the design utilizes Fixed-Point arithmetic and a Pipelined Data Flow, minimizing the switching activity and computational latency inherent in floating-point software implementations. A key contribution of this architecture is its reconfigurability, by utilizing a control-logic-driven multiplexing scheme, the hardware can switch between processing modes in a single clock cycle without the need for FPGA reconfiguration or software overhead. The design was modelled in Verilog HDL and verified using Icarus Verilog, demonstrating functional accuracy across various RGB colour depths. By leveraging custom VLSI design, the project aims to achieve significant reductions in energy consumption compared to traditional software-based approaches, paving the way for long-lasting battery life and wider adoption of edge <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$A I$</tex> in applications like object detection and image recognition on battery-powered devices.

title: Limb Compliance for Underactuated Propulsion in Resistive Media
authors: Subin Chae, Michael Tolley, Nick Gravish
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Soft robots often use appendages that have distributed and anisotropic compliance which allow bending motions that enable locomotion. Anisotropic compliance is especially important for robots that swim in resistive media like fluids or sand since forward motion relies on anisotropic propulsion forces. In this paper we investigate the role of limb compliance in enabling effective swimming of a twoappendage robot through resistive media. We selected joint stiffness values as limb design parameters and performed numerical optimization using granular Resistive Force Theory (gRFT) to maximize displacement per cycle while minimizing motor cost as approximated by the square of the torque. The optimization reveals that asymmetric joint stiffness and tapered configuration outperform symmetric design or asymmetric design with uniform stiffness. We fabricated the limb with 3D printed appendages: polylactic acid (PLA) links and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) joints. We characterized joint stiffness via four-point bending experiments and conducted displacement experiments in glass beads. The results verify that limb compliance affects propulsion performance and motor torque requirements. Also, the body drag and the swimming depth affect the propulsion performance. The study provides guidelines for choosing joint stiffness and robot body drag that increase propulsion while managing reasonable motor torque.

title: A Wearable Human–Machine Interface (HMI) System Based on Colocated sEMG-cFMG Sensing for Hand Gesture Recognition
authors: Rayane Tchantchane, Hao Zhou, Shen Zhang, Gregory Bowring, Melissa Leong, Gursel Alici
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: There has recently been significant research on sensors or electrodes for human-machine-interface (HMI) systems, with a primary focus on single-modality sensing. However, single-modality-based HMIs often face performance and robustness limitations in gesture recognition applications, such as controlling prosthetic devices, since a single sensing modality provides only a partial view of neuro-muscular activity and is therefore unable to distinguish between complex or subtle gesture patterns. To address this, we propose a novel lowfootprint armband-based HMI system that integrates co-located electromyography (EMG) and capacitive-based force myography (cFMG) sensors. This system employs soft textilecapacitive sensing pads as the cFMG platform, onto which EMG electrodes are mounted to enable simultaneous acquisition of both electrical and mechanical muscle signals from the same anatomical site. The armband comprises four pairs of co-located sEMG-cFMG sensors and achieves an average gesture recognition accuracy of 88.60% across six commonly performed gestures, which was higher than sEMG-only (76.40%) and cFMG-only (78.58%), using traditional machine learning techniques. The results presented demonstrate the effectiveness of the HMI system based on the co-located sEMG-cFMG sensing techniques in enhancing the performance of a gesture recognition system.

title: A Hybrid Classification and Clustering Approach for Multi-Class Student Performance Prediction and Profiling
authors: SB Pallavi, S Senthil, Priyanka Rukesh, Ayshwarya B
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Effective educational planning and immediate academic interventions in higher education are dependent upon accurate and clear analysis of the academic performance of a student. Additionally, this research offers a proper machine learning approach that prioritises systematic pre-processing, model selection, interpretability, and student profiling for individualised support. To guarantee data quality and strong model generalisation, extensive data pre-processing methods are first used, such as categorical encoding, missing value treatment, feature scaling, and class-stratified sampling. Then, using important academic and behavioural characteristics like attendance, study habits, and previous academic records, supervised classification models are used to predict multi-class academic performance. With regard to recall, accuracy, precision and F1-score, the Logistic Regression model outperformed with the 96% the ensemble-based classifiers, also achieving a good result of 92%. Permutation-based feature importance is used to find dominant factors influencing academic outcomes in order to improve interpretability. Additionally, unsupervised K-Means clustering is used to identify significant student groups, which are then verified by silhouette analysis and displayed using principal component analysis. The resulting profiles facilitate data-driven, targeted recommendations that are carried out via an interactive application built on Streamlit.

title: A Micro-Vibration-Based Spinal Health Analyzer for Wearable Biomechanical Assessment
authors: Dhavamani K, Vidhya M, Indradevi P, Gayathiri A, Deepika R, Hemaraj M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This paper presents a wearable micro-vibration-based spinal health analyzer for evaluating the mechanical stability of the spine under natural postural conditions. The proposed system employs dual accelerometer sensors positioned at the lumbar and thoracic regions to capture low-amplitude vibration signals generated during routine body movements, without external excitation or posture correction. Acquired signals are processed to extract vibration damping characteristics and frequency-domain features that reflect spinal mechanical behavior. Experimental observations indicate that prolonged static postures and fatigue are associated with reduced vibration attenuation and shifts in dominant frequency components. The proposed approach provides a passive, non-invasive, and cost-effective framework for early spinal stability assessment and long-term ergonomic monitoring.

title: Radioisotope Power Systems (RPS) Deployment and Integration Into Future Missions
authors: Young H. Lee, John Elliott, Benjamin Marti, Michael G. Fifield, Allen T. Guzik, Salvatore M. Oriti, Scott D. Wilson, Stephen G. Johnson, Steven R. Vernon, June F. Zakrajsek, Paul Schmitz
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper details a study evaluating the critical requirements for effective deployment and integration of Radioisotope Power Systems (RPS) in future space missions. It expands upon prior research by comparing various RPS technologies - including the MMRTG, Next Gen RTG, and two conceptual Stirling RPS variants - within the context of a lunar rover mission concept. The methodology is based on a three-pronged, parallel assessment, incorporating the critical interfaces and perspectives of the rover/spacecraft team, a NASA RPS Program development team, and a launch vehicle (LV) integration team. The study yields a comprehensive framework for assessing the feasibility of advanced or novel RPS implementation, complete with a detailed analysis of the resultant impacts on mission requirements and implementation across all three stakeholder perspectives.

title: Linear Approximation of Optimal Reactive Power Flow Model for Inverter-based Power Systems
authors: Junhua Fu, Hua Sun, Yuejian Wu, Xiaoming Dong, Chengfu Wang, Yong Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: To establish a low-carbon power system, high penetration of inverter-based generators (IBGs) driven by renewable energies is integrated into the grid, which presents a profound transition in optimal reactive power flow (ORPF) model. In this paper, a linear approximation method of ORPF model is proposed to consider the operation constraints of IBGs. Based on the steady-state performance of power inverters and linearized power flow equations, internal potential and output current constraints are formulated and linearized by a first-order Taylor approximation. An ORPF solving algorithm for inverter-based power system is also presented in the form of successive linear approximation steps. Case studies in a modified IEEE-39 bus system validates the performance of the proposed method.

title: Segmentation-Guided YOLO-Based Framework for Accurate Multi-Class Skin Cancer Classification with Improved Melanoma Detection
authors: P. Murari Karthikeyan, K. Latha Mallika, B. Naga Sai Vijaya Bhargav, J. Shanthini
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Skin cancer is one of the most common and potentially life-threatening forms of cancer, and early detection is essential for improving patient survival and treatment outcomes. Dermoscopic imaging is widely used by dermatologists for skin lesion examination; however, manual assessment is time-consuming and often subject to interobserver variability. Deep learning approaches have demonstrated significant potential in recent years for automated analysis of skin lesion images. This work elaborates two-stage deep learning system of skin cancer detection and lesions division, which is based on transfer learning and YOLOv11. In the first stage, a segmentation model trained on the ISIC dataset is used to localize skin lesions and accurately extract lesion boundaries. This stage enables accurate identification of clinically relevant lesion regions while minimizing background interference. The second step involves labelling the segmented lesion areas with several skin disease categories with a YOLOv11-based classification model that is trained on the HAM10000 dataset. Transfer learning is used to take advantage of already trained representations, which enhances convergence and generalisation on dermoscopic pictures. The proposed framework is evaluated using both quantitative and qualitative performance metrics. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively integrates lesion localization and classification, achieving strong performance across multiple skin lesion categories. The combination of segmentation and classification into a single structure improves the reliability of diagnostic and offers a extensible base to the enhancement of real-time clinical decision support systems in the future.

title: A Comparative Study of Deep Learning Algorithm Performance for Drowsiness Detection While Driving
authors: Dhammiko Dharmawan, Theresia Ratih Dewi Saputri
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Drowsiness while driving is a major cause of traffic accidents, often leading to fatal outcomes. This study aims to develop a real-time, accurate, and efficient drowsiness detection system based on facial expressions using deep learning models. This study compared the performance of three models, such as YOLOv8n, YOLOv11n, and Faster R-CNN. All of them were trained for 50 epochs using optimized hyperparameters on a combined dataset from UTA, SUST-DDD, and YawDD, representing both controlled and uncontrolled conditions. The performance was evaluated using precision, recall, and mean Average Precision (mAP), as well as computational efficiency via GFlops and inference speed. Results show that YOLOv8n achieved the best overall performance, with precision and recall reaching 99.9 % on the UTA and YawDD datasets, and real-time inference speeds up to 10 fps across devices. User testing revealed that the application is user-friendly and its alarm feature helps maintain driver focus, although limitations were noted in detecting flat facial expressions. Suggestions for improvement include enhancing alarm responsiveness and robustness under varied lighting. The study also observed false positives when relying solely on yawning detection, recommending the integration of features like head pose, eye movement, and body posture for a more comprehensive system.

title: An Artificial Intelligence-Based Decision Support System for Personalized Immunosuppressive Treatment in Kidney Transplant Recipients
authors: A. Syed Musthafa, Lavanya Murugan, Thangavel. S, S. Jagan, G. Jenifa, Fathima H
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Kidney transplant is a life-saving procedure in the management of end-stage renal diseases. However, graft long-term survival entirely relies on the effective management of immunosuppressive medications. Inappropriate dosing of immunosuppressive drugs may result in either severe complications of graft rejection or drug-induced toxicity. This paper proposes an AI-driven framework to analyze immunosuppressive medication patterns in kidney transplant patients using their longitudinal clinical data. For patient risk level assessment, the proposed system integrates data preprocessing, temporal feature modeling, and deep learning-based classification to support clinical decision-making in adjusting immunosuppressive medications. Clinical parameters such as laboratory test results, medication dosage, and patient demographics are leveraged to represent the dynamic response of patients over time. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model yields a classification accuracy of 96.4%, with significant reduction in misclassifying high-risk cases. Results showed that the proposed approach is effective, reliable, and suitable for supporting immunosuppressive therapy optimization in post-kidney transplant care.

title: A Teaching Assistant for Teacher-Student Learning: Knowledge Transfer from Skeleton to Inertial Sensing for Activity Recognition in Industrial Domains
authors: Hongyin Qiao, Qingxin Xia, Hamada Rizk, Takuya Maekawa
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: Human activity recognition (HAR) in industrial domains is important for workflow optimization, throughput estimation, and bottleneck detection. Skeleton-based models achieve high HAR accuracy by exploiting rich spatial and temporal cues, but they are difficult to deploy in industrial sites due to occlusions, camera placement, and privacy concerns. IMU sensors, especially smartwatches, are practical for deployment but lack spatial awareness, resulting in weaker performance. This work aims to enable robust HAR using only a wrist-worn IMU by distilling knowledge from richer modalities. Knowledge distillation allows transferring information from a skeleton teacher to a single-IMU student, but the large modality gap has limited the success of prior teacher–student approaches. To address this issue, we propose a teacher-assistant-student (TAS) learning framework, in which a multi-IMU assistant model bridges the skeleton-based teacher and the single-IMU student. To support TAS, we develop the following techniques: (i) Dense temporal Contrastive Learning, aligning structural representations of skeleton and IMU segments; (ii) Spatial Relationship Learning, guiding models to capture spatial priors from skeleton data; and (iii) Temporal Attention Transfer, distilling attention patterns for key atomic actions. We further boost the robustness to behavioral variation with motion-guided IMU data diversification using physics-based simulation. Experiments on industrial HAR sensor data show that our framework consistently improves single-IMU recognition across diverse operational scenarios, highlighting its potential for practical deployment.

title: Building Footprint Extraction from High-Resolution Satellite Imagery: Evaluating SAM and Lightweight Segmentation Models on xBD Dataset
authors: Shanna Carlynda Fernlie, Nur Afny Catur Andryani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: To this day, building footprint extraction remains a fundamental and challenging task for remote sensing disaster assessment. Lately, Segment Anything Model (SAM) emerged as a robust foundation model capable of prompt-based segmentation. This raised questions about its performance and efficiency compared to lightweight, fully automatic models in specific tasks, such as building footprints extraction. This study evaluates SAM and compares it with lightweight baselines, such as UNet with ResNet18, DeepLabv3+ with MobileNetV2, and SegFormer-B0, using the xBD dataset. Results show that lightweight models achieve stable performance with substantially fewer parameters, with IoU and Dice scores ranging from 0.539 to 0.6 and 0.686 to 0.711, respectively. Under oracle bounding box prompts derived from ground truth masks, SAM demonstrates strong segmentation performance, achieving an upper-bound IoU and Dice scores of 0.836 and 0.909 after fine-tuning only the mask decoder. This demonstrates SAM's maximum segmentation potential when ideal prompts are accessible.

title: Whole-Body Proprioceptive Morphing: A Modular Soft Gripper for Robust Cross-Scale Grasping
authors: Dong Heon Han, Xiaohao Xu, Yuxi Chen, Yusheng Zhou, Xinqi Zhang, Jiaqi Wang, Daniel Bruder, Xiaonan Huang
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Biological systems, such as the octopus, exhibit cross-scale manipulation by adaptively reconfiguring their entire form, a capability that remains elusive in robotics. Conventional soft grippers, while compliant, are mostly constrained by a fixed global morphology, and prior shape-morphing efforts have been largely confined to localized deformations, failing to replicate this biological dexterity. Inspired by this natural exemplar, we introduce the paradigm of collaborative, wholebody proprioceptive morphing, realized in a modular soft gripper architecture. Our design is a distributed network of modular self-sensing pneumatic actuators that enables the gripper to reconfigure its entire topology, achieving multiple morphing states that are controllable to form diverse polygonal shapes. By integrating rich proprioceptive feedback from embedded sensors, our system can seamlessly transition from a precise pinch to a large envelope grasp. We experimentally demonstrate that this approach expands the grasping envelope and enhances generalization across diverse object geometries (standard and irregular) and scales (up to $10 \times$), while also unlocking novel manipulation modalities such as multi-object and internal hook grasping. This work presents a low-cost, easy-to-fabricate, and scalable framework that fuses distributed actuation with integrated sensing, offering a new pathway toward achieving biological levels of dexterity in robotic manipulation. The complete control software, communication protocols, and mechanical CAD files for the gripper are available at https://github.com/ethansab-bit/Soft_Gripper.

title: Printed helicoids with embedded air channels make sensorized segments for soft continuum robots
authors: Annan Zhang, Hanna Matusik, Miguel Flores-Acton, Emily R. Sologuren, Joshua Jacob, Daniela Rus
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Soft robots enable safe, adaptive interaction with complex environments but remain difficult to sense and control due to their highly deformable structures. Architected soft materials such as helicoid lattices offer tunable stiffness and strength but are challenging to instrument because of their sparse geometry. We introduce a fabrication method for embedding air channels into helicoid-based soft continuum robots. Multi-material segments fabricated via vision-controlled jetting in a single print interface with PCBs housing miniature pressure sensors and IMUs for distributed deformation sensing. We characterize the mechanical properties of four helicoid designs and validate the sensor response to fundamental deformation modes. To demonstrate the platform’s scalability, we construct and mechanically evaluate a meter-scale, 14-DoF cable-driven soft arm capable of open-loop trajectory tracking and object grasping, with tactile-based stiffness detection demonstrated using the gripper sensors. This approach establishes a scalable fabrication strategy for sensorized architected materials in large-scale soft robotic systems.

title: AutoScaleDL: An Automated Framework for Scalable Distributed Training in Deep Learning Systems
authors: Anurag Bhargav Gandrakota
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The growing computational demands of modern deep learning models have made distributed training a crucial component of scalable AI systems. This study introduces AutoScaleDL, an automated framework designed to transform and optimize deep learning code for efficient parallel execution across multiple processing units. The system analyzes neural network computation graphs and automatically restructures training workflows to exploit hardware concurrency, communication reduction, and synchronization balance. Implemented within a TensorFlow-based environment, the framework integrates automated graph partitioning, dynamic resource allocation, and adaptive learning synchronization to minimize developer intervention while preserving model accuracy. Experimental evaluations on large-scale neural architectures demonstrate substantial reductions in training time and communication overhead compared to conventional distributed setups. The results highlight the effectiveness of automation in deep learning infrastructure, emphasizing the role of intelligent compilation and code transformation in achieving scalable and efficient training across heterogeneous hardware clusters.

title: A New Protection and Operational Boundary for AC Transmission Lines in the New Type Power System
authors: Yarong Guo, Weilin Hou, Tiezhu Wang, Shicong Ma
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Current differential protection, widely used in power systems, identifies faults based on the characteristics of fault currents. However, in new-type power systems, the control characteristics of power electronic devices pose challenges to the sensitivity and reliability of this protection. This paper proposes a new line polarization current protection principle based on the equivalent parameters of line-to-ground admittance (LGA), and analyzes the operational characteristics of the susceptance-based amplitude criterion and the conductance-based phase criterion for polarization current protection under different fault types. Under the premise of considering the reliability margin of the protection, it quantitatively analyses the operational boundary of polarization current protection. Through simulations in different scenarios, the sensitivity and reliability of the proposed polarization current protection are verified.

title: Exploratory Design of an Extended Reality UAV Path Planning Tool
authors: Luljeta Sinani, Frank Steinicke, Andreas Gerndt, Georgia Albuquerque
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Extended Reality (XR) technologies have shown promise in supporting situation awareness in Uncrewed Aerial Vehicle (UAV) operations, yet UAV mission planning remains largely 2D and desktop-based. In this paper, we explore how XR can support UAV path planning. To this end, we developed an early-stage prototype that allows users to place, grab, and move waypoints in a geo-referenced 3D terrain model using gesture-based input, while visualizing the resulting flight path in real time. To ensure alignment with operator workflows, we adopt a Human-Centered Design (HCD) approach by involving expert users early in the design process. We gather the first impressions from expert users through think-aloud sessions. Drawing on their feedback, we identify key design considerations for XR-based path planning tools, including support for spatial understanding, view switching (e.g., between 2D and 3D immersive views), responsive interaction, and system guidance. Synthesized into generalized design guidelines, these findings aim to inform not only the iterative development of our own prototype but also to serve as a reference for other researchers and developers designing XR-based mission planning tools.

title: Vision-Language Model Informed Relative Navigation for GPS-Denied Environments
authors: Alexander Pasqualina, Alison Peckham, Caleb Craddock, Sneha Laxminarayan, Katelyn Winkleblech, Sandip Sharan Senthil Kumar, Harshang Chhaya, Jared Paquet, J. Humberto Ramos, Michael Anderson
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper presents a relative localization framework for a group of autonomous agents operating without Global Positioning System (GPS) measurements while guided by a Vision-Language Model (VLM). In this framework, one of the agents maintains access to global pose (position and orientation) information via a Magneticbased Navigation system or MagNav, while all other GPS-denied agents utilize relative pose measurements to the MagNav agent for global localization. The guidance of the GPS-denied agents comes from the VLM, enabling the framework to support highlevel mission planning and object recognition tasks for guidance adaptation. The specific VLM initialization allows for flexible, highlevel prompts such as “Detect dangerous objects, and get closer to them,” “Look for strange or out-of-place items,” or “This is a reconnaissance mission; search for relevant features.” In combination with the MagNav-enabled (or a GPS-enabled) agent, points of interest that are recognized by GPS-deficient teammates can be globally localized and navigated to. Simulation and hardware experiments were performed to demonstrate this approach, in which we show that even in GPS-denied settings (and with their inherent positioning uncertainties), autonomous agents can benefit from the semantic reasoning capabilities provided by the VLM to adapt their guidance and decision-making according to the context.

title: The S3LI Vulcano Dataset: A Dataset for Multi-Modal SLAM in Unstructured Planetary Environments
authors: Riccardo Giubilato, Marcus Gerhard Müller, Marco Sewtz, Laura Alejandra Encinar Gonzalez, John Folkesson, Rudolph Triebel
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: We release the S3LI Vulcano dataset, a multi-modal dataset towards development and benchmarking of Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) and place recognition algorithms that rely on visual and LiDAR modalities. Several sequences are recorded on the volcanic island of Vulcano, from the Aeolian Islands in Sicily, Italy. The sequences provide users with data from a variety of environments, textures and terrains, including basaltic or iron-rich rocks, geological formations from old lava channels, as well as dry vegetation and water. The data (rmc.dlr.de/s3li_dataset) is accompanied by an open source toolkit (github.com/DLR-RM/s3li-toolkit) providing tools for generating ground truth poses as well as preparation of labelled samples for place recognition tasks.

title: Common Ground: Standardizing Granular Media Characteristics for Burrowing Robots
authors: Caitlin L. Le, Filippo Fonseca Cagnazzo, Nico Ramos, Esteban I. Figueroa, Colin Creager, Rebecca Kramer-Bottiglio
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Bioinspired soft robotic burrowers are thought to potentially be advantageous in overcoming the constantly changing resistive forces of granular environments. These robotic burrowers have been tested in different granular media, like glass beads and plastic grains, but key properties that describe the media are underreported. With the number of soft burrowers rising, we propose standardized metrics that could be reported to characterize granular media testbeds. In geotechnical engineering, the standard cone penetrometer test (CPT) is used to quantify the penetration resistance in soil, which we use as a benchmark for characterizing testing environments. We perform CPT experiments in dry sand and plastic pellets, comparing derived CPT parameters and analyzing how the different properties of each medium affect CPT results. Physical properties of the medium, alongside CPT parameters, should be reported to enable consistent comparison across different testing conditions and robotic platforms. Understanding how soil properties affect soil state—and thus the required burrowing forces—can help identify and evaluate suitable burrowing methods employed by soft robots for specific granular environments.

title: High-Power Radio Frequency Anti-Satellite (ASAT) Weapons - Technical Aspects of an Emerging Threat
authors: Grzegorz Lubkowski, Michael Suhrke, Marian Lanzrath, Martin Schwarz
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper examines the emerging threat posed by HighPower Radio Frequency (HPRF) weapons to satellite systems. HPRF can induce failures in satellite electronics through intense electromagnetic pulses, offering scalable effects without generating orbital debris. The study reviews feasible HPRF source technologies and introduces a methodology to assess their impact on satellites by linking source parameters with achievable field strengths at tactically relevant ranges. Ground-based, airborne, and space-based deployment concepts are analyzed with respect to propagation limits, power constraints, and platform characteristics. The vulnerability of satellite components is considered via front-door and back-door coupling, forming the basis for estimating operational ranges of experimental HPRF sources. The results highlight that realistic HPRF exposures can exceed current space qualification standards by orders of magnitude, underscoring the need for improved electromagnetic protection in satellite design.

title: AI-Powered Agriculture Rover for Fertilizer Optimization
authors: P. Leninpugalhanthi, K. Nathish, S. Naveen Kumar, A. U. Sudharsan
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Efficient nutrient management is a cornerstone of contemporary precision agriculture, enabling farmers to maximize crop productivity while curbing input costs and mitigating adverse environmental consequences. This paper presents the design, simulation, and field evaluation of an intelligent fertilizer recommendation and autonomous dispensing system driven by deep learning. Rather than relying on conventional chemical assays or costly in situ sensors, the proposed framework employs a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that derives soil nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), and potassium (K) levels directly from RGB soil images. Predicted nutrient concentrations are cross referenced with a crop specific nutrient requirement database to quantify the deficit for each field zone. An autonomous ground rover equipped with a proportional pump valve dispensing unit traverses the field in a systematic row wise pattern guided solely by onboard camera-based path planning eliminating the need for GPS infrastructure. Real time telemetry, coverage mapping, and application logs are streamed to an IoT monitoring dashboard. Field trials on a banana plantation with black loam soil demonstrated an average nutrient prediction error of only 4.38%, and rover guided fertilization raised crop yield by 26.2% compared with conventional manual methods. The simulation first, field validated approach supports scalable deployment of precision farming with minimal capital investment.

title: Constant-Envelope Multicarrier Modulations vs. Multicarrier Modulations in Non-Terrestrial Network Scenarios
authors: Claudio Sacchi, Alberto Tarable, Giuseppe Virone, Simone Morosi, Consorzio Nazionale
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Some recent work highlighted the potential advantages of constant-envelope (CE) multicarrier modulations (CE-OFDM and CE-SC-OFDM) in various wireless scenarios. The strengths of CEmulticarrier modulations have been recognized in terms of nonlinear distortion immunity and improved multipath resilience owing to the augmented diversity against frequency selectivity. As far as the trade-offs are concerned, they should point out: i) the increased bandwidth occupation of the RF signal (at least twice than that of the conventional multicarrier signals), ii) the increased computational complexity due to oversampling, iii) the nonlinear effects involved by arctangent demodulation of the received phase-modulated signal, and, finally, iv) the possibility of exploiting orthogonal multicarrier multiple access only in the downlink. During the activities of the Integrated Terrestrial-Non-Terrestrial Network (ITA-NTN) project, funded by the EU in the framework of NextGenerationEU workprogramme, a wide range of waveforms have been considered for NTN scenarios, including CE multicarrier ones. This paper aims to analyze in detail this typology of waveforms and to contextualize them in NTN application scenarios of technical interest. The performance of CE-multicarrier waveforms will be carefully assessed across different NTN channels (with and without LOS), revealing the influence of parameterization (modulation index and oversampling) on the link performance of these nonconventional PHY-layer formats. The comparison will be conducted with conventional multicarrier modulations (OFDM, SC-OFDM) in a realistic NTN transmission scenario, characterized by nonlinear amplification and phase noise. Some guidelines about the employment of CE multicarrier waveforms in future NTN applications will conclude the paper.

title: Dynamic Characteristics and Enhanced Renewable Energy Control For Large-Scale Hydro–PV And Grid-Forming VSC-HVDC Sending System Following DC Pole-Blocking Fault
authors: Baorong Zhou, Ziqian Yang, Ye Zhang, Wangqianyun Tang, Hancheng Gong, Lin Guan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the accelerated construction of large-scale photovoltaic (PV) bases and hydro stations in Southwest China, the generated power is primarily transmitted via voltage source converter based high voltage direct current (VSC-HVDC). Consequently, the dynamic characteristics of the sending-end system are governed by the control behaviors of power electronic converters. When a monopolar blocking fault occurs in VSC-HVDC, the sending end experiences a significant power surplus, leading to severe transient overvoltage. This paper first reveals the transient voltage characteristics of a hydro-PV base transmitted by grid-forming VSC-HVDC system under DC monopolar blocking fault through electromagnetic transient simulations. Subsequently, the generation mechanism of the voltage of the PV station is analyzed and discussed. Based on this, an improved transient active power control strategy for renewable energy is proposed. This strategy effectively suppresses transient overvoltage during DC blocking faults and significantly reduces the capacity requirements for AC energy dissipation resistors. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed control method is verified by using PSCAD/EMTDC.

title: O-DINO: An Optimized DINO for Accurate Oriented Object Detection in Remote Sensing Imagery
authors: Fei Liu, Kan Zhou, Zheng Sun, Shanshan Ding, Junyi Zhang, Fei Guo, Renwen Chen
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: With the emergence of DINO, Transformer-based detection architectures have achieved significant success across various vision tasks. However, despite DINO’s potential performance advantages, its application in oriented object detection remains insufficiently explored, particularly in dense scenarios where current Transformer-based methods underperform compared to CNN detectors.We identify two primary bottlenecks: the original attention mechanism exhibits misalignment with oriented objects, and DINO’s noisy query generation scheme is not well-adapted to oriented object distributions. Hence, we propose O-DINO, an optimized DINO-based oriented object detector. Specifically, we introduce an Oriented Deformable Attention (ODAttn) module to align features with arbitrary orientations, and a Gaussian Distribution Sampling based Noisy Query Generation (GSNQG) method to better adapt the training process to oriented object geometries. Extensive experiments on three challenging remote sensing imagery datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of O-DINO, achieving state-of-the-art performance on DOTA, DIOR-R, and HRSC benchmarks. Our code will be released at https://github.com/liufeinuaa/rsisdet.git.
