title: A Behavioral Drift-Aware Feature Extraction Method for Transaction Fraud Detection
authors: Lifei Wei, Wenliang Yang, Yu Xie, Jiamin Yao, Junkai Shan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Computational Social Systems
abstract: Transaction fraud detection (TFD) remains a substantial challenge in the digital economy. Feature extraction is crucial in TFD, as relying solely on raw transactional features fails to capture the complex and dynamic nature of fraudulent behavior. Existing methods primarily rely on manually aggregated features, failing to account for the evolving nature of fraud patterns. These limitations undermine the accuracy and adaptability of TFD systems. In this work, we propose a behavioral drift-aware feature extraction (BDFE) method designed to capture evolving fraud patterns by modeling behavioral drift and extracting new feature representations for TFD. BDFE consists of four synergistic modules. The first module is a feature extractor that encodes user transactional behaviors into new representations. The second and third modules are a historical behavior-aware classifier and a current behavior-aware classifier, which jointly guide the extractor to learn more discriminative features by capturing patterns from historical and current transactions, respectively. The fourth module is a behavior discriminator that distinguishes between historical and current behaviors. Through adversarial training, the discriminator encourages the feature extractor to extract unified representations that are invariant to behavioral shifts. This design enables BDFE to learn drift-resilient features, thus enhancing the detection of evolving fraud patterns. Extensive experiments are conducted on real-world transaction datasets and public ones. The results show that BDFE achieves improvements in average precision from 2.32% to 12.17%, in recall from 3.65% to 14.57%, in F1-score from 1.80% to 11.79%, and in G-mean from 2.05% to 8.17% over its peers. These gains demonstrate its superior feature extraction capability and its effectiveness in enhancing TFD performance.

title: Automated Brain Tumor Classification Using Deep Learning and MRI Image Processing
authors: A.M. Elsayad, M. I. Al-Wizrah, A. A. Al-Dosari, A. A. Al-Besher, S. F. Al-Suwais, Z. D. Al-Shehri
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is an automated procedure to diagnose brain tumors, and this represents a significant breakthrough in neuro-oncology. However, there are two basic challenges to the clinical realization of Deep Learning (DL)-based models: the opaque properties of black-box methods, as well as the vulnerability to poor sub-optima, or adversely biased real-life data. The current study suggests a robust, multi-classification framework which is reliable in the manner that it differentiates glioma, meningioma, pituitary tumors and normal tissue. A stringent comparative analysis of state-of-the-art Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models, i.e. VGG19, DenseNet and Inception-V3 have been performed based not only on the standardized data sets but also on a novel conceptual framework called Challenge Test which aims at simulating what would be expected in real imaging artefacts i.e. noisy data and blur estimation. Also, the research compares the effectiveness of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to the traditional data-augmentation protocols in the framework of medical imaging. To reduce the lack of trust between the Artificial Intelligence systems and the clinicians, the study implements a large range of Explainable AI (XAI) procedures, such as Grad-CAM, LIME, and Occlusion Sensitivity. This exploration will provide an effective roadmap to the construction of Trustworthy AI systems as transparent and reliable decision-support partners in essential diagnostic processes by balancing model robustness and interpretability in emergent strategies that apply to both model robustness and interpretability concerns.

title: Learning-based State and Contact Estimation for Tendon-Driven Soft Robot Fingers using Multimodal Sensing
authors: Cornelia Bauer, Dominik Bauer, Nancy S. Pollard
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Soft robotic hands offer compliant and robust interaction but remain limited by the lack of reliable proprioceptive feedback for closed-loop control. This paper presents a sensing and learning approach for proprioception and contactforce estimation in tendon-driven soft fingers that uses multimodal sensing and requires no embedded strain sensors or external visual tracking. Our method combines actuation-side sensing of motor current, tendon displacement, and tendon forces via a load cell, with fingertip sensing using a barometric pressure sensor and a six-axis inertial measurement unit (IMU). We train lightweight temporal models on this multimodal sensor data to predict joint angles, contact forces, and contact locations in real-time at $\mathbf{1 0 0 ~ H z}$. Through systematic ablation studies, we quantify the contribution of each sensing modality and demonstrate that tendonforce sensing via a load cell can be sufficient for obtaining accurate state estimation (<4 % mean absolute error (MAE) in joint angles, <0.23 N contact-force MAE, and $\geq 92 \%$ contactlocation accuracy). While fingertip sensing can further improve accuracy, we show that using just load cell sensing provides robust zero-shot performance when transferred to a two-finger gripper.

title: Neuro-Symbolic Planning for Autonomous Self-healing in Large-Scale Container Orchestration Systems
authors: Sarath Chandra Veerla, R. Krishnamoorthy, R. Naveenkumar, V.P Gladis Pushparathi, Padmavati Yale, Shrikant Upadhyay
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: As modern containerized applications scale across dynamic and distributed environments, ensuring high availability and reliability becomes increasingly complex. The recovery mechanisms that are typically implemented in orchestration platforms such as Docker Swarm or Kubernetes tend to be rule-based, meaning that these systems are not always able to manage unexpected or compound failures. This paper introduces a new framework of Neuro-Symbolic Planning that allows autonomous self-healing of large-scale systems of container orchestration by fusing deep neural networks with symbolic planning logic. The framework consists of a Transformer-based anomaly detector which is trained on time-series telemetry and a symbolic planner which uses PDDL to produce recovery plans using the encoded operational knowledge. When abnormal behavior is detected, the system analyzes the fault context and triggers the most optimal healing plan and minimizes the Mean Time to Recovery (MTTR) and service disruption. The suggested system was tested in an injected fault Docker Swarm testbed, which consisted of 10 distributed nodes that execute heterogeneous microservices. It had a fault detection rate of 97.33, plan execution success rate of 96.7 and average MTTR of 22.3, which was much higher than rule-based and neuralonly recovery baselines. It also had under 10 % overhead in system resources guaranteeing viability in production. It was the neuro-symbolic integration that facilitated interpretable decisions, dynamic adaptation, and strong recovery in varied fault situations. This study shows how hybrid AI systems can be used to improve the resiliency of operations in cloud-native infrastructure.

title: AR-Based Smart Crop Advisory System Using CNN and Geolocation Algorithms
authors: Balasankaran B, Rithick Krishnaraj, Yashwanth Kumar S, Balakrishnan D, K. Sakthivel, Krishnaprakasan K R
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Modern technology nowadays uses satellites to detect climate change and many modern technologies like augmented reality and artificial intelligence are used to access and process any data. While these technologies are present farmers cannot utilize these technologies as they are illiterate and not very well versed. The motive of this application is to ensure farmers get constant updates on their land and a virtual AI assistant to utilize all features present on their land. This application accesses the camera of the mobile device to capture the data of the crop in real-time and returns the advisory content in a matter of a few seconds. Our project utilizes the CNN algorithm for faster, more accurate results and more efficient processing. We also use Geolocation to accurately pinpoint the location of the farmland, and we detect the mineral percentage and the humidity of the area for better efficiency of our methodologies.

title: Zone-Aware Speed Control System Using Gps for Electric Vehicles
authors: Senthil Kumar T, Dharshana P K, Dharshna R, Elakkiyaa M, Gobika S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: As the number of vehicles increases more and more along with urban traffic getting denser, there is, hence, a higher risk of road accidents, particularly in sensitive areas such as school and hospital zones or places where there are specific speed limits. Therefore, our initiative unveils a GPSbased smart vehicle speed control system, which changes the vehicle speeds automatically by the local area. The system first receives key vehicle position data (latitude and longitude) through a GPS module, then it checks the vehicle's environment to see if there are any restriction zones, and if there are, it commands the vehicle to lower the speed. Hence, the novel system is an innovative one that drastically reduces careless violations and thus enforces regulations even without any manual command being given and so, safeguards the students, pedestrians, and patients. Besides limiting speeds, the system integrates both environmental and situational changes so that the overall safety on the road can be improved. An example is that, in hospital zones, the system automatically brings down the level of a car horn so that it won't bother patients and, on the other hand, the vehicle's headlight level is automatically adjusted during nighttime. The system, which is equipped with sensors for detecting oncoming vehicles, changes the intensity of the headlights to low at the moment of the first glare and goes back to full brightness when no vehicles are detected nearby. The suggested framework is a GPS embedded vehicle control and sensor-based automation system for smart and responsive vehicle operation. The initiative may act as a reference design for forthcoming smart vehicle systems, paving the way for a mix of vehicular automation and safety in urban traffic management.

title: A Comprehensive Machine Learning Framework for Stroke Risk Prediction
authors: Syed Farrukh Amin, Mohammed Sufiyan Ilyas
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This study introduces a robust machine learning framework that aims at enhancing stroke risk prediction and supporting global health objectives to reduce stroke-related morbidity and mortality. Utilizing the Kaggle Stroke Prediction Dataset, the research integrates advanced data preprocessing, exploratory data analysis, and the application of five machine learning algorithms: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, XGBoost, and LightGBM. A stacked ensemble model forms the methodological core, achieving superior predictive performance (accuracy 97%, F1-score 0.97, AUC-ROC 0.99) compared to individual models and prior works. Bayesian Optimization ensured optimal hyperparameter selection, while explainability methods such as SHAP and LIME bolstered model interpretability to meet clinical transparency demands. The methodology aligns with the NZ Ngā Tikanga Paihere Framework, embedding principles of data privacy, cultural sensitivity, and fairness. Addressing challenges like data imbalance and computational scalability via SMOTE and distributed computing, the model demonstrated robust performance validated through bootstrapping and crossvalidation. This research advances ethical, accurate, and actionable AI-driven stroke prediction for healthcare applications.

title: An Enhanced Short-Circuit Calculation Approach for Multigrounded Distribution Networks
authors: Ghanshyam Meena, Akhilesh Mathur, Vinay Pratap Singh
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Short-circuit analysis (SCA) is a significant technique for evaluating the functioning of electrical networks under fault circumstances. It allows for precise calculation of fault currents within the entire network, which is critical for calculating the short-term thermal and mechanical characteristics of system parts as well as the right design as well as coordination of protective devices. In this paper, a new method for short-circuit analysis of an unbalanced three-phase four-wire multigrounded distribution system is proposed. The short-circuit analysis method is based on [<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{Y}_{\text {bus }}$</tex>] matrix. The proposed short-circuit method has been tested on a modified unbalanced three-phase fourwire multigrounded IEEE 34-bus test system. To validate the effectiveness of proposed methods, the results of modified IEEE 34-bus test system have been compared with [BIBC] (Bus injection to branch current) base method and the results obtained by PSCAD/EMTDC time domain simulation.

title: Bilateral Force-Feedback Control of Tendon-Driven Flexible Manipulators with Tension Sensors: Minimal-Configuration Evaluation with Internal-Force Compensation
authors: Shota Sasaki, Hisashi Date
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This study proposes a compact force-feedback bilateral teleoperation system for a tendon-driven flexible manipulator using only base-mounted tension sensors and motor angles. A reference tension LUT is obtained in free space, and an external-force component is estimated by subtracting it from the measured tension. Slack-side feedforward (FF) compensation is further introduced to reduce antagonistic effects. Experiments confirm that the follower tracks the leader shape under non-contact conditions, while FF reduces tracking error and residual force components. During obstacle interaction, the extracted force varies consistently with contact, enabling haptic presentation with delays of several tens of milliseconds. Contact timing was stably detected across 405 trials, and relative elasticity differences were distinguishable for low-stiffness materials, whereas high-stiffness targets were dominated by manipulator’s compliance. These results demonstrate a lightweight baseline approach for basic contact detection and force feedback, while highlighting limitations due to quasi-static assumptions and limited effective stiffness.

title: A Lightweight MobileVit Framework for Realtime Plant Leaf Disease Classification in Precision Agriculture
authors: Ezhilarasi M, Mohana Arasi M, Govardhini L, Gokul M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Plant leaf disease has a significant impact on agricultural productivity and global food security; therefore, early identification can lead to improved agricultural practices and global food security. Conventional methods of manually inspecting plants for disease are often subjective (relying on visual identification) and too slow to cope adequately with the scale of modern-day agriculture. As a result, there is a need for a lightweight, efficient real-time hybrid deep learning-based framework to identify plant leaf diseases within precision agriculture. This research assesses a MobileViT-S framework that fuses convolutional and transformer self-attention layers to classify plant leaf diseases. This lightweight architecture achieves a good trade-off between classification accuracy and computation speed. The MobileViT-S framework was trained on and evaluated against the publicly accessible PlantVillage image collection of 20,638 pictures of healthy and diseased leaves distributed among 15 classes. The images were processed using standard techniques (e.g., resizing, normalizing, augmenting) to improve generalizability. The model's hyperparameters (i.e., the Adam optimizer with categorical cross-entropy loss function) were optimized based on three performance metrics (i.e., classification accuracy, precision, recall, and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{F 1}$</tex>-score) across all categories. Experimental results showed a classification accuracy of 99.76 %, as well as very high precision/recall/F1-scores across all categories. The lightweight MobileViT-S architecture enables fast inference and is suitable for low-resource environments. The proposed framework provides a scalable and efficient means of diagnosing plant diseases via the use of artificial intelligence in precision agriculture.

title: Optimization Control Strategy for Power Sharing of PV-VSG Grid-Connected Optimizer
authors: Mingli Zheng, Xinyang Su, Fujun Zheng, Junwei Chai, Zhao Xu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: As photovoltaic penetration increases, the integration of PV systems into the grid poses challenges related to inertia reduction and power sharing among distributed DC modules. This paper proposes an optimization control strategy for power sharing in a PV-VSG grid-connected optimizer system. The proposed control combines fixed power point tracking with an equal voltage-based power sharing method to ensure consistent output voltages across PV modules during both steady-state and transient conditions. This approach mitigates circulating currents and reduces power losses compared to conventional equal power sharing methods. Additionally, the system adopts virtual synchronous generator control on the AC side to provide frequency support without the need for energy storage. The effectiveness of the proposed strategy is validated through hardware-in-the-loop experiments, demonstrating improved transient response, reduced thermal losses, and retained frequency support capability under varying irradiance and grid frequency disturbances.

title: Effect of Heuristic Bias on Crypto Asset Trading Decision Mediated by Risk Tolerance
authors: Bambang Leo Handoko, Arbyn Danendra, Muhammad Rizky, Ang Swat Lin Lindawati
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: The rapid growth and volatility of cryptocurrency markets create an environment where investors frequently rely on mental shortcuts, making heuristic bias a critical psychological factor influencing trading behavior. Understanding how such biases interact with individual risk tolerance is essential for explaining decision-making in crypto asset trading. This study employs a quantitative approach using primary survey data analyzed with Structural Equation ModellingPartial Least Squares (SEM-PLS) via SmartPLS 4. The results show that heuristic bias and risk tolerance significantly affect crypto asset trading decisions. Additionally, heuristic bias significantly affects risk tolerance. Further analysis confirms that risk tolerance mediates the relationship between heuristic bias and crypto asset trading decisions, indicating that investors' risk tolerance partially channels the impact of heuristicdriven judgments into their trading decisions.

title: Label-Efficient Self-Supervised Representation Learning for Chest X-Ray Pathology Classification
authors: S Deepa, G Sharmila, Sheetal, Saravanan MS Sadhana, A Alli, C Thiyagarajan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Chest X-ray evaluation is an important task in diagnosis of thoracic pathology. But the problem lies in getting a data set with labeled medical imaging for training deep learning. Self-supervised learning (SSL) has been found as a remedy to make best use of huge amount of data without labelling for pre-training; hence the feature representations learned by models are robust. This paper, examines thoroughly the implementation of SSL approach for chest X-ray pathology classification using MIMIC-CXR dataset. A contrastive learning approach (SimCLR) has been used for our pre-training, and also to compare with SSL model to a supervised baseline. The results demonstrate that SSL significantly improves diagnostic accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and AUC-ROC across 14 pathologies, highlighting its potential for medical image analysis.

title: Flexible FPGA Accelerator for Real-Time Neuromorphic Optical Flow in Space Applications
authors: Linus Silbernagel, Daniel C. Stumpp, Alan D. George
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Event-based or neuromorphic vision sensors have become popular due to their high temporal resolution, low power consumption, and high dynamic range. These properties make the sensors attractive compared to traditional cameras for many computer-vision tasks that require low latency in resource-constrained environments such as space. An important component of many computer-vision tasks is optical flow, which is the estimation of an object's perceived motion in the scene. Traditional approaches use frame-by-frame displacement of an object for estimating optical flow. However, event-based sensors allow the use of asynchronous pixel-wise event information. This new paradigm enables algorithms to take advantage of the higher temporal resolution of the sensors while avoiding the inefficiency of frame-based algorithms on spatially sparse data. These algorithms need to achieve high performance to keep up with the low-latency event streams from the sensors. Hardware such as field-programmable gate arrays (FPGAs) are commonly used as accelerator platforms because they allow for reconfigurable hardware to tailor the algorithmic implementation. Existing solutions for accelerating event-based optical flow are either too computationally expensive to run on embedded platforms or sacrifice precision in favor of enhanced speed. This research presents an FPGA-based acceleration architecture of a plane-fitting algorithm using a Savitzky-Golay filter for event-based optical-flow calculations. Unlike traditional methods that store a sensor frame, the proposed architecture uses a small history of recent events. This optimization enables the architecture to be expanded to higher-resolution sensors without the memory limitation of storing all event data on-chip. The developed architecture has multiple parameters that can be configured to allow for a tailored FPGA implementation. The architecture was tested on a standard event-based dataset and a recently released simulated space-based dataset. Our experimental results show that this design reaches real-time performance on an embedded platform and achieves a throughput of around 500 Kevts/s. This architecture demonstrates the scalability of event-based optical flow to future high-resolution sensors, delivering low-latency and precise computer-vision capabilities for embedded platforms like those used in space.

title: Learning Experience, Smart Technology, Artificial Intelligence, Robotics and Algorithms (STARA) Competency, and Work Readiness in the AI Era: The Moderating Role of Self-Efficacy
authors: Nurul Sukma Lestari, Pratiwi, Adriani Gunawan, Hana Dessy Natalina, Ika Triana
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: This study examines the influence of learning experiences and competencies in Smart Technology, Artificial Intelligence, Robotics, and Algorithms on work readiness and the moderating role of self-efficacy. The study population consisted of undergraduates who had completed lectures and practical experience. This study adopted a quantitative design using SEM-PLS, with data collected via an online questionnaire consisting of 66 items from 333 respondents. Structural Equation Modeling-Partial Least Squares (SEMPLS) was used to analyze the data. The results showed that learning experiences and STARA competencies had a positive and significant influence on job readiness. Furthermore, selfefficacy significantly moderates this relationship, strengthening the influence of learning experiences on job readiness while weakening the influence of STARA competencies. These findings highlight the complex and context-dependent role of self-efficacy in shaping job readiness in the digital era. This study enhances the literature by synthesizing experiential, technological, and psychological viewpoints into a unified explanatory framework. These findings are significant for higher education institutions as they develop curricula that integrate experiential learning, technical proficiency, and psychological empowerment. For industry and policymakers, these research findings can serve as a basis for designing human resource development programs that adapt to the demands of technology-based work. Therefore, future research is recommended to include additional psychological variables, such as career adaptability, learning motivation, or a digital mindset, and to broaden the research context to include vocational groups, entry-level workers, and cultures to gain a more comprehensive understanding of the factors influencing work readiness in the digital era.

title: Early Detection of Autism Spectrum Disorder Using AI
authors: Hassanin M. Al-Barhamtoshy, Deiaa Eldeen M. Ateia Metawe, Ahmed N. Abdou Issa
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence for early diagnosis of Autism is in the stage of fast development and very promising. It is designed to eliminate the constraints of conventional diagnosis methods that are time consuming and subjective, causing patients to wait longer for an exam. This paper is a detailed look at how AI is being used for early detection, as well as its pros and cons - and what might be next. Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder that presents with abnormal social communication and restricted/repetitive behaviors. Early detection is key to treatment but standard assessments are subjective evaluations of behavior. Eye-tracking technology provides an objective and quantitative method to study visual attention patterns that are perturbed in ASD (e.g., less gaze toward social stimuli). This paper proposes a computational framework incorporating both synthetic and real eye-tracking data for automatic diagnosis of ASD. Therefore, to formulate a reliable machine learning pipeline which can process the eye-tracking recordings to extract discriminative features and train classifiers to differentiate between individuals with ASD and autism subjects. The framework is tested on synthetic data - using this way to guarantee reproducibility- and real datasets.

title: Beyond Communication: On-Modem DNN Inference and Training for Home Appliances
authors: Insung Jung, Seulki Lee
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Communications (PerCom)
abstract: We introduce on-modem learning, which enables a commodity communication modem installed on commercial home appliances to perform inference and training of compact-sized DNNs (deep neural networks) to provide custom intelligent services. Given that 80-90% of commercial smart home appliances are never connected to the network, leaving computing resources of the modem idle, the proposed on-modem learning transforms a learning-incapable modem into an independent self-learning module by utilizing the unused or under-utilized modem resources. To this end, we propose an on-modem DNN inference framework consisting of Data Payload Parser and On-Modem DNN engine. We also propose an on-modem DNN training framework, which enables resource-efficient training of DNNs on the modem via Greedy and Diverse Gradient Accumulation (GDGA), Dynamic Programming-based Back-Propagation (DPBP), and On-Modem Reinforcement Learning (OMRL). To the best of our knowledge, the proposed on-modem learning is the first to execute DNN inference and training on the modem of commercial home appliances. To validate the efficacy of on-modem learning, we implement two on-modem learning applications on the real LG Electronics washing machine, i.e., (1) remaining wash time prediction and (2) UE (unbalance error) forecasting. Our experiment shows that on-modem learning successfully conducts DNN inference and training on the modem of commercial home appliances and provides custom intelligent services to end users.

title: Quantifying Risk Propagation in Agentic AI Networks: A Simulation Framework and Singularity Index Approach
authors: Syed Muhammad Naeem, Syed Hussain Mehdi Raza, Abbas Shabbir, Syed Umarullah Hussaini
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: As agentic AI systems gain autonomy and self-optimization capabilities; they increasingly operate within complex feedback loops that can lead to unexpected emergent behaviors. Small decision errors or misaligned objectives in one agent can cascade and amplify across interconnected subsystems, pushing the overall system toward instability; a phenomenon we call risk propagation near singularity points. In this paper, we present a quantitative framework for modeling and analyzing such dynamics using a simplified multi-agent control environment. Each agent optimizes a local objective under shared global constraints, while a feedback term governs how decisions influence others. Through controlled simulations, we demonstrate how minor perturbations in agent policies can trigger nonlinear escalation of collective risk, measurable via a newly proposed Singularity Index (SI). The results provide insights into designing safety buffers, coordination mechanisms, and trust boundaries for agentic AI systems, particularly as they approach high-autonomy, high-coupling regimes reminiscent of singularity scenarios.

title: A Multi-Objective Unit Commitment Model for Enhancing Operational Flexibility in Power Systems
authors: Mengjie Teng, Yuhong Zhao, Chen Chen, Zehui Yan
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: High penetration of renewable energy sources, such as wind and solar energy, introduces significant uncertainty in power systems and increases the flexibility requirements of the system. To address this issue, this paper proposes a flexible multi-objective unit commitment (FMOUC) optimization model of the power system that simultaneously considers operating cost, renewable energy curtailment, and the flexibility of thermal power units. The augmented ε-constraint method is employed to solve the FMOUC optimization model and obtain the Pareto frontier. Then, the entropy weight double-base-point method is used to determine the most suitable unit commitment strategy. Numerical experiments on a modified 39-bus system demonstrate that the proposed model can effectively improve renewable energy accommodation while reducing operating costs.

title: Probabilistic Connectivity Analysis of Recursive Satellite Release for Formation Initialization
authors: Hideki Yoshikado, Yuta Takahashi
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: In the initial deployment of large-scale distributed space systems using small satellites, achieving a reliable transition to passively stable orbits while maintaining inter-satellite distances within effective control and communication ranges is crucial, particularly given the presence of deployment errors and uncontrolled coasting phases. This study presents a framework for designing formation initialization that provides probabilistic safety guarantees. The scope covers the initial deployment phase-from sequential release by a single carrier to commissioning, control activation, and transition to passive stabilization. Strict separation limits during initialization necessitate low release velocities to minimize relative drift before control activation. However, in the low-velocity regime, the allowable tolerances for release velocity and angular rate errors tighten significantly to satisfy distance constraints, making hardware requirements a critical bottleneck. To address this, we model the initialization sequence as a stochastic process and derive closedform constraints on deployment errors and control activation intervals. These conditions ensure that inter-satellite distances remain within the allowable separation limit with a prescribed probability. Monte Carlo simulations, configured using the error bounds and intervals derived from the proposed constraints, demonstrate that intersatellite distances are successfully maintained within the allowable range. The proposed framework enables the safe initialization of large-scale distributed space systems by translating strict separation constraints into quantifiable hardware requirements.

title: Personality-Driven Consequence Reasoning (PDCR): A Five-Layer Deliberative Architecture for Consequence-Aware Household Robotics
authors: Ayyanar Jeyakrishnan, Bhuvaneswari Subramani, Vivek Raja P S, Aadhityaa S B
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Carrying out multi-step tasks in unstructured environments has been significantly improved with recent Vision-Language-Action (VLA) architectures-based models for household robots. Existing systems still tend to be primarily reactive, maximizing short-horizon task success but without directly reasoning about downstream safety, social or user-dependent consequences. This limitation, which we call consequence blindness, induces a gap between task competence and real-world acceptability in human-centric environments. We propose the Personality-Driven Consequence Reasoning (PDCR), a deliberative framework where multi-dimensional consequence evaluation is directly integrated into the robot decision loop. We propose to split control in two: a high-speed reactive path and a consequence-aware evolution module at the high level to evaluate candidate trajectories according to physical risk, safety, social acceptability and task-functional dimensions. Additionally, this framework integrates an uncertainty-aware user model to update soft behavioral preferences while maintaining fixed hard safety constraints. We instantiate the proposed architecture as a modular embodied stack and evaluate it on simulated, immersive household tasks and safety-critical planning. The results obtained after using the PDCR algorithm show that the actual state of the system is maintained along with the uncertainty estimation on personality probabilities are minimized as compared to existing reactive systems, which makes it executed in a safe environment.

title: Application of Machine Learning in the Predictive Maintenance of Distribution Substations Aimed at Reducing Failures
authors: Mauro Daniel Freire Cueva, Fernando Suarez Vila, Jhosselin Madahi Flores Camayo
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Reliable operation of distribution substations is critical to ensuring continuity of power supply in industrial and residential sectors. This article presents a predictive maintenance framework based on machine learning (ML) aimed at reducing unscheduled failures and optimizing asset management in distribution substations. The methodology integrates Nigel Cross' design model and the Quality Function Deployment (QFD) tool to translate operational needs into technical specifications, supported by SCADA and IoT platforms. Supervised (Random Forest, XGBoost, CNN) and unsupervised (K-Means, autoencoders) models were trained with historical and online monitoring data, considering dissolved gas analysis, temperature, humidity, vibration and thermographic images. The results show that CNN and XGBoost achieved the best performances, exceeding 95 % accuracy and an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F 1$</tex>-score of 0.95, while the Weibull-ML hybrid model estimated the residual useful life of transformers with a mean absolute error of 6.8 %. The implementation of the scheme improved reliability indices (SAIDI by 18.4 % and SAIFI by 20.9 %), reduced corrective costs by 20 %, and extended transformer useful life by 15 %. It is concluded that the combination of robust predictive modeling, digital infrastructure, and explainable artificial intelligence techniques constitutes a replicable and scalable approach for intelligent substation management, in compliance with IEEE/IEC international standards.

title: Developing a Computer-Aided Diagnosis System for Breast Cancer Detection
authors: Abdulaziz M. Alomran, Eyad T. Attar, Ahmad A. Jiman
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) is utilized for the detection and diagnosis of abnormal tumors, including breast cancer, a significant health concern for women, particularly in developing countries like Saudi Arabia. With breast cancer ranking as the second most prevalent cancer globally and the most common in Saudi Arabia, early detection through mammography is critical for effective treatment. The Breast Imaging-Reporting and Data System (BI-RADS) classifies breast cancer stages to inform treatment plans. Researchers are focused on developing CAD systems employing deep learning and conventional machine learning for enhanced mammogram analysis. Despite the high accuracy of conventional methods, challenges remain in handling large datasets. This research aims to develop a CAD system that enhances breast cancer detection through mammogram images. This research utilized a dataset of breast cancer cases from King Abdulaziz University's Sheikh Mohammed Hussein Al-Amoudi Center of Excellence. The dataset was categorized using the BIRADS system and was used to train, validate, and test a classification network for various breast cancer images, incorporating 248 randomly selected images. The analysis process included image preprocessing, where RGB images were converted to grayscale, noise was reduced using Gaussian filters, and contrast was enhanced. Otsu's threshold method facilitated image segmentation, with morphological operators applied to refine the region of interest. For classification, texture and shape features were extracted, analyzed, and selected using ANOVA1-test. The classification was conducted using Artificial Neural Networks (ANN). This developed CAD system utilizing an ANN classifier to assist doctors in detecting breast cancer, achieving 96.0% accuracy.

title: Replacing Subspace Tracking Methods With Dynamic Mode Decomposition
authors: Efrain H. Gonzalez, Carole R. Hall, Cove J. Kramer, Curtis K. Madsen, Craig M. Vineyard, Jason Adams
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Dynamic mode decomposition (DMD) is a physically interpretable algorithm used for modeling systems whose state changes over time. Originally, DMD was used as a way of extracting dynamic modes from fluid flow data, but it has since seen successes in the fields of neuroscience, epidemiology, finance, and video processing. A key problem in the video processing domain is the ability to separate the background and foreground in a scene. Several algorithms, ranging from the supervised to the unsupervised, have been developed to tackle this problem. Among them are a few streaming DMD methods which have been created because of DMD's ability to extract spatio-temporal modes and its computational efficiency. Several subspace tracking methods have also been developed to address the background modeling problem. Subspace tracking methods are often associated with principal component analysis (PCA). Previous studies have indicated that DMD methods have an advantage over PCA-based methods in several application domains due to PCA's limited ability to model temporal dynamics effectively. Our interest is to study whether there is a benefit to using streaming DMD based methods over subspace tracking methods for background modeling. Among the PCA-related algorithms that have been used for this purpose are the fast approximated power iteration (FAPI) algorithm and the fast data projection method (FDPM). FAPI and FDPM have been shown to be computationally efficient methods for developing a background model in real-time and FAPI is a part of the backbone of a method that has been popularized for background modeling called ARCS FAPI. Despite its many successes, ARCS FAPI is known to be limited in its ability to handle platform motion and clouds. In this work, we study whether streaming DMD methods hold an advantage over a version of ARCS FAPI and FDPM using two methods of comparison on simulated datasets. We test their ability to model background in real-time by comparing the absolute percent error across all video frames and then compare the ability of each algorithm to differentiate between pixels associated with the background and those associated with the targets by using an <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$F_{1}$</tex>-score. We found that both FAPI and FDPM performed similarly and were much more computationally efficient than the DMD methods. However, one of the DMD methods was shown to outperform those algorithms on a dataset with platform motion and jitter. Our results indicate that there is potential for DMD methods to be successful in remote sensing applications.

title: Security Challenges in Space-Based Delay Tolerant Networks
authors: Mohammad Abdus Salam, Robert Short
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Space-based Delay Tolerant Networks (DTNs) are crucial for communication in extreme environments such as interplanetary missions and satellite constellations. These networks face significant challenges due to long communication delays, intermittent connectivity, and changing network topologies. This paper explores key security issues in space-based DTNs, including data authentication, confidentiality, integrity, trust management, and defense against denial-of-service (DoS) attacks. Traditional authentication methods are insufficient for space-based DTNs, requiring delay-tolerant schemes to maintain data integrity over long periods. Data confidentiality is critical, as sensitive information must be protected from interception, though traditional encryption can be resource intensive. Data integrity is safeguarded through technologies like hash chains and blockchain-based verification. Trust management is difficult in intermittent communication scenarios, leading to the development of reputation-based models. DoS attacks also pose a threat to these resource-limited networks, with rate-limiting and traffic management systems proposed as countermeasures.

title: Pediatric Pneumonia Detection From Chest X-Rays Using Ensemble Deep Learning and Explainable AI
authors: Areeb Ahmed, Ankith Gowda B. S., Sneha E., Ankit Kumar, M. Farida Begam
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Pediatric pneumonia is among the top causes of children deaths across the globe, particularly in regions where radiologists are scarce. The analysis of chest radiograph images through automated machine learned algorithms has the potential to provide scalable and automated diagnoses of pneumonia. This study proposes an ensemble-based deep learning approach that leverages multiple pretrained CNN models to identify pediatric pneumonia. Different backbone architectures are explored and ensembled at the probability level to derive a robust and generalized solution. To improve interpretability, Grad-CAM is applied to highlight diagnostically relevant regions in chest X-ray images. Experimental results demonstrate excellent classification performance, highlighting the potential of the solution to allow for computer-aided detection in under-resourced environments.

title: Uncertainty in Orbit: Bayesian Spatiotemporal Modeling of Orbital Debris Proliferation
authors: Rachel Sholder
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The accumulation of orbital debris threatens the resilience of satellite infrastructure, human spaceflight, and the sustainability of future missions. Existing environment models provide valuable deterministic forecasts but often lack explicit treatment of uncertainty and spatial heterogeneity-factors critical for risk-informed decision making. To address this gap, this study introduces a Bayesian spatiotemporal framework for orbital debris forecasting using the Integrated Nested Laplace Approximation (INLA). INLA enables efficient inference for latent Gaussian models, providing a scalable alternative to simulation-based Bayesian methods while yielding full posterior distributions. INLA is particularly well-suited to problems like space debris modeling, where data may be sparse, uncertain, or unevenly distributed in space and time. INLA captures heterogeneity by discretizing orbital space (e.g., altitude and inclination) into a spatial lattice and representing temporal evolution through random-walk components. The framework also integrates heterogeneous data sources-including satellite catalogs (TLEs), fragmentation event records, and environment model outputs. INLA's probabilistic forecasting of debris accumulation and collision risk yields uncertainty-aware projections critical for decision-making. While INLA has been successfully applied in epidemiology and ecology, this work represents its first application to orbital debris. By quantifying uncertainty, identifying risk hot spots, and producing exceedance probabilities, INLA establishes a novel probabilistic toolkit to support mitigation planning, active debris removal prioritization, and the sustainability of nearEarth space.

title: Energy-Aware Scheduling of Parallel Jobs in HPC Systems Using an Improved Golden Jackal Optimization Based on Joint Opposite Selection
authors: Mohammadreza Parvizimosaed, Amirhossein Damia, Alireza Bakhshai, Erfaneh Khanmohammadi
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The rapid growth in scale and complexity of HighPerformance Computing (HPC) systems has made energy consumption a critical operational challenge. It is necessary to create scheduling methods that consider energy consumption while maintaining system efficiency. However, scheduling parallel jobs while taking into account resource limits and multiple criteria, such as energy use, completion time, and throughput, is known to be NP-Hard. This paper presents an energy-aware scheduling framework for high-performance computing (HPC) environments, employing an enhanced variant of the Golden Jackal Optimization (GJO) algorithm, specifically based on Joint Opposite Selection (GJO-JOS). The proposed algorithm effectively balances the search process and stops premature convergence by using strategies like Selective Leading Opposition (SLO) during exploitation and Dynamic Opposite (DO) during exploration. Experimental results compare the proposed method against standard GJO and six prominent meta-heuristic algorithms. The findings demonstrate that GJO-JOS achieves the lowest energy consumption among all tested algorithms, surpassing Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) while offering superior stability and competitive throughput.

title: Evaluation of Optimized LSTM and Moving Average in SME Inventory Forecasting and Simulation
authors: Intan Rahmatillah, Patah Herwanto, Ivan Diryana Sudirman
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Demand quantity determination plays a crucial role in operations management, particularly in improving purchasing decisions and stock management. This article considers the evaluation of daily demand forecasting performance in a food and beverage SME using real data from sales trans- actions, focusing on the main ice cream product. The methods used include a seven-day moving average (MA(7)) baseline model, a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model without optimization, and an LSTM optimized using a grid search approach. Model performance is measured using RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) and MAPE (Mean Absolute Percentage Error), but also through unit-based inventory simulations to assess operational impact. The study showed that the optimized LSTM produced the best performance with an RMSE of 1.65 and a MAPE of 45.06%—better than MA(7) with an RMSE of 1.84 and a MAPE of 46.60%. From an operational perspective, the best LSTM configuration was able to completely eliminate lost demand, although it resulted in an excess inventory of 1.57 units.

title: Enabling Reliable, Fault-Tolerant Autonomous Lunar Habitats with High-Performance Spaceflight Computing
authors: Sarkis S. Mikaelian
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The lunar surface presents unfavorable constraints and harsh living conditions. To address these challenges, autonomous habitats will require complex integrated systems that combine advanced software, high-performance hardware, and cutting-edge sensors to ensure sustainability, safety, and operational efficiency. Consequently, maintaining a sustainable presence on the Moon requires reliable infrastructure and efficient development, precise monitoring, and utilization of resources within a lunar installation. These elements are essential not only to ensure that lunar settlement can be long-term, self-sustaining, and resource-efficient, but also to serve as a foundation for future missions and eventual human habitation on Mars. Humans are not native to the Moon; therefore, our survival and ability to thrive will depend on autonomous systems that can foster safety and resilience through high-availability architectures, graceful degradation, and highly fault-tolerant spaceflight hardware capable of continuing operation during failures. This requires advanced human-rated distributed systems architectures with specialized electronics, scalable capabilities, and an integrated design approach. Unlike current practices focused on short-term missions and regularly maintained components, permanent lunar compute systems must be designed for extended operations beyond mission durations. This paper explores the necessity of transitioning toward fault-tolerant, highly autonomous hardware systems designed for multi-year missions. It also identifies critical subsystems that require high levels of autonomy, supported by radiation-hardened processors and extreme thermal loads, which are essential to mitigate long-term degradation and ensure sustainable lunar habitation. Finally, the paper aligns with NASA's identified Civil Space Shortfalls, particularly in high-performance onboard computing, advanced data acquisition, extreme-environment avionics, radiation monitoring and countermeasures, and autonomous health management. It proposes NASA's new High-Performance Spaceflight Computing (HPSC) processor as a turnkey solution, delivering 100 times the performance-per-watt of legacy rad-hard CPUs and enabling onboard AI, edge computing, and fault-tolerant features essential for sustained lunar autonomy and beyond.

title: Considering Transmission System Vulnerability to Extreme Weather Events in Resilience Assessment of Distribution Systems
authors: Avishek Sapkota, Rajesh Karki, Bram Noble
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The increase in prolonged and widespread power outages resulting from extreme weather events has led to a growing focus on developing methods for distribution system resilience assessment. However, the evaluation frameworks published in available literature perform distribution system resilience assessment in isolation from transmission system analysis. This paper addresses a critical gap in distribution system resilience assessment by recognizing the impact of transmission system vulnerability on the performance of the distribution system during extreme weather events. The proposed framework considers different operational scenarios based on the damage and restoration of the relevant transmission section and the distribution network following an extreme wind event. Case studies on a modified IEEE 69-bus system involving different investment strategies demonstrate that available resilience methodologies significantly underestimate the overall impact of an extreme event. The findings highlight how transmission failures reduce the effectiveness of the distribution-level investments, emphasizing the need for a coordinated, system-wide approach to resilience planning.

title: Data-driven Modeling and Feedforward Control of Millimeter-Scale Vine Robots
authors: Sukjun Kim, Sha Yi, Xiaolong Wang, Michael T. Tolley, Tania K. Morimoto
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Vine robots at a millimeter-scale enable safe and adaptive navigation through confined and delicate environments such as inside the human body. However, accurate modeling and control remain challenging due to nonlinear material behavior, hysteresis, and complex environmental interactions. This work presents a data-driven framework for modeling and control of millimeter-scale vine robots. By leveraging a tracking method that uses visual fiducial markers (AprilTags), we demonstrated accurate and robust tracking of the tip without affecting the performance of the robot. We trained a neural network to predict the position of the tip of the robot in the next timestep based on the actuation and previous states of the robot. A feedforward control algorithm built on this model enabled autonomous trajectory following of a 5 mm diameter vine robot, achieving a root mean square error of 5.17 mm. These results demonstrate the potential of data-driven approaches for achieving accurate modeling and autonomous control of small-scale vine robots.

title: A Quantitative Comparison of Centralised and Distributed Reinforcement Learning-Based Control for Soft Robotic Arms
authors: Linxin Hou, Qirui Wu, Zhihang Qin, Neil Banerjee, Yongxin Guo, Cecilia Laschi
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This paper presents a quantitative comparison between centralised and distributed multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) architectures for controlling a soft robotic arm modelled as a Cosserat rod in simulation. Using PyElastica and the OpenAI Gym interface, we train both a global Proximal Policy Optimisation (PPO) controller and a Multi-Agent PPO (MAPPO) under identical budgets. The study systematically varies the number of controlled sections n and evaluates the performance of the arm to reach a fixed target in three scenarios: default baseline condition, recovery from external disturbance, and adaptation to actuator failure. Quantitative metrics used for the evaluation are mean action magnitude, mean final distance, mean episode length, and success rate. The results show that there are no significant benefits of the distributed policy when the number of controlled sections $n \leq 4$. In very simple systems, when $n \leq 2$, the centralised policy outperforms the distributed one. When n increases to $4{\lt}n \leq 12$, the distributed policy achieves higher final success rates under the same training budget. In these systems, distributed policy promotes a stronger success rate, resilience, and robustness under local observability and converges faster given the same sample size. However, centralised policies take much less time to train the same size of samples. These findings highlight the trade-offs between centralised and distributed policy in reinforcement learning-based control for soft robotic systems and provide actionable design guidance for future sim-to-real transfer in soft rod-like manipulators.

title: VMD-GRU Based Ultra-Short-Term Wind Speed Forecasting System
authors: Sanae Arkhouch, Ikrame Arrassi, Ilyass El Myasse, Nabil Benaya, Faiza Dib, Khaddouj Ben Meziane
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This paper proposes a solution for ultra-short-term wind speed forecasting over horizons of 10, 20 and 30 minutes using real SCADA data from a wind turbine. The proposed approach combines Variational Mode Decomposition (VMD) with a Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) neural network in a hybrid forecasting framework. VMD is first applied to decompose the original wind speed signal into several intrinsic mode functions (IMFs) that separate low- and high-frequency variations and reduce the non-stationary behaviour of the series. The selected IMFs are then used as inputs to the GRU model in order to better capture short-term temporal dependencies and rapid wind fluctuations. The proposed VMD-GRU model is evaluated using SCADA measurements and compared with conventional deep learning models including CNN, LSTM and GRU trained directly on the raw data. Performance is assessed using RMSE, MAE and the coefficient of determination <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{2}$</tex>. The results show that the hybrid model achieves lower RMSE and MAE and higher R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> across all three horizons, demonstrating its effectiveness and robustness for ultra-short-term wind speed forecasting.

title: When Usefulness Meets Emotion: Explaining Post-Adoption Engagement in Social Media
authors: Adele B L Mailangkay, Yulia Ery Kurniawati, Sudiana, Hendry Hartono, Muhammad Shabir Shaharudin, Sasmoko
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: This study aims to analyze the factors influencing social media acceptance by integrating constructs from the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM), Information System Success Model (ISSM), and Expectation Confirmation Model (ECM). A total of 650 valid responses were collected from social media users through an online questionnaire. The proposed model includes six constructs: Performance Expectancy and System Quality as independent variables, Perceived Usefulness and User Satisfaction as mediating variables, and User Engagement and Continuance Intention as dependent variables. Data were analyzed using Partial Least Squares Structural Equation Modeling (PLS-SEM) with SmartPLS 4. The results show that Performance Expectancy and System Quality have significant positive effects on Perceived Usefulness and User Satisfaction. Furthermore, User Satisfaction strongly influences User Engagement, which subsequently drives users' Continuance Intention to use social media platforms. All hypotheses were supported with t-values above 1.96 and p-values below 0.05, indicating robust relationships among constructs. This study provides theoretical implications for extending TAM and ISSM in the context of social media, and practical insights for platform developers to enhance user experience and engagement through improved system quality and perceived usefulness. This findings highlight user engagement as the strongest proximal predictor of continuance intention, reinforcing its role as a behavioral mechanism in post-adoption contexts.

title: Cardiac Arrhythmia Detection Using IoT and Machine Learning
authors: Chaithra S, R Jayanthi, Charvi M J, Chandana A U
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Cardiac arrhythmia, a critical heart rhythm disorder, requires precise detection and classification to prevent severe complications, including stroke and cardiac arrest. This study develops a predictive system that employs advanced machine learning algorithms to analyze electrocardiogram (ECG) data for identifying and classifying various types of arrhythmias. The methodology consists of preprocessing techniques such as noise filtering, feature extraction, and dimensionality reduction through random forest. These approaches streamline the dataset for efficient training and reduce overfitting. Classifiers like random forest, were used to determine their effectiveness in handling imbalanced data sets. The system architecture integrates hardware components such as ESP32 microcontrollers, ECG sensors, and heart rate monitors for real-time data acquisition and monitoring. The collected data is processed locally and transmitted for further analysis. Preprocessing stages involve cleaning missing or inconsistent data and selecting features to identify the most relevant attributes for arrhythmia classification. The Framework is designed to facilitate scalability, low power consumption, and real-time data visualization, making it suitable for integration into a remote healthcare system.

title: A Method for Constructing Parameter Security Domains of Grid-Connected Converters Considering Control Interactions
authors: Yong Sun, Baoju Li, Jiajun Zhang, Guannan Wu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The integration of a large number of renewable energy grid-connected voltage-source converters (VSCs) has significantly increased the power-electronization of power systems. The interactions among power electronic devices, control loops, and the grid across multiple time scales have led to various stability issues. This paper proposes a method for constructing parameter security domains for grid-connected converters, taking into account control interactions. First, based on the mathematical model of the converter control system, a closed-loop transfer function model is established that incorporates grid strength and various control loops, specifically for the DC voltage outer loop. Subsequently, a multidimensional parameter security domain construction method is proposed, considering control interactions. By examining the relative constraints of bandwidths, this study quantifies the interactions among control loops of the converter and between the controller and a weak grid. Based on the Nyquist stability criterion and phase margin, the stable boundaries of control bandwidth ratios are identified and mapped into the parameter security domain. Finally, the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed method are verified through PSCAD/EMTDC simulations.

title: Smart E-Vehicle Using Inductive Wireless Charging System
authors: Boopathy S., D. Gowthami, Sowmiya K, D Faridha Banu, Gokul Anand K R, Dinesh E
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Transportation infrastructure could undergo a revolution thanks to the possibility presented by the electric vehicle (EV) technology's rapid growth. The development of wireless charging systems for electric vehicles (EVs) that are specifically incorporated into roadside infrastructure is one of the most promising breakthroughs. This abstract examines the idea of EV wireless charging with an emphasis on its application on public roads. Wireless charging, sometimes referred to as inductive charging, uses electromagnetic fields to transfer energy between a receiver installed in the car and a charging pad buried in the road, doing away with the need for physical hookups. Enhanced ease, less wear and tear on connectors, and the possibility of dynamic charging while the car is moving are just a few benefits of this approach. This study introduces roadside wireless vehicle charging using the Internet of Things (IOT).

title: 3D-Printed Kresling-Origami Soft Actuators Facilitate Drag Reduction by Rotational Penetration for Burrowing
authors: Frauke Wassmuth, Lily D. Chambers
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: For soft robotic exploration, subterranean environments remain largely inaccessible due to high frictional resistance during penetration. In nature, rotational penetration, as observed in Erodium seeds, effectively reduces drag. Inspired by this mechanism, this work presents pneumatically driven soft actuators with Kresling origami patterns that generate simultaneous rotation and axial motion. To overcome the limitations of molding complex geometries, a 3D printing process using soft thermoplastic polyurethane (Shore A hardness 70) was developed and optimized for airtightness. Kinematic and isometric force analyses of cylindrical and conical Kresling actuators demonstrated performance comparable to that of molded counterparts. Drag reduction capabilities were validated using stacked conical actuators, which achieved 22 to 52% lower penetration resistance compared to a non-rotating pyramidal actuator across different substrates, including compacted soil, dry sand, and gelatin. These results demonstrate that incorporating rotational actuators into burrowing robotic systems can significantly enhance penetration efficiency, offering a promising strategy for subsurface exploration.

title: Two-Stage Stochastic Scheduling of Grid-connected Wind-Hydrogen-Storage Systems with Electrolyser Clusters under Wind Power Uncertainties
authors: Yuting Deng, Fulin Fan, Jialong Zhou, Jinhai Jiang, Chuanyu Sun, Rui Xue, Kai Song
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Hydrogen production from wind farms suffers from the intermittent and uncertain nature of wind generation, which heightens the importance of short-term energy storage units and grid connection in stabilising the power supply to electrolysers and enhancing overall economics. To facilitate multi-unit coordination of a grid-connected wind-hydrogen-storage (WHS) system, this paper proposes a two-stage day-ahead stochastic scheduling model for a transmission-level WHS system with electrolyser clusters subject to uncertain wind power forecasts. First, a total hydrogen production efficiency curve is proposed for electrolyser clusters by combining uniform operation and hot-standby modes of clusters, which enables fast estimations on the optimal number and loads of operating electrolysers from the total cluster load. Then, a two- stage stochastic optimisation model is developed for day-ahead system schedules. In specific, the optimal electrolyser cluster loads are regarded as the first-stage variables that are consistent across random scenarios, while the second-stage stochastic variables including energy storage outputs and energy exchange with grid in each scenario are optimised separately to balance power flows across the system. The stochastic scheduling model proposed here is tested based on a grid-connected WHS system comprising a 76MW wind farm with a 75MW point of common coupling, five 3MW electrolysers and a 15MW/30MWh battery energy storage. Simulation results indicate that the stochastic scheduling achieves stable and efficient operation of electrolyser clusters under all the random scenarios while maximising the overall benefit from green hydrogen production and electricity market trades.

title: Emergency Vehicle Recognition: a Cnn Approach for Efficient Classification
authors: G. Sukanya, Gattam Mohan Krishna Sai, Gandham Bhanu Deekshit
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Detection of the emergency vehicles in heavy traffic might be a challenging issue since lots of vehicles exist in traffic and also there might be occlusions. Nevertheless, using deep learning and computer vision techniques we can generate a viable solution to this issue. The high impact to emergency vehicles passing the specific road is occasioned by the traffic congestion. Relieving the congestion can be achieved through various ways such as detecting the congestion through video analytics which is more accurate and is capable of counting the type of vehicles at a given traffic signal. Traffic lights to identify emergency vehicles early may help avoid time wastage in emergency vehicles transition processes. To eliminate this issue, the proposed model recognizes the emergency vehicle within the signal and transmits the data to the signal control authorities to create a green corridor. The approach entails a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with a deep application that identifies emergency vehicles in the video stream. CNNs suit best image-based classification and they have shown intriguing outcomes in object detection and recognition. The model architecture is Xception using RMSprop to optimize it in a shorter time. Freezing the original convolutional layers and training the last few layers is called transfer learning and it is associated with optimizing the prediction values. The system, according to Flask framework and CNN Xception Architecture, will identify emergency vehicles in traffic signal and produce the green corridor of emergency vehicles of the signal that will ease the delay and make emergency services efficient.

title: Feasibility of Renewable Energy Solutions for Residential EV Charging in India: A Techno-Economic Perspective
authors: Garima Panwar, Sandeep Pilaniya, Sunanda Sinha
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: In India, the transition to electric vehicles (EVs) is constrained by high upfront cost, insufficient charging infrastructure, and dependence on fossil-fuel based grid electricity. To address this, the study examines the economic, technical, and environmental feasibility of integrating solar photovoltaic (PV), wind and battery storage for residential EV charging stations using HOMER Pro. System performance was assessed using key indicators such as the levelized cost of energy (LCOE), net present cost (NPC) and environmental impact. The results indicate that a PV-grid hybrid system offers the lowest LCOE of ₹6.14/kWh with a payback period of 7.59 years, while wind integration reduces emissions by 16.42%. Sensitivity analysis indicates that increasing PV capacity lowers LCOE but raises initial capital investment and payback period. Overall, the findings provide practical insights for designing cost-effective and low-emission residential EV charging systems, supporting informed decision-making for EV users, system designers, and policymakers.

title: Beyond the Bid: Unpleasant Surprises in Firm Fixed Price Spacecraft Contracts on NASA Missions
authors: Rachel Sholder
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Firm Fixed Price (FFP) contracts are well-suited for missions with standardized buses and stable, well-defined payloads. However, for complex missions with bespoke spacecraft, evolving payloads, or immature technologies, FFP contracts may lead to higher overall costs compared to Cost Plus Fixed Fee (CPFF) contracts. This paper explores hypotheses for why this cost inversion occurs, presents preliminary data to support these claims, and proposes a framework for discerning mission attributes that indicate whether a FFP or a CPFF contract is expected to yield the best cost outcome. This analysis is especially timely in light of recent executive orders promoting commercial solutions, heightening the need to align contract structure with mission complexity to avoid unintended financial consequences.

title: Side-Looking SAR Using 12-18 GHz FMCW Radar Integrated with C3 Class Hexacopter
authors: Lee Taylor, Fernando Rodríguez-Morales, Jilu Li, Carlton Leuschen
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper presents initial images from a side-looking Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) collected using a <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$12-18 \text{GHz}$</tex> ultra-wideband frequency modulated continuous wave (FMCW) radar integrated into a C3/Group-2 class Aurelia X6 Hexacopter. We performed SAR processing with motion compensation on data collected in the spring of 2025 in Lawrence KS, USA. The Hexacopter operated at a nominal altitude of 75 m above ground level (AGL) with a 45° radar look angle. We successfully generated preliminary SAR images that correlate well with visible spectrum satellite imagery of the area near the Clinton International Model Airport. This paper focuses on data processing considerations leading to the SAR images. The significance of this investigation is demonstrating the ability to produce high resolution SAR images using data from a cost-effective FMCW radar with ultra-wideband capabilities integrated with a commercially available unmanned aerial system (UAS). Continued efforts are being made to collect additional data using a two-receive-channel version of the FMCW radar and evaluate single-pass interferometric SAR (InSAR) processing techniques.

title: Bridging Local Interpretability: Analyzing SHAP Importance and Counterfactual Sensitivity for Model Transparency
authors: Raga Sri Sudha Paramkusam, Prasant Kumar Mohanty
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: AI systems are being widely adopted in decision processes in the field of healthcare, it has become imperative to ensure that there is transparency and interpretability in machine learning models. Several methods for explaining machine learning models have been proposed, with little being done to compare attribution-based methods with counterfactual-based methods for explaining machine learning models. In this study, we investigate the relationship between SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) and Counterfactual Explanations for understanding machine learning interpretability from both descriptive and prescriptive perspectives. To achieve this, six machine learning models were trained on a heart disease dataset, with the top SHAP features being compared with counterfactual features. From the analysis, it is clear that counterfactuals can be used to effectively understand how sensitive the machine learning model is to feature variation, while SHAP provides stable feature importance across all machine learning models. Additionally, non-linear models like XG-Boost and Neural Networks have higher differences, while linear models like Logistic Regression have higher similarities in SHAP feature importance and counterfactual feature variations. These results demonstrate that combining SHAP-based attribution with counterfactual sensitivity analysis provides a complete and reliable approach to XAI, which can improve interpretability as well as decision support in real-world applications.

title: Observability Analysis of Simultaneous Localization & Calibration for Cooperative Radio Navigation
authors: Alexis Marino, Robert Pöhlmann, Emanuel Staudinger
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Future robotic surface exploration missions, e.g. on Mars, demand robust and accurate navigation solutions. Since no Satellite Navigation or infrastructure exists on Mars, accurate navigation remains challenging. Cooperative radio navigation emerged as a key technology in such scenarios. Methods based on signal propagation time, such as round-trip time ranging, require sub-nanosecond Time-of-Flight measurement accuracy. However, environmental factors, e.g. temperature changes, can affect group delays of radio transceivers, introducing ranging bias. Simultaneous Localization and Calibration (SLAC) has been proposed to jointly estimate node positions and ranging bias as a calibration parameter, correcting such biases online. However, the success of this calibration process relies on the observability of the calibration and localization parameters, ensuring they can be accurately estimated and corrected using available measurements. To cope with that, we conduct an observability analysis of SLAC, leveraging the equivalence between the full column rank of the Fisher Information Matrix and the measurement Jacobian matrix. This analysis leads to the derivation of necessary and sufficient conditions for observability, which determine the minimum number of static and mobile nodes required for SLAC, and identifies geometric configurations that result in strong, weak, or non-observable systems. We further highlight scenarios where observability is inherently impossible and propose practical solutions for both cooperative and non-cooperative navigation. The analysis is validated through extensive simulations and real-world experiments using radio nodes integrated into static and mobile platforms. The experiments confirm the theoretical findings and demonstrate that SLAC can achieve observability under realistic deployment conditions.

title: Designs, linear codes, plateaued functions, and their interconnections
authors: Jong Yoon Hyun, Jieun Kwon, Jiaxin Wang, Yansheng Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: In this paper, we mainly investigate profound interconnections between combinatorial designs, linear codes, and Boolean functions. Firstly, we present a generic construction method for designs derived from Boolean functions and give a new concept of non-symmetric designs with the triple symmetric difference property (TSDP). Secondly, we provides an alternative proof for addition designs derived from plateaued functions, which need not be simple or symmetric. We characterize simple 2-designs on 2m−r points arising from r-plateaued functions in m variables, and show that addition designs from such functions with no nonzero linear structure satisfy the TSDP but not the double one, yielding non-symmetric simple 2-designs. Thirdly, we primarily explore the equivalence relationships between designs, linear codes, and plateaued functions. These investigations help resolve two open problems posed by Ding and Tang (Designs from Linear codes, Singapore: World Scientific, 2022: Problems 14.20, 14.23).We also compute the automorphism groups of addition designs of r-plateaued functions and of the linear codes of addition designs. This work extends results by Bending (SDP designs and their automorphism groups, Ph.D. thesis, 1993), and Dempwolff and Neumann (Des. Codes Cryptogr., 57, 373–381, 2010). Finally, we yield new Boolean functions producing two families of a 2-design whose parameters coincide with those of the complement of a point-hyperplane design and a TSDP design, despite being non-isomorphic.

title: Design of a Reliable Transmission Protocol and High-Performance Flow Control Algorithm for Mosaic Cameras over 10-Gigabit Links
authors: Zhe Geng, Zhiyue Wang, Qi Feng, Jian Wang, Hongfei Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Nuclear Science
