Case studies demonstrate that the model improves market revenue while maintaining operational feasibility in industrial parks participating in competitive energy markets.

title: Multi Label Classification of Eye Power Glass Frames for Material, Price, and Supported Power Prediction Using ResNet-18
authors: Vijay Ravichandran, Rajkumar K
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Nowadays consumers of the eye glasses because of power is increasing mainly due to higher digital screen usage in daily life. On a high-level, eye glass consists two components: Frame & Glass with Lense. Most of the people expects good style and appearance in wearing eye glasses & most commercial platforms also focuses these aspects only. While purchasing the eye glasses, Still many people choosing the frame by just looking only. It's very hard for consumer to get to know the functional properties of the eye glass frame just by looking at it. User should have an awareness about the basic details of the frame before making it purchase like whether a selected frame is suits to his power or not, selected frame material is durable or not & frame has reasonable price or not. Because, visual comfort is essential for a consumer to run their day-to-day life. This Paper aims to give basic details awareness of the eye glass frame by comparing the user data with Lenskart data who is one of the top sellers in the eye glass market. In this work, a vision based multi class learning approach is proposed to predict the basic detail category from the user's eye glass frame image data. The system takes input image from the user via web-based user interface then passed to the preprocessing stage. Pre-processed user image passed to a deep learning model for prediction which is trained on collected dataset from Lenskart website. ResNet 18 model used as backbone to extract the features from eye glass frame image data. The model is designed to predict three attributes: Price Type, Material Type & Supported Power Type. The predicted results will return to the user interface by the model to help the users before making purchase.

title: Fine-Tune Segment Anything Model (SAM) on Custom Data
authors: B. Alekya Himabindu, M. Tryphosa, C. Bharathi, S. Farhin, S. Arshiya, S. Rambabu
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: The paper introduces a sensible strategy that deciphers finetuning of the lightweight mask, with heavy image encoder and prompt encoder held constant. This greatly lowers computation expenses, decreases the potential risk of overfitting and is adaptable using a relatively small amount of GPU resources. The work also presents an algorithmic way of converting custom datasets to prompt-driven training sets through the automatic generation of point and bounding box prompts with reference to ground-truth masks. To achieve high edge precision and mask quality, the model is trained on a composite loss function a mixture of Binary Cross-Entropy(BCE) and Dice loss that is effective to align the boundaries and minimize leakage of masks. The experiments show that the fine-tuning SAM is capable of making significant gains in the segmentation accuracy and robustness on domain-specific data over the zero-shot baseline, and still maintaining good generalization properties.

title: ReinforceML: A Reinforcement Learning-Based Framework for Automated Machine Learning Pipeline Selection
authors: Vasanth S, Madhan J, S. Sarumathi
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: End-to-end machine learning (ML) involves multiple interdependent stages such as exploratory data analysis (EDA), preprocessing, feature engineering, model and hyperparameter selection, and reporting. Executing this workflow well still demands expertise and time, which can be a barrier for students and non-specialists. Existing automated machine learning (AutoML) tools mainly optimise over algorithm and hyperparameter spaces and are often used as black boxes, providing little insight into the resulting pipeline. This paper presents ReinforceML, a reinforcement learning (RL)-based framework that automatically assembles tabular ML pipelines and exports them as fully commented Jupyter notebooks. The system extracts meta-features from a dataset, selects preprocessing and feature extraction strategies, chooses a model family with key hyperparameters, and learns these decisions using rewards based on validation performance and computational cost. Evaluation on six benchmark datasets indicates that ReinforceML achieves competitive performance (89.8 % accuracy, within 0.8 % of Auto-sklearn) while reducing end-to-end search time by approximately 20 % and providing transparent, reproducible notebooks. By bridging AutoML with interpretable, notebook-based workflows, ReinforceML makes ML pipelines more accessible, reproducible and educational.

title: NLP-Driven Patient Interaction and Smart Triage Dashboard for Hospitals
authors: Layan Y. Basaheih, Yara H. Baeshen, Reem A. Alwesabi, Badya F. Badr, Hala F. Albatati, Amnah A. AlSulami
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Emergency Departments (EDs) frequently face overcrowding and triage inconsistencies, which can delay critical care. To address this, we propose an end-to-end Smart Emergency Triage System that integrates a mobile application for patient symptom reporting with real-time physiological vital sign monitoring. The system also includes a station screen used at the triage station to link patient identity and collect vital signs through IoT sensors, as well as a doctor dashboard that allows clinical staff to monitor patient data and predicted severity levels in real time. The core of this system is a dual-stream Hybrid Deep Learning architecture. A Natural Language Processing (NLP) stream utilizing a Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network processes unstructured subjective complaints, while a parallel Dense network evaluates objective physiological indicators (e.g., <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{SpO}_{2}$</tex>, Pulse). These multimodal inputs are fused to accurately classify patients into one of the five Canadian Triage and Acuity Scale (CTAS) levels. However, existing approaches primarily rely on retrospective clinical data and lack real-time pre-arrival patient input, integration with IoT-based vital monitoring, and direct communication with hospital systems. Evaluated using a strict GroupShuffleSplit strategy to prevent data leakage, the model achieved an overall classification accuracy of 91.04 %. The results demonstrate the framework's capability to effectively prioritize critical cases, bridging the gap between theoretical predictive modeling and deployable real-time clinical decision support tools to optimize ED workflows.

title: SafeMind Pro: AI-Driven Cognitive and Safety Monitoring Helmet for Industrial Workers
authors: Rayan Ibrahim Alqadham Mohammed, Mohammed Aqeel Daghar, Layan Abdulla Alghamdi
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: Cognitive fatigue and reduced alertness are major contributors to industrial accidents and human-factor errors in safety-critical environments. This paper presents SafeMind Pro, an AI-driven smart industrial helmet designed for real-time monitoring of workers' cognitive and physiological states. The proposed system integrates multimodal sensing technologies including electroencephalography (EEG) sensors for brainwave activity, eye-tracking modules for attention monitoring, heart-rate variability (HRV) sensors derived from PPG/ECG signals for stress assessment, and inertial measurement unit (IMU) sensors for motion behavior analysis. A hybrid machine-learning architecture combining Random Forest and CNN-LSTM models is used to analyze multimodal physiological data streams. EEG frequency bands (alpha, beta, theta), blink patterns, HRV metrics, and head-movement signals are extracted and used as classification features. Experimental evaluation was conducted on 24 participants performing simulated industrial tasks, and results demonstrate classification accuracy ranging between <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{8 4 \%}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\text{9 1 \%}$</tex> across five cognitive states, including Alert, Mild Fatigue, Severe Fatigue, High Stress, and Attention Loss. The system achieved average precision of 0.88 and recall of 0.87, with end-to-end detection latency between 0.8 and 1.3 seconds, enabling real-time safety intervention. When critical fatigue conditions are detected, the helmet provides vibration and auditory alerts and can trigger safety responses through a supervisory dashboard. By integrating wearable sensing technologies, artificial intelligence, and human-factors engineering, SafeMind Pro transforms traditional personal protective equipment from passive protection into a predictive and proactive safety solution aligned with Industry 4.0 smart manufacturing systems.

title: An Analysis of the Effectiveness and Potential of AI Tool in Interior Design and its Implementation in Education
authors: Mona F. Aljedaani, Lujain A. Jamalallail
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This study explores the effectiveness of artificial intelligence AI applications in various interior design disciplines and creative industries, compared to traditional design software such as Computer-Aided Design and Building Information Modeling. The study aims to assess the ability of AI tools to enhance creativity, efficiency, and user satisfaction in education and practice. The research adopted a mixed-methods approach combining quantitative and qualitative data to gain a comprehensive understanding of the impact of AI on interior design. The methodology includes a case study on interior design students supported by a structured survey targeting designers, architects, and design students experienced in using AI platforms. By analyzing the results of the case studies and the survey, the outputs of projects completed using AI tools were compared with those completed using traditional software. The analysis focused on elements of creativity, the quality of visual output, speed of execution, and user satisfaction. It also identified challenges faced by participants, such as the learning curve, difficulty integrating with traditional tools, and concerns related to intellectual property rights and plagiarism. The study demonstrated that AI tools beat traditional design software in several aspects, especially in enhancing creativity, speeding design processes, and improving user experience. The results also showed that integrating AI into education and professional practice can revolutionize the quality of production and innovation in interior design. However, challenges remain, such as the need for specialized training and the development of clear policies to protect intellectual property and ensure the ethical use of smart tools.

title: Two-Stage Causal Inference for Sectoral Electricity Consumption Via Transfer Entropy
authors: Guoshu Lai, Xiaorui Qian, Gaole Cai, Huawei Hong, Lingling Zhu, Yuying Chen, Xiangpeng Zhan
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Uncovering causal relationships in electricity consumption time series is critical for optimizing energy management and informing industrial policy in the era of digital transformation and dual-carbon targets. Traditional causal discovery methods face significant limitations when applied to sectoral consumption data, as they typically rely on linearity and stationarity assumptions that fail to capture the complex, nonstationary dynamics inherent in modern electricity systems. To address these challenges, this paper develops a novel two-stage causal graph construction framework that progressively refines candidate relationships through correlation screening followed by frequency domain cosine similarity analysis, effectively filtering out spurious correlations while preserving genuine causal linkages. The method employs automatic binning for probability density estimation and enforces directed acyclic graph (DAG) structure through iterative cycle removal, effectively handling non-stationarity while quantifying directional influences with optimal time lags. We further employ SARIMAX prediction and PCMCI methods for comprehensive causal validation. Applied to actual data from ten consumption categories over 63 months in a certain province in southern China, our framework provides a causally interpretable representation of the complex temporal dependencies among electricity consumption sectors. This establishes the foundation for demand forecasting models and departmental coordination policies.

title: AI-Fusion Smart Helmet:Real Time Speed Enforcement, Alcohol Emergency Response for Enhanced Two-Wheeler Safety
authors: C. Sundhar, R. Bharath Kumar, K. Kesavan, V. Pradeep
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Bikers are very susceptible to road accidents due to impaired driving and nonobservance of safety rules. This paper introduces an AI-Fusion Smart Helmet, an IoT-based system that enhances two-wheeler safety through real-time helmet verification, intoxication detection, and adaptive speed control using TinyML-based prediction models.

title: Impact of d-axis Current on a Passively Levitated Self-Bearing PM Machines Considering Nonlinear Electrodynamic and Reluctant Effects
authors: Adrien Robert, Joachim Van Verdeghem, Bruno Dehez
publish: IEEE Transactions on Energy Conversion
abstract: Self-bearing machines use rotor magnetic levitation to suppress friction and reach high rotation speeds. Recent studies have considered the integration of electrodynamic suspension in permanent magnet self-bearing machines in order to achieve fully passive levitation, improving the compactness and reliability. However, the influence of the d-axis current injected by the power supply on the dynamic behavior has not been addressed so far, as previous models were based on hypotheses that resulted in axial force and torque being independent of this current component. This paper develops an electromechanical model of an electrodynamic thrust self-bearing machine that accounts for the reluctant effects, both axial and angular, and the nonlinear evolution of magnetic flux linkage, revealing an impact of the d-axis current. Based on this model, quasi-static expressions of the suspension currents and the electromagnetic forces are derived and analyzed. A prototype is then tested in various conditions to compare model predictions with measurements. The results demonstrate that d-axis current injection can modulate the suspension force, even at zero speed.

title: Dynamic Graph-enhanced Event Refinement for Temporal Sentence Grounding of Micro-moments_supp1-3695790.pdf
authors: Mingjin Kuai
publish: N/A
abstract: Temporal Sentence Grounding in Videos (TSGV) aims to localize video moments that are semantically consistent with a given query. Despite the proliferation of approaches to TSGV in recent years, existing methods still face limitations in localizing micro-moments in videos and struggle to capture meaningful fine-grained event information within them. To address these challenges, we propose a Dynamic Graph-enhanced Event Refinement (DGER) framework specifically designed for the Temporal Sentence Grounding of Micro-moments (Micro-TSG) task. This framework efficiently explores event-aware information among text queries, long videos, and micro-moments. Our approach begins with a Query-specific Event-aware module that includes a modality-specific encoding module, a multi-modal fusion module, and an event- and query-refinement processing module. These components comprehensively leverage fine-grained event information both within and across modal contexts. Additionally, we have designed a Dynamic Information Propagation module that automatically refines the structure of attentional regions based on the Graph Information Bottleneck (GIB) principle, enhancing the attentional mechanism's ability to efficiently extract key features from significantly short-depth signals. Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms all state-of-the-art techniques on the Charades-STA and TACoS datasets, achieving comparable results on the ActivityNet Captions dataset.

title: EMT-Based Sequence Analysis of a Converter-Dominated Microgrid Under Fault and Load Disturbances: Case Study of the NELHA Microgrid
authors: Adewale Adegboyega, Saeed Sepasi, Zakaria Afshar
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Converter-dominated microgrids exhibit dynamic behaviors during faults and disturbances that differ fundamentally from those of synchronous-machine–dominated systems. This paper develops an electromagnetic transient (EMT) model of a converter-dominated microgrid and applies sequence-domain analysis based on instantaneous symmetrical components to evaluate its dynamic behavior under three scenarios: a single-line-to-ground (SLG) fault, a balanced three-phase short circuit, and a nonlinear load step. Both grid-connected and islanded operating modes are evaluated under matched disturbance types, locations, and timing, using mode-specific source configurations representative of actual microgrid operation. The simulations reveal a pronounced dependence on the operating mode. In grid-connected operation, the grid provides high fault currents, limits neutral shift, and enables rapid voltage recovery. In islanded mode, the effective positive- and zero-sequence impedances are higher, resulting in a reduced fault current, a pronounced zero-sequence voltage, and a larger neutral displacement. The time-domain post-processing clarifies the disturbance structure: SLG faults produce pronounced zero-sequence excursions and stronger phase asymmetry, whereas the 3ϕ faults remain phase-symmetric in the abc domain and are dominated by the positive-sequence response. The nonlinear load produces measurable asymmetry in the projected sequence quantities together with significant harmonic distortion in the currents. The EMT model captures early-cycle asymmetry, control-imposed current limiting, and sequence interactions that cannot be represented in phasor-domain root-mean-square (RMS) studies. These EMT-derived indicators provide grounding- and protection-relevant insights that can support grounding review, relay-sensitivity checks, and control assessment in converter-dominated microgrids.

title: Distribution and Aggregation: Continual Learning Principles for Evolving Traffic Networks
authors: Shiyu Yang, Qunyong Wu, Chuxi Fan
publish: IEEE Internet of Things Journal
abstract: Real-world spatiotemporal networks continuously evolve, with new nodes emerging, traffic patterns changing, and network topology expanding. This evolution challenges spatiotemporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) to achieve continual learning: adapting to new data while mitigating catastrophic forgetting, scaling to growing networks without retraining from scratch, and maintaining prediction accuracy without incurring unbounded computational costs. The core dilemma is balancing model plasticity with stability as networks expand over time. To address these challenges, we propose DAA, a novel continual learning approach with multi-level adaptive tuning, following two fundamental principles guided by systematic analysis: distribution and aggregation, which effectively addresses the above issues through tensorized hierarchical parameters. Specifically, we deploy lightweight adapters with learnable importance weights across multiple network levels, leverage tensor-train decomposition to compress adapter representations, and jointly optimize with the base STGNN. This approach ensures that the model can sequentially learn from spatiotemporal data streams while adapting to network evolution and retaining learned knowledge. Experiments on multi-year traffic datasets demonstrate that DAA outperforms state-of-the-art baseline methods in effectiveness, generality, and efficiency. The code will be released at https://github.com/OvOYu/DAA.

title: Criss-Cross Deletion Correcting Codes: Optimal Constructions with Efficient Decoders
authors: Yubo Sun, Gennian Ge
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory
abstract: This paper addresses fundamental challenges in two-dimensional error correction by constructing optimal codes for criss-cross deletions. We consider an n×n array X over a q-ary alphabet Σq := {0, 1, . . . , q−1} that is subject to a (tr, tc)-criss-cross deletion, which involves the simultaneous removal of tr rows and tc columns. A code C ⊆ Σn×n q is defined as a (tr, tc)-criss-cross deletion correcting code if it can successfully correct these deletions. Our primary technical contributions are as follows: • Theoretical Bounds: We derive a sphere-packing type lower bound and a Gilbert-Varshamov type upper bound on the redundancy of optimal codes. Our results indicate that the optimal redundancy for a (tr, tc)-criss-cross deletion correcting code lies between (tr, tc)n log q+(tr, tc) log n+ Oq,tr,tc (1) and (tr, tc)n log q+2(tr, tc) log n+Oq,tr,tc (1), where the logarithm is on base two, and Oq,tr,tc (1) is a constant that depends solely on q, tr, and tc. • Optimal Constructions: For the case of (1, 1)-criss-cross deletions, we propose two families of constructions that achieve 2n log q + 2 log n + Oq(1) bits of redundancy. This redundancy is optimal up to an additive constant term Oq(1), which depends solely on q. One family is designed for non-binary alphabets, while the other addresses arbitrary alphabets. For the case of (tr, tc)-criss-cross deletions, we provide a strategy to derive optimal codes when both unidirectional deletions occur consecutively. • Efficient Decoders: We propose decoding algorithms with a time complexity of O(n2) for our codes, which are optimal for two-dimensional scenarios.

title: Harnessing Cellular Network Data for Accurate Transportation Mode Recognition
authors: Kalamkas Zhagyparova, Nour Kouzayha, Hesham ElSawy, Ahmed Bader, Tareq Al-Naffouri
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Transportation mode recognition plays a pivotal role in urban planning, environmental research, and smart mobility services. This study introduces a novel data-driven framework leveraging radio access network (RAN) measurements from cellular infrastructure to classify four modes of transportation-car, bus, walk, and train-with high accuracy. Unlike conventional methods that rely on GPS or motion sensors, which are power-intensive and raise privacy concerns, our approach capitalizes on the widespread availability of cellular infrastructure to build the cellular signal features dataset used for classification, ensuring a more energy-efficient, non-intrusive, and scalable solution. Data was collected across two urban cities and a rural university campus, capturing realistic mobility patterns. The proposed system employs gradient-boosted decision trees for classification, achieving a high average <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$F_{1}$</tex-math></inline-formula>-score of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$93.7\%$</tex-math></inline-formula>. By relying on user-independent inference, the model demonstrates robust performance without requiring personalized data. The results highlight the potential of cellular network data for real-time, privacy-preserving transportation mode detection in support of smart mobility systems.

title: A Multiport Solid-State Transformer by Modular Multilevel Converter and Quadruple Active-Bridge for Future Power Grid
authors: Md Sanwar Hossain, Md. Rabiul Islam, Shuvra Prokash Biswas, Danny Sutanto, Kashem M. Muttaqi, Sharmin Shila
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industry Applications
abstract: With the increasing penetration of distributed energy sources, dc loads, and energy storage systems into the power grid, the demand for compact and highly efficient solid-state transformers (SSTs) is rapidly growing. This paper presents a modular multiport SST topology designed to deliver power to low-voltage ac (LVac) and low-voltage dc (LVdc) ports from medium-voltage ac (MVac) and medium-voltage dc (MVdc) ports. The proposed SST architecture employs a modular multilevel converter (MMC), a quadruple active-bridge (QAB) converter with a high-frequency transformer, and a two-level voltage source inverter. The MMC generates a stable dc-link voltage from MVac and MVdc inputs, while the QAB ensures galvanic isolation and voltage step-down to the LVdc level using fewer components. The final LVac output is achieved through an efficient two-level voltage source inverter stage. An advanced modulation technique is implemented to reduce total harmonic distortion (THD) and enhance the efficiency of the inverter. Simulation studies conducted in MATLAB/Simulink confirm that the proposed multiport SST maintains quality power across all ports and meets IEEE standards. Additionally, power losses and efficiency of the inverter are evaluated using the PLECS simulation platform. Results show a significant improvement, achieving 50.48% THD and 84.26% efficiency of the inverter at unity modulation index and 10 kHz operating frequency, outperforming conventional approaches. Experimental validation using a reduced-scale prototype further verifies the practical viability and effectiveness of the proposed SST system and modulation technique.

title: Semantic Communication-Enhanced Split Federated Learning for Vehicular Networks: Architecture, Challenges, and Case Study
authors: Lu Yu, Zheng Chang, Ying-Chang Liang
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Vehicular edge intelligence (VEI) is vital for future intelligent transportation systems. However, traditional centralized learning in dynamic vehicular networks faces significant communication overhead and privacy risks. Split federated learning (SFL) offers a distributed solution but is often hindered by substantial communication bottlenecks from transmitting high-dimensional intermediate features and can present label privacy concerns. Semantic communication offers a transformative approach to alleviate these communication challenges in SFL by focusing on transmitting only task-relevant information. This article leverages the advantages of semantic communication in the design of SFL, and presents a case study the semantic communication- enhanced U-Shaped split federated learning (SC-USFL) framework that inherently enhances label privacy by localizing sensitive computations with reduced overhead. It features a dedicated semantic communication module (SCM), with pre-trained and parameter-frozen encoding/decoding units, to efficiently compress and transmit only the task-relevant semantic information over the critical uplink path from vehicular users to the edge server (ES). Furthermore, a network status monitor (NSM) module enables adaptive adjustment of the semantic compression rate in real-time response to fluctuating wireless channel conditions. The SC-USFL framework demonstrates a promising approach for efficiently balancing communication load, preserving privacy, and maintaining learning performance in resource-constrained vehicular environments. Finally, this article highlights key open research directions to further advance the synergy between semantic communication and SFL in the vehicular network.

title: Array-Multiplexed CNN Hardware Accelerator for End-Side Fault Diagnosis in Resource-Constrained Wireless Sensor Networks
authors: Hao Tan, Baoping Tang, Hao Fu, Chunhua Zhao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Deep learning (DL) is gradually becoming the most widely used computational approach in the field of mechanical fault diagnosis. However, the restricted hardware resources of mechanical vibration wireless sensor networks (WSNs) pose a challenge for DL deployment. In this article, we propose a convolutional neural networks (CNNs) hardware accelerator to address this challenge. First, a strategy of operator-sharing processing element (PE) arrays is employed to reduce the consumption of hardware logic resources. Various floating-point operation modes are achieved by configuring the input port data of the PE unit. Second, a memory mapping controller (MMC) is used to control the address mapping and data interactions between PE arrays, operators, and memories based on the index of the model. Finally, the accelerator employs a definable number of PE units and clock frequencies to make decisions based on available hardware logic resources. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first instance of deploying a hardware accelerator for CNNs on a mechanical vibration WSNs node. Meanwhile, we validate the CNN model using the gearbox dataset from the Driveline Diagnostic Simulator (DDS), and develop a WSNs node equipped with a Field Programmable Gate Array (FPGA) to validate the accelerator's performance. The experimental results demonstrate that, compared to the high-performance microcontroller at the same power consumption level, our deployment solution achieves a 10-fold improvement in inference speed while maintaining the accuracy of fault diagnosis.

title: Collaborative Block-Term Tensor Decomposition and Its Application to Image Processing
authors: Hao Zhang, Xi-Le Zhao, Junjun Pan, Kai-Wen Guo, Jiashu Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Signal Processing
abstract: The rank-(L,M,N) block-term tensor decomposition (termed as BTD for convenience), which decomposes a tensor into a summation of multiple block-terms, has shown promise in multidimensional signal processing. However, in realworld applications, different components of a tensor usually have common and individual structures (e.g., shared dictionaries and individual coefficients), while BTD neglects the common structures across different components. Moreover, the corresponding algorithms for BTD (e.g., alternating least squares algorithm (BTD-ALS)) are computationally expensive for large-scale real data. To address these problems, we propose a collaborative block-term tensor decomposition (termed as CBTD), which decomposes a tensor into a summation of different block-terms multiplied by shared matrices. Compared with BTD, CBTD can better capture the structure of the data (i.e., the shared dictionaries and individual coefficients of multiple components), which is important especially in the challenging noisy/missing cases; and CBTD essentially projects the original tensor into a low-dimensional latent tensor by the shared factor matrices and then performs BTD on the latent tensor, which allows us to design more efficient algorithms. Moreover, we discuss the connection between BTD and CBTD and establish the essential uniqueness for CBTD. Empowered with CBTD, we propose the multidimensional signal reconstruction model and develop the corresponding proximal alternating minimization-based algorithm (CBTD-PAM) with the convergence guarantee. Extensive experiments on multispectral images and gray videos under Gaussian/sparse noise removal and tensor completion tasks demonstrate that the CBTD-PAM improves the reconstruction performance over competing methods in most cases. Especially, compared to the BTD-ALS, CBTD-PAM not only delivers higher quality results but also achieves substantial speedup.

title: TraceFix: Repairing Agent Coordination Protocols with TLA+ Counterexamples
authors: Shuren Xia, Qiwei Li, Taqiya Ehsan, Jorge Ortiz
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Conference on AI and Agentic Systems
abstract: None

title: A Novel Two-Stage Trading Strategy of D-2 Contracts Decomposition for Wind Farms: Tackling Dual Settlement and Implementation Rules
authors: Xili Du, Haochen Gong, Youbo Liu, Yuben Tang, Sashuang Sun, Zhuoyu Wang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: In China, wind farms’ entire output entering the market is jointly regulated by the market operator and system operator. Consequently, their revenues face dual challenges: market-related and technical. At the market level, before participating in the spot market (D), medium- and long-term bilateral power contracts must be decomposed into a deterministic power curve two day ahead (D-2) as baseline profile. Conventional decomposition methods ignore the optimal weight allocation between bilateral deals and spot prices, which may result in excessive deviation between the D-2 and on-grid curves, potentially causing unexpected revenue losses. At the dispatch level, operational penalties caused by physical offsets between the predicted and on-grid curves need to be carefully considered to avoid increasing operating costs. We propose a two-stage approach: aiming to maximize generation profit, Stage 1 comprehensively analyzes the effects of price differences and deviation thresholds across markets. Based on this analysis, it proposes a D-2 curve decomposition method that couples D-2 output forecasting with the contract decomposition decision process. To minimize operating costs, Stage 2 optimizes reported forecasted power and on-grid power by considering the impact of D-2 power. It then designs a two-stage trading strategy for wind farms to coordinate settlement and dispatch. Case studies demonstrate that the proposed method enhances wind farms’ market competitiveness, while analyzing the impact of spot market prices and decomposition coefficients on revenue, thus providing support for wind farm participation in markets.

title: Building an Autonomous Cyber Defense System Through Generative AI Framework
authors: Rachel Dcosta, Shahbaz Pervez, Seyed Ebrahim Hosseini
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This research develops and evaluates the D-GEN-SOC, a Security Operation Center (SOC) framework that is defensively oriented and combines open-source telemetry (Wazuh), incident coordination (The Hive), and a lightweight AI augmentation grounded in explainable automation. The project prototypes a modular architecture that is designed for transparent, ethical, and auditable SOC decision support. This study adopted a Design Science Research (DSR) approach to design, implement and evaluate the D-GEN-SOC framework. A controlled lab environment was deployed on an Ubuntu-based system to simulate realistic activity and generate attack that were synthetic, such as SSH brute-force attempts. These performances were consumed by Wazuh, analysed, and automatically forwarded to TheHive using a Python-based connector. A local AI module that summarizes those alerts using Gemini 1.5's free-tier API, which creates a small summary report that is stored alongside for analyst and human-in-the-loop (HITL) validation. The assessment scripts measured the Mean Time to Detect (MTTD) and alert-forwarding latency across 20 alerts, and a prototype was achieved with an MTTD of 84.83s and a Wazuh → TheHive latency of 29.55 s, demonstrating a near-real-time detection and a reliable coordination between the SIEM and SOAR layers. This visualization also includes a timeline graph and a histogram that reveals a stable sub-minute response cycle. During the experiments, the Cortex analyser layer was unstable, and the results confirmed that even partial AI augmentation, log summarization, prioritization, and metrics computation can improve the explainability and readiness for analyst without replacing the human authority. D-GEN-SOC offers a culturally aligned blueprint for NZ organizations to integrate AI within open-source SOC ecosystems. The findings also demonstrated the measures that improved MTTD by ∼53%), MTTR by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(\approx \mathbf{4 0 \%})$</tex>, and fewer false-positive reductions by <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(10 \%)$</tex>, validating the framework' s operational feasibility.

title: Thermal Management and Packaging of High-Power Polarization-Diverse Arrays Utilizing Additive Manufacturing
authors: Max Tannenbaum, Dante Dimenichi, Nathan Fairlie, Wiley Holcombe
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper details the structural and thermal management aspects of a fully-integrated X-band polarization-diverse active electronically scanned array (AESA) subarray developed by GTRI. The system is suitable for airborne and ground-based system applications that require high transmit power with configurable polarization. It consists of bespoke eight-element transmit/receive (T/R) modules, liquid-cooled cold plates optimized to reduce thermal gradients between high power amplifiers, power/control electronics, electro-magnetic interference (EMI) lids for improved isolation between channels, and a 64-element radiator. The mechanical engineering approach and novel fabrication techniques made the packaging and cooling for this design possible. Key mechanical features include the use of metal 3D-printed structures, liquid cooling for an array application, and components developed to mitigate downtime for future fielded applications. The hardware supports a high-power AESA with full polarization control and elevated duty cycle capability to facilitate the demands of future multi-function radiofrequency (RF) systems.

title: NiMEx: Smart Rover Swarms for Concurrent Mars Missions and Adaptive Role Reassignment
authors: Nidhi D. Mandrekar
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This research presents a swarm-based robotic system NiMEx (Next-Generation Intelligent Miniature Explorers) designed to enhance the scientific yield and operational efficiency of future Mars exploration missions. NiMEx consists of eight autonomous miniature rovers with each rover under 30 inches in size. The rovers are grouped into three categorical classes i.e. Aquabot, AstroBiobot and Geobot. Each rover is equipped with mission specific instruments such as spectrometers, ground-penetrating radar, laser induced fluorescence systems and rotary drills which can perform targeted tasks such as mineral analysis, biosignature detection and geo sampling. The swarm architecture is optimized using distributed autonomy with real-time inter rover communication and a localization strategy. Localization is achieved by using a beacon enabled triangulation and ultra-wideband (UWB) inter rover tracking along with Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) technique. One rover serves as a navigation hub which generates terrain maps and broadcasts positional data to the swarm. Task allocation is governed by decentralized algorithms that dynamically assign roles based on rover capabilities, location and energy reserves. Data transmission is facilitated through a mesh network with priority queuing, compression and redundancy protocols to ensure reliable delivery to the base station. The biggest advantage of NiMEx framework is that it is designed to execute concurrent missions, adaptive role specialization and make autonomous decisions. It offers a scalable and resilient framework for future planetary exploration. This approach significantly reduces latency, increases coverage and enhances the likelihood of detecting scientifically valuable phenomena across diverse Martian terrains.

title: Vehicle to Grid Algorithms Applied to Residential and Business Low Voltage Microgrids
authors: Lorenzo Frattale Mascioli, Roberto Menichelli, Andrea Golino, Riccardo Loggia, Maria Martino, Andrea Bonfiglio, Maria Carmen Falvo, Luigi Martirano
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: The huge growth in the electric vehicle (EV) market is leading to a situation where the power grid will have to meet ever-increasing energy demand. Without advanced energy management systems, this would cause significant congestion and stability challenges. The paper presents a unified system that combines a home energy management system (HEMS) for a residential microgrid with PV, a battery energy storage system (BESS), and a Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G) and Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) energy management for a microgrid of corporate parking lots for EVs. The system harmonizes objectives, constraints, and key performance indicators (KPIs) in both contexts. This research proposes and validates optimization algorithms through simulations that aim to reduce grid dependence, maximize the use of local resources, and meet the needs of both the user and the grid operator.

title: Cyberbullying Classification for Multilingual Context Using Deep Learning
authors: Gaayathri M, Jenet Shirely J, S Hrudya, Rajasekaran N, Ramkumar S, S. Krithika
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Detecting abusive language in the context of multilingual social media is a very sensitive issue, especially in the case with Indian languages, where code-mixing and linguistic diversity are not an exception. In this paper, a comparative analysis of three deep learning models will be performed, namely an optimized Multilingual BERT (mBERT), XLM-RoBERTa, and a CNN-BiLSTM model with Attention, evaluated on a dataset of the MACD (Multilingual Abusive Comment Dataset) comprising of text in five of the major Indian languages. The same preprocessing pipeline was used to have uniformity in all the models. The experimental results indicate clearly that transformer-based models are much better than the traditional sequence architectures. In their turn, mBERT showed the best performance with the accuracy of 86.08 % and the macro-F1 of 0.860, closely matched with XLM-RoBERTa, which has the macro-F1 of 0.857 and the AUC of 0.935. Conversely, the CNN-BiLSTM-Attention model was less accurate, efficient but limited vocabulary and more OOV. All in all, the comparison illustrates that mBERT and XLM-RoBERTa are the best models to be used in detecting abusive language with robust multi-linguistic support and this explains why they can be used in real-life content moderation tools.

title: Evaluation of the InGaP, GaAs, and Si-Nanowire Multijunction Four-Terminal Bifacial Solar Cell
authors: Sakti Prasanna Muduli, Pranoy Makode, Paresh Kale
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Multijunction bifacial solar cells combine multiple cells to generate power from both the front and rear sides simultaneously. The architecture significantly enhances total energy yield and conversion efficiency beyond the theoretical limit of single-junction cells, making the architecture an up-and-coming option for applications where both high performance and spatial light utilization are critical. The study investigated bifacial solar cell combinations using three single-junction cells (GaAs, InGaP thin-film, and Si-nanowire (SiNW) heterojunction) under reflected light from concrete, dry grass, and black sand. For the double-junction cell, GaAs is preferred as the top cell, with SiNW as the bottom cell for dry grass and black sand, and GaAs for concrete. The triple-junction cell uses an InGaP//GaAs tandem as the top cell, while maintaining the same bottom-cell preferences. Ultimately, selecting between the double- and triple-junction designs depends on balancing the efficiency gain with the increased fabrication cost, providing insight into optimal photovoltaic combinations for commercial bifacial solar cells.

title: Knowledge-Enhanced LLM for Intelligent Power System Fault Handling
authors: Liangcai Zhou, Linlin Liu, Zhihao Sun
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Power system fault handling is time-critical and knowledge-intensive, requiring dispatchers to rapidly retrieve and synthesize guidance from operating procedures, emergency plans, and historical cases. In practice, manual search is slow, error-prone, and sensitive to operator experience, which can degrade decision quality under stress. This paper presents a knowledge-enhanced large language model (LLM) decision-support framework for fault handling based on retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). A domain knowledge base is constructed via data–knowledge fusion, integrating structured fault-related data (e.g., real-time alarms and historical event trajectories) with unstructured expert resources (e.g., operating procedures and disposal plans). For a given operator query, the framework retrieves the most relevant evidence from the knowledge base and conditions the LLM to generate professional, context-grounded responses, thereby reducing unsupported generation and improving answer traceability. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms (i) a general-purpose LLM without domain knowledge enhancement and (ii) conventional manual/keyword-based retrieval in accuracy and relevance for fault-handling queries. The proposed framework provides a practical approach for improving the efficiency and reliability of decision support in power-system dispatching.

title: Monolithic Units: Actuation, Sensing, and Simulation for Integrated Soft Robot Design
authors: Trevor Exley, Anderson Brazil Nardin, Petr Trunin, Diana Cafiso, Lucia Beccai
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: This work introduces the Monolithic Unit (MU), an actuator-lattice-sensor building block for soft robotics. The MU integrates pneumatic actuation, a compliant lattice envelope, and candidate sites for optical waveguide sensing into a single printed body. In order to study reproducibility and scalability, a parametric design framework establishes deterministic rules linking actuator chamber dimensions to lattice unit cell size. Experimental homogenization of lattice specimens provides effective material properties for finite element simulation. Within this simulation environment, sensor placement is treated as a discrete optimization problem, where a finite set of candidate waveguide paths derived from lattice nodes is evaluated by introducing local stiffening, and the configuration minimizing deviation from baseline mechanical response is selected. Optimized models are fabricated and experimentally characterized, validating the preservation of mechanical performance while enabling embedded sensing. The workflow is further extended to scaled units and a two-finger gripper, demonstrating generality of the MU concept. This approach advances monolithic soft robotic design by combining reproducible co-design rules with simulation-informed sensor integration.

title: Edge-Based Facial Recognition System for Smart School Bus Monitoring Using IoT
authors: Dhai eddine Salhi, Majdi Rawashdeh, Awny Alnusair, Ali Karime
publish: 2026 International Conference on AI Innovations and Industry (ICAIII)
abstract: This paper presents an Internet of Things (IoT)-based system for real-time student identification and monitoring in school transportation environments. The proposed solution integrates an ESP32-CAM edge device with an artificial intelligence facial recognition model to automatically identify students when boarding or leaving the school bus. The system architecture includes sensing, gateway, server, and application layers, enabling real-time communication between the bus device, cloud services, and a mobile application used by parents and school administrators. For the facial recognition component, facial encodings were extracted from images and processed using preprocessing techniques such as face alignment, normalization, and data augmentation. Experiments were conducted using a dataset derived from the Labeled Faces in the Wild (LFW) dataset. Several models were evaluated, and an optimized Support Vector Machine (SVM) combined with Principal Component Analysis (PCA) achieved the best performance with an accuracy of 99.60 %. The prototype demonstrates real-time recognition with processing latency below 200 ms, enabling automated attendance and instant notifications. The proposed architecture offers a scalable and cost-effective solution for improving safety and monitoring in school transportation systems.

title: A Hybrid Ai-Driven Framework for Intrusion Prevention in Encrypted Network Traffic
authors: Sameer Sachin Dhande, Nishikant Vijay Gawade, Shreya Santosh Gawade, Mahesh Anandrao Gangarde
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Encrypted network traffic limits payload inspection and makes conventional intrusion prevention less effective. This paper presents a hybrid AI-driven framework that combines flow-level statistical learning with sequence-aware deep inference to detect and prevent malicious activity without decrypting user content. The framework integrates three stages: traffic profiling, multi-model threat inference, and policy-based prevention. In the profiling stage, bidirectional flow features are extracted from encrypted sessions, including temporal behavior, packet size dynamics and connection metadata. In the inference stage, a stacked architecture fuses gradient-boosted classification with a lightweight recurrent detector to improve sensitivity to both known and evolving attack patterns. In the prevention stage, adaptive response policies map model confidence and attack criticality to enforcement actions such as rate limiting, connection quarantine and selective blocking. Experiments on encrypted traffic benchmarks and mixed enterprise traces show that the proposed framework improves detection robustness while maintaining low processing overhead suitable for near real-time deployment. Comparative evaluation demonstrates higher precision-recall balance than single-model baselines, with stable performance under class imbalance and traffic drift. The results indicate that hybrid learning with policy-aware control provides a practical path for privacy-preserving intrusion prevention in modern encrypted networks.

title: Geometry Adaptive Deep Q-Network for UAV-Based Emitter Localization in Cluttered RF Environments
authors: Christopher Peters, Michael Watts, Eric C. Larson, Mitchell A. Thornton
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: This paper presents a prototype geometry-adaptive deep Q-network (DQN) for cooperative unmanned aerial vehicle localization of radio-frequency emitters in dense multipath environments. UAVs act as reconfigurable array elements and are repositioned by a reinforcement learning controller built on time-difference-of-arrival measurements, with rewards shaped by composite uncertainty reduction, signal strength, and trajectory efficiency. Monte Carlo simulations show the DQN reduces error by up to 60 % within fewer repositioning steps as compared to baseline cases. Digital twin evaluations in the Cyber Autonomy Range using Keysight EXata, that incorporates multipath, fading, and hardware inaccuracies, demonstrates rapid convergence in Rician fading and multipath conditions. These results demonstrate robust performance beyond idealized models and establish a foundation for scalable cooperative UAV-based emitter localization systems.

title: Over 25 Years of International Cooperation of Gravity Missions: Past, Present, Future
authors: Neil Dahya, Felix Landerer, William Klipstein, Michael Gross, Omair Khan, David Wiese, Christopher Mccullough, Robert Sharrow, Haley Tooper, Otfrid Liepack, Brent Ware, Nico Brandt, Hauke Thamm
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The GRACE and GRACE-FO Earth missions, launched in 2002 and 2018 respectively, enable monitoring of global mass changes with far-reaching impact on our understanding of Earth's changing water distribution (surface and ground water over land, glacier melt, and sea level change). The nearly continuous monthly data from these satellite gravity missions, through the international partnership and cooperation between NASA/JPL, GFZ, DLR, Airbus, and ONERA, provide the science communities with unique and highly valuable observations of Earth's changing water cycle and climate system, which is now in its <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$3^{\text {rd }}$</tex> decade. Increasingly, this unique knowledge is informing decisionmakers and stakeholders for managing vital water resources sustainably and assessing climate risks, in support of NASA's Earth Science to Action strategy. Identified as a Designated Observable in the 2017 Decadal Survey, the continued observations of Mass Change will be achieved by the NASA-DLR partnership on the forthcoming GRACEContinuity mission, currently in implementation and scheduled for launch in December 2028, to continue this cornerstone climate measurement. Just like the previous GRACE missions, GRACEContinuity (GRACE-C) builds on successful past partnerships with NASA/JPL, GFZ (German Research Center for Geoscience), DLR (German Aerospace Center) and ONERA (French Aerospace Lab), to ensure mission success with a fast-paced schedule and minimum cost impact to NASA. GRACE-C entered Phase C in May of 2024, after completing the System Requirements Review, Mission Design Review and Preliminary Design Review within the previous 12 months. The GRACE-C is currently preparing for System Integration Review at the end of October 2025 and Phase D start in 2026.

title: ECPN: Eccentricity-Compensated Polar Normalization for Iris Image Analysis in NAFLD Prediction
authors: Mahalakshmi, K. Nandhini
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Trends in Advanced Computing (ICCTAC)
abstract: Iris normalization is a very important phase of the iris image analysis process, whether it is identification-oriented or medical. In the existing approach, the pupil is considered to be exactly located in the middle of the iris. But practically, the pupil is not located exactly in the middle of the iris. If the patterns of the iris cannot be scrutinized properly, then the result of the medical test will not be proper. ECPN (Eccentricity-Compensated Polar Normalization) is a newly proposed approach. In this approach, the location of the center of the iris will be corrected dynamically. The results are presented as a pilot study to analyze how the iris normalization impact classification result. When ECPN is applied to the geometric correction of 80 iris samples, it shows the result of 66.5% geometric correction and 10.2% alignment improvement over the existing approach. In the medical test of fatty liver disease, its result improves the accuracy of the test result up to 12.5% (68.8% vs 56.2%). These findings show that proper geometric correction helps obtain the key characteristics necessary for the prediction of fatty liver disease. The approach is very fast, as it takes 0.15 seconds per image.

title: Soft Pop-up Serial Robot with Cable-Hydraulic Hybrid Actuation for Advanced Endoscopy
authors: Hanqi Lou, Ying Shu, Jianlin Yang, Zhangxi Zhou, Mark Runciman, George Mylonas
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: Endoscopic submucosal dissection (ESD) requires compact and dexterous instruments capable of operating safely within confined gastrointestinal spaces. This paper presents a soft serial pop-up robot designed to assist key procedural steps of ESD, including tissue lifting and dissection. The robot employs a hybrid actuation scheme that combines hydraulic pouch inflation for deployment with cable-driven actuation for folding and distal manipulation, and is fabricated from PE/PET/PE laminate films using a rapid, mold-free laser-welding process. The device can be folded into a compact $32 \times 33 \mathrm{~mm}$ profile for insertion, and each modular joint provides up to 100° rotational range. Validation includes open-loop trajectory execution and joystick-based teleoperation, with sub-millimeter mean tracking accuracy achieved in teleoperation. Functional demonstrations on silicone phantom grasping and ex vivo tissue dissection further support the feasibility of hybridly actuated soft serial robots as endoscopic assistive tools.

title: GPU-Accelerated EMI Filtering for High-Speed Automotive Infotainment Streams
authors: Ujwal Kumar N L, Bharath Y S, Arun M
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: Modern electric and autonomous vehicles generate millions of telemetry samples per second, requiring submillisecond latency to detect mechanical friction and electromagnetic interference (EMI). This paper presents a high-throughput, parallelized GPU processing engine engineered for real-time anomaly detection and blind filtering. A thorough review of the literature shows the problems with traditional hardware shielding and CPU-based digital signal processing (DSP). To address this, we offer pseudo code for both existing sequential methods and our suggested parallel architecture. We make it clear what is new by using the pseudo code to show how we are moving from a linear sequential bottleneck to a fully parallel mapped thread execution. We discuss the order of complexity and show how the filtering phase is parallelized under SIMT execution when there are concurrent anomalies. The large, independent nature of frequency bins makes them very suitable for GPU implementation. We explain how we optimized our GPU library and kernel using cuFFT and cuBLAS, as well as how we organized memory using zero-copy Direct Memory Access (DMA) pinned memory to cut VRAM footprints in half. Profiling tools like (nvprof) show that the custom anomaly detection logic only uses 1.36 % of the active GPU window. Results and discussions show that the GPU was 31.64 times faster than the CPU FFTW3 baseline (4.451 ms vs. 140.807 ms) when tested on a dataset with 4.19 million points. It was able to handle multiple faults at the same time while still recovering the base signal.

title: Advancing Green Campus Infrastructure through Multi-Branch Solar Tree Technology: Design, Implementation, and Performance
authors: Maria Enggar Santika, Budihardja Murtianta, Laurentius Fidel Tondang, Faradila Octavia Nabila, Amadeo Wisesa
publish: 2026 16th International Conference on Power, Energy, and Electrical Engineering (CPEEE)
abstract: Universities that are in the green campus program but have limited space for the application of green energy innovation require implementation strategies that maximize the generation of green energy in limited land in an innovative way. This research presents a methodology for improving campus infrastructure through a Multi-Branch Single Stem (MBSS) solar tree architecture specifically designed for sustainable campus energy applications. This research integrates geometric modeling, solar radiation calculations, shadow analysis to calculating the resulting output power and then comparing it with conventional land-based system solar panel models. The resulting data is displayed via the Blynk application. Experimental validation carried out through field monitoring for seven days of the MBSS physical prototype under meteorological conditions in Salatiga, Indonesia showed good performance advantages compared to land-based panel installations. The MBSS configuration gets an average power output of 24.50 W with a system efficiency of 12.27% compared to the land-based panel configuration. Performance analysis found that the three-dimensional branching topology effectively captures direct and diffuse components of solar radiation and minimizes shadows between panels. This solar tree application framework allows higher education institutions to implement renewable energy infrastructure without requiring large areas of land or sacrificing existing campus functions.

title: A Full-Order Model Reduction Method for Large-Capacity Photovoltaic Systems in Weak-Grid Conditions Considering Time-Scale Modes
authors: Xiang Li, Jun Yang, Chaohao Qian, Jingyi Xie, Boxi Li
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: With the increasing penetration of renewable energy resources, weak-grid conditions are becoming increasingly common. As a key power source, large-capacity photovoltaic (LC-PV) systems exhibit inertia-support dynamics with multistage coupling and high-order behavior across multiple time scales, leading to high model complexity and low computational efficiency in stability analysis and control design. To address these issues, this paper considers a grid-connected LC-PV system. First, a full-order small-signal model is developed, and multi-time-scale modes are identified. Then, the full-order model is reduced using singular perturbation theory. Simulation results show that, under typical disturbances, the terminal frequency response of the resulting seventh-order reduced-order model closely matches those of the electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation model and the full-order model. The proposed approach substantially reduces model complexity while maintaining accuracy and interpretability, thereby improving the efficiency of stability analysis and simulation for grid-connected LC-PV systems under weak-grid conditions.

title: Vehicle Detection with Size-Based Categorization Using YOLO Models for Traffic Lane Prioritization
authors: Hendry Anastacia Chandra, Gede Putra Kusuma, Pandu Wicaksono
publish: 2026 International Conference on Current Research in Artificial Intelligence and Data Science (ICCRAIDS)
abstract: Traffic congestion in urban areas remains a major challenge due to increasing vehicle volume and limited road infrastructure, requiring more adaptive traffic monitoring approaches. Recent advances in deep learning enable camera-based vehicle detection systems to support realtime traffic analysis. This study presents a comparative analysis of YOLOv5 and YOLOv8 models for vehicle detection with size-based categorization to support traffic lane prioritization. Four model variants—YOLOv5n, YOLOv5l, YOLOv8n, and YOLOv8l—are evaluated under identical experimental settings using detection accuracy and real-time performance metrics, including mAP@50, mAP@50–95, inference time, and frames per second (FPS). Experimental results show that YOLOv8n achieves a detection accuracy of 0.93 mAP@50 while maintaining real-time performance at approximately 97 FPS, outperforming larger models in terms of inference speed. Large-scale models demonstrate slightly higher localization accuracy, but with significantly lower processing speed. Based on a comprehensive trade-off analysis between accuracy and real-time capability, YOLOv8n is identified as the most suitable model for real-time traffic lane prioritization. The proposed approach demonstrates the potential of size-based vehicle detection to support more adaptive and responsive traffic management systems.

title: Robotic Fruits with Tunable Stiffness and Sensing: Towards a Methodology for Developing Realistic Physical Twins of Fruits
authors: Saitarun Nadipineni, Keshav Pandiyan, Kaspar Althoefer, Shinichi Hirai, Thilina Dulantha Lalitharatne
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th International Conference on Soft Robotics (RoboSoft)
abstract: The global agri-food sector faces increasing challenges from labour shortages, high consumer demand, and supply-chain disruptions, resulting in substantial losses of unharvested produce. Robotic harvesting has emerged as a promising alternative; however, evaluating and training soft grippers for delicate fruits remains difficult due to the highly variable mechanical properties of natural produce. This makes it difficult to establish reliable benchmarks or data-driven control strategies. Existing testing practices rely on large quantities of real fruit to capture this variability, leading to inefficiency, higher costs, and waste. The methodology presented in this work aims to address these limitations by developing tunable soft physical twins that emulate the stiffness characteristics of real fruits at different ripeness levels. A fiber-reinforced pneumatic physical twin of a kiwi fruit was designed and fabricated to replicate the stiffness at different ripeness levels. Experimental results show that the stiffness of the physical twin can be tuned accurately over multiple trials $(97.35-99.43 {\%}$ accuracy). Gripping tasks with a commercial robotic gripper showed that sensor feedback from the physical twin can reflect the applied gripping forces. Finally, a stress test was performed over 50 cycles showed reliable maintenance of desired stiffness ($0.56-1.10 {\%}$ error). This work shows promise that robotic physical twins could adjust their stiffness to resemble that of real fruits. This can provide a sustainable, controllable platform for benchmarking and training robotic grippers.

title: Coordinated Optimization of Wind-Photovoltaic-Storage Configuration for Park Microgrids
authors: Yiwei Xiao, Binyong Cao, Rui Wu, Feng Chen
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The temporal mismatch between wind and photovoltaic output and load demand in park microgrids leads to severe renewable curtailment and high electricity purchase costs. To address this issue, this paper establishes a mixed-integer linear programming model that incorporates the full life-cycle cost of energy storage. The model coordinately optimizes storage power, capacity, and operational strategy to minimize total power supply cost. A case study based on typical daily load and renewable generation data from three parks is conducted, comparing the economic performance of scenarios with no storage, a fixed configuration, and the optimized configuration. The results demonstrate that after optimization, the renewable energy consumption rate in each park reaches 100%, total power supply cost is reduced by 4%–8%, and curtailment is eliminated. The proposed model provides an effective quantitative decision-making tool for energy storage planning in park microgrids.

title: Mission Concept to Track Convective Cloud Systems With Satellite-Based Phased-Array Radars
authors: Jean F. Ghantous, Christopher Williams
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: In recent years, there has been increasing interest in leveraging spaceborne platforms to improve our understanding of convective-core storm systems. NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory (JPL)-led Investigation of Convective Updrafts (INCUS), will deploy a constellation of three nadir-pointing radar-equipped satellites to observe storm evolution as nadir time-height curtains spaced in time by 0,30, and 120 second intervals [1]. While this configuration offers valuable snapshots of convective development, it is inherently limited by sparse temporal sampling per ground location due to the strictly nadir-pointing geometry of each satellite overpass. In contrast to three nadir snapshots of a convective storm, this study proposes an alternative architecture of continuously sampling the convective system at the same geospatial location during a satellite overpass. This concept leverages phased-array antenna (PAR) technology, which enables electronic beam steering to maintain continuous pointing of storm evolution over a 160 second interval. Continuous observation of storm life cycles would enable timeprofile reflectivity factor measurements of cloud systems, offering unprecedented insight into storm structure and evolution. To assess the feasibility of a single satellite mission architecture, this study used current space-based precipitation radar performance as a technology baseline. Specifically, the nadir-pointing INCUS mission and the cross-track scanning capabilities of NASA/JAXA Global Precipitation Mission (GPM) dual-frequency PAR system. We simulated a Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite with the requirement of tracking a single convective storm during an overpass. Using the GPM Ka-band PAR specifications, a storm remains in view for over 160 s as the beam points <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\pm 51^{\circ}$</tex> off-nadir.

title: Evaluation of Crew Emergency Return Capability During Lunar Terrain Excursions Using a Motion Table-Based Vehicle Simulator
authors: Harry L. Litaker, Omar S. Bekdash, Blake S. Chakalis, Lourdes S. Ibrahim, Cory D. Foreman, Lee K. Bingham, Adrienne M. Ondrusek
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: The Lunar Terrain Vehicle (LTV) is an unpressurized lunar rover, capable of carrying two crewmembers and equipment during lunar surface exploration traverses. During these traverses, mission operations must protect for a worst-case contingency of an incapacitated crewmember needing emergency return to a safe haven such as the habitat or crew lander. The LTV can traverse up to <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{1 0}$</tex> kilometers (km) away from the nearest safe haven therefore it is imperative for mission planners to understand how quickly the crew can drive the vehicle back in the event of an emergency. The purpose of the study was to determine the vehicle speeds and duration it would take an LTV operator to complete such a rapid return traverse. The traverse was simulated using the Johnson Space Center's Systems Engineering Simulator (SES) motion table facility. This facility has a 6 degree-of-freedom motion table with a virtual reality headset based lunar South Pole lighting and terrain simulation. The study team collected vehicle speeds, traverse duration, rock collisions with the vehicle, and vibration and jerk data from the simulator, for six test subjects completing the same emergency return traverse. This study is an early look at what is achievable from a vehicle control and safety perspective to provide an understanding of what rescue times may be as the National Aeronautical and Space Administration (NASA) prepares concepts of operations and timelines for Artemis lunar surface missions.

title: Failure Mechanism Analysis and Permanent Magnet Retrofit Strategy for Reducers in Nuclear Power Plant Filtration Systems
authors: Yingfeng Xiong, Yiwei Mao, Hanbin Chen, Jie Yang, Huiqiang Lin, Wu Yang
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: The Circulating Water Filtration System (CFI) is a key component of the ultimate heat sink in nuclear power plants. The reliability of its driving reducer is directly related to the safe and stable operation of nuclear power units. Addressing the frequent failures of CFI vertical reducers in multiple domestic nuclear power units—such as bearing damage, worm shaft fracture, connecting bolt fracture, and excessive motor vibration. This study identifies the rigid cantilever connection as the root cause of component failures. A retrofit strategy utilizing non-contact permanent magnet (PM) transmission is proposed to isolate vibration and eliminate cantilever stress. Finite element analysis (FEA) and experimental verification confirm that the retrofit reduces peak equivalent stress by 86.2% (from 42 MPa to 5.8 MPa) and shifts the first-order natural frequency from 16.3 Hz to 84.2 Hz, effectively avoiding resonance zones.

title: Intelligent Vehicle Surveillance System Using the Combination of ABPR and SSN Algorithms to Achieve Higher Predictive Accuracy in Traffic Safety
authors: Nithya P, Prakasham V, Karthikeyan C, Gokul S, Harish Ragul K, Janasruthi S
publish: 2026 4th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning Applications Theme: Healthcare and Internet of Things (AIMLA)
abstract: This work proposes the use of an Advanced Vehicle Monitor System that incorporates Adaptive Behavior Pattern Recognition techniques in combination with Signal Sensor Normalization. The real-time information of the vehicle operators and the vehicle features is collected by the Internet of Things, and the Hybrid Adaptive Behavior Pattern Recognition-Signal Sensor Normalization approach is used to evaluate the information. The proposed system is validated relative to the Threshold Algorithm approach via statistical testing in SPSS. The experimental results show that the hybrid approach generates better values for accuracy of 88.02 %, Precision of 89.68 %, Recall of 88.92 % and F1-score of 87.40 %. The independent samples <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$t$</tex>-test shows that the improvement in performance is significant at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<0.05$</tex>. The results show that the proposed approach is reliable and effective for monitoring the health of vehicle operators in ensuring smart vehicle safety.

title: An Imitation Based Reinforcement Learning Method for Power Network Reconfiguration Under Random Line Fault Scenarios
authors: Chenhao Zhang, Yu Zou, Xueping Gu
publish: 2026 11th Asia Conference on Power and Electrical Engineering (ACPEE)
abstract: Rapid power network reconfiguration is crucial for minimizing outage duration and mitigating impacts on industrial, commercial, and residential customers during power system restoration after a large–scale blackout. However, in large-scale blackout scenarios caused by extreme disasters, the process is significantly challenged by random line faults, which are prone to occur. To address network reconfiguration under such random fault conditions, the conventional approaches fall short due to the following drawbacks: the pre-set restoration plans are unable to adapt to complex and volatile fault scenarios; optimization-based methods are time-consuming due to numerous iterative calculations; and reinforcement learning-based methods struggle to learn efficiently in complex environments. The introduction of expert strategies can guide the training process of reinforcement learning, thereby enhancing the agent's performance. Consequently, this paper proposes an imitation-based reinforcement learning method that utilizes a two-stage training model, combining imitation learning and reinforcement learning, to accelerate model training and achieve efficient network reconfiguration. The proposed method is validated on the IEEE 39-bus system, and its performance is compared with the traditional optimization algorithms and standard reinforcement learning algorithms. The results show that proposed method exhibits superior solution speed and yields higher-quality power network reconfiguration strategies.

title: Interplanetary Generalization and Adaptation of Deep Stereo on Mars
authors: Yifei Liu, Brandon Rothrock, R. Michael Swan, Hosei O, Sebastian Scherer, Masahiro Ono
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: Stereo vision is critical for the autonomous operation of past and ongoing Mars rovers, including Spirit, Opportunity, Curiosity, and Perseverance. These rovers employ a classical blockmatching stereo algorithm to estimate depth from a pair of navigation cameras for terrain mapping. The choice of this algorithm was motivated primarily by the computational constraints of radiationhardened flight hardware and heritage from previous Mars rover missions. However, recent data from Perseverance have revealed cases where stereo perception was significantly degraded, leading to multiple instances where autonomous navigation was no longer possible. This work makes three contributions to advancing stereo vision for planetary robotics. First, we analyzed Perseverance's onboard stereo algorithm and identified several contributing factors that lead to degraded performance: opposition effect due to low solar phase angle and featureless terrain. Using a dataset curated from the entire Perseverance mission to date, we provide an empirical analysis of stereo performance under these conditions. Second, we evaluated how advanced deep learning-based methods mitigtate these failures through interplanetary generalization and adaptation. We assess: 1) robustness to opposition effect, 2) zero-shot generalizationperformance on Mars of models trained solely on terrestrail data, and 3) rapid adaptation-improvement when fine-tuned with limited Mars data. Representative deep stereo approaches, including cost-volume and iterative optimization methods, transformer-based architectures, and foundation models, demonstrated substantially improved robustness to opposition effect and featureless terrain. For adaptation, models with weaker zero-shot performance improve rapidly across all geological units when trained on images from a single unit. We further analyzed computational trade-offs and found that several models are feasible on existing or near-future spacequalified processors. Third, we publicly release our curated dataset of 16,768 Perseverance NAVCAM stereo pairs with geological unit annotations and onboard stereo's outputs to facilitate future research. These results demonstrate promising potential of deep stereo models for future robotic missions to Mars and beyond.

title: Review of Advanced Informatics and Display Systems for Extravehicular Activity Operations
authors: Joshua T. Elston, Jacob R. Keller, Matthew J. Miller, Susannah B. Paletz, Lauren B. Landon, Paromita Mitra, John A. Karasinski, Jessica J. Marquez
publish: 2026 IEEE Aerospace Conference
abstract: New operational challenges exist for coordination and collaboration amongst spatially and temporally distributed teams as NASA prepares to return to the Lunar surface and beyond to execute ambitious Extravehicular Activities (EVAs). Novel strategies will be required to effectively execute human surface exploration activities where communication and bandwidth latencies disrupt coordination. Additionally, testing novel strategies for EVAs can be challenging, requiring an approximation of distant planetary bodies, specific concepts of operations (ConOps), and prototype information management/decision support technologies. This paper compiles existing literature on EVA analog environments and associated decision support software systems (DSS), providing an overview of the state of the art for simulated EVA mission environments and supporting technologies. Prototype software explicitly supporting EVA planning, execution, and post-hoc analysis was the primary focus, as these capabilities are most aligned with anticipated shifts in crew responsibility and future needs.

title: Influence of Aluminum Oxide $(\text{Al}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3})$ Nanoparticles on the Field-Induced Birefringence and Optical Response of Propylene Carbonate
authors: Zetty Nurazlinda Zakaria, Nuriziani Hussin, Muhammad Shakir Laili
